\text{Savings} &\approx \frac{600{,}000 - 400{,}012}{600{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 33.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

For longer labels ($\ell = 12$), savings exceed **60%** — making factors indispensable in large-scale data engineering pipelines.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing "scalar" in R** — R has no scalar type. State "vector of length 1" or lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing `class()` with `typeof()`** — `class()` is user-defined abstraction; `typeof()` is the storage mode.
> 3. **Skipping the `levels` attribute** when describing factors — it is the defining abstraction layer.
> 4. **Forgetting that data frames are lists** — always mention the equal-length constraint.
> 5. **Not showing method dispatch trace** for S3 questions — examiners want the `UseMethod` step explicitly mentioned.
> 6. **Ignoring coercion rules** — always cite the precedence: `logical → integer → double → character`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

### 🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist
- ✅ **Every R object is a vector or recursive structure** built on atomic types.
- ✅ **Six atomic types:** `numeric`, `integer`, `character`, `logical`, `complex`, `raw`.
- ✅ **Coercion precedence:** `logical < integer < double < character`.
- ✅ **Vectors are 1D homogeneous**; matrices are 2D with column-major storage.
- ✅ **Lists are recursive and heterogeneous**; accessed via `[[ ]]` or `$`.
- ✅ **Data frames are constrained lists** with equal-length columns.
- ✅ **Factors are integer vectors + levels attribute** — preferred for categorical data.
- ✅ **S3 abstraction = `class` attribute + generic dispatch via `UseMethod`**.
- ✅ **S4 abstraction = formal classes with typed slots and validity checks**.
- ✅ **Memory order:** raw < logical < integer < double < character (in bytes).
- ✅ **`typeof()` reveals storage type**; **`class()` reveals abstraction layer**.
- ✅ **`attributes()` exposes metadata** — names, dim, class, levels, dimnames.

### 🧠 Mnemonic for Coercion Ladder
> **"Lions In Den Chased"** = **L**ogical → **I**nteger → **D**ouble → **C**haracter.

### 🎯 Key Board-Exam Verbs to Expect
- "Define / List" (3 marks) → recall
- "Differentiate" (7 marks) → comparison table
- "Explain with example" (7 marks) → code + reasoning
- "Illustrate with diagram" (7 marks) → flowchart + description

> **Total Study Time Estimate:** 2.5 hours for first-pass mastery; 30 minutes for revision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dataset Types: Attribute Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dataset Types: Attribute Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Dataset?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **dataset** is a collection of data objects (also called *records*, *points*, *vectors*, *patterns*, *events*, *cases*, *samples*, *observations*, or *entities*) and their corresponding **attributes** (also called *variables*, *characteristics*, *features*, *fields*, or *dimensions*).

In the context of data mining and visualization, a dataset is best understood through the formal tuple:
$$\text{Dataset} = \{O, A, V\}$$

where:
- $O$ = Set of data objects (rows)
- $A$ = Set of attributes (columns)
- $V$ = Set of values (entries of the data matrix)

The data is typically represented as an $n \times p$ matrix, where $n$ is the number of objects and $p$ is the number of attributes.

### 1.2 What is an Attribute?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> An **attribute** is a data field representing a characteristic or property of a data object. For a person, examples of attributes include *age*, *height*, *weight*, *gender*, and *salary*. Attributes are the columns of the data matrix, while objects are the rows.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a dataset as a **spreadsheet** in Microsoft Excel or a Google Sheet. Each row is a *person* (data object), and each column header is a *characteristic* (attribute) being measured. For example, in a class register:

| Name (Object ID) | Age (Attribute 1) | Gender (Attribute 2) | GPA (Attribute 3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aswin | 20 | Male | 8.5 |
| Divya | 21 | Female | 9.1 |

Here, **Age, Gender, and GPA are attributes**, while the values like "20", "Male", and "8.5" are the **attribute values**. The *type* of each attribute dictates what kind of mathematical operations we can perform on it.

### 1.4 Why Distinguish Attribute Types?

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** Not all attributes are created equal. Applying a **mean** to a "zip code" attribute or a **distance formula** to "gender" is mathematically meaningless. Understanding attribute types is the **first step** in choosing the correct visualization, similarity measure, and statistical operation.

### 1.5 General Attribute Classification

Attributes are broadly classified into two main families:

1. **Categorical (Qualitative) Attributes** — describe a *quality* or *category* (e.g., eye color, blood type, marital status).
2. **Numeric (Quantitative) Attributes** — express a *measurable quantity* expressed as a number (e.g., age, salary, temperature).

Within these, sub-categories exist (nominal, ordinal, discrete, continuous), each with progressively more mathematical structure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dataset Types (KTU Module 1 Coverage)

Datasets come in various structural forms. KTU Module 1 explicitly focuses on recognizing the following dataset types:

#### 2.1.1 Record Data
- A collection of records, each consisting of a fixed set of data fields (attributes).
- This is the standard "tabular" form — the most common dataset structure.
- **Example:** Customer records in a CRM database (Customer ID, Name, Age, Purchase History).

#### 2.1.2 Transaction (Market Basket) Data
- Each record (transaction) contains a set of items.
- Used heavily in association rule mining.
- **Example:** A shopping cart at a supermarket: `{Bread, Butter, Milk}`.

#### 2.1.3 Graph-Based Data
- A graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is a set of nodes (objects) and $E$ is a set of edges (relationships).
- Two flavours: *objects connected by graphs* (e.g., web pages with hyperlinks) and *objects as graphs* (e.g., chemical compounds).
- **Example:** Social networks (Facebook friends), molecular structures.

#### 2.1.4 Ordered (Sequential) Data
- Records that have a temporal or positional ordering.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Sequential Data** (e.g., bioinformatics gene sequences)
  - **Temporal Sequence Data** (e.g., stock prices over time)
  - **Spatial Data** (e.g., geographic information)

#### 2.1.5 Spatial, Temporal, and Spatiotemporal Data
- **Spatial:** Geographic / spatial coordinates (latitude, longitude).
- **Temporal:** Time-indexed data.
- **Spatiotemporal:** Both space and time (e.g., GPS tracking of vehicles).

### 2.2 Attribute Types — Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 Categorical (Qualitative) Attributes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Categorical attributes** take on values that represent *categories* or *labels*. They describe "what kind" rather than "how much."

**Sub-type A: Nominal Attributes**
- Categories with **no intrinsic order**.
- Examples: Eye color {Blue, Brown, Green}, Blood group {A, B, AB, O}, Marital status {Single, Married, Divorced}.
- **Allowed operations:** Mode, Frequency, Contingency tables, $\chi^2$ test.

**Sub-type B: Binary Attributes**
- A special case of nominal with only **two categories**: 0 or 1.
- *Symmetric binary:* Both values carry equal weight (e.g., Gender: Male/Female).
- *Asymmetric binary:* Outcomes are not equally important (e.g., Medical test: Positive/Negative — the rarer outcome is more informative).

**Sub-type C: Ordinal Attributes**
- Categories with a **meaningful order**, but the differences between consecutive values are **not necessarily equal**.
- Examples: Education level {High School < Bachelors < Masters < PhD}, Satisfaction rating {Low, Medium, High}.
- **Allowed operations:** Median, Percentiles, Rank correlation, Non-parametric tests.

#### 2.2.2 Numeric (Quantitative) Attributes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numeric attributes** are measurable quantities expressed numerically. They support arithmetic and algebraic operations.

**Sub-type A: Discrete Attributes**
- Have **finite or countably infinite** set of values.
- Often integers or whole numbers.
- Examples: Number of children, Count of emails, Roll numbers.
- **Allowed operations:** All numeric operations.

**Sub-type B: Continuous Attributes**
- Have **real numbers** as values, often obtained via measurement.
- Can take any value in a range (often a real interval).
- Examples: Height, Weight, Temperature, Salary.
- **Allowed operations:** All numeric operations + calculus (integration, differentiation).

### 2.3 Operations Allowed on Attribute Types

The type of attribute strictly determines which mathematical operations are meaningful.

| Attribute Type | Categorical? | Numeric? | Mode | Median | Mean | Variance | Distance | Correlation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ |
| Binary (Symmetric) | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ (Jaccard) | ✘ |
| Binary (Asymmetric) | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ (Jaccard) | ✘ |
| Ordinal | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ (rank-based) | ✔ (Spearman) |
| Discrete | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ (Euclidean) | ✔ (Pearson) |
| Continuous | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ (Euclidean) | ✔ (Pearson) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** As you move down the table (Nominal → Ordinal → Interval → Ratio), the *amount of structure* and the *number of permissible operations* increase.

### 2.4 Transformation of Attributes

Attributes can sometimes be converted from one type to another:

- **Nominal → Ordinal:** Requires discovering or imposing an order (e.g., sorting blood groups by frequency).
- **Discrete → Continuous:** By binning/interval creation (e.g., age in years → age groups: [0-18, 19-30, 31-60, 60+]).
- **Continuous → Discrete:** By discretization (e.g., temperature in °C → {Cold, Warm, Hot}).

> [!WARNING]
> Information loss is a one-way street. Converting continuous → discrete **loses precision** and cannot be reversed without loss.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Healthcare:** Patient records (record data) + DNA sequences (ordered data) drive diagnostic models.
- **E-Commerce:** Market-basket datasets power recommendation engines.
- **Telecommunications:** Spatiotemporal call-data records (CDR) drive network optimization.
- **Smart Cities:** Spatial + temporal + graph data inform traffic and utility planning.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Notation & Equations:**

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Context |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{dom}(A)$ | Domain of attribute $A$ | Set of all possible values of $A$ |
| $f: O \times A \to V$ | Data function | Maps (object, attribute) to a value |
| $n$ | Number of data objects | Rows in the data matrix |
| $p$ | Number of attributes | Columns in the data matrix |
| $\text{Mean}(\mu)$ | $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Numeric (continuous/discrete) only |
| $\text{Median}$ | Middle value when sorted | Ordinal, Discrete, Continuous |
| $\text{Mode}$ | Most frequent value | All attribute types |
| $\text{Var}(\sigma^2)$ | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$ | Numeric only |
| $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$ | $\text{Cov} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu_X)(y_i - \mu_Y)$ | Numeric only |
| $\rho(X, Y)$ | Pearson correlation | $\rho = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | Numeric only |
| $r_s$ | Spearman rank correlation | Ordinal attributes |
| $J(A, B)$ | Jaccard coefficient | $J = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Binary (asymmetric) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Point:** Pearson correlation requires *interval/ratio* data. For ordinal data, use Spearman's $r_s$. For nominal/binary, use $\chi^2$ test of independence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Mean is Meaningless for Nominal Data

Consider a nominal attribute with domain $\text{dom}(A) = \{\text{Red}, \text{Green}, \text{Blue}\}$.

Assume we encode these as integers: Red = 1, Green = 2, Blue = 3.

Suppose we have 4 data objects with color values: {Red, Green, Blue, Red}.

**Step 1: Compute the arithmetic mean (naively):**
$$\mu = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 1}{4} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75$$

**Step 2: Interpret the result:**
The value "1.75" corresponds to **no real category** in the domain. "1.5 times Red" is meaningless. The mean is a function defined on ordered, additive scales, but the nominal scale has neither order nor additivity.

**Step 3: Conclusion:**
Since there is no intrinsic order, the operation "sum" of categorical labels is undefined. Therefore, the **mean is not a valid statistic** for nominal attributes. The only meaningful statistics are the **mode** and **frequency distribution**.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU exam question** (often framed as "Why can't we compute the mean of 'eye colour'?").

### 3.2 Derivation: Jaccard Coefficient for Asymmetric Binary Attributes

Given two binary vectors $A$ and $B$ representing two objects:

$$A = (1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1)$$
$$B = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0)$$

**Step 1: Identify the four cardinal counts.**
- $f_{11}$ = number of positions where both are 1
- $f_{10}$ = number of positions where $A=1$ and $B=0$
- $f_{01}$ = number of positions where $A=0$ and $B=1$
- $f_{00}$ = number of positions where both are 0

Counting position-by-position (indexed 1 to 7):
- Index 1: $A=1, B=1$ → $f_{11}$
- Index 2: $A=0, B=1$ → $f_{01}$
- Index 3: $A=0, B=0$ → $f_{00}$
- Index 4: $A=1, B=1$ → $f_{11}$
- Index 5: $A=1, B=0$ → $f_{10}$
- Index 6: $A=0, B=1$ → $f_{01}$
- Index 7: $A=1, B=0$ → $f_{10}$

**Step 2: Sum the counts.**

$$f_{11} = 2, \quad f_{10} = 2, \quad f_{01} = 2, \quad f_{00} = 1$$

Verification: $f_{11} + f_{10} + f_{01} + f_{00} = 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7$ ✓ (matches vector length)

**Step 3: Apply the Jaccard formula.**
$$J(A, B) = \frac{f_{11}}{f_{11} + f_{10} + f_{01}} = \frac{2}{2 + 2 + 2} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333$$

**Step 4: Interpret.**
A Jaccard coefficient of 0.333 means that the two objects share 33.3% of their "1"-attributes. The 0-0 matches ($f_{00} = 1$) are **ignored** because the attribute is *asymmetric* (1 = "Yes, has property"; 0 = "No / unknown" — non-1 values carry little weight).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: How to Choose the Right Statistic

A high-yield KTU question is: *"Given a dataset with attributes X, Y, Z, what statistic do you use for each?"*

Use the following decision matrix:

| Attribute Observation | Attribute Type | Recommended Statistic | Forbidden Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Blood group: A/B/AB/O" | Nominal | Mode, Frequency | Mean, Median |
| "Gender: M/F" | Binary (Symmetric) | Mode, Jaccard | Mean |
| "Disease test: +ve / -ve" | Binary (Asymmetric) | Jaccard (ignore neg.) | Mean |
| "Rank: 1st/2nd/3rd" | Ordinal | Median, Spearman | Mean, Pearson |
| "Number of rooms: 3" | Discrete (Numeric) | Mean, Median, Std Dev | None of the basic ones |
| "Height: 172.5 cm" | Continuous (Numeric) | Mean, Variance, Pearson | None of the basic ones |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Allowed Operations

| Property | Nominal | Ordinal | Interval | Ratio (Continuous Numeric) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency of occurrence | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Mode | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Order (Ranking) | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Median, Quartiles | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Summable / Subtractable | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| True Zero Point | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ |
| Mean, Variance, Pearson | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interval vs. Ratio:** Both are numeric. *Interval* data has no true zero (e.g., temperature in °C, where 0°C is arbitrary). *Ratio* data has a meaningful zero (e.g., height = 0 cm means no height). This is why you can say "twice as tall" but **not** "twice as cold" in °C.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Complete Attribute Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Attribute"]:::root
    A --> B["Categorical Qualitative"]:::cat
    A --> C["Numeric Quantitative"]:::num
    B --> D["Nominal"]:::sub
    B --> E["Binary"]:::sub
    B --> F["Ordinal"]:::sub
    E --> G["Symmetric Binary"]:::bin
    E --> H["Asymmetric Binary"]:::bin
    C --> I["Discrete"]:::sub
    C --> J["Continuous"]:::sub
    I --> K["Integer Counts"]:::leaf
    J --> L["Real Valued Measurements"]:::leaf
    classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#00d4ff,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cat fill:#16213e,stroke:#f72585,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef num fill:#16213e,stroke:#06ffa5,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef sub fill:#0f3460,stroke:#ffd60a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bin fill:#533483,stroke:#ff9f1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#2b2d42,stroke:#8d99ae,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Dataset Types Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    DS["Dataset Types"]:::root
    DS --> R["Record Data"]:::rec
    DS --> T["Transaction Data"]:::trx
    DS --> G["Graph Based Data"]:::grf
    DS --> O["Ordered Data"]:::ord
    DS --> ST["Spatiotemporal Data"]:::st
    R --> R1["Fixed Schema Tabular"]:::leaf
    R --> R2["Document Data"]:::leaf
    T --> T1["Market Basket"]:::leaf

    G --> G2["Objects as Graphs Molecules"]:::leaf
    O --> O1["Sequential DNA"]:::leaf
    O --> O2["Temporal Time Series"]:::leaf
    O --> O3["Spatial Geographic"]:::leaf
    ST --> ST1["GPS Tracking"]:::leaf
    ST --> ST2["Weather Maps"]:::leaf
    classDef root fill:#0b132b,stroke:#5bc0be,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rec fill:#1c2541,stroke:#a8dadc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef trx fill:#1c2541,stroke:#e63946,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef grf fill:#1c2541,stroke:#f1faee,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ord fill:#1c2541,stroke:#ffd166,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef st fill:#1c2541,stroke:#06d6a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Operations Permitted by Attribute Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAT["Categorical Domain"]
        N["Nominal"]:::a
        B["Binary"]:::b
        ORD["Ordinal"]:::c
    end
    subgraph NUM["Numeric Domain"]
        D["Discrete"]:::d
        CONT["Continuous"]:::e
    end
    N -->|mode freq| OP1["Categorical Ops"]:::op
    B -->|jaccard| OP1
    ORD -->|median rank| OP2["Ordinal Ops"]:::op
    D -->|mean var| OP3["Full Numeric Ops"]:::op
    CONT -->|mean var corr| OP3
    classDef a fill:#264653,stroke:#e76f51,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef b fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#e9c46a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef c fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef d fill:#f4a261,stroke:#264653,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef e fill:#e76f51,stroke:#f4a261,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef op fill:#003049,stroke:#fcbf49,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Data Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RA["Raw Dataset n x p Matrix"]:::stage1
    PA["Profile Attributes"]:::stage2
    AT["Attribute Type Detection Nominal Ordinal Numeric"]:::stage3
    ST["Statistic Selection Mode Median Mean"]:::stage4
    OP["Preprocessing Encoding Discretization Normalization"]:::stage5
    VZ["Visualization Bar Plot Histogram Heatmap"]:::stage6
    MD["Modeling Clustering Classification Regression"]:::stage7
    RA --> PA --> AT --> ST --> OP --> VZ --> MD
    classDef stage1 fill:#03045e,stroke:#00b4d8,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stage2 fill:#023e8a,stroke:#48cae4,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stage3 fill:#0077b6,stroke:#90e0ef,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef stage4 fill:#0096c7,stroke:#caf0f8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef stage5 fill:#00b4d8,stroke:#ade8f4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef stage6 fill:#48cae4,stroke:#03045e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef stage7 fill:#90e0ef,stroke:#03045e,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Differentiate between nominal and ordinal attributes with examples.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Nominal | Ordinal |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Categories with no intrinsic order | Categories with a meaningful rank |
| Order | Not meaningful | Meaningful |
| Distance | Cannot be measured | Cannot be measured (only order) |
| Example | Eye colour {Blue, Brown, Green} | Education level {High School < Bachelors < PhD} |
| Statistic | Mode, Frequency | Median, Percentile, Rank correlation |

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for key difference, 1 for examples)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. Why is the arithmetic mean not a meaningful statistic for nominal attributes?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. The arithmetic mean requires the values to lie on an *interval or ratio scale* with meaningful additivity and order. Nominal attributes have **no intrinsic order** and **no numerical meaning**.
2. Assigning numeric codes (e.g., Red = 1, Blue = 2) is **purely arbitrary**; the resulting mean (e.g., 1.5) does not correspond to any real category.
3. Hence, the mean is mathematically defined but **semantically meaningless** for nominal data. The only valid statistics are **mode** and **frequency distribution**.

(3 marks: 1 mark each for the three points)

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the different types of datasets used in data mining. List and briefly describe at least **five** types of datasets with one example each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A survey records the following attributes for 6 customers: Customer ID, Gender (M/F), Marital Status (Single/Married), Age (years), Income (₹), and Purchase Frequency (count). Classify each attribute as Nominal, Binary, Ordinal, Discrete, or Continuous, and justify your classification. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Five Types of Datasets (7 marks):**

1. **Record Data:** The standard tabular form where each row is a record and each column is an attribute. All records have the same fixed set of attributes. *Example:* Customer database table with fields (ID, Name, Age, Income).

2. **Transaction / Market-Basket Data:** A special form of record data where each record contains a *set of items*. The focus is on itemset presence, not fixed fields. *Example:* A supermarket transaction list — `{Bread, Milk, Butter}`.

3. **Graph-Based Data:** Represented as a graph $G = (V, E)$. *Objects as nodes with edges* (e.g., web pages connected by hyperlinks), or *objects as graphs themselves* (e.g., chemical compounds as molecular graphs). *Example:* Facebook social network.

4. **Ordered / Sequential Data:** Records where there is a meaningful sequence (temporal or positional). Includes *sequential data* (e.g., DNA strings) and *temporal data* (e.g., stock prices over time).

5. **Spatial Data:** Data containing geographic / spatial information such as latitude, longitude, and elevation. *Example:* A GIS dataset mapping rainfall across a region.

6. **Spatiotemporal Data:** Data combining *both* space and time. *Example:* GPS trajectory of a delivery vehicle recorded at 1-minute intervals.

(Marks: 1 mark for each type + 0.5 each for example = total 7)

---

**(b) Attribute Classification (7 marks):**

| Attribute | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Customer ID | **Nominal / Identifier** | A unique label with no inherent order or arithmetic meaning. |
| Gender (M/F) | **Binary (Symmetric)** | Exactly two categories; both values carry equal weight. |
| Marital Status (Single/Married) | **Binary (Symmetric)** or **Nominal** | Two categories, equal importance. |
| Age (years) | **Continuous Numeric** | Measured on a real scale with a true zero. |
| Income (₹) | **Continuous Numeric** | A measurable monetary quantity on a ratio scale. |
| Purchase Frequency (count) | **Discrete Numeric** | A non-negative integer count. |

**Justification Summary:**

- Customer ID is a *nominal identifier*; even if it's a number, arithmetic on it is meaningless.
- Gender is *symmetric binary* since M and F are equally informative.
- Age, Income, and Purchase Frequency are *numeric*. Age and Income are *continuous* (can take any real value within a range), while Purchase Frequency is *discrete* (counts only).
- Marital Status is *binary* (or nominal if more options like Divorced are added).

(Marks: 1 mark for each correct classification with a brief justification, totalling 7)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **two major categories of attributes** (categorical and numeric) with their sub-types. For each sub-type, give one example and list the **permissible operations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Two customers A and B have the following binary attributes representing their purchase behaviour (1 = bought, 0 = not bought) across 5 product categories: $A = (1, 0, 1, 1, 0)$ and $B = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$. Compute the **Jaccard coefficient** between A and B. Interpret the result. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Attribute Categories & Sub-types (7 marks):**

**1. Categorical (Qualitative) Attributes**
Attributes that represent categories / labels. They are not mathematically additive.

- **Nominal:** No order. *Example:* Eye colour {Blue, Brown}. *Ops:* Mode, Frequency, $\chi^2$ test.
- **Binary:** Two categories. *Example:* Gender {M, F}. *Ops:* Mode, Jaccard coefficient.
- **Ordinal:** Meaningful order, no equal intervals. *Example:* Rank {1st, 2nd, 3rd}. *Ops:* Median, Spearman correlation.

**2. Numeric (Quantitative) Attributes**
Attributes measured on a numerical scale with mathematical structure.

- **Discrete:** Finite / countably infinite integer values. *Example:* Number of children. *Ops:* Mean, Variance, Euclidean distance.
- **Continuous:** Real values in an interval. *Example:* Height (cm). *Ops:* Mean, Variance, Pearson correlation, calculus.

(3 marks for the two major categories; 4 marks for sub-types, examples, and operations)

---

**(b) Jaccard Coefficient Computation (7 marks):**

Given $A = (1, 0, 1, 1, 0)$ and $B = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0)$, length $n = 5$.

**Step 1: Count the four cardinal frequencies.**

| Index | $A_i$ | $B_i$ | Category |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | $f_{11}$ |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | $f_{01}$ |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | $f_{10}$ |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | $f_{11}$ |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | $f_{00}$ |

**Step 2: Aggregate counts.**

$$f_{11} = 2, \quad f_{10} = 1, \quad f_{01} = 1, \quad f_{00} = 1$$

**Step 3: Apply the Jaccard formula.**
$$J(A, B) = \frac{f_{11}}{f_{11} + f_{10} + f_{01}} = \frac{2}{2 + 1 + 1} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

A Jaccard coefficient of **0.5** indicates that customers A and B share **50% of their common purchases**, considering only the items that were bought (i.e., the value 1). The agreement on items *not* bought ($f_{00} = 1$) is intentionally ignored, which is appropriate for **asymmetric binary attributes** where 1 represents the presence of a meaningful event (a purchase).

(1 mark for table, 1 mark for counts, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer, 2 marks for interpretation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Discrete" with "Categorical":** Roll numbers are *discrete numeric*, not categorical.
> 2. **Treating ordinal as numeric:** Calculating the "average" rank (1.5) is conceptually inconsistent.
> 3. **Forgetting $f_{00}$ in Jaccard:** Many students incorrectly include $f_{00}$ in the denominator.
> 4. **Mixing up symmetric and asymmetric binary:** For symmetric binary, $f_{00}$ matters; for asymmetric, it doesn't.
> 5. **Calling zip code "numeric":** It is *nominal* (a categorical identifier), not a number despite appearance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Dataset & Attribute Types**

- **Dataset** = collection of data objects characterized by attributes.
- A dataset is represented as an $n \times p$ **data matrix** ($n$ objects, $p$ attributes).
- **Dataset types:** Record, Transaction, Graph, Ordered (Sequential/Temporal), Spatial, Spatiotemporal.
- **Attribute hierarchy:** Categorical (Nominal, Binary, Ordinal) vs. Numeric (Discrete, Continuous).
- **Nominal:** No order, no arithmetic. Stats: Mode, Frequency.
- **Binary:** Two categories; Symmetric vs. Asymmetric distinction.
- **Ordinal:** Ordered, but unequal intervals. Stats: Median, Spearman.
- **Discrete:** Integer-valued counts. Stats: All numeric.
- **Continuous:** Real-valued measurements. Stats: All numeric + calculus.
- **Mean is forbidden** for nominal, binary, and ordinal attributes.
- **Jaccard coefficient** is used for **asymmetric binary** attributes.
- **Pearson correlation** is valid only for **interval/ratio (numeric)** data.
- **Spearman rank correlation** is the safe choice for **ordinal** data.
- **Information loss** when going Continuous → Discrete is irreversible.
- For **visualization:** Bar charts for categorical, Histograms / Box plots for numeric, Line charts for time series.
- Always **profile attributes** before analysis: type, range, missing values, distribution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semantics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semantics in Data Visualization** refers to the systematic study and formal definition of *meaning* — the rules, mappings, and conventions that govern how data attributes, variables, and their values are translated into perceptually meaningful visual representations (marks, channels, encodings). It is the layer of a visualization that answers the question: *"What does this visual element represent in the real world?"*

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, **Semantics** establishes the **conceptual link** between the **data domain** (the abstract entities and attributes being measured) and the **visual domain** (the graphical marks, positions, colors, sizes, and shapes rendered on screen). Without a well-defined semantic layer, a chart is merely a collection of geometric shapes devoid of interpretive value.

The three foundational pillars of visualization semantics are:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Semantic Types** | Classification of data attributes (Nominal, Ordinal, Quantitative, Temporal) |
| **Semantic Roles** | Assignment of meaning to visual variables (e.g., color = category, length = magnitude) |
| **Semantic Mapping** | The bijective function $f: D \rightarrow V$ that binds data $D$ to visual channels $V$ |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Semantics as a "Translator at the United Nations"** 🌍
>
> Imagine a diplomat (your visualization) speaking two languages simultaneously: **Data Language** (numbers, categories, timestamps) and **Visual Language** (circles, bars, colors, axes). **Semantics is the interpreter** that ensures every word in Data Language is correctly *pronounced* in Visual Language. If the translator gets confused and says "red" when they mean "danger," the whole audience misinterprets the message. Similarly, if you map a *quantitative* variable (like temperature) to a *nominal* visual channel (like shape), the chart loses its precision and meaning.

### 1.3 Why Semantics Matters in Engineering Visualization

For B.Tech engineers analyzing sensor data, simulation outputs, IoT streams, or statistical models, semantic clarity ensures that:

- **Correct Interpretability** — A red dot is *not* accidentally read as "high value" when it actually means "sensor 3."
- **Reproducibility** — Two engineers looking at the same chart derive identical conclusions.
- **Accessibility** — Semantically tagged visualizations can be read by screen readers and assistive technologies.
- **Cognitive Load Reduction** — Well-mapped semantics leverage the human brain's pre-attentive processing capabilities (**~200 milliseconds** for the visual cortex to identify semantic encodings like color and position).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** According to Cleveland & McGill's 1984 perceptual hierarchy (still the gold standard in 2024), the ranking of visual channels by semantic accuracy is:
> Position > Length > Angle > Area > Volume > Color Hue > Color Saturation.
> **KTU frequently tests this hierarchy.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Layers of Visualization Semantics

Visualization semantics can be decomposed into a **three-layer architecture**, as formalized by the Munzner Visualization Analysis framework (the reference model adopted in KTU's reference materials):

1. **Domain Semantics (What does the data mean?)**
   - Defines the *meaning* of every column, row, and cell in the dataset.
   - Example: A column labeled `temperature_C` semantically means "the measure of thermal energy in degrees Celsius at a given timestamp."

2. **Encoding Semantics (How is meaning mapped to visuals?)**
   - Specifies which visual channel carries which data attribute.
   - Example: `temperature_C` → *vertical position (y-axis)*, `timestamp` → *horizontal position (x-axis)*.

3. **Presentation Semantics (How is it styled for human perception?)**
   - Adds non-data ink: titles, legends, axis ticks, color palettes, fonts.
   - Example: Using a *red-to-blue diverging palette* for above/below average temperature.

### 2.2 Semantic Types of Data Attributes

Bertin's **Visual Semiotics** (1967) and the later extensions by Mackinlay classify data into **semantic types**, each with its own valid visual encodings:

| Semantic Type | Definition | Valid Visual Channels |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal (Categorical)** | Discrete labels with no inherent order (e.g., city names, species) | Color hue, shape, spatial region |
| **Ordinal** | Discrete labels with a meaningful order (e.g., low/medium/high) | Position, color saturation, size, density |
| **Quantitative (Interval/Ratio)** | Numeric, with meaningful arithmetic operations | Position, length, area, slope, color luminance |
| **Temporal** | Cyclic or linear time-ordered values | Position along time axis, animation |
| **Spatial/Geographic** | Coordinates tied to physical space | Position on map projection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** Mapping a *Nominal* attribute to a *quantitative* channel (like length) is considered a **semantic violation** and is one of the most frequently penalized mistakes in KTU answer sheets.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the high-yield formula and rule sheet for Semantics, structured for last-minute KTU revision.

| Concept | Mathematical / Formal Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Mapping Function** | $f: A \rightarrow C$ | A function mapping data attribute $A$ to visual channel $C$ |
| **Injectivity Constraint** | $\forall a_1, a_2 \in A: a_1 \neq a_2 \Rightarrow f(a_1) \neq f(a_2)$ | Each unique data value must map to a unique visual value |
| **Perceptual Effectiveness Score** | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot p_i$ | Where $w_i$ is the weight of channel $i$, $p_i$ is its perceptual accuracy |
| **Mackinlay's Ranking (Effectiveness)** | Position > Length > Angle > Area > Slope > Volume > Density > Saturation > Hue | Ordered list of most-to-least accurate visual encodings |
| **Semantic Resolution** | $R = \log_2(N)$ | The number of discriminable steps $N$ in a visual channel |
| **Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio** | $DR = \frac{\text{Data Ink}}{\text{Total Ink}}$ | Higher is better; redundant non-data ink reduces semantic clarity |
| **Semantic Gap** | $G_s = \vert S_{perceived} - S_{actual} \vert$ | Difference between what the viewer perceives vs. what the data actually means |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Rule Compliance:** All vertical bars in mathematical expressions above use the symbol `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In your KTU answer sheet, use proper `|...|` notation in math mode.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing:** Semantic mapping of vibration frequency (quantitative) to color hue (red = danger) on a real-time dashboard allows operators to identify machine faults in **<1 second**.
- **Medical Imaging:** Semantic encoding of tumor density (quantitative) to luminance (brightness) enables radiologists to detect anomalies with **>95% sensitivity**.
- **Smart City IoT:** Semantic role assignment ensures that PM2.5 levels (quantitative) are mapped to **position on a choropleth map**, not to **shape** — preserving perceptual accuracy.
- **Network Security Dashboards:** Nominal attack types are mapped to **distinct color hues** for instant categorization, while the *count* of attacks is mapped to **bar length**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Data to Semantic Visualization

**Step 1 — Identify the Dataset Domain**
A dataset $D$ consists of $n$ records, each with $k$ attributes. Formally:

$$D = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n\}$$

$$r_i = (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k)$$

**Explanation:** Each record $r_i$ is a tuple of $k$ semantic attributes (e.g., `timestamp`, `temperature`, `humidity`, `sensor_id`).

**Step 2 — Classify Each Attribute by Semantic Type**
For each attribute $a_j$, determine its semantic type $T_j \in \{N, O, Q, T, S\}$ (Nominal, Ordinal, Quantitative, Temporal, Spatial).

$$T_j = \text{classify}(a_j)$$

**Explanation:** This is the *Domain Semantics* step — assigning meaning to data. For example, `sensor_id = "S03"` is classified as **Nominal** because it is a label with no order.

**Step 3 — Choose Visual Channels for Each Attribute**
Select a visual channel $c_j$ from the available set $C = \{$position, length, angle, area, color hue, color saturation, shape, slope$\}$ such that $c_j$ is semantically valid for $T_j$.

**Explanation:** This is the *Encoding Semantics* step. The rule: $c_j$ must be drawn from the "Valid Visual Channels" column of the table in Section 2.2.

**Step 4 — Define the Semantic Mapping Function**
Construct the mapping function $f$ that pairs each attribute to its visual channel:

$$f: A \rightarrow C, \quad f(a_j) = c_j$$

**Explanation:** The function must be **injective** (one-to-one) — two different data values cannot map to the same visual mark, or the semantic meaning is lost.

**Step 5 — Apply the Mapping to Render the Visualization**
For each record $r_i$, instantiate the visual mark $m_i$:

$$m_i = \text{render}(f(a_1), f(a_2), \dots, f(a_k))$$

**Explanation:** The mark is the on-screen geometric primitive (point, bar, line, etc.) whose visual properties are determined by the semantic mapping.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Semantic Channel Effectiveness

Mackinlay (1986) assigned **perceptual effectiveness scores** based on Cleveland & McGill's experimental results. Below is the formal scoring for a typical quantitative encoding task:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{position}} &= 1.00 \quad \text{(most accurate, baseline reference)} \\
E_{\text{length}} &= 0.965 \\
E_{\text{angle}} &= 0.900 \\
E_{\text{area}} &= 0.690 \\
E_{\text{volume}} &= 0.610 \\
E_{\text{hue}} &= 0.400 \\
E_{\text{saturation}} &= 0.350 \quad \text{(least accurate for quantitative data)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation Logic:** Each score is the proportion of correct answers given by human subjects when asked to compare two marks encoded with the channel. Position is normalized to 1.0 because it is the **gold standard** for quantitative judgment.

**Engineering Interpretation:** If you are visualizing **wind speed data**, using `length` (bars) is **~2.5x more accurate** than using `color hue` (e.g., green/blue/red) for the same purpose. This is why bar charts dominate engineering dashboards.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Resolving Semantic Ambiguity

**Problem:** A dataset has 8 sensors. We want to encode the sensor ID (nominal) and the battery percentage (quantitative 0–100%). We have access to: color hue, position, size.

**Step 1 — Semantic type classification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sensor\_id}} &= \text{Nominal} \\
T_{\text{battery}} &= \text{Quantitative}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Constraint check (injectivity for nominal):**
Hue can encode $\log_2(12) \approx 3.58$ discriminable steps at comfortable viewing — for **8 sensors**, this is acceptable (8 unique hues available in a categorical palette).

**Step 3 — Constraint check (accuracy for quantitative):**
Battery should be mapped to **position** (score 1.00) rather than **size** (score 0.690) or **hue** (score 0.400) to maximize perceptual accuracy.

**Step 4 — Final semantic mapping:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f(\text{sensor\_id}) &= \text{color hue (categorical palette)} \\
f(\text{battery}) &= \text{vertical position (y-axis)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Resulting Visual:** A scatter plot with sensor names encoded by distinct colors and battery levels encoded by vertical position. **Semantic accuracy = 1.00 (position) + 0.40 (hue) = 1.40 composite score.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Semantic Mapping Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end semantic pipeline** from raw data to rendered visualization, with each block representing a semantic transformation step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D] --> B[Domain Semantics Layer]
    B --> B1[Attribute Classification]
    B1 --> B2[Identify Semantic Types]
    B2 --> B3{Nominal?}
    B2 --> B4{Ordinal?}
    B2 --> B5{Quantitative?}
    B2 --> B6{Temporal?}
    B3 --> C[Encoding Semantics Layer]
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C
    C --> C1[Select Visual Channel per Attribute]
    C1 --> C2[Define Mapping Function f]
    C2 --> D[Presentation Semantics Layer]
    D --> D1[Add Axes, Legends, Titles]
    D --> D2[Apply Color Palettes]
    D --> D3[Optimize Data Ink Ratio]
    D1 --> E[Final Visualization]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Human Perceptual System]
    F --> G[Semantic Interpretation]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
    style G fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

### 4.2 Semantic Type to Visual Channel Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Data Attribute] --> B{Identify Type}
    B -->|Nominal| C1[Color Hue]
    B -->|Nominal| C2[Shape]
    B -->|Nominal| C3[Spatial Region]
    B -->|Ordinal| D1[Position on Ordered Axis]
    B -->|Ordinal| D2[Color Saturation]
    B -->|Ordinal| D3[Size Ordered]
    B -->|Quantitative| E1[Position X Y]
    B -->|Quantitative| E2[Length]
    B -->|Quantitative| E3[Angle Slope]
    B -->|Temporal| F1[Horizontal Position Time Axis]
    B -->|Temporal| F2[Animation Frame]

    style A fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style B fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
    style C1 fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#004d40
    style C2 fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#004d40
    style C3 fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#004d40
    style D1 fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#1a237e
    style D2 fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#1a237e
    style D3 fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#1a237e
    style E1 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00
    style E2 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00
    style E3 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00
    style F1 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style F2 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

### 4.3 The Semantic Gap — Perceptual vs. Data Reality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actual Data Value: 87.5 degrees C] --> B[Semantic Mapping f]
    B --> C[Visual Mark: Long Red Bar]
    C --> D[Viewer Perception]
    D --> E{Interpretation}
    E -->|Correct| F[Reads 87.5 C]
    E -->|Semantic Gap Exists| G[Misreads as 50 C or 120 C]
    F --> H[Small Gap Gs near 0]
    G --> I[Large Gap Gs significantly above 0]

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define **Semantics** in the context of data visualization. List any **four semantic types** of data attributes with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Semantics in data visualization refers to the rules and mappings that define the meaning of visual elements in terms of the underlying data attributes. It bridges the data domain and the visual domain by specifying what each visual mark represents.

> **Semantic types (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Nominal** — discrete categories with no order (e.g., city name: "Kochi", "Trivandrum")
> 2. **Ordinal** — ordered categories (e.g., rating: "Poor", "Average", "Good")
> 3. **Quantitative** — numeric values with arithmetic meaning (e.g., temperature: 27.5°C)
> 4. **Temporal** — time-ordered values (e.g., timestamp: 2024-03-15 14:30)

---

**Q2.** State **Cleveland & McGill's perceptual hierarchy** of visual encodings in decreasing order of accuracy. Why is **position** considered the most accurate channel?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Hierarchy (2 marks):** Position > Length > Angle > Area > Slope > Volume > Density > Color Saturation > Color Hue.

> **Why position is most accurate (1 mark):** Position along a common scale leverages the human visual cortex's highly developed ability to perform **subtractive comparison** (judging the difference between two aligned points). Studies show that viewers can decode positional differences in **<100 ms** with near-perfect accuracy, making it the gold standard for encoding quantitative information.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Explain the **three layers of visualization semantics**: Domain Semantics, Encoding Semantics, and Presentation Semantics. Provide a real-world engineering example for each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing IoT system records **5 attributes** for 1000 machines: `machine_id` (string), `operating_temperature_C` (float), `pressure_kPa` (float), `error_code` (categorical: "E01"–"E05"), and `timestamp` (datetime). Design a complete semantic mapping specification for a multi-attribute dashboard. Justify each channel choice using Mackinlay's effectiveness scores. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Three Layers of Semantics (7 marks):**

**Layer 1 — Domain Semantics (2 marks):**
This layer defines *what the data means* in the real world. It involves identifying entities, attributes, and their relationships in the dataset.

> *Engineering Example:* In a structural health monitoring system, the dataset contains attributes like `vibration_amplitude_mm` (semantically: "the magnitude of physical oscillation of a bridge joint, measured in millimeters") and `load_tons` (semantically: "the static weight applied to the bridge deck in metric tons").

**Layer 2 — Encoding Semantics (2.5 marks):**
This layer specifies *how* data attributes are translated into visual channels such as position, length, color, or shape. The mapping must respect the semantic type constraints (e.g., quantitative data → position/length, nominal data → hue/shape).

> *Engineering Example:* The `vibration_amplitude_mm` is encoded by **vertical position** on the y-axis (effectiveness score = 1.00), while `machine_status` (Running/Idle/Failed) is encoded by **color hue** — green/grey/red (effectiveness score = 0.40 for categorical differentiation).

**Layer 3 — Presentation Semantics (2.5 marks):**
This layer refines the visualization for human perception by adding non-data elements: titles, axis labels, legends, color palettes, gridlines, and annotations. The goal is to maximize the **data-ink ratio** while ensuring clarity.

> *Engineering Example:* A colorblind-safe diverging palette (blue → white → red) is applied to the vibration amplitude so that engineers with deuteranopia can still interpret the chart correctly. The title reads "Bridge Joint Vibration — Last 24 Hours" and the y-axis is labeled in mm with major gridlines every 0.5 mm.

---

**(b) Semantic Mapping for the Manufacturing IoT Dashboard (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Semantic Type Classification (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{machine\_id}} &= \text{Nominal} \\
T_{\text{operating\_temperature\_C}} &= \text{Quantitative} \\
T_{\text{pressure\_kPa}} &= \text{Quantitative} \\
T_{\text{error\_code}} &= \text{Nominal} \\
T_{\text{timestamp}} &= \text{Temporal}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Channel Assignment with Justification (3 marks):**

| Attribute | Semantic Type | Visual Channel | Effectiveness Score | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `timestamp` | Temporal | Horizontal position (x-axis) | 1.00 | Time-series alignment requires position |
| `operating_temperature_C` | Quantitative | Vertical position (y-axis) | 1.00 | Highest accuracy for magnitude comparison |
| `pressure_kPa` | Quantitative | Point size (bubble area) | 0.69 | Acceptable secondary quantitative channel |
| `machine_id` | Nominal | Color hue (categorical palette) | 0.40 | Only valid encoding for nominal labels |
| `error_code` | Nominal | Shape (circle/triangle/square) | 0.40 | Shape distinguishes sub-categories within hue group |

**Step 3 — Composite Effectiveness Verification (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{composite}} &= 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.69 + 0.40 + 0.40 \\
&= 3.49 \text{ (out of theoretical max 5.00)}
\end{aligned}$$

This score is acceptable for a multi-attribute dashboard where the trade-off between dimensionality and perceptual accuracy is necessary.

**Final Visualization:** A bubble chart with time on x-axis, temperature on y-axis, pressure encoded by bubble size, machine ID by color, and error codes by shape — a **semantically valid, multi-attribute engineering dashboard**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss the concept of the **semantic gap** in data visualization. How does it affect decision-making in engineering systems, and what are **three design strategies** to minimize it? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the use of **color hue** versus **position** for encoding a quantitative variable such as `voltage_V` in a circuit monitoring dashboard. Use **Mackinlay's effectiveness scores** and the concept of **semantic resolution** in your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) The Semantic Gap (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The semantic gap is the discrepancy between the *information intended to be conveyed* by the visualization designer and the *information actually perceived* by the viewer. Formally:

$$G_s = \vert S_{\text{perceived}} - S_{\text{actual}} \vert$$

**Impact on Engineering Decision-Making (2 marks):**
A large semantic gap can lead to:
- **False alarms** — A red color encoded to mean "warning" might be perceived as "critical," triggering unnecessary shutdowns.
- **Missed alerts** — A subtle saturation difference for "high temperature" might be overlooked, causing equipment damage.
- **Misdiagnosis** — In medical device dashboards, semantic gaps can lead to incorrect clinical interpretations.

**Three Design Strategies to Minimize the Gap (3 marks):**
1. **Use high-accuracy channels** for critical quantitative data — prefer position (score 1.00) over hue (score 0.40).
2. **Add redundant encoding** — combine color hue with text labels or icon shapes to reinforce meaning.
3. **Apply perceptual uniformity** — use perceptually uniform colormaps like Viridis instead of rainbow palettes, ensuring equal perceptual steps across the data range.

---

**(b) Color Hue vs. Position for `voltage_V` (7 marks):**

**Effectiveness Comparison (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{position}} &= 1.00 \\
E_{\text{hue}} &= 0.40 \\
\Delta E &= 1.00 - 0.40 = 0.60 \quad \text{(60% accuracy advantage for position)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Semantic Resolution Analysis (2 marks):**
For a voltage range of 0–250 V with step size 5 V (50 distinct values):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{position}} &= \log_2(50) \approx 5.64 \text{ bits} \\
R_{\text{hue}} &= \log_2(12) \approx 3.58 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(typical categorical palette)}
\end{aligned}$$

Position can resolve **2x more discriminable steps** than hue at standard viewing conditions.

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
For `voltage_V` (a quantitative variable with high precision requirements), **position is unequivocally the superior semantic channel**. Hue should be reserved for *secondary* categorical groupings (e.g., nominal labels like "Phase A", "Phase B", "Phase C"). The composite dashboard should pair both: position for voltage magnitude, hue for phase identification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "semantics = meaning of colors" — this is *too narrow*. Semantics is the **entire mapping system**, not just color. Expect to **lose 1 mark** for this in 3-mark questions and **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Conflating *semantic types* with *data types* (e.g., calling a string "quantitative" because it contains numbers like "007"). Remember: **semantic type is about meaning, not representation**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to provide *engineering examples*. KTU examiners reward application context — purely abstract answers lose 20–30% of available marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not using the exact term "semantic mapping function $f$" in long answers. The notation is a **high-scoring keyword** that signals depth of understanding.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **Semantics** is the **meaning layer** of visualization — it binds *data* to *visual marks*.
- The **three layers** are: **Domain → Encoding → Presentation**.
- **Semantic types**: Nominal, Ordinal, Quantitative, Temporal, Spatial.
- **Cleveland & McGill hierarchy**: Position > Length > Angle > Area > Volume > Density > Saturation > Hue. **Position is king.**
- The **semantic mapping function** is $f: A \rightarrow C$, and it must be **injective**.
- **Mackinlay's scores** (0.40 to 1.00) quantify the perceptual effectiveness of each visual channel.
- **Semantic gap** $G_s = \vert S_{\text{perceived}} - S_{\text{actual}} \vert$ must be minimized through high-accuracy channels and redundant encoding.
- **Nominal data → hue/shape; Ordinal data → saturation/ordered position; Quantitative data → position/length.**
- **Semantic resolution** $R = \log_2(N)$ determines the number of discriminable steps.
- For **R-based visualization**, use `aes()` mappings in **ggplot2** (e.g., `aes(x = timestamp, y = voltage, color = machine_id, size = pressure)`).
- **Tufte's data-ink ratio** should be maximized — remove chartjunk that adds no semantic value.
- In KTU exams, always **define, classify, justify, and exemplify** — this 4-step structure guarantees full marks on semantics questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Abstraction: Analyse, Produce, Search, Query
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Task Abstraction: Analyse, Produce, Search, Query

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Task Abstraction** is the foundational first step in the visualization design methodology (formalized by Tamara Munzner, *Visualization Analysis and Design*, MIT Press) where the **domain-specific question** raised by a user is translated into a **generic, domain-independent visualization task**. It decouples *what* the user wants to know from *how* the data should be visually encoded.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, task abstraction is the **bridge between raw domain data and the visual encoding pipeline**. It answers the question: *"What action does the user perform on the data?"*

The four canonical high-level task categories (per the KTU syllabus and Munzner's taxonomy) are:

| # | Task Type | Action Direction | User Goal |
|---|-----------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Analyse** | Consume (read existing data) | Confirm, understand, compare existing information |
| 2 | **Produce** | Produce (generate new data) | Create new data records, derivations, or annotations |
| 3 | **Search** | Consume (find specific target) | Locate a known/unknown target within the dataset |
| 4 | **Query** | Consume (filter/aggregate) | Identify a subset matching a constraint |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a massive library (your dataset).
> - **Analyse** → You are a *critic* reading existing books to understand the library's collection.
> - **Produce** → You are an *author* writing new books to add to the shelves.
> - **Search** → You are a *detective* looking for one specific book (you may or may not know its exact title).
> - **Query** → You are a *researcher* asking *"show me all books published after 2020 on machine learning"*.

The crucial insight: **Analyse, Search, and Query are *consumption* tasks (the user reads existing data)**, whereas **Produce is the only *generation* task** (the user creates new data).

### 1.3 Why Task Abstraction Matters in R

In R (`ggplot2`, `shiny`, `plotly`, `tidyverse`), correctly identifying the task type determines:
- The **geom layer** chosen (e.g., `geom_point` for search, `geom_bar` for query aggregation).
- The **interactive widget** used (e.g., `selectInput` for query, `brush` for search).
- The **shiny reactivity model** (eventReactive for search, observeEvent for produce).
- The **algorithm complexity** expected (O(1) lookup vs. O(n) browse vs. O(log n) indexed search).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often frame questions as *"Given a user wants to find outliers in a sales dataset, classify the task type."* The four words *Analyse, Produce, Search, Query* are the *only* permitted first-level answers in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Dive into Each Task Type

#### 2.1.1 ANALYSE (Consume → Confirm/Understand)
- **Definition:** The user consumes *all* the data (or a large subset) to draw conclusions, confirm a hypothesis, or build mental models.
- **User Intent:** *"I want to understand the overall behaviour of the data."*
- **Visual Idioms:** Scatter plots, histograms, box plots, parallel coordinates, dashboards.
- **Sub-tasks (per Munzner):** `Consume → Present → Encode → Arrange → ...` and the *Analyze* family includes: **Discover**, **Present**, **Enjoy**, **Annotate**, **Record**.
- **R Implementation:** `ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) + geom_point()` for exploratory analysis.

#### 2.1.2 PRODUCE (Generate → Create New Data)
- **Definition:** The user *creates* new data items — annotations, derived columns, model outputs, or new records.
- **User Intent:** *"I want to add something new to the dataset or derive a new value."*
- **Visual Idioms:** Drawing tools, annotation layers, model-output overlays, `geom_smooth` regression bands.
- **Sub-tasks:** **Record** (save findings), **Annotate** (mark important points), **Derive** (compute new attributes).
- **R Implementation:** `mutate()`, `geom_text()`, `geom_smooth(method = "lm")`, `shiny` input widgets that write to `reactiveValues()`.

#### 2.1.3 SEARCH (Consume → Find a Target)
- **Definition:** The user wants to find *one or more specific targets* (data points, records, features).
- **Three Sub-Modes (Must Memorize for KTU):**

| Search Sub-Type | Target Knowledge | Example |
|-----------------|------------------|---------|
| **Lookup** | Known identifier/ID | *"Show me customer ID 4521"* |
| **Locate** | Known property, unknown ID | *"Show me all red dots on the map"* |
| **Browse** | Unknown target, exploratory | *"Show me what's there"* |

- **R Implementation:** `filter()`, `grep()`, `which()`, `nearPoints()` in `shiny` for click-based search.

#### 2.1.4 QUERY (Consume → Filter/Aggregate)
- **Definition:** The user poses a *boolean or range constraint* and expects a *subset or summary* in return. It is **not about finding one specific target** but about identifying a *set* defined by properties.
- **Three Sub-Modes:**

| Query Sub-Type | Output | Example |
|----------------|--------|---------|
| **Identify** | A subset of points matching a property | *"Which districts have rainfall > 3000 mm?"* |
| **Compare** | Two or more subsets side-by-side | *"Compare sales of North vs. South region"* |
| **Summarize** | An aggregate statistic | *"What is the average income of group X?"* |

- **R Implementation:** `dplyr::filter()`, `group_by() %>% summarise()`, `dplyr::count()`.

### 2.2 The "Consume vs. Produce" Binary — A Crucial Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most-tested KTU concept:** *Analyse, Search, and Query are **consumption** tasks; **Produce** is the only **generation** task.* If the user's action *creates* a new data element, the answer is **Produce**. Otherwise, it is one of the three consumption tasks.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the entire task-abstraction decision logic into a single cheat-sheet for KTU 2024 ESE preparation.

| Question the User Asks | Task Type | R Function Family | Visual Idiom |
|------------------------|-----------|-------------------|--------------|
| "What is happening overall?" | **Analyse** | `summary()`, `str()`, `ggplot()` | Histogram, scatter, boxplot |
| "What is the trend / relationship?" | **Analyse (Present)** | `cor()`, `geom_smooth()` | Line chart, regression overlay |
| "Add a label / annotation / new column" | **Produce** | `mutate()`, `geom_text()` | Annotated chart, dashboard widget |
| "Find the row where ID = 4521" | **Search (Lookup)** | `filter(id == 4521)`, `which()` | Highlighted single point |
| "Find all rows where city = 'Kochi'" | **Search (Locate)** | `filter(city == "Kochi")` | Highlighted multiple points |
| "Let me explore freely" | **Search (Browse)** | `shiny` brush, `ggplotly` | Linked scatter, pan & zoom |
| "Show me all with sales > 1 lakh" | **Query (Identify)** | `filter(sales > 100000)` | Filtered scatter/table |
| "Compare Q1 vs Q2 sales" | **Query (Compare)** | `group_by(quarter) %>% summarise()` | Grouped bar / box |
| "Average sales by region" | **Query (Summarize)** | `summarise(mean(sales))` | Aggregated bar |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Dashboards (Analyze):** Monitoring patient vitals — read-only consumption.
- **CAD Tools (Produce):** Engineer draws new geometric primitives — generation.
- **Geological Survey (Search):** Geologist locates mineral deposits — target finding.
- **SQL-based BI Reports (Query):** *"Total revenue from South region last month"* — boolean filtering + aggregation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Algorithmic Derivation of Task Identification

Since *Task Abstraction* is a classification methodology, the "derivation" here is the **decision procedure** for mapping a domain question to a task type. Below is the step-by-step logical chain.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the Direction of Data Flow**

$$
D_{flow} = \begin{cases} \text{Produce}, & \text{if the user action creates a new datum} \\ \text{Consume}, & \text{if the user reads an existing datum} \end{cases}
$$

> **Logic:** We first ask: *Is the user a writer (Producer) or a reader (Consumer)?* If the answer is "writer — they are adding a label, a row, or a derived column," the task is **Produce**. This is the *first and most decisive* branch in the KTU examiner's expected solution path.

---

**Step 2 — If Consume, Identify the Cardinality of the Expected Output**

$$
N_{output} = \begin{cases} N = 1, & \text{Search (Lookup)} \\ 1 < N < \vert D \vert, & \text{Query} \\ N = \vert D \vert, & \text{Analyse} \end{cases}
$$

where $\vert D \vert$ denotes the cardinality (total size) of the dataset $D$.

> **Logic:** The second question is: *How many records does the user want to see?*
> - If they want **all** records (or a global summary), it is **Analyse**.
> - If they want a **subset defined by a property** (not a specific ID), it is **Query**.
> - If they want a **specific target** (one item or a few identified items), it is **Search**.

---

**Step 3 — Within Search, Determine the Target Knowledge Level**

$$
K_{target} = \begin{cases} \text{ID known}, & \text{Lookup} \\ \text{Property known, ID unknown}, & \text{Locate} \\ \text{Target unknown, exploratory}, & \text{Browse} \end{cases}
$$

> **Logic:** The third question is: *Does the user already know what they are looking for?*
> - **Lookup:** "Show me transaction TX-9921" (ID is given).
> - **Locate:** "Show me all transactions over ₹50,000" (property is given, but the actual records are not pre-known).
> - **Browse:** "Just show me everything" (no target specified).

---

**Step 4 — Within Query, Identify the Output Operation**

$$
O_{query} = \begin{cases} \text{Filter only}, & \text{Identify} \\ \text{Two-set contrast}, & \text{Compare} \\ \text{Statistical aggregate}, & \text{Summarize} \end{cases}
$$

> **Logic:** The fourth question is: *What does the user do with the filtered subset?*
> - **Identify:** Simply mark the subset (e.g., highlight points above a threshold).
> - **Compare:** Place two or more subsets side-by-side.
> - **Summarize:** Reduce the subset to a single statistic (mean, median, count, sum).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Style

> **Domain Question:** *"A data scientist wants to mark the top 5 customers in a sales database with a red star and save these annotations for future reports."*

**Applying Step 1:**

$$
D_{flow} = \text{Produce} \quad \text{(the user creates new annotations)}
$$

**Why it is NOT Search or Query:** Although "top 5 customers" *sounds* like a query, the *primary action* is the *creation of star annotations* — a new visual element. The filtering is a *sub-step* that supports the *generation* task. KTU examiners award full marks only when students correctly identify the **primary** task, not the supporting sub-step.

**R Implementation Sketch:**

```r
top5 <- sales_data %>% arrange(desc(revenue)) %>% slice(1:5)
ggplot(sales_data, aes(x = spend, y = revenue)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_point(data = top5, color = "red", size = 4, shape = 8) +
  geom_text(data = top5, aes(label = customer_id), vjust = -1)
```

The `geom_point(... shape = 8)` and `geom_text(...)` calls are the **Produce** layer; the `filter`-equivalent (`slice(1:5)`) is merely a helper.

---

### 3.3 Complexity Cheat-Sheet for Search and Query

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the **computational complexity** of search/query operations. Memorize the following:

$$
T_{lookup}(n) = O(1) \quad \text{(hash-indexed ID retrieval)}
$$

$$
T_{locate}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(linear scan over all } n \text{ records)}
$$

$$
T_{query}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(full table scan for boolean filter)}
$$

$$
T_{analyse}(n) = \Theta(n) \quad \text{(must visit every datum for summary statistics)}
$$

> **Key Insight:** Analyse tasks are *unavoidably* linear in $n$ because, by definition, they consume the entire dataset. Search and Query can be sub-linear *only* when auxiliary data structures (hash maps, B-trees) are pre-built.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Task Abstraction Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Domain Question] --> B{Is user creating new data?}
    B -- Yes --> P1[PRODUCE]
    B -- No --> C{Consumption task}

    C --> D{How many records desired?}
    D -- All or summary --> AN1[ANALYSE]
    D -- A specific subset --> E{Subset defined by property or target?}

    E -- By a specific target --> F{Target identifier known?}
    F -- Yes, ID given --> S1[SEARCH - LOOKUP]
    F -- No, just a property --> S2[SEARCH - LOCATE]
    F -- No target, just explore --> S3[SEARCH - BROWSE]

    E -- By a constraint --> G{Output operation type?}
    G -- Just mark the subset --> Q1[QUERY - IDENTIFY]
    G -- Compare two subsets --> Q2[QUERY - COMPARE]
    G -- Reduce to a statistic --> Q3[QUERY - SUMMARIZE]

    P1 --> R1[R: mutate, geom_text, geom_smooth]
    AN1 --> R2[R: ggplot, hist, summary]
    S1 --> R3[R: filter id == X, which]
    S2 --> R4[R: filter city == K, grep]
    S3 --> R5[R: shiny brush, plotly zoom]
    Q1 --> R6[R: dplyr filter, select]
    Q2 --> R7[R: group_by then compare]
    Q3 --> R8[R: summarise, count, mean]
```

### 4.2 Consume-vs-Produce Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONSUME["CONSUMPTION TASKS (Read-Only)"]
        AN[Analyse]
        SR[Search]
        QR[Query]
    end
    subgraph PRODUCE["PRODUCTION TASK (Write)"]
        PR[Produce]
    end
    DATA[(Dataset D)] --> AN
    DATA --> SR
    DATA --> QR
    PR --> DATA
    AN -.derives insight.-> USER((User))
    SR -.finds target.-> USER
    QR -.filters subset.-> USER
    PR -.adds annotation.-> USER
```

### 4.3 Search Sub-Task Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Search Task Entry] --> CK{User knows target ID?}
    CK -- Yes --> LK[LOOKUP - O of 1 via hash index]
    CK -- No, knows property --> LC[LOCATE - O of n linear scan]
    CK -- No, exploratory --> BR[BROWSE - interactive UI pan zoom]
    LK --> EX1[Example: Show customer 4521]
    LC --> EX2[Example: Show all red points]
    BR --> EX3[Example: Let me explore the map]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU:** In ESE answers, always pair each task name with **(a) the cardinality question** and **(b) the R idiom**. A full-mark answer typically includes a small ASCII or hand-drawn version of the decision tree above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]`
**Define task abstraction in data visualization. Differentiate between "Search" and "Query" tasks with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Task abstraction is the process of translating a domain-specific question into a generic, domain-independent visualization task, decoupling the *what* (intent) from the *how* (visual encoding).
- **Search vs. Query (2 marks):**
  - **Search** finds a *specific target* by ID or known property (e.g., *"Show me transaction TX-9921"*).
  - **Query** identifies a *subset defined by a constraint* (e.g., *"Show me all transactions over ₹50,000"*).
  - The key difference: Search targets a **known specific record**; Query targets a **set defined by a property**.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**Why is "Produce" considered fundamentally different from Analyse, Search, and Query? Illustrate with an R example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fundamental difference (2 marks):** Produce is the *only generation task* — the user *creates* new data (annotations, derived columns, model outputs). Analyse, Search, and Query are *consumption tasks* — they only read existing data.
- **R example (1 mark):** `df <- df %>% mutate(profit = revenue - cost)` and `ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + geom_text(aes(label = customer_id))` — both add *new* information not present in the original dataset.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Classify the following user actions into the four task abstraction types (Analyse, Produce, Search, Query). For each, justify in one sentence and name the appropriate R function family. **(7 marks)**

1. *"Compute the mean salary of employees in the IT department."*
2. *"Highlight the data point for employee ID 1042 in the scatter plot."*
3. *"Add a regression line showing the trend of salary vs. experience."*
4. *"Let me pan and zoom freely across the sales timeline."*
5. *"Compare the average sales of 2022 vs. 2023."*
6. *"Mark all transactions above ₹1 lakh with a red flag."*
7. *"Generate a new column 'profit_margin' as profit divided by revenue."*

**(b)** A banking dashboard displays 1 million transactions. A user wants to (i) view the distribution of transaction amounts, (ii) find all suspicious transactions above ₹5 lakh, and (iii) add a personal note to three specific transactions. Classify each subtask and propose a visualization pipeline in R (`ggplot2` + `shiny`) that supports all three. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Question A:**

**(a) — 7 marks (1 mark per correct classification + R family):**

1. **Query — Summarize (1 mark).** The user wants an aggregate statistic (mean). R family: `summarise()` / `aggregate()`. *(1 mark)*
2. **Search — Lookup (1 mark).** A specific target ID is given. R family: `filter(id == 1042)` / `which()`. *(1 mark)*
3. **Produce (1 mark).** The regression line is a *new derived visual* not originally in the data. R family: `geom_smooth(method = "lm")`. *(1 mark)*
4. **Search — Browse (1 mark).** Free exploration with no specific target. R family: `plotly::ggplotly()` with `layout(dragmode = "pan")`. *(1 mark)*
5. **Query — Compare (1 mark).** Two subsets contrasted. R family: `group_by(year) %>% summarise(mean_sales)`. *(1 mark)*
6. **Query — Identify (1 mark).** Marking a subset defined by a threshold. R family: `filter(amount > 100000)` + highlight layer. *(1 mark)*
7. **Produce (1 mark).** Creating a *new derived column*. R family: `mutate(profit_margin = profit / revenue)`. *(1 mark)*

**(b) — 7 marks:**

- **(i) Analyse (2 marks):** Distribution of 1M transactions → **histogram** or **density plot**. R pipeline: `ggplot(transactions, aes(x = amount)) + geom_histogram(bins = 50) + scale_x_log10()`. The use of `scale_x_log10` is essential because financial data is heavy-tailed.
- **(ii) Query — Identify (2 marks):** Filter and flag. R pipeline: `suspicious <- transactions %>% filter(amount > 500000)`. Visualization: overlay red points on the histogram using `geom_histogram() + geom_vline(data = summarise(suspicious, threshold = 500000), aes(xintercept = threshold), color = "red")`.
- **(iii) Produce (2 marks):** Adding user notes. R pipeline: `reactiveValues(notes = data.frame(id = c(), note = c()))` with an `observeEvent(input$save_note, {...})` block in `shiny`. A `textInput` widget captures the note; a `mutate` adds it to the dataset.
- **Integration (1 mark):** All three are combined in a single `shiny` app with a `tabsetPanel` containing three tabs: *Distribution*, *Suspicious*, *My Notes*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the three sub-modes of **Search** (Lookup, Locate, Browse) and the three sub-modes of **Query** (Identify, Compare, Summarize) with one engineering example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing plant collects 50,000 sensor readings per day from 200 machines. Design a task-abstraction map showing which tasks (Analyse / Produce / Search / Query) a quality engineer would perform during a typical day. For each task, identify the expected time complexity and the most appropriate R visualization. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Question B:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

**Search sub-modes (1.5 marks per pair: 0.5 for definition + 0.5 for example + 0.5 for distinguishing feature):**

1. **Lookup** — A *known* identifier is supplied. Engineering example: *"Show sensor reading for machine ID 47 at 14:00."* The user has the **ID**; the system retrieves in $O(1)$ time.
2. **Locate** — A *property* is supplied, ID unknown. Engineering example: *"Show all machines whose temperature exceeded 90°C."* The user supplies a **filter condition**; the system scans in $O(n)$ time.
3. **Browse** — No target specified. Engineering example: *"Let me scroll through the 3D CAD assembly to find anything unusual."* This is **exploratory**; the user has no prior target.

**Query sub-modes (1.5 marks per pair):**

4. **Identify** — Mark a subset. Engineering example: *"Highlight all welds with porosity > 5% on the X-ray scan."* Output: a **marked subset**.
5. **Compare** — Two or more subsets contrasted. Engineering example: *"Compare defect rates of Line A vs. Line B in the last quarter."* Output: a **side-by-side comparison**.
6. **Summarize** — Reduce to a statistic. Engineering example: *"What is the median cycle time of CNC machines this week?"* Output: a **single number** (or small vector).

**(b) — 7 marks — Task Abstraction Map:**

| Time of Day | Task | Type | Complexity | R Visualization |
|-------------|------|------|-----------|-----------------|
| 08:00 | Morning overall review of all 200 machines | **Analyse** | $\Theta(n)$ | `facet_wrap` scatter matrix |
| 09:30 | Find machine #47's temperature history | **Search (Lookup)** | $O(1)$ via indexed data.table | `geom_line` with highlight |
| 10:15 | Locate all machines above vibration threshold | **Search (Locate)** | $O(n)$ | Filtered scatter with red points |
| 11:00 | Identify machines with mean efficiency < 80% | **Query (Identify)** | $O(n)$ | `dplyr::filter` + table |
| 12:00 | Compare efficiency of Machine 1 vs. Machine 2 | **Query (Compare)** | $O(1)$ post-aggregation | Grouped bar chart |
| 14:00 | Compute average downtime per shift | **Query (Summarize)** | $O(n)$ | Aggregated `geom_col` |
| 15:00 | Add annotations to the daily report chart | **Produce** | $O(1)$ writes | `geom_text` + `ggrepel` |
| 16:00 | Free exploration of the dashboard | **Search (Browse)** | Interactive | `plotly` with brush |

**(Mark allocation:** 1 mark for correct classification of each of the 8 rows × 0.5 = 4 marks, 2 marks for complexity justification, 1 mark for the R visualization choice.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Produce with Query:** Students often classify *"Add a new column"* as Query. It is **Produce** because the action *generates* data, not consumes it. **Lose up to 2 marks** for this error.
> 2. **Confusing Search (Locate) with Query (Identify):** Both filter data.
