> **Conclusion:** The contraction of a $(1,1)$ tensor produces a **scalar field**. By the quotient rule, since contraction commutes with coordinate change in a well-defined way, this confirms that a "lowered-upper" index pair in a tensor field always yields a tensor of lower order.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Coordinate Rotation in 2D

> [!TIP]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question**: given a tensor in one coordinate system, compute its components after a rotation.

**Given:** A symmetric $(0,2)$ tensor field at a point has components in basis $\{x^1, x^2\}$:

$$T_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

The coordinate system is rotated by an angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{x}^1 &= \cos\theta \, x^1 + \sin\theta \, x^2 \\
\tilde{x}^2 &= -\sin\theta \, x^1 + \cos\theta \, x^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Find:** The components $\tilde{T}_{ab}$ in the new basis.

### Step 1: Compute the Jacobian Matrices

The forward Jacobian $\partial x / \partial \tilde{x}$ is the inverse of $\partial \tilde{x} / \partial x$:

$$\frac{\partial \tilde{x}^a}{\partial x^i} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \frac{\partial x^i}{\partial \tilde{x}^b} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Covariant Transformation Law

$$\tilde{T}_{ab} = \frac{\partial x^i}{\partial \tilde{x}^a} \cdot \frac{\partial x^j}{\partial \tilde{x}^b} \cdot T_{ij}$$

### Step 3: Compute $\tilde{T}_{11}$ Explicitly

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{T}_{11} &= \frac{\partial x^1}{\partial \tilde{x}^1} \cdot \frac{\partial x^1}{\partial \tilde{x}^1} \cdot T_{11} + \frac{\partial x^1}{\partial \tilde{x}^1} \cdot \frac{\partial x^2}{\partial \tilde{x}^1} \cdot T_{12} \\
&\quad + \frac{\partial x^2}{\partial \tilde{x}^1} \cdot \frac{\partial x^1}{\partial \tilde{x}^1} \cdot T_{21} + \frac{\partial x^2}{\partial \tilde{x}^1} \cdot \frac{\partial x^2}{\partial \tilde{x}^1} \cdot T_{22}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting the Jacobian entries and the given $T_{ij}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{T}_{11} &= (\cos\theta)(\cos\theta)(3) + (\cos\theta)(\sin\theta)(1) \\
&\quad + (\sin\theta)(\cos\theta)(1) + (\sin\theta)(\sin\theta)(2)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{T}_{11} &= 3\cos^2\theta + \cos\theta\sin\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta + 2\sin^2\theta \\
&= 3\cos^2\theta + 2\sin^2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
&= 3\cos^2\theta + 2\sin^2\theta + \sin(2\theta)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Remaining Components (General Result)

For a 2D rotation, the full transformation is:

$$\tilde{T} = R \, T \, R^{\top}, \qquad R = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

**Verification (Determinant is rotation-invariant):**

$$\det(T) = (3)(2) - (1)(1) = 5$$

$$\det(\tilde{T}) = \det(R) \det(T) \det(R^{\top}) = (1)(5)(1) = 5 \ \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The determinant (and trace) of a tensor are **scalar invariants** — they do not change under coordinate transformations. This is precisely the property that makes tensors suitable for physical laws that must be coordinate-independent.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Property** | **Scalar Field** | **Vector Field** | **General Tensor Field** |
|:-------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|:-------------------------|
| Type $(r, s)$ | $(0, 0)$ | $(1, 0)$ or $(0, 1)$ | $(r, s)$ arbitrary |
| # Components | $1$ | $n$ | $n^{r+s}$ |
| Transformation Law | Invariant (no change) | $\tilde{V}^a = \dfrac{\partial \tilde{x}^a}{\partial x^i} V^i$ | Multilinear in Jacobians |
| Visualized as | Heat map / Contour plot | Arrow / Streamline plot | Glyphs (ellipsoids) / Tensor field plots |
| R Package Example | `ggplot2::geom_raster` | `ggplot2::geom_vector` | `gstat`, `waveslim` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of Tensor Fields

The following block diagram illustrates the **hierarchical structure** of geometric objects on a manifold, culminating in the general tensor field.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Manifold M"] --> B["Scalar Field"]
    A --> C["Vector Field"]
    A --> D["Covector 1-Form Field"]
    A --> E["Tensor Field T^r_s"]
    C --> F["Type 1 0"]
    D --> G["Type 0 1"]
    E --> H["Type 2 0"]
    E --> I["Type 0 2"]
    E --> J["Type 1 1"]
    E --> K["Type r s General"]
    H --> L["Stress Tensor"]
    H --> M["Stress Energy Tensor"]
    I --> N["Metric Tensor g_munu"]
    I --> O["Electromagnetic F_munu"]
    J --> P["Kronecker Delta"]
    J --> Q["Deformation Gradient"]
    K --> R["Riemann Curvature"]
    style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#d4a017,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style D fill:#c62828,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style E fill:#4a148c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style K fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style R fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation of the Flowchart:**
- Every geometric object lives on a smooth manifold $\mathcal{M}$.
- The arrows from **Tensor Field** show that **type** $(r, s)$ is the fundamental classifying attribute.
- Notable specializations: a **Riemann Curvature Tensor** is a type $(1, 3)$ tensor field; a **Kronecker Delta** is the canonical type $(1, 1)$ identity tensor field.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Transformation Law Architecture

The following block diagram shows how a change of coordinates flows through the Jacobian machinery to produce the new tensor field components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Old Coordinates x_i"] --> B["Jacobian dx/dxtilde"]
    A --> C["Old Components T_i_j"]
    B --> D["Contravariant factor dxtilde/dx_i"]
    C --> E["Tensor Product Assembly"]
    D --> E
    F["New Coordinates xtilde_a"] --> G["Inverse Jacobian dx/dxtilde"]
    G --> E
    E --> H["New Components Ttilde_a_b"]
    H --> I["Tensor Field in New Chart"]
    style A fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **old components** $T^i_{\ j}$ are multiplied by **Jacobian factors** to produce the **new components** $\tilde{T}^a_{\ b}$.
- The number of Jacobian factors equals the **total rank** of the tensor.
- This is exactly the architecture used by R packages internally when re-gridding multivariate spatial data.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Algebraic Operations on Tensor Fields

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Tensor A type r1 s1"] --> C["Tensor Product"]
    B["Tensor B type r2 s2"] --> C
    C --> D["Tensor AB type r1+r2 s1+s2"]
    D --> E["Contraction over index pair"]
    E --> F["Tensor of type r1+r2-1 s1+s2-1"]
    F --> G["Symmetrization or Antisymmetrization"]
    G --> H["Final Tensor of Type r s"]
    I["Metric Tensor g_munu"] --> J["Raise or Lower Indices"]
    H --> J
    J --> K["Equivalent Tensor with Reassigned Indices"]
    style A fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style K fill:#4a148c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:**
- The diagram traces the canonical algebraic workflow: **product → contraction → (anti)symmetrization → index manipulation**.
- Index manipulation is enabled by a **non-degenerate metric tensor**, which is the assumption underlying most engineering applications (e.g., strain analysis in solid mechanics).

---

## 4.4 Visualization Strategy Table (R-Oriented)

> [!TIP]
> **Data Visualization Connection:** Even though R does not have a built-in "tensor field plotter", the following visualization strategies are standard for tensor fields in scientific computing, and the listed R packages approximate them.

| **Tensor Type** | **Glyph Used for Visualization** | **Information Encoded** | **R / External Tool** |
|:----------------|:---------------------------------|:------------------------|:----------------------|
| $(0, 2)$ Symmetric SPD | **Ellipsoid** at each point | Eigen-directions & magnitudes | `rgl` package, `plot3D` |
| $(0, 2)$ Symmetric | **Superquadric glyph** | Full tensor contents | `tensorflow` + custom shaders |
| $(2, 0)$ | **Streakline / LIC** | Directional flow | `ggplot2` + `metR` |
| $(0, 0)$ | **Color / contour** | Scalar value | `ggplot2::geom_raster` |
| $(1, 0)$ | **Arrow** | Direction + magnitude | `ggplot2::geom_segment` |
| $(1, 1)$ | **Parallelepiped glyph** | Linear transformation | `rgl` + `plotly` |
| $(1, 3)$ Riemann | **Tensor glyph in 3D** | Local curvature | ParaView / VisIt |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions are **direct, definition/conceptual** questions requiring crisp answers (typically 60-80 words).

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a tensor field of type $(r, s)$ on a smooth manifold. Give one engineering example.**

**Model Answer:**

A tensor field of type $(r, s)$ on a smooth manifold $\mathcal{M}$ is a smooth assignment $T : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_p(\mathcal{M})$ that associates a multilinear map $T(p) : (T_p\mathcal{M})^r \times (T^*_p\mathcal{M})^s \to \mathbb{R}$ to every point $p \in \mathcal{M}$, with $r$ contravariant and $s$ covariant slots. Its components satisfy the multilinear transformation law under coordinate change.

**Engineering Example:** The Cauchy stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ in solid mechanics, which is a symmetric type $(0, 2)$ tensor field describing internal forces per unit area on any surface element.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the transformation law for a mixed tensor of type $(1, 1)$ and explain the role of the Jacobian factors.**

**Model Answer:**

For a mixed tensor $T^i_{\ j}$ of type $(1, 1)$, the components transform under $x^i \to \tilde{x}^a$ as:

$$\tilde{T}^a_{\ b} = \frac{\partial \tilde{x}^a}{\partial x^i} \cdot \frac{\partial x^j}{\partial \tilde{x}^b} \cdot T^i_{\ j}$$

The factor $\partial \tilde{x}^a / \partial x^i$ is the **contravariant Jacobian** (associated with the up index), and $\partial x^j / \partial \tilde{x}^b$ is the **covariant Jacobian** (associated with the down index). They ensure the tensor's geometric meaning is preserved across coordinate systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B Module questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice**. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below are two alternative questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2]`

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the transformation law for the components of a contravariant tensor field of rank 2.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** A type $(2, 0)$ tensor field $T$ with components $T^{ij}$ in coordinates $x^i$.

**Step 1 — Tensor expansion in the original basis:**

$$T = T^{ij} \ \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i} \otimes \frac{\partial}{\partial x^j}$$

**Step 2 — Express the basis vectors in the new coordinates** using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x^i} = \frac{\partial \tilde{x}^a}{\partial x^i} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \tilde{x}^a}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the tensor expansion:**

$$T = T^{ij} \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{x}^a}{\partial x^i} \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{x}^b}{\partial x^j} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \tilde{x}^a} \otimes \frac{\partial}{\partial \tilde{x}^b}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the new components:**

$$\boxed{\tilde{T}^{ab} = \frac{\partial \tilde{x}^a}{\partial x^i} \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{x}^b}{\partial x^j} \cdot T^{ij}}$$

Each contravariant index contributes one contravariant Jacobian factor, confirming the rank-2 contravariant transformation law. (7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for derivation, 2 for final boxed result.)

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Given a symmetric tensor at a point with components $T_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ in the basis $\{x^1, x^2\}$, find its components after a $45°$ counter-clockwise rotation. Verify that the trace and determinant are invariant.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rotation matrix:**

$$R = \begin{pmatrix} \cos 45° & \sin 45° \\ -\sin 45° & \cos 45° \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Transformation law for a $(0, 2)$ tensor:**

$$\tilde{T} = R \, T \, R^{\top}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intermediate product $M = R \, T$:**

$$M = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 7 & 5 \\ -3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tilde{T} = M \, R^{\top}$:**

$$R^{\top} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\tilde{T} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 7 & 5 \\ -3 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 12 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Invariant verification:**

| Invariant | Original $T$ | Rotated $\tilde{T}$ |
|:---------:|:------------:|:-------------------:|
| Trace | $5 + 3 = 8$ | $6 + 2 = 8$ ✓ |
| Determinant | $5 \cdot 3 - 2 \cdot 2 = 11$ | $6 \cdot 2 - (-1)(-1) = 11$ ✓ |

Both invariants match, confirming the **coordinate-independence** of scalar quantities derived from tensors. (7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for matrix computation, 2 for invariant verification.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2]`

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Define a covariant tensor field. State and prove the transformation law for a rank-2 covariant tensor field.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A covariant tensor field of type $(0, s)$ at a point $p$ assigns to each point a multilinear map from $(T_p\mathcal{M})^s \to \mathbb{R}$.

**Statement of the Law:** For a $(0, 2)$ covariant tensor $T_{ij}$, under coordinate change $x^i \to \tilde{x}^a$:

$$\boxed{\tilde{T}_{ab} = \frac{\partial x^i}{\partial \tilde{x}^a} \cdot \frac{\partial x^j}{\partial \tilde{x}^b} \cdot T_{ij}}$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Write the dual basis in old coordinates: $\{dx^i\}$ satisfying $dx^i(\partial/\partial x^j) = \delta^i_j$.

**Step 2:** The covariant basis in new coordinates transforms as:

$$dx^i = \frac{\partial x^i}{\partial \tilde{x}^a} \, d\tilde{x}^a$$

This is because a 1-form transforms via the Jacobian transpose of the coordinate change (dual to the vector transformation).

**Step 3:** The tensor in old coordinates:

$$T = T_{ij} \, dx^i \otimes dx^j$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$T = T_{ij} \cdot \frac{\partial x^i}{\partial \tilde{x}^a} \cdot \frac{\partial x^j}{\partial \tilde{x}^b} \cdot d\tilde{x}^a \otimes d\tilde{x}^b$$

**Step 5:** Identifying the transformed components:

$$\tilde{T}_{ab} = \frac{\partial x^i}{\partial \tilde{x}^a} \cdot \frac{\partial x^j}{\partial \tilde{x}^b} \cdot T_{ij} \qquad \blacksquare$$

Each covariant (lower) index contributes one **covariant Jacobian** $\partial x / \partial \tilde{x}$. (7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for proof, 2 for boxed result.)

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** The metric tensor in 2D polar coordinates is $g_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & r^2 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the components of the metric tensor in Cartesian coordinates $(x, y)$ and verify the line element $ds^2 = dx^2 + dy^2$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Coordinate transformation:**

$$x = r\cos\phi, \qquad y = r\sin\phi$$

The inverse transformation:

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \qquad \phi = \arctan(y/x)$$

**Step 2 — Jacobian of the transformation $(\tilde{x}, \tilde{y}) \to (r, \phi)$:**

$$\frac{\partial (r, \phi)}{\partial (x, y)} = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}} & \dfrac{y}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}} \\ \dfrac{-y}{x^2+y^2} & \dfrac{x}{x^2+y^2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{x}{r} & \dfrac{y}{r} \\ \dfrac{-y}{r^2} & \dfrac{x}{r^2} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Jacobian (for covariant transformation):**

$$\frac{\partial (x, y)}{\partial (r, \phi)} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\phi & -r\sin\phi \\ \sin\phi & r\cos\phi \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the covariant transformation law:**

$$\tilde{g}_{\alpha\beta} = \frac{\partial x^i}{\partial \tilde{x}^\alpha} \cdot \frac{\partial x^j}{\partial \tilde{x}^\beta} \cdot g_{ij}$$

Computing term by term:

- $\tilde{g}_{11} = \cos^2\phi \cdot 1 + (r\sin\phi)(0)(\text{terms}) + \ldots = \cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi = 1$
- $\tilde{g}_{22} = r^2\sin^2\phi \cdot 1 + r^2\cos^2\phi \cdot 1 = r^2(\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi) = r^2$
- $\tilde{g}_{12} = \tilde{g}_{21} = 0$ (by direct computation)

**Step 5 — Result in Cartesian form using $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$:**

$$\tilde{g}_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & x^2 + y^2 \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{(in terms of } r^2 = x^2 + y^2\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the line element:**

$$ds^2 = g_{11} \, dr^2 + 2 g_{12} \, dr \, d\phi + g_{22} \, d\phi^2$$

$$ds^2 = dr^2 + r^2 d\phi^2$$

Using $dr = (x\,dx + y\,dy)/r$ and $d\phi = (x\,dy - y\,dx)/r^2$:

$$ds^2 = \frac{(x\,dx + y\,dy)^2}{r^2} + r^2 \cdot \frac{(x\,dy - y\,dx)^2}{r^4} = dx^2 + dy^2 \qquad \blacksquare$$

(7 marks: 2 for Jacobian, 2 for metric components, 2 for line element simplification, 1 for final verification.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Tensor Field Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing contravariant and covariant indices** in the transformation law ($-$2 to $-3$ marks). Remember: up indices use $\partial \tilde{x}/\partial x$; down indices use $\partial x / \partial \tilde{x}$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the summation convention** when expanding the transformation. Every repeated index (one up, one down) must be summed over all $n$ dimensions.
>
> 3. **Not specifying the type $(r, s)$** when defining a tensor. Examiners expect explicit type declaration: *"a tensor of type $(1, 2)$"* not just *"a tensor."*
>
> 4. **Skipping the chain rule justification** in derivation questions. You must show that the basis vectors (or covectors) transform via the chain rule — that is the geometric *reason* for the transformation law.
>
> 5. **Mixing index positions** in the metric tensor operations: $g_{\mu\nu}$ is the metric, $g^{\mu\nu}$ is its inverse. Raising an index requires multiplying by $g^{\mu\nu}$ with the up index in the desired position.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the symmetry property** when handling tensors like the stress tensor $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ji}$ — this can simplify calculations significantly if used.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Tensor Fields**

- ✅ A **tensor field** is a smooth assignment of a tensor to every point of a manifold.
- ✅ Tensor fields are classified by their **type $(r, s)$** with $r$ contravariant and $s$ covariant indices.
- ✅ The **transformation law** is the defining property: each up index uses $\partial \tilde{x}^a / \partial x^i$, each down index uses $\partial x^j / \partial \tilde{x}^b$.
- ✅ **Tensor product** $\otimes$ combines types additively: $(r_1, s_1) \otimes (r_2, s_2) = (r_1+r_2, s_1+s_2)$.
- ✅ **Contraction** $C$ sums over one up + one down index, reducing rank by 2.
- ✅ **Symmetrization** $T_{(ab)} = \tfrac{1}{2}(T_{ab} + T_{ba})$; **antisymmetrization** $T_{[ab]} = \tfrac{1}{2}(T_{ab} - T_{ba})$.
- ✅ The **Kronecker delta** $\delta^i_j$ is the identity type $(1, 1)$ tensor.
- ✅ The **metric tensor** $g_{\mu\nu}$ (and inverse $g^{\mu\nu}$) raises and lowers indices.
- ✅ **Scalar invariants** (trace, determinant) are **coordinate-independent**.
- ✅ **Key examples:** stress-energy $T^{\mu\nu}$ (physics), metric $g_{\mu\nu}$ (geometry), diffusion $D_{ij}$ (medical imaging), Cauchy stress $\sigma_{ij}$ (engineering).
- ✅ In **R**, multivariate spatial data (e.g., covariance matrices at each grid point) are often best modeled as $(0, 2)$ tensor fields, with visualization using ellipsoid glyphs.
- ✅ The **covariant derivative** $\nabla_j V^i = \partial_j V^i + \Gamma^i_{jk} V^k$ extends the partial derivative to tensor fields on curved spaces.
- ✅ The **Riemann curvature tensor** $R^i_{\ jkl}$ measures the failure of covariant derivatives to commute — a rank-4 tensor field central to general relativity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrange Networks and Trees: Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arrange Networks and Trees: Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Network Visualization** is the graphical representation of relational data structures where entities (called **nodes** or **vertices**) are connected by pairwise relationships (called **edges**, **links**, or **connections**). In the context of the R ecosystem (specifically the `ggraph`, `igraph`, and `tidygraph` packages), a **network** is a formal data object comprising a node table and an edge table, where layout algorithms are applied to position vertices in 2D/3D space while preserving topological relationships.

A **Tree** is a special, acyclic sub-class of a network — a connected, undirected graph containing **N nodes and exactly (N−1) edges**, with no cycles. Trees encode **hierarchical** or **taxonomic** relationships and are typically rendered using **dendrograms**, **phylogenetic trees**, or **icicle/sunburst plots**.

The term **"Connections"** in this topic refers to the visual encoding of edges — i.e., how relationships between nodes are drawn (straight lines, curves, arcs, bundled splines, or ribbons) — and how the layout of those connections communicates the underlying data structure (clusters, paths, flows, hierarchies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme expects students to be able to (a) distinguish between graph and tree layouts, (b) select an appropriate layout algorithm for a given relational dataset, (c) implement visualization in R using `ggraph` / `igraph`, and (d) critically evaluate the readability of a network diagram.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are the **HR manager of a company** mapping out who reports to whom and who collaborates with whom on cross-team projects:

- **Nodes** = Employees (drawn as circles).
- **Edges / Connections** = Working relationships (drawn as lines between employees).
- **A Network (graph)** captures *all* relationships — including cross-team collaborations, which can form loops (e.g., Alice → Bob → Charlie → Alice, a circular project loop). This is a **graph**, not a tree.
- **A Tree** captures only the *reporting hierarchy* (a boss cannot report to two people simultaneously, and there are no loops). The **organizational chart** on the company wall is therefore a **tree** — every employee has exactly one "path upward" to the CEO.
- **Connections** are the **lines on the chart** — and *how* you draw them matters: a single straight line says "simple relationship"; a curved arc with arrowheads says "directed flow"; a thick colored ribbon says "strong tie."

> [!NOTE]
> **Mental Model:** A *graph* is like a **city map** with all possible roads (including roundabouts / cycles). A *tree* is like a **river tributary system** — water flows one way, branches split, but it never loops back on itself.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Term | Definition | Common Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertex / Node** | A single entity in the network | $V$ |
| **Edge / Link** | A connection between two nodes | $E$ |
| **Degree** | Number of edges incident to a node | $\deg(v)$ |
| **Weighted Edge** | An edge with an associated strength/cost value | $w_{ij}$ |
| **Directed Edge** | An asymmetric connection (A → B ≠ B → A) | arc |
| **Path** | A sequence of vertices connected by edges | $P(v_1, v_n)$ |
| **Cycle** | A path that returns to its starting vertex | — |
| **Tree** | A connected graph with $\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$ | $T$ |
| **Forest** | A disjoint union of trees | — |
| **Root** | The topmost vertex in a rooted tree | $r$ |
| **Leaf** | A vertex with degree 1 in a tree | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Networks vs. Trees

### 2.1.1 The Formal Distinction

A **graph** $G$ is formally defined as an ordered pair:

$$
G = (V, E)
$$

where $V$ is a finite, non-empty set of vertices and $E \subseteq \{ \{u, v\} : u, v \in V, u \neq v\}$ is a set of edges (for undirected graphs) or $E \subseteq V \times V$ (for directed graphs).

A **tree** is a *constrained* graph satisfying the following three equivalent axioms (any one of which implies the other two):

1. $G$ is **connected** and **acyclic** (no cycles).
2. $G$ is **connected** and has exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges.
3. $G$ is **acyclic** and adding any new edge would create a cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for visualization:** A *graph* requires a layout algorithm that can resolve cycles (e.g., **Fruchterman-Reingold**, **Kamada-Kawai**, **force-directed**). A *tree* can use specialized hierarchical layouts (e.g., **Reingold-Tilford**, **dendrogram**, **radial**), which are computationally cheaper and produce visually unambiguous hierarchies.

### 2.1.2 Layout Algorithms (How Connections Are Positioned)

The **layout** is the function $L: V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^2$ that assigns a 2D coordinate to every node. KTU expects familiarity with these:

**(a) Force-Directed Layouts (best for general graphs)**
- Treat edges as **springs** (Hooke's Law) and nodes as **charged particles** (Coulomb's Law).
- The system iterates to minimize an energy function:

$$
E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} \frac{k \, d_{ij}^2}{2} - \sum_{(i,j) \in E} k_{ij} \cdot \log d_{ij}
$$

  where $d_{ij}$ is Euclidean distance between nodes $i$ and $j$, $k$ is the ideal edge length, and $k_{ij}$ is the stiffness of edge $(i,j)$.
- Algorithms: **Fruchterman-Reingold (FR)**, **Kamada-Kawai (KK)**.

**(b) Hierarchical / Tree Layouts (best for trees)**
- **Reingold-Tilford Algorithm:** Recursively places subtrees so that no overlap occurs and siblings are equidistant from their parent. Produces clean, balanced trees.
- **Dendrogram:** A tree rendered with vertical/horizontal axis encoding distance — used for **hierarchical clustering** output.
- **Radial Layout:** Root at center, branches radiating outward — saves horizontal space.

**(c) Circular & Arc Layouts**
- Nodes placed on a circle; edges drawn as chords. Effective for *small dense networks* where each node is roughly equivalent (no hierarchy).

### 2.1.3 Connection (Edge) Encoding Styles

The visual choice of how to draw an edge encodes information:

| Edge Style | Information Encoded | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight line** | Simple presence of connection | Sparse networks |
| **Curved / Arc** | Direction, multiple non-crossing edges | Bipartite graphs |
| **Bundled spline (Bezier)** | Cluster flow, reduces visual clutter | Dense networks (Holten's algorithm) |
| **Ribbon / Tapering** | Edge weight (thickness = weight) | Weighted networks, migrations |
| **Arrowhead** | Directed flow | DAGs, dependency graphs |
| **Dashed** | Indirect / latent connection | Probabilistic edges |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Concept} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Notes} \\
\hline
\text{Edges in a tree} & \vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - 1 & \text{Tree constraint} \\
\hline
\text{Edges in a forest of } k \text{ trees} & \vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - k & \text{Disjoint union} \\
\hline
\text{Handshake Lemma} & \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert & \text{Undirected graph} \\
\hline
\text{Average degree} & \bar{d} = \frac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert} & \text{Undirected} \\
\hline
\text{Weighted sum of degrees} & \sum_{v} \sum_{u} w_{vu} = 2 \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} & \text{Weighted graph} \\
\hline
\text{Degree centrality} & C_D(v) = \frac{\deg(v)}{N - 1} & N = \vert V \vert \\
\hline
\text{Shortest path length} & d(u, v) = \min \, \text{edges between } u \text{ and } v & \text{BFS/Dijkstra} \\
\hline
\text{Betweenness centrality} & C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}} & \sigma_{st} = \text{shortest paths} \\
\hline
\text{Cophenetic distance} & c(u, v) = h(\text{LCA}(u, v)) & \text{Tree of clusters} \\
\hline
\text{Force-directed energy} & E = \sum_{(i,j)} \tfrac{1}{2} k (d_{ij} - L)^2 & \text{Fruchterman–Reingold} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** The **Handshake Lemma** $\sum \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ applies ONLY to *undirected* simple graphs. For *directed* graphs, the corresponding identity is $\sum \text{in-degree} = \sum \text{out-degree} = \vert E \vert$. Students frequently lose marks by writing the wrong coefficient.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Social Network Analysis (SNA):** Identify influencers (high betweenness centrality) in a telecommunications call-graph.
- **Bioinformatics:** Phylogenetic trees of viral strains inform epidemiological tracking.
- **Software Engineering:** Dependency graphs of R packages or microservices reveal bottlenecks and circular imports.
- **Transportation:** Road and airline networks — minimum spanning trees optimize cable/fiber laying.
- **Hierarchical Data:** Org charts, file systems, decision trees in ML are all naturally trees.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Verifying the Tree Property

> **Problem:** Given an undirected graph with vertex set $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and edge set $E = \{(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (B,E), (C,F)\}$, verify whether it is a tree.

### Step 1 — Count vertices and edges

$$
\vert V \vert = 6, \quad \vert E \vert = 5
$$

### Step 2 — Check the tree-edge identity

$$
\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 5 = 6 - 1 = 5 \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 3 — Check acyclicity (no loops)

Traverse from $A$: $A \to B \to D$ (leaf), $A \to B \to E$ (leaf), $A \to C \to F$ (leaf). No vertex is revisited. The graph is **acyclic**.

### Step 4 — Check connectivity (single connected component)

From $A$ we reach $B, C, D, E, F$ — all 6 vertices in one component.

### Step 5 — Conclude

All three axioms of a tree are satisfied. **The graph is a tree.** $A$ is the natural root; leaves are $\{D, E, F\}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Building a Dendrogram from Hierarchical Clustering

> **Problem:** Cluster the 4 points $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 2, x_3 = 5, x_4 = 7$ using **single-linkage** agglomerative clustering and draw the dendrogram. Compute the **cophenetic distance** between $x_1$ and $x_3$.

### Step 1 — Compute the pairwise distance matrix

$$
D = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 1 & 4 & 6 \\
1 & 0 & 3 & 5 \\
4 & 3 & 0 & 2 \\
6 & 5 & 2 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Distances are absolute differences $d(x_i, x_j) = \vert x_i - x_j \vert$.

### Step 2 — Agglomerative merging (single linkage)

**Iteration 1:** Smallest off-diagonal value is $D_{12} = 1$. Merge $\{1, 2\}$ into cluster $C_{12}$ at height $h = 1$.

**Update matrix using single linkage:** $d(C_{12}, j) = \min(d(1,j), d(2,j))$.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix}
C_{12} & x_3 & x_4 \\
0 & \min(4,3) = 3 & \min(6,5) = 5 \\
3 & 0 & 2 \\
5 & 2 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Iteration 2:** Smallest is $D_{34} = 2$. Merge $\{3, 4\}$ into $C_{34}$ at height $h = 2$.

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix}
C_{12} & C_{34} \\
0 & \min(3,5,5,2) = 2 \\
2 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Iteration 3:** Merge $C_{12}$ and $C_{34}$ at height $h = 2$.

### Step 3 — Record merge sequence

$$
(1,2) \rightarrow 1, \quad (3,4) \rightarrow 2, \quad (C_{12}, C_{34}) \rightarrow 3
$$

### Step 4 — Compute cophenetic distance $c(x_1, x_3)$

The **Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA)** of leaves $1$ and $3$ is the root merge (height 2). Therefore:

$$
c(x_1, x_3) = h(\text{LCA}(1, 3)) = 2
$$

### Step 5 — Dendrogram representation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Height} \; h = 3 &: \quad \text{ROOT} \\
\quad \backslash & \quad \quad \backslash \\
\text{Height} \; h = 1 & : \; \{1, 2\} \quad\quad \{3, 4\} \;\; \text{Height} \; h = 2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

(Wait — correction: the merge of $\{3,4\}$ happens at $h=2$ before the final merge at $h=2$. In single-linkage, the final merge of $C_{12}$ and $C_{34}$ occurs at $h=2$ as well, but the dendrogram distinguishes the two branch heights properly when ties are broken by the merge order.)

The **cophenetic matrix** records the height at which each pair first joins:

$$
C = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 1 & 2 & 2 \\
1 & 0 & 2 & 2 \\
2 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\
2 & 2 & 2 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cophenetic distance matters:** It is the metric *induced* by the dendrogram. A good clustering yields a cophenetic matrix highly correlated with the original $D$ (measured by the **cophenetic correlation coefficient** $\rho_c$, ideally $\geq 0.75$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — R Code for Network & Tree Visualization (ggraph + igraph)

> **Problem:** Construct a small weighted graph in R, compute degree centrality, and visualize it with `ggraph`. Then plot a hierarchical clustering dendrogram.

### Step 1 — Install and load packages

```r
install.packages(c("igraph", "ggraph", "tidygraph", "dplyr"))
library(igraph)
library(ggraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(dplyr)
```

### Step 2 — Define nodes and edges

```r
nodes <- tibble(name = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E"))
edges <- tibble(
  from = c("A", "A", "B", "C", "D"),
  to   = c("B", "C", "D", "E", "E"),
  weight = c(2.5, 1.0, 3.2, 0.8, 1.5)
)
```

### Step 3 — Build a tidy graph object

```r
graph_obj <- tbl_graph(nodes = nodes, edges = edges, directed = FALSE)
graph_obj
```

### Step 4 — Compute degree centrality

```r
graph_obj <- graph_obj %>%
  mutate(degree = centrality_degree(),
         betweenness = centrality_betweenness())
```

The degree centrality of node $v$ is:

$$
C_D(v) = \frac{\deg(v)}{N - 1} = \frac{\deg(v)}{4}
$$

For our graph: $\deg(A) = 2, \deg(B) = 2, \deg(C) = 2, \deg(D) = 2, \deg(E) = 2$ — so all $C_D = 0.5$.

### Step 5 — Visualize with `ggraph` (Fruchterman-Reingold layout, arc connections)

```r
ggraph(graph_obj, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = weight),
                 alpha = 0.7,
                 color = "steelblue",
                 show.legend = TRUE) +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree),
                  color = "tomato") +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name),
                 repel = TRUE, size = 5) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Network Visualization: Fruchterman-Reingold Layout",
       subtitle = "Edge width = weight, Node size = degree")
```

### Step 6 — Build and plot a dendrogram

```r
hc <- hclust(dist(nodes$name), method = "single")
ggraph(hc, layout = "dendrogram") +
  geom_edge_diagonal() +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = label), hjust = 0.5) +
  theme_void()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection styling in `ggraph`:** `geom_edge_link()` → straight lines, `geom_edge_arc()` → curved, `geom_edge_diagonal()` → 45° connectors, `geom_edge_fan()` → bundled for parallel edges, `geom_edge_density()` → density fill between clusters.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Network-to-Visualization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Relational Data] --> B[Node Table]
    A --> C[Edge Table]
    B --> D[igraph tbl_graph object]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Layout Selection}
    E -->|Undirected| F[Fruchterman-Reingold]
    E -->|Hierarchical| G[Reingold-Tilford Dendrogram]
    E -->|Dense| H[Force-Directed Bundled]
    E -->|Radial| I[Radial Tree]
    F --> J[Node Position Coordinates]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Edge Connection Styling]
    K --> L[Link Arc Diagonal Fan Density]
    L --> M[Final Rendered Visualization]
    M --> N[Analytic Overlay: Centrality Clusters]
```

## 4.2 Network Graph Example (5 nodes, weighted)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA((A)) ---|w=2.5| nodeB((B))
    nodeA((A)) ---|w=1.0| nodeC((C))
    nodeB((B)) ---|w=3.2| nodeD((D))
    nodeC((C)) ---|w=0.8| nodeE((E))
    nodeD((D)) ---|w=1.5| nodeE((E))
```

## 4.3 Tree (Hierarchical) Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    root((ROOT)) --> left((X1))
    root((ROOT)) --> right((X2))
    left((X1)) --> leaf1((A))
    left((X1)) --> leaf2((B))
    right((X2)) --> leaf3((C))
    right((X2)) --> leaf4((D))
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Layout to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is the data hierarchical?] -->|Yes| Q2[Number of leaves more than 50?]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Is the graph dense?]
    Q2 -->|No| A1[Use Reingold-Tilford Dendrogram]
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Use Radial or Collapsible Tree]
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Use Force-Directed with Edge Bundling]
    Q3 -->|No| A4[Use Fruchterman-Reingold or Kamada-Kawai]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Reference:** The choice of layout is the single most important decision in network visualization. A poorly chosen layout can make a small graph unreadable (e.g., a 200-node cycle graph rendered as a star).

## 4.5 Connection Style Comparison (Visual Encodings)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph styleA[STYLE A Straight Link]
      a1((A)) --- a2((B))
    end
    subgraph styleB[STYLE B Arc]
      b1((A)) o--o b2((B))
    end
    subgraph styleC[STYLE C Bundled]
      c1((A)) ~~~ c2((B))
    end
```

(Conceptual — `ggraph` selects `geom_edge_link`, `geom_edge_arc`, or `geom_edge_bundle_*` in actual R rendering.)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
> **Differentiate between a network (graph) and a tree. State the three equivalent conditions that define a tree mathematically.**

**Model Answer:**

A **network (graph)** is a general data structure of vertices connected by edges, which *may contain cycles*. A **tree** is a *special* type of graph that is **acyclic** and **connected**. The three equivalent conditions defining a tree $T = (V, E)$ are:

1. $T$ is connected and has no cycles.
2. $T$ is connected and has exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges.
3. $T$ has no cycles and adding any new edge creates exactly one cycle.

Additionally, a tree has exactly one **simple path** between any two vertices — a property used heavily in shortest-path algorithms. *(3 marks: 1 for distinction, 2 for the three conditions.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — KTU Question Bank 2024]**
> **What is the Handshake Lemma? In a directed graph with 5 nodes and 8 edges, what is the sum of in-degrees equal to?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Handshake Lemma** states that in any undirected graph, the sum of degrees of all vertices equals twice the number of edges:

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert
$$

For a **directed graph**, the corresponding identity is:

$$
\sum_{v \in V} \text{indeg}(v) = \sum_{v \in V} \text{outdeg}(v) = \vert E \vert
$$

For 8 directed edges:

$$
\sum \text{indeg}(v) = 8
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for undirected formula, 1 for directed formula, 1 for numerical answer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Fruchterman-Reingold force-directed layout algorithm** used in network visualization. Describe its energy formulation and the role of attractive and repulsive forces. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate how to visualize a weighted network in R using the `ggraph` and `igraph` packages. Show how edge width can encode weight and node size can encode degree centrality. Provide the complete R code and output description. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **Fruchterman-Reingold (FR) algorithm** treats the network as a physical system:

- **Nodes** are treated as **charged particles** that repel each other.
- **Edges** are treated as **springs** that pull connected nodes together.
- The system relaxes to a **minimum-energy configuration**, which is rendered as the final layout.

The energy function minimized at each iteration is:

$$
E = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} \frac{k^2}{d_{ij}} - \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \frac{d_{ij}^2}{k}
$$

where:
- $d_{ij}$ = Euclidean distance between nodes $i$ and $j$,
- $k = C \sqrt{\frac{\text{Area}}{N}}$ is the **ideal edge length**, with $C$ a tunable constant.

**Repulsive force** (between all pairs, derived from the first term):

$$
F_r(d_{ij}) = \frac{k^2}{d_{ij}}
$$

**Attractive force** (only between connected pairs, from the second term):

$$
F_a(d_{ij}) = \frac{d_{ij}^2}{k}
$$

At equilibrium, connected nodes cluster at distance $\approx k$, and unconnected nodes are pushed apart. The algorithm runs $O(N^2)$ per iteration (or $O(N \sqrt{N})$ with the Barnes-Hut quadtree optimization).

**Advantages:** Aesthetically pleasing, reveals community structure.
**Disadvantages:** Non-deterministic (different runs → different layouts), slow for huge graphs.

*(7 marks: 2 for energy formulation, 2 for force terms, 1 for parameters, 2 for advantages/disadvantages.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

```r
library(igraph)
library(ggraph)
library(tidygraph)
library(dplyr)

# Define nodes
nodes <- tibble(name = c("Server", "DB", "Auth", "Cache", "API", "Client"))

# Define weighted edges
edges <- tibble(
  from = c("Server", "Server", "Auth", "DB", "Cache", "Client"),
  to   = c("DB", "Auth", "DB", "Cache", "API", "Server"),
  weight = c(5.0, 2.0, 1.5, 1.0, 2.5, 4.0)
)

# Build tidy graph
net <- tbl_graph(nodes = nodes, edges = edges, directed = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(degree = centrality_degree())

# Visualize with ggraph
ggraph(net, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = weight),
                 alpha = 0.7,
                 color = "darkblue",
                 arrow = arrow(length = unit(3, "mm")),
                 end_cap = circle(3, "mm")) +
  geom_node_point(aes(size = degree),
                  color = "orange", show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.3, 2.5)) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Microservice Dependency Network",
       subtitle = "Fruchterman-Reingold Layout")
```

**Output description:** A force-directed plot with 6 nodes positioned so that strongly-connected nodes cluster together. The `Server` node is centrally located (highest degree), with thick edges to `DB` and `Client`. Edge arrows indicate the direction of dependency. *(7 marks: 2 for data definition, 2 for graph construction, 2 for visualization, 1 for description.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model 2024 Alternative]**

> **(a)** Define **hierarchical clustering** and explain the **single-linkage** method. Show the step-by-step merging process for the distance matrix below, and draw the resulting dendrogram. *(7 marks)*

$$
D = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 4 & 8 & 9 \\
4 & 0 & 5 & 10 \\
8 & 5 & 0 & 3 \\
9 & 10 & 3 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

> **(b)** Write an R program to perform hierarchical clustering on the `iris` dataset (using the first 4 numeric columns) and plot the dendrogram with `ggraph`. Explain the concept of **cophenetic distance**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Hierarchical clustering** builds a tree of clusters either:
- **Agglomeratively** (bottom-up): start with each point as its own cluster, merge the two closest clusters iteratively.
- **Divisively** (top-down): start with one cluster, recursively split.

**Single linkage** uses the minimum inter-cluster distance as the merge criterion:

$$
d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a,\, y \in C_b} d(x, y)
$$

**Step-by-step merges for the given matrix $D$:**

**Iteration 1:** Smallest value = $D_{12} = 4$. Merge $C_1 = \{1, 2\}$ at height $h = 4$.

Update distances using single linkage:

$$
d(C_1, 3) = \min(8, 5) = 5, \quad d(C_1, 4) = \min(9, 10) = 9
$$

Updated matrix:

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix}
C_1 & 3 & 4 \\
0 & 5 & 9 \\
5 & 0 & 3 \\
9 & 3 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Iteration 2:** Smallest value = $D_{34} = 3$. Merge $C_2 = \{3, 4\}$ at height $h = 3$.

Update: $d(C_1, C_2) = \min(5, 9, 9, 3) = 3$.

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix}
C_1 & C_2 \\
0 & 3 \\
3 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Iteration 3:** Merge $C_1$ and $C_2$ at height $h = 3$.

**Dendrogram:** A binary tree with $\{1, 2\}$ joining at $h=4$, $\{3, 4\}$ joining at $h=3$, then the two clusters joining at $h=3$ (final root).

```
            ┌──── 1
      ┌─────┤
      │     └──── 2
   ───┤
      │           ┌──── 3
      └───────────┤
                  └──── 4
```

**Merge sequence:** $(3,4)$ at 3 → $(1,2)$ at 4 → $(\{3,4\}, \{1,2\})$ at 3.

*(7 marks: 1 definition, 2 single-linkage concept, 2 step-by-step, 2 dendrogram.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

```r
library(igraph)
library(ggraph)

# Compute distance matrix from iris numeric columns
d <- dist(iris[, 1:4])

# Perform hierarchical clustering
hc <- hclust(d, method = "single")

# Convert hclust to dendrogram object
dendro <- as.dendrogram(hc)

# Plot with ggraph
ggraph(hc, layout = "dendrogram") +
  geom_edge_diagonal() +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = label),
                 angle = 90, hjust = 1, size = 2.5) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Hierarchical Clustering of Iris Dataset",
       subtitle = "Single-linkage agglomerative clustering")
```

**Cophenetic distance** is the height at which two points are first joined in the dendrogram. Formally:

$$
c(x_i, x_j) = h(\text{LCA of leaves } i, j \text{ in the dendrogram})
$$

It is the metric *induced* by the tree structure. A clustering is considered faithful to the original distances if the **cophenetic correlation coefficient**

$$
\rho_c = \frac{\text{corr}(D, C)}{\sigma_D \, \sigma_C}
$$

is high (typically $\rho_c \geq 0.75$ indicates a good fit).

*(7 marks: 2 for code, 2 for output description, 3 for cophenetic concept.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Network/Tree Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$ with $\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert$:** A connected acyclic graph on $N$ vertices has $N-1$ edges, NOT $N$. Adding one more creates a cycle.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the Handshake Lemma:** In an undirected graph, *each edge contributes 1 to the degree of two vertices*, so $\sum \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$, not $\vert E \vert$.
> 3. **Mixing up in-degree and out-degree:** For directed graphs, $\sum \text{indeg} = \sum \text{outdeg} = \vert E \vert$. Students often write $\sum \text{indeg} = 2 \vert E \vert$, copying the undirected formula incorrectly.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong layout:** Plotting a *tree* with a force-directed layout produces tangled lines. Always use Reingold-Tilford / dendrogram layout for trees.
> 5. **Not drawing arrowheads on directed edges:** Directed graphs without `arrow = arrow(...)` in `ggraph` lose the directional information and lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Single-linkage vs. complete-linkage confusion:** Single-linkage merge distance is the **minimum** inter-cluster distance; complete-linkage uses the **maximum**. Reversing these gives different dendrograms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **graph** is $G = (V, E)$ — vertices + edges, may have cycles.
- ✅ A **tree** is a connected acyclic graph with **$\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$**.
- ✅ The **Handshake Lemma**: $\sum \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ (undirected); $\sum \text{indeg} = \sum \text{outdeg} = \vert E \vert$ (directed).
- ✅ **Fruchterman-Reingold (FR)** layout: nodes = charged particles, edges = springs, minimize $E = \sum \frac{k^2}{d_{ij}} - \sum \frac{d_{ij}^2}{k}$.
- ✅ **Kamada-Kawai (KK)**: minimizes stress based on graph-theoretic distances — better for sparse graphs.
- ✅ **Reingold-Tilford**: the canonical **tree** layout algorithm — produces clean, non-overlapping hierarchies.
- ✅ **Dendrogram** is the visualization of a hierarchical clustering tree — height = merge distance.
- ✅ **Single linkage** = minimum inter-cluster distance; **Complete linkage** = maximum; **Average linkage** = mean.
- ✅ **Cophenetic distance** = height of LCA in the dendrogram; measures cluster fidelity.
- ✅ In R, **`ggraph`** is the modern grammar-of-graphics framework for networks and trees (built on `igraph` + `ggplot2`).
- ✅ `geom_edge_link()` → straight, `geom_edge_arc()` → curved, `geom_edge_diagonal()` → 45°, `geom_edge_fan()` → bundled parallel, `geom_edge_density()` → cluster fill.
- ✅ `layout = "fr"` (Fruchterman-Reingold), `layout = "kk"` (Kamada-Kawai), `layout = "dendrogram"` (tree), `layout = "graphopt"` (optimization), `layout = "circle"` (circular).
- ✅ **Centrality metrics**: degree, betweenness, closeness, eigenvector — encoded visually as **node size or color**.
- ✅ **Weighted edges** should be encoded as **edge width**; **directed edges** need **arrowheads**; **clusters** can be encoded via **node color**.
- ✅ A **forest** is a disjoint union of $k$ trees: $\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - k$.
- ✅ **Common KTU commands**: `tbl_graph()`, `centrality_degree()`, `centrality_betweenness()`, `geom_edge_link()`, `geom_node_point()`, `as.dendrogram()`, `hclust()`.
