# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Matrix Views in R** refer to the set of **indexing, subsetting, and extraction operations** that allow a programmer to look at, retrieve, or manipulate specific portions of a 2-dimensional `matrix` object — including single cells, entire rows, entire columns, sub-matrices, diagonals, and conditionally filtered elements.

In R, a `matrix` is a **homogeneous, rectangular, two-dimensional data structure** that stores values of a single atomic data type (`numeric`, `character`, `logical`, etc.) arranged in a fixed number of **rows × columns**. The "view" of a matrix is determined by *which indices* you pass to the square-bracket `[ ]` operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Concept:** In R, a matrix is internally stored as a **vector with a `dim` attribute**. This is why matrix indexing in R is conceptually an *extension of vector indexing* — but extended to two dimensions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **movie theatre seating chart** displayed on a wall:

| | | | | |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A1** | A2 | A3 | A4 | A5 |
| **B1** | B2 | B3 | B4 | B5 |
| **C1** | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 |

- The **entire chart** = the full matrix.
- **Row 1 (A1, A2, A3, A4, A5)** = a *row view* of the matrix.
- **Column 2 (A2, B2, C2)** = a *column view* of the matrix.
- **Single seat C3** = a *cell view* of the matrix.
- **Box A3 to B4** = a *sub-matrix view*.
- **A1, B2, C3** (diagonal) = a *diagonal view*.

In R, you "look at" any of these views simply by changing what numbers you write inside the square brackets: `mat[1, ]`, `mat[ , 2]`, `mat[3, 3]`, `mat[1:2, 3:4]`, `diag(mat)`.

---

## 1.3 Why Matrix Views Matter in Data Visualization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the context of **PBCDT604 — Data Visualization**, matrix views are the *gateway* to plotting, because:
> - Heatmaps require extracting **column views** as x, y, z axes.
> - Bar charts of grouped data require **row views** as categorical labels.
> - Pair plots and correlation matrices rely on extracting the **upper/lower triangular views**.
> - Image plots (`image()`, `persp()`) plot the entire matrix as a 2D surface.

**Standard R metrics to remember in bold:**
- **`nrow(m)`** → number of rows in matrix `m` → returns an **integer**.
- **`ncol(m)`** → number of columns in matrix `m` → returns an **integer**.
- **`dim(m)`** → returns a vector of length **2**: `c(rows, cols)`.
- **`length(m)`** → total elements = **`nrow × ncol`**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an R Matrix

A matrix in R is constructed primarily using the `matrix()` function:

```r
matrix(data, nrow, ncol, byrow, dimnames)
```

| Argument | Meaning | Default |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `data` | The source vector of values | — |
| `nrow` | Number of rows | — |
| `ncol` | Number of columns | — |
| `byrow` | If `TRUE`, fill row-by-row; if `FALSE`, fill column-by-column | `FALSE` |
| `dimnames` | A `list` of length 2 giving row and column names | `NULL` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU point:** You only need to specify **either** `nrow` **or** `ncol`. R computes the other automatically from the length of the data vector.

---

## 2.2 The Four Core Matrix Views

### View 1 — Single Cell View: `mat[i, j]`
Returns the element located at the **i-th row** and **j-th column**.

### View 2 — Row View: `mat[i, ]`
Returns the **entire i-th row** as a *vector*. *(Unless `drop = FALSE` is used, in which case it stays a matrix.)*

### View 3 — Column View: `mat[ , j]`
Returns the **entire j-th column** as a *vector*.

### View 4 — Sub-Matrix View: `mat[i1:i2, j1:j2]`
Returns a rectangular **sub-matrix** spanning rows `i1..i2` and columns `j1..j2`.

---

## 2.3 Advanced Matrix Views

### Negative Indexing View: `mat[-i, ]` or `mat[ , -j]`
Returns the matrix **excluding** row `i` (or column `j`).

### Logical Indexing View: `mat[mat > 5]`
Returns a *vector* of all elements satisfying the condition. Combine with row/column selection: `mat[mat[ ,1] > 5, ]`.

### Diagonal View: `diag(mat)` and `mat[row(mat) == col(mat)]`
Extracts the **main diagonal** elements of a square matrix.

### Upper / Lower Triangular View
```r
mat[upper.tri(mat)]   # all elements above the main diagonal
mat[lower.tri(mat)]   # all elements below the main diagonal
```

### Named View: `mat["RowName", "ColName"]`
If `dimnames` are set, you can index using **character row/column names**.

---

## 2.4 The `drop = FALSE` Parameter — A KTU Favourite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall to remember:** When you select **exactly one row or one column** from a matrix, R *automatically* simplifies the result to a **vector**. To **preserve the matrix structure** (keeping dimensions intact), use `drop = FALSE`.

```r
m <- matrix(1:9, nrow = 3)
m[1, ]         # returns: 1 2 3  (a vector)
m[1, , drop = FALSE]   # returns: 1 2 3  (still a 1x3 matrix)
```

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | R Syntax | Result Type | Example |
|-----------|----------|-------------|---------|
| Total elements | `length(m)` | integer | `9` for 3×3 |
| Dimensions | `dim(m)` | integer vector | `c(3, 3)` |
| Number of rows | `nrow(m)` | integer | `3` |
| Number of columns | `ncol(m)` | integer | `3` |
| Single cell | `m[i, j]` | single value | `m[2, 3]` |
| Full row | `m[i, ]` | vector / matrix* | `m[1, ]` |
| Full column | `m[ , j]` | vector / matrix* | `m[ , 2]` |
| Sub-matrix | `m[i1:i2, j1:j2]` | matrix | `m[1:2, 2:3]` |
| Exclude row | `m[-i, ]` | matrix | `m[-1, ]` |
| Exclude column | `m[ , -j]` | matrix | `m[ , -3]` |
| Diagonal | `diag(m)` | vector | `diag(m)` |
| Upper triangle | `m[upper.tri(m)]` | vector | — |
| Lower triangle | `m[lower.tri(m)]` | vector | — |
| Conditional | `m[m > k]` | vector | `m[m > 5]` |
| Named access | `m["r1", "c1"]` | value | — |
| Preserve matrix | `m[i, , drop=FALSE]` | matrix | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Cells marked with `*` in the "Result Type" column depend on whether `drop = FALSE` is used.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Application of Matrix Views |
|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Civil Engineering** | Extracting stress/strain rows from FEM matrices for beam analysis |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Slicing admittance matrices (`Y-bus`) per bus for power-flow studies |
| **Computer Science** | Pulling image-pixel rows/columns for edge-detection filters |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Extracting rows of a Jacobian matrix for robot-arm kinematics |
| **Data Science** | Building train/test splits via row-subsetting before modelling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Constructing a 3×3 Matrix and Exploring All Views

### Step 1: Create the data vector
```r
v <- 1:9
```
The vector `v` is: `1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9`

### Step 2: Convert vector → 3×3 matrix
```r
m <- matrix(v, nrow = 3, ncol = 3)
```

The resulting matrix `m` (filled **column-by-column** by default) is:

$$
m = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 & 7 \\ 2 & 5 & 8 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Derive the **cell view** `m[2, 3]`
- Row index `i = 2`, Column index `j = 3`.
- The element in the **2nd row, 3rd column** of the matrix above is `8`.

$$
m[2,3] = 8
$$

### Step 4: Derive the **row view** `m[1, ]`
- All elements in the 1st row.

$$
m[1, ] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 & 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5: Derive the **column view** `m[ , 2]`
- All elements in the 2nd column.

$$
m[ , 2] = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6: Derive the **sub-matrix view** `m[1:2, 2:3]`
- Take rows 1 and 2, intersect with columns 2 and 3.

$$
m[1:2, 2:3] = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 7 \\ 5 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 7: Derive the **diagonal view** `diag(m)`
- The main-diagonal elements are at positions `(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)`.

$$
\text{diag}(m) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 8: Derive the **upper-triangular view** `m[upper.tri(m)]`
- `upper.tri(m)` returns a `TRUE/FALSE` matrix marking elements *above* the main diagonal.

$$
\text{upper.tri}(m) = \begin{bmatrix} \text{F} & \text{T} & \text{T} \\ \text{F} & \text{F} & \text{T} \\ \text{F} & \text{F} & \text{F} \end{bmatrix}
$$

- The corresponding values are pulled out column-by-column:

$$
m[\text{upper.tri}(m)] = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 9: Derive the **negative-index view** `m[-2, ]`
- Exclude the 2nd row entirely.

$$
m[-2, ] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 & 7 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Logical (Conditional) View

### Step 1: Build a 4×4 matrix
```r
A <- matrix(1:16, nrow = 4, ncol = 4)
```

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & 9 & 13 \\ 2 & 6 & 10 & 14 \\ 3 & 7 & 11 & 15 \\ 4 & 8 & 12 & 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: View only elements greater than 10
```r
A[A > 10]
```

- The values satisfying `A > 10` are: `13, 14, 15, 16, 11, 12` (extracted column-by-column).

$$
A[A > 10] = \begin{bmatrix} 11 & 12 & 13 & 14 & 15 & 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: View rows where the **1st column** is greater than 2
```r
A[A[ , 1] > 2, ]
```

- Rows whose 1st column element is `> 2` → rows 3 and 4.

$$
A[A[ ,1] > 2, ] = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 7 & 11 & 15 \\ 4 & 8 & 12 & 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — The `drop = FALSE` Matrix-Preservation Trick

### Step 1: Extract row 1 normally
```r
m[1, ]          # Output: 1 4 7   (R has dropped it to a vector)
```

### Step 2: Extract row 1 preserving matrix shape
```r
m[1, , drop = FALSE]
```

$$
m[1, , \text{drop=FALSE}] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 & 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- The output **is still a matrix of dimension 1×3**, confirmed by `dim(m[1, , drop = FALSE])` returning `c(1, 3)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Many R functions (like `rbind`, `cbind`, `apply`) require a matrix input. If a row-extraction silently becomes a vector, downstream operations can fail or produce unexpected errors.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Row-and-Column Naming View

### Step 1: Create a named matrix
```r
scores <- matrix(c(85, 90, 78, 92, 88, 76),
                 nrow = 3, ncol = 2,
                 dimnames = list(c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol"),
                                 c("Math", "Science")))
```

$$
\text{scores} = \begin{bmatrix} & \text{Math} & \text{Science} \\ \text{Alice} & 85 & 90 \\ \text{Bob} & 78 & 92 \\ \text{Carol} & 88 & 76 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: View Carol's Math score
```r
scores["Carol", "Math"]    # Output: 88
```

### Step 3: View the entire Science column by name
```r
scores[ , "Science"]
```

$$
\text{scores}[ , \text{"Science"}] = \begin{bmatrix} 90 \\ 92 \\ 76 \end{bmatrix}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Matrix View Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Matrix m] --> B[Single Cell View]
    A --> C[Row View]
    A --> D[Column View]
    A --> E[Sub-Matrix View]
    A --> F[Diagonal View]
    A --> G[Triangular View]
    A --> H[Logical View]
    A --> I[Named View]

    B --> B1["m i j"]
    C --> C1["m i blank"]
    C --> C2["m i blank drop FALSE"]
    D --> D1["m blank j"]
    E --> E1["m i1:i2 blank j1:j2"]
    E --> E2["m -i blank"]
    E --> E3["m blank -j"]
    F --> F1["diag m"]
    G --> G1["upper.tri m"]
    G --> G2["lower.tri m"]
    H --> H1["m m greater k"]
    H --> H2["m m blank 1 greater k blank"]
    I --> I1["m rowname blank colname"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: From Vector to Multi-View Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    raw[Raw Data Vector v] --> A[matrix v nrow ncol]
    A --> B[Index Cell m i j]
    A --> C[Index Row m i blank]
    A --> D[Index Col m blank j]
    A --> E[Slice Sub m 1:3 blank 2:4]
    A --> F[Diagonal diag m]
    A --> G[Logical Filter m greater 10]
    B --> out1[Single Numeric Value]
    C --> out2[Vector of length ncol]
    D --> out3[Vector of length nrow]
    E --> out4[Smaller Matrix]
    F --> out5[Diagonal Vector]
    G --> out6[Filtered Vector]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Index Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Matrix m of size r times c] --> stepA[Receive Index Expression]
    stepA --> stepB{Index Type?}
    stepB -->|Positive Integer| stepC[Subset by Position]
    stepB -->|Negative Integer| stepD[Exclude by Position]
    stepB -->|Logical Vector| stepE[Filter by TRUE FALSE]
    stepB -->|Character Vector| stepF[Lookup by Name]
    stepC --> stepG[Apply drop Argument]
    stepD --> stepG
    stepE --> stepG
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH{Selection is 1D?}
    stepH -->|Yes and drop TRUE| stepI[Return Vector]
    stepH -->|Yes and drop FALSE| stepJ[Return 1 by n Matrix]
    stepH -->|No| stepK[Return Matrix]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q:** Define a *matrix view* in R. List any **four** distinct views you can obtain from an R matrix with their indexing syntax.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *matrix view* in R refers to the way in which elements, rows, columns, or sub-portions of a `matrix` object are accessed and returned using the `[ ]` operator with appropriate row/column indices. The four distinct views are:
1. **Single Cell View:** `m[i, j]` — returns one element.
2. **Row View:** `m[i, ]` — returns the i-th row.
3. **Column View:** `m[ , j]` — returns the j-th column.
4. **Sub-Matrix View:** `m[i1:i2, j1:j2]` — returns a rectangular block.

*(Keyword matrix: definition + four views + four correct syntaxes = full 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q:** What is the purpose of the `drop = FALSE` argument when extracting a row or column from a matrix in R? Illustrate with an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When `drop = TRUE` (the default), R **simplifies** a single row or column into a one-dimensional *vector*, losing the matrix structure. Setting `drop = FALSE` **preserves the matrix shape** of the output, returning a 1×n or n×1 matrix instead.

**Example:**
```r
m <- matrix(1:9, nrow = 3)
m[1, ]              # Returns vector: 1 4 7
m[1, , drop = FALSE]   # Returns 1x3 matrix:  1 4 7
```

This is essential when passing the output to functions that expect a matrix (e.g., `rbind`, `apply`).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the different types of matrix views in R with suitable syntax. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Given the matrix `M` below, write R code to extract: (i) the element at row 2, column 3, (ii) the 2nd row, (iii) the 3rd column, and (iv) the sub-matrix formed by rows 1–2 and columns 2–3. Also extract the diagonal elements. *(7 marks)*
>
> $$
> M = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 & 30 \\ 40 & 50 & 60 \\ 70 & 80 & 90 \end{bmatrix}
> $$

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Types of Matrix Views (7 marks)**

In R, a matrix is a 2D homogeneous structure, and **matrix views** describe all possible ways of accessing its elements:

1. **Cell View** `M[i, j]` — extracts a single element.
2. **Row View** `M[i, ]` — returns the i-th row as a vector.
3. **Column View** `M[ , j]` — returns the j-th column as a vector.
4. **Sub-Matrix View** `M[i1:i2, j1:j2]` — returns a rectangular sub-block.
5. **Negative Index View** `M[-i, ]` or `M[ , -j]` — excludes a row/column.
6. **Logical View** `M[M > k]` or `M[M[ , 1] > k, ]` — filters by condition.
7. **Diagonal View** `diag(M)` — returns main diagonal.
8. **Triangular View** `M[upper.tri(M)]` / `M[lower.tri(M)]` — extracts triangle.
9. **Named View** `M["rowname", "colname"]` — uses labels from `dimnames`.
10. **Preserved View** `M[i, , drop = FALSE]` — keeps the matrix structure intact.

**Part (b) — Worked R Code (7 marks)**

```r
M <- matrix(c(10, 20, 30,
              40, 50, 60,
              70, 80, 90), nrow = 3, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)
```

- (i) Element at (2, 3): `M[2, 3] → 60`
- (ii) 2nd row: `M[2, ] → 40 50 60`
- (iii) 3rd column: `M[ , 3] → 30 60 90`
- (iv) Sub-matrix (rows 1–2, cols 2–3):

```r
M[1:2, 2:3]
```

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 20 & 30 \\ 50 & 60 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- Diagonal: `diag(M) → 10 50 90`

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]**
>
> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
> **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram and R code, demonstrate how to construct a 4×4 numeric matrix and apply **five** different matrix-view operations on it. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Discuss the role of the `drop` argument, negative indexing, and logical indexing in matrix views. Demonstrate each with an example. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Five Views on a 4×4 Matrix (7 marks)**

```r
v <- 1:16
M <- matrix(v, nrow = 4, ncol = 4,
            dimnames = list(c("R1","R2","R3","R4"),
                            c("C1","C2","C3","C4")))
```

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} & C1 & C2 & C3 & C4 \\ R1 & 1 & 5 & 9 & 13 \\ R2 & 2 & 6 & 10 & 14 \\ R3 & 3 & 7 & 11 & 15 \\ R4 & 4 & 8 & 12 & 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Five views applied:**

| # | View | R Code | Result |
|---|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Cell (3,2) | `M[3, 2]` | `7` |
| 2 | Row 2 | `M[2, ]` | `2 6 10 14` |
| 3 | Column 4 | `M[ , 4]` | `13 14 15 16` |
| 4 | Sub-matrix (rows 2–3, cols 1–2) | `M[2:3, 1:2]` | `2 6 / 3 7` |
| 5 | Diagonal | `diag(M)` | `1 6 11 16` |

**Part (b) — Role of `drop`, negative & logical indexing (7 marks)**

1. **`drop` argument:** Forces R to *keep the matrix shape* of the result; without it, single-row/column selections auto-coerce to vectors. `M[1, , drop = FALSE]` returns a 1×4 matrix.

2. **Negative indexing:** Used to *exclude* specific rows or columns by prefixing `-`.
   ```r
   M[-1, ]        # excludes row 1
   M[ , -c(1,4)]  # excludes columns 1 and 4
   ```

3. **Logical indexing:** Used to *filter* elements or rows by a Boolean condition.
   ```r
   M[M > 8]              # all values > 8
   M[M[ ,1] %% 2 == 0, ] # only rows with even 1st-column values
   ```

Together, these three techniques give complete programmatic control over **which portion of the matrix you view** and **what shape the returned object retains** — a critical skill for data pre-processing in visualization tasks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `byrow = TRUE`** — When data must appear row-wise, students often leave it at the default `FALSE` and get a transposed-looking answer. **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Losing matrix structure** — Extracting a single row/column without `drop = FALSE` and submitting it as a "matrix view" answer. Examiners expect the student to *explicitly state* the structural loss and how to prevent it. **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing `diag(M)` with `M[upper.tri(M)]`** — They are *not* the same; `diag` returns only the main diagonal (one element per row), while `upper.tri` returns the entire upper half. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the `nrow/ncol` argument** when constructing a matrix from a long vector — R will produce a *single-column* matrix and the student is marked wrong. **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Not writing output for sub-question (b) parts** — KTU examiners in Part B (programming) always want both the **R code** *and* the **resulting value/matrix** clearly displayed. Code without output loses half marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 A **matrix view** = *any indexing operation* on an R matrix: cell, row, column, sub-matrix, diagonal, triangle, logical, or named.
- 📌 A matrix is internally a **vector with a `dim` attribute** of length **2**.
- 📌 `M[i, j]` = cell; `M[i, ]` = row; `M[ , j]` = column; `M[i1:i2, j1:j2]` = sub-matrix.
- 📌 Use **`drop = FALSE`** to keep a single row/column as a matrix and not a vector.
- 📌 **Negative indexing** (`-i`, `-j`) excludes the indicated row/column.
- 📌 **Logical indexing** (`M > k`, `M[M[ ,1] > k, ]`) is the most powerful filter for data-visualization pipelines.
- 📌 `diag(M)` returns the **main diagonal** as a vector; `upper.tri(M)` and `lower.tri(M)` return triangular views.
- 📌 The `matrix()` function fills values **column-by-column** by default — set `byrow = TRUE` for row-wise fill.
- 📌 `nrow(M)`, `ncol(M)`, `dim(M)`, `length(M)` are the **four key dimension functions** you must memorize.
- 📌 Named matrices use `dimnames = list(row_names, col_names)` and support `M["rowname", "colname"]` access.
- 📌 In KTU exams, always show **both the R code and the resulting output** for full marks on programming sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Containment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Containment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Containment in Data Visualization — Core Definition & Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Data Visualization and Visual Analytics**, **Containment** is a fundamental **visual encoding primitive** used to represent **hierarchical (tree-structured) relationships** between data elements through **spatial enclosure** — that is, by placing a child element *physically inside* the visual region occupied by its parent element. This stands in direct contrast to **Connection-based** representations, which use *lines, edges, or links* to convey relationships.

Formally, given a rooted tree $\mathcal{T} = (V, E)$ with root $r$, a containment layout $L$ assigns each node $v \in V$ a bounded region $\mathcal{R}(v) \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ such that for any parent $p$ and child $c$:

$$\mathcal{R}(c) \subset \mathcal{R}(p) \quad \text{and} \quad \mathcal{R}(c) \cap \mathcal{R}(c') = \emptyset \;\; \forall\, c \neq c' \in \text{children}(p)$$

This guarantees **strict nesting** (no overlap among siblings) and **transitive enclosure** (descendants remain inside ancestors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Containment is one of the two primary idioms for visualizing **tree/hierarchical data** (the other being connection/node-link). The KTU examiner specifically tests whether students can articulate the **trade-offs between space efficiency, aspect ratio, and readability** when choosing between Treemaps, Sunburst, Icicle plots, and Venn/Euler diagrams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Russian Doll" Intuition

Imagine a set of **Russian nesting dolls (Matryoshka)**. Each doll contains smaller dolls inside it. To see the family tree of a particular doll, you only need to **open it up** — the smaller doll inside is its child, and the *smallest* one at the center is a leaf.

> A **containment visualization works exactly like this**:
> - The **outermost rectangle/arc** = the root category (e.g., "All Products")
> - **Dolls nested inside** = sub-categories (e.g., "Electronics")
> - **Smaller dolls inside those** = individual items (e.g., "Laptop, Phone, Tablet")

You understand the hierarchy at a single glance because the **spatial position itself carries the relational meaning** — no arrows or connecting lines are needed. The eye instantly recognizes: *"Whatever lies inside this region belongs to that category."*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties

Key evaluation metrics used to assess the quality of containment layouts:

| Metric | Symbol | Ideal Value | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Aspect Ratio | $\rho$ | $\rho \rightarrow 1$ | Closeness of each rectangle to a square |
| Readability | $\mathcal{L}$ | Maximize | User can compare sibling areas visually |
| Space Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = 1$ | Fraction of canvas used (no whitespace) |
| Stability | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = 1$ | Same data → same layout (determinism) |
| Occlusion | $\omega$ | $\omega = 0$ | Hidden elements not desirable |

> [!NOTE]
> The **aspect ratio** is mathematically the most important property: for a rectangle with width $w$ and height $h$, $\rho = \max(w,h) / \min(w,h)$. The closer $\rho$ is to 1, the easier it is for a human to **visually compare** the rectangle's area to its neighbors (Tufte's principle of *perceptual uniformity*).

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Containment Visualizations

The major families of containment-based visualizations covered in KTU Module 2 are:

1. **Treemaps** — Rectangular subdivision (Shneiderman, 1991)
2. **Sunburst / Multi-level Pie Charts** — Polar/radial subdivision
3. **Icicle Plots** — Horizontal rectangular subdivision, root on one edge
4. **Venn Diagrams** — Overlapping (set-intersection) containment
5. **Euler Diagrams** — Area-proportional set relationships
6. **Nested Circles / Circle Packing** — Circular containment
7. **Stepped Area / Cascade Charts** — Containment as filled regions in 2D plots

These are explored rigorously in subsequent sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Containment Encodes Information

Containment visualizations operate on three orthogonal visual channels simultaneously:

- **Position (Spatial Layout):** Where the region is placed (root vs. leaf, left vs. right).
- **Area / Size:** The magnitude or weight of the data item (e.g., revenue, population, frequency).
- **Color / Texture (Secondary Channel):** An additional categorical or ordinal variable layered on top.

The **why** behind containment's effectiveness lies in the human visual system's pre-attentive processing: humans can **instantly judge "inside-of" relationships** without reading legends or tracing lines. This is a Gestalt principle known as **Law of Common Region** or **Law of Enclosure**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Input:** A weighted tree $T$ where each leaf has a positive weight $w_i > 0$, and internal node weights are sums of their descendants.
2. **Layout Engine:** An algorithm partitions the 2D canvas recursively.
3. **Encoding:** Each subtree receives a region whose **area is proportional to its total weight**.
4. **Output:** A 2D arrangement where enclosure depth = tree depth, and area = quantitative value.

### 2.1.2 The Core "Why" — Three Engineering Justifications

- **Why area?** Human perception of **area is more accurate** than length when comparing magnitudes (Cleveland & McGill, 1984 perceptual hierarchy).
- **Why nesting?** It exploits **Gestalt enclosure**, the strongest pre-attentive cue for grouping.
- **Why no edges?** It eliminates **edge-crossing** and **visual clutter**, the two biggest problems of node-link diagrams for large trees.

## 2.2 Containment vs. Connection — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU question** — know the trade-offs cold.

| Property | Containment (Treemap/Sunburst) | Connection (Node-Link) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Space Usage | Excellent (fills canvas) | Poor (whitespace heavy) |
| Sibling Comparison | Easy (adjacent areas) | Difficult (scattered nodes) |
| Path Tracing | Hard (no explicit path) | Easy (follow edges) |
| Scalability (nodes) | Thousands of leaves | Few hundred max |
| Deep Hierarchies | Excellent | Poor (edge crossings) |
| Best Use Case | Part-to-whole, weighted | Topological structure |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the Containment topic. **Vertical bars use $\vert$ to preserve table integrity.**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Aspect Ratio | $\rho_i = \dfrac{\max(w_i, h_i)}{\min(w_i, h_i)}$ | Lower $\rho$ → more square-like; ideal: $\rho \leq \beta$ (worst-case bound) |
| Total Weight | $W_{\text{root}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i$ | Sum of leaf weights assigned to canvas area $A$ |
| Scale Factor | $s = \dfrac{A}{W_{\text{root}}}$ | Converts abstract weight to pixel area |
| Area of Node $i$ | $A_i = w_i \cdot s$ | Preserves weight-to-area proportionality |
| Worst-Case Aspect Ratio | $\beta = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 4\gamma^2}}{2\gamma}$ | Bound used in squarified treemaps where $\gamma = w_{\max}/w_{\text{avg}}$ |
| Pixel-Row Layout | $h_{\text{row}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{row}}}{w_{\text{rect}}}$ | Height of row in slice-and-dice (horizontal slice) |
| Squarify Step | $\min \rho \;\text{s.t.}\; \sum w_i \cdot s = A_{\text{row}}$ | Optimization goal in squarified treemap |
| Space Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{\text{used}}}{A_{\text{canvas}}}$ | Ratio of used area; ideal: $\eta = 1$ |
| Sunburst Angular Width | $\theta_i = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{w_i}{W_{\text{parent}}}$ | Angular slice in radians for child $i$ |
| Icicle Bar Height | $h_i = H \cdot \dfrac{w_i}{W_{\text{root}}}$ | Constant height per level in icicle plot |
| Venn Overlap | $\vert A \cap B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cup B \vert$ | Inclusion–exclusion principle for 2 sets |
| Euler 3-Set Union | $\vert A \cup B \cup C \vert = \sum \vert \cdot \vert - \sum \vert \cdot \cap \cdot \vert + \vert A \cap B \cap C \vert$ | Inclusion–exclusion for 3 sets |
| Circle Packing Radius | $r_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{w_i \cdot A}{\pi \cdot W_{\text{parent}}}}$ | Area-preserving radius for nested circles |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

Containment visualizations are the **industry standard** for:

- **Disk Usage Analyzers** (WinDirStat, macOS Storage, DaisyDisk) — Treemaps of file sizes.
- **Stock Market Visualization** (FinViz, Bloomberg Heatmaps) — Sector → Industry → Company.
- **Sports Analytics** — Team → Player → Pass network statistics.
- **Genomics & Phylogenetics** — Evolutionary trees as nested clusters.
- **Budget & Financial Reporting** — Department → Sub-department → Line item.
- **GenAI Token Budgets & Compute Allocation** — Modern LLM dashboards.

> [!NOTE]
> In **R**, the canonical packages are `treemap`, `treemapify` (ggplot2 extension), `sunburstR`, `d3r`, and `plotly`. KTU lab exams frequently ask students to generate a treemap from a given data frame using `treemap()`.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Squarified Treemap Layout Algorithm

The **squarified treemap** (Bruls, Huijsen & van Wijk, 2000) is the gold-standard containment algorithm. It is the **single most testable derivation** in the KTU Containment module. Below is the full step-by-step mathematical treatment.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Given a sorted list of $n$ weights $w_1 \geq w_2 \geq \ldots \geq w_n > 0$ and a rectangular canvas of width $W$ and height $H$ (with $W \geq H$), partition the canvas into $n$ rectangles, one per weight, such that:

- Rectangle $i$ has area $A_i = w_i \cdot s$ where $s = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$.
- The **maximum aspect ratio** across all rectangles is **minimized**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the scale factor $s$.**

$$s = \frac{W \cdot H}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$$

This ensures $\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i = W \cdot H$ (perfect space efficiency, $\eta = 1$).

**Step 2 — Define the "row" abstraction.**

A *row* is a contiguous subset of rectangles $\{w_{i}, w_{i+1}, \ldots, w_{k}\}$ arranged along the shorter edge of the remaining canvas. The total weight of the row is:

$$W_{\text{row}} = \sum_{j=i}^{k} w_j$$

If the row is placed along a strip of width $w_{\text{rect}}$ (the *current shorter edge of the remaining canvas*), each rectangle $j$ in the row gets height:

$$h_j = \frac{w_j \cdot s}{w_{\text{rect}}}$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case aspect ratio of the row.**

For a row placed in a strip of width $w_{\text{rect}}$ with row weight $W_{\text{row}}$, the worst aspect ratio across all rectangles in the row is:

$$\rho_{\text{row}} = \max\left( \frac{w_{\text{rect}}^2 \cdot W_{\text{row}}}{s \cdot w_{\min}^2},\; \frac{s \cdot w_{\max}^2}{w_{\text{rect}}^2 \cdot W_{\text{row}}} \right)$$

This is obtained by considering the tallest rectangle ($\rho = h_{\max}/w_{\text{rect}}$) and the widest rectangle ($\rho = w_{\text{rect}}/h_{\min}$).

**Step 4 — Squarify decision function $\mathcal{S}$.**

Define $\mathcal{S}(\text{row}, w_{k+1}) = \rho$ as the worst aspect ratio if we *add* the next weight $w_{k+1}$ to the current row. The algorithm **keeps adding** weights to the row as long as:

$$\mathcal{S}(\text{row} \cup \{w_{k+1}\}) \leq \mathcal{S}(\text{row})$$

The moment adding the next weight *worsens* the ratio, the current row is **finalized** (laid out permanently), and a new row begins.

**Step 5 — Layout the finalized row.**

The finalized row occupies a perpendicular strip of width $w_{\text{rect}}' = \dfrac{W_{\text{row}} \cdot s}{H_{\text{remain}}}$, where $H_{\text{remain}}$ is the current short edge of the remaining canvas. The remaining canvas is then *rotated 90°* (i.e., the new short edge becomes the previous long edge), and the algorithm recurses.

**Step 6 — Convergence / Termination.**

The algorithm terminates when all $n$ weights are placed. Complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ if weights are pre-sorted; sorting adds another $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$, giving a total worst-case of $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sunburst Angular Partition (Polar Containment)

For a **sunburst** (radial treemap), the parent node is a full circle of $2\pi$ radians. Children are placed as **angular wedges** along the outer arc.

**Step 1 — Total angular budget.**

$$\Theta_{\text{parent}} = 2\pi \;\; \text{radians}$$

**Step 2 — Angular width of child $i$.**

$$\theta_i = \Theta_{\text{parent}} \cdot \frac{w_i}{\sum_{j \in \text{children}} w_j} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{w_i}{W_{\text{parent}}}$$

**Step 3 — Recursive subdivision.**

Each child becomes a parent for the next level, and its own $\Theta_{\text{parent}} = \theta_i$ is recursively subdivided:

$$\theta_{i,k} = \theta_i \cdot \frac{w_{i,k}}{\sum_{l} w_{i,l}} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{w_i}{W_{\text{root}}} \cdot \frac{w_{i,k}}{W_i}$$

**Step 4 — Radial position (concentric rings).**

The depth $d$ of a node determines its radial band $r_d$:

$$r_d = r_{\text{inner}} + d \cdot \Delta r, \quad \text{where} \;\; \Delta r = \frac{r_{\text{outer}} - r_{\text{inner}}}{D_{\max}}$$

and $D_{\max}$ is the maximum depth of the tree.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Icicle Plot Geometry

Icicle plots are the **1-D analogue** of treemaps (effectively a horizontal bar chart, hierarchically nested).

**Step 1 — Bar dimensions at depth $d$.**

Each node at depth $d$ is drawn as a rectangle:
- **Width:** $w_d^{(i)} = W_{\text{canvas}} \cdot \dfrac{w_i}{W_{\text{root}}}$
- **Height:** $h_d = \dfrac{H_{\text{canvas}}}{D_{\max}}$ (constant per level)

**Step 2 — Vertical stacking.**

All nodes at depth $d$ are aligned along the horizontal baseline, and depth-$d+1$ bars are placed directly below depth-$d$ bars (root at top). The recursive nesting is preserved because children always start and end at their parent's horizontal boundaries.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Containment Visualization

| Use-Case Constraint | Best Containment Choice | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compare $\geq 100$ weighted leaves | Squarified Treemap | Best space + aspect ratio |
| Show hierarchy depth clearly | Icicle Plot | Linear layout, easy path tracing |
| Emphasize cyclic / whole-to-part | Sunburst | Angular encoding intuitive for pie/whole |
| Show set intersections | Venn / Euler Diagram | Native overlap semantics |
| Many levels, sparse data | Circle Packing | Organic look, scales well |
| Time + Hierarchy | Cascade / ThemeRiver | Containment as flowing area |
| Single tree, static report | Treemap with labels | Print-friendly, readable |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** If a question gives a dataset with **$>$ 5 levels of hierarchy and 1 attribute**, the answer is **Treemap**. If it gives **2 attributes (one categorical, one quantitative) at 3 levels**, the answer is **Sunburst** (categorical → angular, quantitative → arc length/radius).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Containment Layout Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Hierarchical Data T = V, E] --> B[Weight Aggregator]
    B --> C[Weighted Tree with Leaf Weights w1 to wn]
    C --> D{Containment Algorithm Selector}
    D --> E1[Squarified Treemap Engine]
    D --> E2[Sunburst Polar Engine]
    D --> E3[Icicle Linear Engine]
    D --> E4[Circle Packing Engine]
    E1 --> F1[Rectangular Region Map R of v]
    E2 --> F2[Polar Region Map Theta v, r v]
    E3 --> F3[Banded Region Map x v, y v]
    E4 --> F4[Circular Region Map cx v, cy v, r v]
    F1 --> G[Renderer with Color Hue Map]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Final 2D Canvas Output]
```

## 4.2 Containment vs. Connection — Conceptual Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONN["CONNECTION-BASED (Node-Link)"]
        A1[Root] --- B1[Child 1]
        A1 --- B2[Child 2]
        A1 --- B3[Child 3]
        B1 --- C1[Leaf 1A]
        B1 --- C2[Leaf 1B]
        B2 --- C3[Leaf 2A]
        B3 --- C4[Leaf 3A]
        B3 --- C5[Leaf 3B]
    end
    subgraph CONT["CONTAINMENT-BASED (Treemap)"]
        A2[Root Region]:::root
        A2 -. contains .-> B1R[Child 1 Region]:::child
        A2 -. contains .-> B2R[Child 2 Region]:::child
        A2 -. contains .-> B3R[Child 3 Region]:::child
        B1R -. contains .-> C1R[Leaf 1A]:::leaf
        B1R -. contains .-> C2R[Leaf 1B]:::leaf
        B2R -. contains .-> C3R[Leaf 2A]:::leaf
        B3R -. contains .-> C4R[Leaf 3A]:::leaf
        B3R -. contains .-> C5R[Leaf 3B]:::leaf
    end
    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef child fill:#4a7ba6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#a8c8e1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Recursive Subdivision Flow (Treemap Engine Internals)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Canvas W x H and sorted weights] --> S1[Initialize empty layout and empty row]
    S1 --> S2{Take next weight w k plus 1}
    S2 --> S3{Adding wk plus 1 to current row improves aspect ratio?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Add wk plus 1 to current row]
    S4 --> S2
    S3 -->|No| S5[Finalize current row: lay out perpendicular strip]
    S5 --> S6[Reduce canvas: remove finalized strip area]
    S6 --> S7{Any weights remain?}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Rotate canvas 90 degrees and reset row]
    S8 --> S2
    S7 -->|No| S9[Output final layout with n rectangles]
```

## 4.4 Sunburst Layered Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Layer 0: Full 2pi circle = Root] --> L1[Layer 1: Three wedges A, B, C of angles thetaA, thetaB, thetaC]
    L1 --> L2A[Layer 2 inside A: sub wedges A1 and A2 of theta A1, theta A2]
    L1 --> L2B[Layer 2 inside B: sub wedge B1 of theta B1]
    L1 --> L2C[Layer 2 inside C: sub wedges C1, C2, C3 of theta C1, theta C2, theta C3]
    L2C --> L3C1[Layer 3 inside C1: leaves C1x, C1y]
    L2C --> L3C2[Layer 3 inside C2: leaf C2z]
```

    PR1 --> PR2[Sort Children by Weight Descending]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define containment as a data visualization technique. Mention any two containment-based visualization methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Containment is a visual encoding technique in which **hierarchical relationships between data elements are represented by spatial enclosure** — child elements are drawn physically *inside* the region of their parent. This exploits the Gestalt principle of **Common Region / Enclosure**, allowing users to interpret parent-child relationships pre-attentively without following connecting edges.

Two containment-based methods:
1. **Treemap** — Hierarchical data is encoded as nested rectangles; area of each rectangle is proportional to its weight.
2. **Sunburst Diagram** — Hierarchical data is encoded as nested angular wedges around a central root circle; arc length encodes the weight.

*(Examiner's split: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for the two methods = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between containment-based and connection-based visualization of hierarchical data. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Containment | Connection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Encoding Primitive | Spatial enclosure (nesting) | Edges / links between nodes |
| Strength | Excellent space utilization, scales to thousands of nodes | Clear parent-child paths, easy topology tracing |

- **Advantage of Containment:** Maximizes use of screen real estate; ideal for large trees (e.g., disk usage dashboards).
- **Advantage of Connection:** Easier to trace the exact path from root to any leaf, which is critical for tree traversal tasks.

*(Examiner's split: 2 marks comparison + 0.5 marks each for advantages = 3 marks.)*

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

> **KTU Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice below. Each question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a) Explain the squarified treemap algorithm in detail. Clearly state the objective function, the row-based decision rule, and the worst-case aspect ratio formula. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The squarified treemap algorithm, proposed by **Bruls, Huijsen, and van Wijk (2000)**, is the standard algorithm for laying out weighted hierarchical data as nested rectangles with **near-square aspect ratios**.

**Objective Function:** Given a sorted list of weights $w_1 \geq w_2 \geq \ldots \geq w_n > 0$ and a canvas of dimensions $W \times H$, the goal is to **minimize the maximum aspect ratio** across all $n$ rectangles:

$$\min_{L} \max_{i \in [1,n]} \rho_i \quad \text{where} \quad \rho_i = \frac{\max(w_i, h_i)}{\min(w_i, h_i)}$$

subject to:
- Area preservation: $w_i \cdot h_i = w_i \cdot s$ where $s = WH / \sum w_j$
- No overlap among siblings
- Complete coverage of canvas ($\eta = 1$)

**Row-Based Decision Rule:** The algorithm builds a *row* — a sequence of consecutive rectangles placed perpendicular to the shorter edge of the remaining canvas. For each candidate weight $w_{k+1}$, the algorithm computes the resulting worst-case aspect ratio if $w_{k+1}$ is added to the current row:

$$\mathcal{S}(\text{row} \cup \{w_{k+1}\}) = \max\left(\frac{w_{\text{strip}}^2 \cdot W_{\text{row,new}}}{s \cdot w_{\min,\text{row}}^2}, \frac{s \cdot w_{\max,\text{row}}^2}{w_{\text{strip}}^2 \cdot W_{\text{row,new}}}\right)$$

If $\mathcal{S}(\text{row} \cup \{w_{k+1}\}) \leq \mathcal{S}(\text{row})$, $w_{k+1}$ is appended; otherwise the current row is **finalized** (laid out), the canvas is reduced, and a new row begins. The algorithm terminates when all weights are placed.

**Worst-Case Aspect Ratio:** The squarified algorithm guarantees that no rectangle's aspect ratio exceeds the theoretical bound:

$$\beta = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 4\gamma^2}}{2\gamma}, \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma = \frac{w_{\max}}{w_{\text{avg}}}$$

For practical data, $\beta$ is typically bounded between 1 and 2, making the rectangles perceptually easy to compare.

**Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ for layout, plus $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ for pre-sorting.

**Practical Engineering Impact:** Squarified treemaps are used in **disk analyzers, financial dashboards, and large-scale hierarchical monitoring tools** because they scale to tens of thousands of leaves while preserving comparative accuracy.

*(Examiner's split: 2 marks objective, 3 marks decision rule, 1 mark complexity, 1 mark application = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) With a labelled diagram, explain the Sunburst visualization. How is the angular width of each wedge computed? How does it encode multi-level hierarchies? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Labelled Diagram (in text-form ASCII for clarity):**

```
                  Layer 1 (Root = 2π)
                 ╱─────────────────╲
               ╱         ╱│           ╲
             ╱         ╱   │            ╲
           ╱     A   ╱     │   B         ╲
          │        ╱       │              │
          │      ╱─────────┤───────────────│
          │    ╱  A1  │ A2  │   B1   │  B2  │
           ╲ ╱        │     │        │      ╱
            ╳─────────┴─────┴────────┴─────╳
                   Layer 2 (sub-wedges)
```

**Description:** A sunburst is a **radial containment visualization** where:
- The **innermost ring** is the root, occupying a full $2\pi$ radians.
- The **next ring outward** divides the root's arc among its children, each receiving a wedge whose **arc length (angular width) is proportional to its weight**.
- Each subsequent ring recursively subdivides the parent wedge.

**Angular Width Computation:** For a parent node with total weight $W_p$ and a child $c$ with weight $w_c$, the angular width of $c$'s wedge is:

$$\theta_c = 2\pi \cdot \frac{w_c}{W_p}$$

For a leaf at depth $d$ with accumulated weight factor $f$, the full angular width is:

$$\theta_{\text{leaf}} = 2\pi \cdot f = 2\pi \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{d} \frac{w_{\text{child at level }k}}{W_{\text{parent at level }k-1}}$$

The **radial position** (which ring) is determined by depth:

$$r_d = r_{\text{inner}} + d \cdot \Delta r, \quad \Delta r = \frac{r_{\text{outer}} - r_{\text{inner}}}{D_{\max}}$$

**Multi-Level Hierarchy Encoding:**
1. **Depth → Radial distance** (which ring the wedge lies in)
2. **Parent-child → Angular containment** (child wedge is a sub-arc of parent)
3. **Weight → Arc length** (quantitative channel)
4. **Category → Color hue** (secondary categorical channel)

**Advantages:** Whole-to-part intuition matches pie charts; multiple rings make deep hierarchies visible; radial symmetry is visually appealing for cyclic/whole data.

**Limitations:** Outer rings have more area than inner rings (slight perceptual bias); very thin wedges become unreadable beyond $\sim 5$ levels.

*(Examiner's split: 2 marks diagram, 2 marks angular formula, 2 marks hierarchy encoding, 1 mark advantages/limitations = 7 marks.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Compare and contrast Treemaps, Sunburst diagrams, and Icicle plots as containment-based visualizations. Use a comparison table covering at least five dimensions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | Treemap | Sunburst | Icicle Plot |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Geometry** | Rectangular (2D) | Polar / Wedge (2D) | Rectangular, 1-D bars (2D layout) |
| **Weight Encoding** | Rectangle area | Arc length (angular width) | Bar width (1D) |
| **Aspect Ratio Goal** | $\rho \to 1$ (squarified) | Implicitly rectangular; wedges naturally fit | All bars share same height; $\rho$ depends on bar widths |
| **Space Efficiency $\eta$** | $\eta = 1$ (fills canvas) | $\eta < 1$ (inner circle unused) | $\eta = 1$ (stacked bands) |
| **Path Tracing (root → leaf)** | Difficult (no explicit path) | Moderate (follow outward rings) | Easy (read top-to-bottom) |
| **Scalability (leaves)** | Excellent ($\sim$10,000+) | Moderate ($\sim$1,000) | Good ($\sim$5,000) |
| **Best Use Case** | Disk usage, financial maps | Cyclic / whole-to-part budgets | File system browsers, taxonomy viewers |
| **Reading Direction** | Variable (depends on layout) | Clockwise (innermost → outermost) | Top-to-bottom (root → leaves) |
| **Aesthetic Appeal** | High (mosaic look) | Very high (radial symmetry) | Moderate (blocky) |
| **Typical R Package** | `treemap`, `treemapify` | `sunburstR`, `plotly` | Custom ggplot2, `dendextend` |

**Key Insight:** Treemaps are the most **space-efficient** but the hardest for *path tracing*; Icicle plots are the easiest for *path tracing* but waste vertical space; Sunburst diagrams balance aesthetics with hierarchy clarity but suffer at high node counts.

*(Examiner's split: 5 marks table content, 2 marks key insight / conclusion = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Explain the role of the **Law of Common Region** (Gestalt) in justifying containment visualizations. Further, derive the area-preservation constraint mathematically for a treemap. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Law of Common Region (Gestalt Principle):**
The Gestalt psychologists established that human visual perception groups elements that share an *enclosed region* as belonging together. This is the **Law of Common Region** (also called the **Law of Enclosure**). It states:

> *Elements located within a closed boundary are perceptually grouped as a single unit, even in the absence of other grouping cues.*

In containment visualizations, this principle is exploited as follows:
- The visual boundary of a rectangle / wedge / circle *automatically* signals "these elements belong to the same group."
- Users do not need to follow edges or read legends; the **spatial position itself** carries the grouping semantic.
- This is the *primary perceptual justification* for why containment is so effective compared to node-link diagrams, which rely on the **Law of Proximity** and **Law of Continuity** (weaker grouping cues).

**Mathematical Derivation of Area-Preservation Constraint:**

Consider a tree with leaves having weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n > 0$ and a rectangular canvas of width $W$ and height $H$, total area $A = W \cdot H$.

**Goal:** Allocate area $A_i$ to each leaf $i$ such that the area is **directly proportional to the weight**.

$$A_i = k \cdot w_i \quad \text{for some constant } k > 0$$

**Constraint 1 — Total area conservation (perfect space efficiency $\eta = 1$):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i = A = W \cdot H$$

**Substituting:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} k \cdot w_i = W \cdot H \implies k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = W \cdot H$$

**Solving for $k$ (the scale factor $s$):**

$$k = s = \frac{W \cdot H}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

**Therefore the area of leaf $i$ is:**

$$A_i = w_i \cdot s = w_i \cdot \frac{W \cdot H}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$$

**Verification:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i = s \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = (W \cdot H / \sum w_j) \cdot \sum w_j = W \cdot H = A$ ✓

**Internal Nodes:** For an internal node $p$ with children $C(p) = \{c_1, \ldots, c_m\}$, its total area is the sum of its children's areas:

$$A_p = \sum_{c \in C(p)} A_c$$

This **recursive aggregation** is what makes containment preserve the tree structure naturally — the parent's region *automatically* contains all descendants.

**Aspect Ratio Side-Constraint:** While preserving area, the algorithm also tries to keep $\rho_i = \max(w_i, h_i) / \min(w_i, h_i) \leq \beta$ for visual readability. This is the squarified-treemap objective discussed earlier.

*(Examiner's split: 2 marks Gestalt principle, 3 marks derivation, 1 mark verification, 1 mark internal-node recursion = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing *containment* with *overlap*:** Containment is **strict nesting** (child $\subset$ parent). Venn overlap is *intersection* ($A \cap B$), not containment. Conflating these two costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the scale factor $s$:** Many students write $A_i = w_i$ directly. Always include $s = WH / \sum w_j$ for full credit.
> 3. **Missing Gestalt justification:** When asked "why does containment work?", students describe the algorithm but forget the **perceptual (Gestalt) foundation**. The examiner expects at least one sentence on **Common Region / Enclosure**.
> 4. **Mixing up Sunburst arc length vs. angle:** Arc length and angle are linearly related, but for a *ring* at radius $r$, arc length $= r \cdot \theta$. Always specify the encoding precisely.
> 5. **In treemap questions, drawing the tree as a node-link diagram:** This is a **containment** question — the answer must use **nested regions**, not a graph. Drawing nodes with edges = 0 marks for the visualization part.
> 6. **Squarified algorithm — wrong inequality direction:** The decision rule is $\mathcal{S}(\text{row} \cup \{w_{k+1}\}) \leq \mathcal{S}(\text{row})$ (improvement or equal). Reversing this is a logic error worth 2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Containment = spatial enclosure**; **Connection = edges/links** — the two main tree-visualization idioms.
- **Containment exploits Gestalt's Law of Common Region** for pre-attentive grouping.
- **Treemaps** are the workhorse: area $\propto$ weight, squarified algorithm minimizes aspect ratio.
- **Squarified Treemap** complexity: $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$; scale factor $s = WH / \sum w_i$.
- **Sunburst**: angular width $\theta_c = 2\pi \cdot w_c / W_p$; depth → ring index.
- **Icicle Plot**: width $\propto$ weight; root at top; bands stacked vertically.
- **Venn/Euler**: show set intersections; use inclusion–exclusion for area math.
- **Area preservation formula:** $A_i = w_i \cdot s$ where $s = A_{\text{canvas}} / \sum w_j$.
- **Aspect ratio formula:** $\rho_i = \max(w_i, h_i) / \min(w_i, h_i)$; ideal $\rho \to 1$.
- **Decision rule for squarify:** keep adding weight to row *iff* new worst ratio $\leq$ old worst ratio.
- **Trade-off table** to memorize: Treemap (best scaling, worst path), Sunburst (best aesthetics, moderate), Icicle (best path, moderate scaling).
- **R packages for KTU lab:** `treemap`, `treemapify`, `sunburstR`, `plotly`, `d3r`, `euler` (for Euler diagrams).
- **Engineering applications:** disk analyzers, stock heatmaps, budget dashboards, genomics, sports analytics.
- **Examiner's hot topic:** squarified algorithm derivation + Sunburst angular formula + Gestalt justification = guaranteed 14-mark question.

> **End of Module 2 — Containment Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PBCDT604)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Map Color: Color Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Map Color: Color Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Color Theory** in the context of data visualization is the systematic study and application of color models, color harmonies, and perceptual principles used to encode quantitative and categorical information in statistical graphics and thematic maps. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, color theory is treated as the perceptual and mathematical backbone of the **Aesthetic Layer** of the Grammar of Graphics, governing how the human visual system decodes mapped variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** Color Theory is the discipline that defines the mathematical representation of colors (color spaces), the perceptual relationships between them (color harmony), and the rules for assigning colors to data values (color scales) such that the visual encoding is accurate, accessible, and unbiased.

A **Map Color** in thematic cartography specifically refers to the systematic assignment of hue, saturation, and luminance to spatial polygons or points to represent an underlying statistical variable (e.g., population density, rainfall, COVID-19 cases).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef creating a **flavor map** of a menu. You do not randomly sprinkle salt; you carefully decide which dishes are **spicy (red)**, **sour (yellow-green)**, or **sweet (pink)** so that the diner can immediately read the menu. Color theory in data visualization is the chef's "flavor rulebook."

- **Hue** is the *name* of the color (red, green, blue) — like the *type* of spice.
- **Saturation** is the *intensity* of the color — like *how strong* the spice is.
- **Lightness (Luminance)** is *how much light* the color carries — like whether the dish is mild or bold.
- **Hue → Categorical data** (different species, different countries).
- **Saturation/Lightness → Ordinal/Quantitative data** (low to high values).

> [!NOTE]
> **Perceptual Rule (Cleveland & McGill, 1984):** The human eye is most accurate at decoding **position** along a common scale, then **length**, then **angle**, then **area/saturation**, and finally **color hue**. This is why quantitative maps should *not* use rainbow hue scales.

## 1.3 Core Color Metrics (Standard Units)

| Metric | Standard Unit | Range | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Red, Green, Blue | **8-bit channel intensity** | $[0, 255]$ | $R, G, B$ |
| Hue | **Degrees** | $[0^\circ, 360^\circ]$ | $H$ |
| Saturation | **Percentage** | $[0\%, 100\%]$ | $S$ |
| Lightness | **Percentage** | $[0\%, 100\%]$ | $L$ |
| Hex Code | **Hexadecimal string** | `00`–`FF` | `#RRGGBB` |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Color Theory

### Pillar 1: Color Models (Color Spaces)
A **color model** is a mathematical coordinate system for representing colors. The most important ones for KTU 2024 are:

**(a) RGB Model (Additive — for screens)**
Combines Red, Green, and Blue light. Used in monitors, projectors, and R's default graphics device.
- White $= R + G + B$ at maximum.
- Black $= R = G = B = 0$.

**(b) HSL / HSV Model (Cylindrical — for human perception)**
- **Hue** $(H)$: Angle on the color wheel.
- **Saturation** $(S)$: Purity / vividness of the color.
- **Lightness/Value** $(L/V)$: Brightness.

**(c) CMYK Model (Subtractive — for print)**
Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key (black). Used for printed thematic atlases.

**(d) CIE Lab / LCh (Perceptually uniform)**
Designed so that the Euclidean distance between two colors corresponds to perceived difference. Used in **ColorBrewer** and `viridis` palettes.

### Pillar 2: Color Harmony
Harmony refers to aesthetic combinations:
- **Complementary:** Opposite hues ($180^\circ$ apart).
- **Analogous:** Adjacent hues ($30^\circ$ apart).
- **Triadic:** Three hues equally spaced ($120^\circ$ apart).
- **Monochromatic:** Single hue, varying $L$.

### Pillar 3: Color Scales (Palettes)
This is the most exam-relevant pillar. There are **three canonical types**:

| Scale Type | Use Case | Example Palette |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative (Categorical)** | Nominal data — no order | `Set1`, `Dark2`, `Accent` |
| **Sequential** | Ordered/quantitative low → high | `Blues`, `Viridis`, `Greens` |
| **Diverging** | Ordered data with meaningful midpoint | `RdBu`, `BrBG`, `PiYG` |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Equations** the KTU Board Examiner expects you to write for full marks in numerical/conversion problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RGB to Hex | $\#RRGGBB$ where each channel is base-16 of $R,G,B \in [0,255]$ | Two hex digits per channel |
| 2 | Hex to RGB | $R = 16 \cdot H_1 + H_2$ | Reverse of above |
| 3 | RGB (0–1) to HSL | See Section 3 for derivation | $\max = \max(R,G,B), \min = \min(R,G,B)$ |
| 4 | Lightness (HSL) | $L = \frac{\max + \min}{2}$ | Range $[0,1]$ |
| 5 | Saturation (HSL) | $S = \frac{\max - \min}{1 - \vert 2L - 1 \vert}$ | Zero when $\max = \min$ |
| 6 | Hue (HSL) | $H = 60^\circ \cdot \begin{cases} \frac{G-B}{\Delta} & \text{if } R = \max \\ \frac{B-R}{\Delta}+2 & \text{if } G = \max \\ \frac{R-G}{\Delta}+4 & \text{if } B = \max \end{cases}$ | $\Delta = \max - \min$ |
| 7 | Luminance (perceived) | $Y = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$ | Rec. 709 (HDTV) |
| 8 | Color Distance (CIE Lab) | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_1-L_2)^2 + (a_1-a_2)^2 + (b_1-b_2)^2}$ | $\Delta E < 1$: not perceptible |
| 9 | Number of Distinct Hues | $\approx 12$ for qualitative, $\leq 7$ recommended | Tetrachromatic limit |
| 10 | Brewer's Rule | Sequential = single hue, Diverging = two hues meeting at neutral | ColorBrewer principle |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GIS / Smart City Dashboards:** Sequential green ramp encodes population density on Kerala district maps.
- **Medical Imaging:** Diverging `RdBu` highlights hot/cold regions in MRI/CT scans.
- **Election Cartography:** Diverging blue-red maps of US swing states.
- **Accessibility:** ColorBrewer palettes are colorblind-safe — critical for public-facing KTU student projects.
- **Print Media:** CMYK conversion ensures atlas colors match RGB screen output.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** Whenever a question asks "which palette is appropriate?", answer using the **data type** (nominal → qualitative, ordinal/quantitative → sequential, diverging quantitative → diverging) — never by aesthetic preference.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: RGB $\rightarrow$ HSL Conversion

This derivation is a **frequently asked 14-mark problem** in KTU ESE. The standard algorithm (CSS Color Module Level 3) is shown in full.

### Given
Input: $R, G, B \in [0, 1]$ (normalized RGB triplet).

### Step 1 — Compute max and min channel values
$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \max(R, G, B) \\
m &= \min(R, G, B) \\
\Delta &= M - m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $M$ is the brightest channel, $m$ is the darkest, and $\Delta$ is the *chroma* (color span).

### Step 2 — Compute Lightness $L$
$$
L = \frac{M + m}{2}
$$

**Logic:** $L$ is the arithmetic midpoint of the brightest and darkest channel.

### Step 3 — Compute Saturation $S$
$$
S = \begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } \Delta = 0 \\
\dfrac{\Delta}{1 - \vert 2L - 1 \vert} & \text{if } \Delta > 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Logic:** When $L = 0.5$ (mid-gray), denominator $= 1$, so $S = \Delta$. When $L \to 0$ or $L \to 1$ (very dark/bright), the denominator shrinks, allowing pure colors to keep full saturation.

### Step 4 — Compute Hue $H$
$$
H = 60^\circ \times
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } \Delta = 0 \\
\left( \dfrac{G - B}{\Delta} \mod 6 \right) & \text{if } R = M \\
\left( \dfrac{B - R}{\Delta} + 2 \right) & \text{if } G = M \\
\left( \dfrac{R - G}{\Delta} + 4 \right) & \text{if } B = M
\end{cases}
$$

**Logic:** Hue is the angular position on the color wheel; we pick the sector by the dominant channel and then position within that sector via the ratio of the other two channels.

### Step 5 — Final Adjustment
If $H < 0^\circ$, add $360^\circ$:
$$
H_{\text{final}} = (H + 360^\circ) \mod 360^\circ
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Convert $RGB = (255, 102, 0)$ (a vivid orange) to HSL.

**Step A — Normalize**
$$
R = 1.000, \quad G = 0.400, \quad B = 0.000
$$

**Step B — Compute $M$, $m$, $\Delta$**
$$
M = 1.000, \quad m = 0.000, \quad \Delta = 1.000
$$

**Step C — Compute $L$**
$$
L = \frac{1.000 + 0.000}{2} = 0.500
$$

**Step D — Compute $S$**
$$
S = \frac{1.000}{1 - \vert 2(0.500) - 1 \vert} = \frac{1.000}{1 - 0} = 1.000
$$

**Step E — Compute $H$**
Since $R = M$:
$$
H = 60^\circ \times \left( \frac{G - B}{\Delta} \right) = 60^\circ \times \left( \frac{0.400 - 0.000}{1.000} \right) = 24^\circ
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{H = 24^\circ, \quad S = 100\%, \quad L = 50\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The hex code for $(255, 102, 0)$ is `#FF6600`. KTU Board answers often expect you to show this connection.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Palette

| Data Type | Variable Example | Palette Type | R Function | Sample Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal (no order)** | District names | Qualitative | `brewer.pal(8, "Set2")` | 8 distinct hues |
| **Ordinal / low→high** | Rainfall (mm) | Sequential | `brewer.pal(9, "Blues")` | Light → Dark Blue |
| **Diverging quantitative** | Temperature anomaly | Diverging | `brewer.pal(11, "RdBu")` | Red ← White → Blue |
| **Cyclical** | Wind direction | Custom cyclic | `rainbow_hcl(12)` | Wraps around 360° |
| **Bivariate** | Pop × Income | Bivariate | `bivariatemap::bivariate_color()` | 2D matrix of hues |

## 3.4 Perceptual Uniformity — The $\Delta E$ Derivation

For CIE Lab, the color difference (used by `viridis` and `cmocean`):
$$
\Delta E_{ab}^* = \sqrt{(L_2^* - L_1^*)^2 + (a_2^* - a_1^*)^2 + (b_2^* - b_1^*)^2}
$$

**Interpretation (KTU Board expected):**
- $\Delta E < 1$ → difference not perceptible to the human eye.
- $1 \le \Delta E \le 2$ → perceptible through close observation.
- $\Delta E > 5$ → clearly different colors.

This is why **perceptually uniform palettes** like `viridis` are preferred over RGB-rainbow palettes in modern data visualization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Color Theory Taxonomy (Mermaid Mind Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Color Theory))
    ColorModels
      RGB
      HSL
      HSV
      CMYK
      CIELab
    ColorHarmony
      Complementary
      Analogous
      Triadic
      Monochromatic
    ColorScales
      Qualitative
      Sequential
      Diverging
      Cyclical
    ColorPerception
      Trichromacy
      OpponentProcess
      Colorblindness
    Applications
      ThematicMaps
      Heatmaps
      PrintMedia
      WebDashboards
```

## 4.2 RGB-to-HSL Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input RGB triplet R, G, B in 0 to 255"] --> B["Normalize to 0 to 1"]
    B --> C["Compute M, m, Delta"]
    C --> D{"Delta equals 0?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["H is undefined, S equals 0, L equals M"]
    D -- No --> F["Compute L equals M plus m over 2"]
    F --> G["Compute S equals Delta over 1 minus 2L minus 1"]
    G --> H{"Find dominant channel"}
    H -- R max --> I["H equals 60 times G minus B over Delta"]
    H -- G max --> J["H equals 60 times B minus R over Delta plus 2"]
    H -- B max --> K["H equals 60 times R minus G over Delta plus 4"]
    I --> L["Adjust H to range 0 to 360 degrees"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    E --> M["Output H, S, L values"]
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thematic Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DATA["Data Layer"]
        A1["Shapefile GeoJSON"]
        A2["Attribute Table CSV"]
    end
    subgraph AESTHETIC["Aesthetic Layer"]
        B1["Variable Mapping aes fill equals variable"]
        B2["Color Scale Function scale fill brewer"]
        B3["Palette Selection brewer.pal n equals 7 type equals Seq"]
    end
    subgraph GEOM["Geometry Layer"]
        C1["Polygon Layer geom sf"]
        C2["Theme Layer theme minimal"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        D1["Static Map PNG"]
        D2["Interactive Map Leaflet"]
    end
    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Choosing a Color Palette

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Identify Data Type"] --> B{"Is the variable nominal or categorical?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Use Qualitative Palette: Set1, Set2, Dark2, Accent"]
    B -- No --> D{"Is there a meaningful midpoint zero or threshold?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Use Diverging Palette: RdBu, BrBG, PiYG"]
    D -- No --> F{"Is the variable ordered low to high?"}
    F -- Yes --> G{"Is it single hue or multi hue?"}
    G -- Single --> H["Use Sequential Single Hue: Blues, Greens, Greys"]
    G -- Multi --> I["Use Sequential Multi Hue: Viridis, Plasma, Inferno"]
    F -- No --> J["Re-examine variable type"]
    C --> K["Apply scale fill brewer palette equals Set1"]
    E --> L["Apply scale fill gradient2 midpoint equals 0"]
    H --> M["Apply scale fill distiller palette equals Blues"]
    I --> N["Apply scale fill viridis option equals plasma"]
```

## 4.5 The Color Wheel (Hue Circle Schematic)

```mermaid
pie title Hue Distribution at Full Saturation and 50% Lightness
    "Red 0 degrees" : 30
    "Yellow 60 degrees" : 30
    "Green 120 degrees" : 30
    "Cyan 180 degrees" : 30
    "Blue 240 degrees" : 30
    "Magenta 300 degrees" : 30
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Color Theory. Differentiate between qualitative, sequential, and diverging color palettes with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Color Theory** is the systematic study of color models, color harmony, and color scales used to encode data in visual form. It ensures that visual encoding is accurate, accessible, and perceptually uniform.

| Type | Use | Example | R Code |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative** | Nominal / Categorical data | `Set1` (8 distinct hues) | `brewer.pal(8, "Set1")` |
| **Sequential** | Ordered / Quantitative (low→high) | `Blues` (light→dark) | `brewer.pal(9, "Blues")` |
| **Diverging** | Quantitative with meaningful midpoint | `RdBu` (red↔white↔blue) | `brewer.pal(11, "RdBu")` |

*(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for the table + 0.5 for example)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the RGB color model. State two of its limitations in data visualization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **RGB (Red, Green, Blue)** model is an **additive** color model in which colors are produced by combining varying intensities of red, green, and blue light. Each channel is represented by an integer in $[0, 255]$ (8-bit) or a real number in $[0, 1]$.

**Limitations:**
1. **Not perceptually uniform** — The Euclidean distance between two RGB triplets does *not* correspond to perceived color difference. A small RGB change may appear large visually.
2. **Not colorblind-safe** — RGB combinations involving pure red/green confuse ~8% of male viewers (deuteranopia).
3. **Not intuitive for humans** — Designers find it hard to "eyeball" a target RGB triplet, unlike HSL where you adjust hue, saturation, and lightness independently.

*(1 mark model + 1 mark each for two limitations)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Derive the formulas to convert an RGB color triplet $(R, G, B)$ with values in $[0, 1]$ to the HSL color space. Show all intermediate steps.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define extrema and chroma.**
$$
M = \max(R, G, B), \quad m = \min(R, G, B), \quad \Delta = M - m
$$

**Step 2 — Lightness formula derivation.**
The lightness is the midpoint of the lightest and darkest channels:
$$
L = \frac{M + m}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Saturation formula derivation.**
Saturation measures the *relative* chroma, normalized by the maximum possible chroma at that lightness. The maximum chroma at lightness $L$ is $1 - \vert 2L - 1 \vert$ (a linear interpolation between 0 at $L=0.5$ reaching 1 at the extremes). Therefore:
$$
S = \begin{cases} 0, & \Delta = 0 \\ \dfrac{\Delta}{1 - \vert 2L - 1 \vert}, & \Delta > 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Hue formula derivation.**
Hue is the angular position on the color wheel. We divide the wheel into six $60^\circ$ sectors keyed on the dominant channel:

$$
H = 60^\circ \cdot
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } \Delta = 0 \\
\dfrac{G - B}{\Delta} \pmod 6 & \text{if } R = M \\
\dfrac{B - R}{\Delta} + 2 & \text{if } G = M \\
\dfrac{R - G}{\Delta} + 4 & \text{if } B = M
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalization to $[0, 360^\circ]$.**
$$
H_{\text{final}} = (H + 360^\circ) \bmod 360^\circ
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Award 1.5 marks for the chroma definition, 2 marks for $L$ and $S$, and 3.5 marks for the $H$ piecewise derivation.

---

#### **(b) [7 marks] Convert the RGB color $(R, G, B) = (51, 153, 255)$ to HSL. Identify the hex code and the perceptual luminance using the Rec. 709 formula.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Normalize.**
$$
R = \tfrac{51}{255} = 0.200, \quad G = \tfrac{153}{255} = 0.600, \quad B = \tfrac{255}{255} = 1.000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $M$, $m$, $\Delta$.**
$$
M = 1.000 \;(B), \quad m = 0.200 \;(R), \quad \Delta = 0.800
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $L$.**
$$
L = \frac{1.000 + 0.200}{2} = 0.600
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S$.**
$$
S = \frac{0.800}{1 - \vert 2(0.600) - 1 \vert} = \frac{0.800}{1 - 0.200} = \frac{0.800}{0.800} = 1.000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $H$.** Since $B = M$:
$$
H = 60^\circ \cdot \left( \frac{R - G}{\Delta} + 4 \right) = 60^\circ \cdot \left( \frac{0.200 - 0.600}{0.800} + 4 \right) = 60^\circ \cdot (-0.5 + 4) = 60^\circ \cdot 3.5 = 210^\circ
$$

**Step 6 — Hex code.**
$$
51 = 0x33, \quad 153 = 0x99, \quad 255 = 0xFF
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{Hex Code} = \#3399FF}
$$

**Step 7 — Rec. 709 Luminance.**
$$
Y = 0.2126(0.200) + 0.7152(0.600) + 0.0722(1.000) = 0.0425 + 0.4291 + 0.0722 = 0.5438
$$
$$
\boxed{Y \approx 54.4\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** $H = 210^\circ$, $S = 100\%$, $L = 60\%$, Hex = `#3399FF`, Luminance = **0.544**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### **(a) [7 marks] Explain the CIE Lab color space and the $\Delta E$ color difference formula. Why is it preferred over RGB for designing colorblind-safe palettes?**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of CIE Lab.**
CIE Lab (or CIELAB) is a perceptually uniform color space defined by the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) in 1976. It has three axes:
- $L^*$: Lightness from 0 (black) to 100 (white).
- $a^*$: Red-green axis (negative = green, positive = red).
- $b^*$: Yellow-blue axis (negative = blue, positive = yellow).

**Step 2 — Derivation of $\Delta E$.**
The color difference between two colors $(L_1^*, a_1^*, b_1^*)$ and $(L_2^*, a_2^*, b_2^*)$ is the Euclidean distance:
$$
\Delta E_{ab}^* = \sqrt{(L_2^* - L_1^*)^2 + (a_2^* - a_1^*)^2 + (b_2^* - b_1^*)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Perceptual interpretation thresholds.**
- $\Delta E < 1$: Imperceptible.
- $1 \le \Delta E \le 2$: Perceptible only on close inspection.
- $2 < \Delta E \le 10$: Perceptible at a glance.
- $\Delta E > 10$: Colors appear more similar than opposite.

**Step 4 — Why Lab is preferred over RGB.**
1. **Perceptual uniformity** — Equal numerical changes correspond to equal perceived changes.
2. **Device independence** — Lab is based on human vision, not on a specific display.
3. **Colorblind simulation fidelity** — Lab-based simulations of protanopia/deuteranopia/tritanopia are more accurate.
4. **Palette design** — Tools like `viridis` and ColorBrewer are designed in Lab space, ensuring equal perceptual steps.

---

#### **(b) [7 marks] Compare and contrast sequential, diverging, and qualitative color palettes. For each, provide a real-world thematic-map example and write the corresponding R code to generate a 7-color palette using the `RColorBrewer` package.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Qualitative Palettes.**
- **Used for:** Nominal / Categorical data (no inherent order).
- **Example:** District-wise land-use type map of Kerala (Forest, Agriculture, Urban, Water, Wetland, Barren, Others).
- **R Code:**
```r
library(RColorBrewer)
display.brewer.pal(7, "Set2")
pal <- brewer.pal(7, "Set2")
```

**Step 2 — Sequential Palettes.**
- **Used for:** Ordered quantitative data (low → high).
- **Example:** Choropleth map of literacy rate across Kerala districts.
- **R Code:**
```r
display.brewer.pal(7, "Blues")
pal_seq <- brewer.pal(7, "Blues")
```

**Step 3 — Diverging Palettes.**
- **Used for:** Quantitative data with a meaningful midpoint (often 0).
- **Example:** Choropleth map of rainfall deviation from the long-period average across Kerala.
- **R Code:**
```r
display.brewer.pal(7, "RdBu")
pal_div <- brewer.pal(7, "RdBu")
```

**Comparison Table (final 2 marks):**

| Feature | Qualitative | Sequential | Diverging |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data type | Nominal | Ordinal / Quantitative | Quantitative w/ midpoint |
| Color count | Up to 12 | 3–9 | 3–11 |
| Lightness | Constant | Monotonic ramp | Symmetric around center |
| Example | `Set1`, `Paired` | `Blues`, `Greens` | `RdBu`, `BrBG` |
| Midpoint emphasis | No | No | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT use a rainbow palette (`rainbow()`) for ordered data** — it is *not* perceptually uniform and introduces false boundaries.
> 2. **Do NOT use a diverging palette when there is no meaningful midpoint** — it forces the reader to perceive a break that does not exist.
> 3. **Always show `display.brewer.pal()` output in your exam notebook** if the question asks for visual comparison.
> 4. Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by confusing **HSL hue** (a degree) with **RGB intensity** (a unitless 0–255 number). State the units explicitly.
> 5. Do not write the RGB-to-HSL derivation without first normalizing the inputs to $[0, 1]$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid-Fire):**

- **Definition:** Color Theory = Color Models + Color Harmony + Color Scales.
- **Three canonical scales:** Qualitative (nominal), Sequential (ordered), Diverging (midpoint).
- **Color models to memorize:** RGB (additive, screens), CMYK (subtractive, print), HSL/HSV (perceptual), CIE Lab (perceptually uniform).
- **Hex code format:** `#RRGGBB`, each pair in `00`–`FF`.
- **RGB-to-HSL intermediate steps:** Compute $M, m, \Delta$ → $L = (M+m)/2$ → $S$ depends on $\Delta$ and $L$ → $H$ uses the dominant channel.
- **Lightness range:** $L \in [0, 1]$ (or $0\%–100\%$).
- **Hue range:** $H \in [0^\circ, 360^\circ]$.
- **Rec. 709 luminance:** $Y = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$.
- **Perceptual-uniformity test:** $\Delta E$ in CIE Lab space.
- **Qualitative palette hue limit:** $\le 7$–$8$ for safe categorical reading.
- **ColorBrewer author:** Cynthia Brewer (Penn State) — palette tool for cartography.
- **`viridis` palettes:** `viridis`, `magma`, `plasma`, `inferno`, `cividis` — all perceptually uniform, colorblind-safe.
- **KTU-trap palettes to AVOID:** `rainbow()`, `heat.colors()` for ordinal data.
- **R functions to memorize:** `brewer.pal(n, name)`, `display.brewer.pal(n, name)`, `scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Blues")`, `scale_fill_viridis_c()`, `scale_fill_gradient2(midpoint = 0)`.
- **For maps specifically:** `tmap`, `ggplot2 + geom_sf()`, `leaflet` — all accept ColorBrewer palettes.
- **Colorblind types:** Protanopia (no red), Deuteranopia (no green), Tritanopia (no blue) — affects ~8% of males.
- **Cleveland-McGill hierarchy:** Position > Length > Angle > Area > Saturation > Hue. Choose scales accordingly.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers: hue in degrees, $L$/$S$ in %.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Map Color: Color Theory**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Color Maps and Other Channels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Color Maps and Other Channels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Visual Channels — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Channels** (also called *visual variables* or *encoding channels*) are the perceptual dimensions along which a viewer decodes information from a graphical mark. In the grammar of graphics, every datum is mapped to a position on the canvas plus one or more *retinal* or *spatial* channels (hue, size, shape, length, area, orientation, texture, motion, etc.).

In the formal taxonomy proposed by **Jacques Bertin** (1967) and later extended by **Cleveland & McGill (1984)** and **Mackinlay (1986)**, channels are ranked by their *effectiveness* — i.e., the accuracy and speed with which a human can perceive quantitative differences.

### 1.2 Color Maps — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Color Map** (or *color palette* / *colormap*) is a continuous or discrete function that maps a scalar (or categorical) data value to a specific color in a perceptually-defined color space such as **sRGB**, **CIELAB**, or **CIELUV**. Formally:

$$
C: \mathbb{R} \to \mathcal{C} \quad \text{where} \quad \mathcal{C} \subseteq [0,1]^3
$$

where the output is a triplet of normalized color coordinates (R, G, B) or (L\*, a\*, b\*).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thermometer Translator"

> [!NOTE]
