```r
#' Convert Celsius to Fahrenheit
#' @param c Numeric temperature in Celsius
#' @return Numeric temperature in Fahrenheit
#' @export
#' @examples
#' celsius_to_fahrenheit(0)
celsius_to_fahrenheit <- function(c) {
  f <- (c * 9 / 5) + 32
  return(f)
}
```

**Step 4: Generate documentation using roxygen2**

```r
document()
```

This creates the `.Rd` help file in `man/` and updates `NAMESPACE`.

**Step 5: Install the package locally**

```r
install("tempconvert")
```

**Step 6: Test the package**

```r
library(tempconvert)
celsius_to_fahrenheit(100)
#> [1] 212
```

**Step 7: Build and check**

```r
build("tempconvert")  # produces .tar.gz
check("tempconvert")  # CRAN-style validation
```

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

The six essential package management functions:

| # | Function | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `install.packages("pkg")` | Install from CRAN | `install.packages("dplyr")` |
| 2 | `library(pkg)` | Load and attach | `library(dplyr)` |
| 3 | `require(pkg)` | Conditional load | `if (require(ggplot2)) { plot(...) }` |
| 4 | `update.packages("pkg")` | Update existing | `update.packages("ggplot2")` |
| 5 | `remove.packages("pkg")` | Uninstall | `remove.packages("ggplot2")` |
| 6 | `installed.packages()` | List installed | `installed.packages()[, "Package"]` |
| 7 | `packageVersion("pkg")` | Version check | `packageVersion("dplyr")` |
| 8 | `help(package = "pkg")` | Browse help | `help(package = "MASS")` |
| 9 | `vignette("pkg")` | Read tutorials | `vignette("dplyr")` |
| 10 | `detach("package:pkg")` | Unload | `detach("package:dplyr")` |

> **Valuation 14-mark split:** (a) Seven procedural steps × 1 mark each; (b) Six functions × ~1.2 marks each with example.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `library()` with `require()`** — both load packages, but only `require()` returns a logical. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you swap them.
> 2. **Mixing `Depends` and `Imports` in DESCRIPTION** — `Depends` *attaches* the package (puts on search path); `Imports` only *loads* it. Use `Imports` in modern packages.
> 3. **Forgetting `document()` after writing roxygen comments** — without `document()`, the `.Rd` files and `NAMESPACE` won't be regenerated, leading to "object not found" errors.
> 4. **Missing `roxygen2` tags like `@export`** — functions without `@export` are treated as *internal* and become inaccessible to users.
> 5. **Confusing `install.packages` (downloads) with `library` (loads)** — the package must be installed *once*, but loaded in *every* new R session.
> 6. **Forgetting that `sessionInfo()` is required for reproducibility** in any submitted report.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — R Packages:**
> - ⭐ **Definition**: An R package is a reusable bundle of R code, data, docs, and tests.
> - ⭐ **Base packages** (auto-included): `base`, `utils`, `stats`, `graphics`, `datasets`, `methods`.
> - ⭐ **Three core operations**: `install.packages` (disk) → `library` (memory) → use.
> - ⭐ **Install vs Load**: Install once, load every session.
> - ⭐ **`library()` halts on error; `require()` returns `FALSE`**.
> - ⭐ **Repositories**: CRAN (general), Bioconductor (bioinformatics), GitHub (development).
> - ⭐ **GitHub install syntax**: `devtools::install_github("user/repo")`.
> - ⭐ **Mandatory files**: `DESCRIPTION`, `NAMESPACE`, `R/`, `man/`.
> - ⭐ **`DESCRIPTION` fields**: `Package`, `Version`, `Authors@R`, `License`, `Depends`, `Imports`, `Suggests`.
> - ⭐ **`Depends` vs `Imports`**: `Depends` attaches; `Imports` only loads.
> - ⭐ **`NAMESPACE`**: Controls exports and imports, prevents name clashes.
> - ⭐ **roxygen2 tags**: `#' @param`, `#' @return`, `#' @export`, `#' @examples`.
> - ⭐ **`document()` regenerates** `.Rd` and `NAMESPACE` from roxygen comments.
> - ⭐ **`sessionInfo()`** lists R version + loaded packages — required for reproducibility.
> - ⭐ **`find("fn")` and `search()`** help debug namespace conflicts.
> - ⭐ **Tidyverse** = `ggplot2` + `dplyr` + `tidyr` + `readr` + `purrr` + `tibble` + …
> - ⭐ **`create_package()`** initializes a package; **`build()`** creates `.tar.gz`; **`check()`** runs CRAN-style tests.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the R programming language, a **variable** is a symbolic name (an identifier) bound to a value stored in the computer's Random Access Memory (RAM). The value may be a primitive data type (numeric, integer, character, logical, complex, or raw), a data structure (vector, matrix, list, data frame, factor), a function, or even an expression. R is a *dynamically typed* language, meaning a single variable can be reassigned to values of different types during program execution without explicit type declaration.

The general syntax for variable assignment in R is:

$$ \text{variable\_name} \;\texttt{<-}\; \text{value} $$

or, equivalently:

$$ \text{variable\_name} \;\texttt{=}\; \text{value} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, Module 3 of PBCDT604 expects students to master **variable creation, naming rules, assignment operators, data types, type-checking functions, and scoping**. These directly map to **Course Outcome CO3**: *“Apply R programming constructs for data manipulation and analysis.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Labeled Storage Box

Imagine a row of empty cardboard boxes in a warehouse (your computer's RAM). Each box has a **sticker label** (the variable name) and can hold **one item at a time** (the value).

- When you write `x <- 25`, you are pasting the sticker **"x"** on a box and placing the number **25** inside it.
- When you later write `x <- "Hello"`, you remove the old item and put a piece of paper saying **"Hello"** in the *same* box. R does not complain because R is dynamically typed.
- If you write `x <- y`, you are not copying the contents of box *y* into box *x* permanently; you are simply making box *x* point to whatever box *y* is currently pointing to (this is *reference semantics* for environments).

## 1.3 Why Variables Matter in Data Visualization

In data analytics with R, every column of a data frame, every computed mean, and every plot label is stored in a variable. Mastering variable handling is the **foundation** for cleaning, transforming, and visualizing data using packages such as `ggplot2` and `dplyr`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reserved Words Cannot Be Variable Names:** `if`, `else`, `repeat`, `while`, `function`, `for`, `TRUE`, `FALSE`, `NULL`, `Inf`, `NaN`, `NA`, `NA_integer_`, `NA_real_`, `NA_complex_`, `NA_character_`, `...`, and `break` are reserved and must never be used as identifiers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Rules for Variables in R

1. **Creation is Assignment:** R has no separate "declaration" step. A variable is created the moment a value is bound to a name.
2. **Case Sensitivity:** `Score`, `score`, and `SCORE` are three different variables.
3. **Naming Composition:** Names may contain letters, digits, dot (`.`), and underscore (`_`), but must **start with a letter or a dot (not followed by a digit)**.
4. **Left-Hand Side Evaluation:** The name on the left of `<-` must be a syntactically valid symbol.
5. **Automatic Type Inference:** The R interpreter inspects the right-hand side and assigns the appropriate type internally (`mode()` and `typeof()` reveal it).
6. **Dynamic Re-typing:** A variable can hold a numeric value in one statement and a character string in the next.
7. **Environment Binding:** Each binding lives inside an *environment* (global, local, function, package namespace), which determines *scope*.

## 2.2 Assignment Operators

| Operator | Direction | Typical Usage | KTU Exam Tip |
|----------|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| `<-` | Right to Left | Standard R idiom | Preferred in published code; **always use this in exams** |
| `<<-` | Right to Left (to parent) | Assigns in enclosing scope | Used for global assignment from inside functions |
| `=` | Right to Left | Argument passing in function calls | Avoid for ordinary assignment; KTU examiners often penalise |
| `->` | Left to Right | Right-to-left style | Rare, less readable |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Variable Inspection Functions

| Function | Purpose | Example | Returns |
|----------|---------|---------|---------|
| `ls()` | Lists all variables in current environment | `ls()` | Character vector of names |
| `ls(pattern="x")` | Lists variables matching a regex | `ls(pattern="^m")` | Subset of names |
| `rm(x)` | Removes variable `x` | `rm(x)` | `NULL` (side-effect) |
| `rm(list=ls())` | Removes **all** variables | `rm(list=ls())` | Clears workspace |
| `exists("x")` | Checks if variable exists | `exists("y")` | `TRUE` / `FALSE` |
| `typeof(x)` | Internal storage type | `typeof(5L)` | `"integer"` |
| `class(x)` | Object class (OOP) | `class(5L)` | `"integer"` |
| `mode(x)` | Atomic mode | `mode(5L)` | `"numeric"` |
| `is.numeric(x)` | Type predicate | `is.numeric(5)` | `TRUE` / `FALSE` |
| `as.integer(x)` | Coercion to integer | `as.integer(5.7)` | `5` |
| `assign("x", 10)` | Programmatic assignment | `assign("x", 10)` | Side-effect |
| `get("x")` | Programmatic retrieval | `get("x")` | Value bound to `x` |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IoT & Sensor Analytics:** Each sensor reading is bound to a variable before statistical aggregation.
- **Machine Learning Pipelines:** Model coefficients, accuracy metrics, and hyper-parameters are stored as variables before visualization.
- **Civil/Structural Data Logging:** Stress, strain, and load readings are assigned to named variables for time-series plots.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression values are held in variables before generating heat-maps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Variable Lifecycle in R

## 3.1 Creating and Inspecting Variables

**Step 1 — Numeric Assignment**

$$ \texttt{marks} \;\texttt{<-}\; 85 $$

Explanation: The identifier `marks` is bound to the numeric literal `85`. Internally, R allocates a 64-bit double-precision floating-point storage cell.

**Step 2 — Verify the Value**

$$ \texttt{marks} $$
Returns `85`.

**Step 3 — Inspect the Type**

$$ \texttt{typeof(marks)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{"double"} $$

$$ \texttt{is.numeric(marks)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{TRUE} $$

**Step 4 — Integer Coercion**

$$ \texttt{marks\_int} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{as.integer(marks)} $$

$$ \texttt{typeof(marks\_int)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{"integer"} $$

Explanation: The suffix `L` (e.g., `85L`) or the function `as.integer()` forces integer storage (32-bit).

**Step 5 — Character Variable**

$$ \texttt{student\_name} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{"Ananya"} $$

$$ \texttt{nchar(student\_name)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 6 $$

Explanation: `nchar()` returns the number of characters, confirming that the variable stores a string of length 6.

**Step 6 — Logical Variable**

$$ \texttt{passed} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{marks} \;\texttt{>=}\;\texttt{40} $$

$$ \texttt{passed} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{TRUE} $$

Explanation: R performs the comparison `marks >= 40` (i.e., `85 >= 40`), which evaluates to logical `TRUE`, and binds this logical constant to `passed`.

**Step 7 — Complex Variable**

$$ \texttt{z} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{3} \;\texttt{+}\;\texttt{4i} $$

$$ \texttt{typeof(z)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{"complex"} $$

Explanation: The presence of the imaginary literal `4i` forces R to allocate complex storage (two doubles: real and imaginary parts).

## 3.2 Dynamic Re-typing Demonstration

**Step 1 — Initial binding:**

$$ \texttt{x} \;\texttt{<-}\; 10 $$

$$ \texttt{typeof(x)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{"double"} $$

**Step 2 — Re-bind to a character string:**

$$ \texttt{x} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{"Data Science"} $$

$$ \texttt{typeof(x)} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{"character"} $$

Explanation: The original binding is destroyed by the garbage collector (R's memory manager). The same identifier `x` now points to a freshly allocated string object. This demonstrates *dynamic typing*.

## 3.3 Workspace Management

**Step 1 — Create multiple variables:**

$$ \texttt{a} \;\texttt{<-}\; 1, \quad \texttt{b} \;\texttt{<-}\; 2, \quad \texttt{c} \;\texttt{<-}\; 3 $$

**Step 2 — List them:**

$$ \texttt{ls()} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{[1] "a" "b" "c"} $$

**Step 3 — Remove one:**

$$ \texttt{rm(b)} $$

$$ \texttt{ls()} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{[1] "a" "c"} $$

**Step 4 — Selective removal by pattern:**

$$ \texttt{rm(list = ls(pattern = "^[a]$"))} $$

Explanation: The `pattern` argument accepts a regular expression. The regex `^[a]$` matches the single-letter name `a` only.

## 3.4 Scoping — Global vs Local

**Step 1 — Global assignment:**

$$ \texttt{counter} \;\texttt{<-}\; 0 $$

**Step 2 — Function that uses a local variable:**

$$ \texttt{increment} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{function()} \;\texttt{\{} \;\texttt{local\_x} \;\texttt{<-}\; 10 \;\texttt{\}} $$

Explanation: `local_x` exists only inside the function body. Once the function exits, `local_x` is garbage-collected.

**Step 3 — Function that modifies the global variable:**

$$ \texttt{increment\_global} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{function()} \;\texttt{\{} \;\texttt{counter} \;\texttt{<<-}\; \texttt{counter} \;\texttt{+}\;\texttt{1} \;\texttt{\}} $$

Explanation: `<<-` walks up the scope chain until it finds an existing binding for `counter` and updates it in place. This is the **only** way to write to a global variable from inside a function.

## 3.5 Worked Numeric Example — Computing a Statistic

Given the dataset of five student marks stored as a vector:

$$ \texttt{marks} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{c(72, 85, 90, 60, 78)} $$

**Step 1 — Compute the mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{mean\_marks} &\;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{mean(marks)} \\
&\;\texttt{=}\; \dfrac{72 + 85 + 90 + 60 + 78}{5} \\
&\;\texttt{=}\; \dfrac{385}{5} \\
&\;\texttt{=}\; 77
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the standard deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{sd\_marks} &\;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{sd(marks)} \\
&\;\texttt{=}\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{5}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}} \\
&\;\texttt{=}\; \sqrt{\dfrac{(72-77)^2 + (85-77)^2 + (90-77)^2 + (60-77)^2 + (78-77)^2}{4}} \\
&\;\texttt{=}\; \sqrt{\dfrac{25 + 64 + 169 + 289 + 1}{4}} \\
&\;\texttt{=}\; \sqrt{\dfrac{548}{4}} \\
&\;\texttt{=}\; \sqrt{137} \\
&\;\approx\; 11.7047
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Store the result in a single summary variable:**

$$ \texttt{summary\_stats} \;\texttt{<-}\; \texttt{c(mean = mean\_marks, sd = sd\_marks)} $$

$$ \texttt{summary\_stats} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \texttt{mean   sd} \quad \texttt{77.0000 11.7047} $$

Explanation: A *named numeric vector* is created. The names `"mean"` and `"sd"` allow retrieval via `summary_stats["mean"]`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Variable Lifecycle — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Program Start]) --> B[Variable Declared or Assigned]
    B --> C[Memory Allocated in Current Environment]
    C --> D[Value Bound to Identifier]
    D --> E{Variable in Use?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Read / Modify / Reassign]
    F --> E
    E -->|No longer referenced| G[Garbage Collector Frees Memory]
    G --> H([Resource Released])
    D --> I[Type Checking Functions Available]
    I --> J([typeof, class, is.numeric])
```

Explanation: This block diagram maps the complete **lifecycle of a variable** in R — from declaration to garbage collection. The right-hand branch shows the diagnostic functions (`typeof`, `class`, `is.numeric`) that a programmer uses *without altering* the binding.

## 4.2 Scoping Hierarchy — Mermaid Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[R Global Environment - Top Level] --> F1[Function Environment - myFunc]
    F1 --> F2[Function Environment - innerFunc]
    R --> P1[Package Namespace - ggplot2]
    R --> P2[Package Namespace - dplyr]
    F1 --> S1[Local Variable x]
    R --> S2[Global Variable x]
    S1 -.shadowed.-> S2
```

Explanation: When a variable is referenced, R searches **outward** from the current environment to the global environment. The dashed "shadowed" arrow represents the principle that a local `x` masks a global `x` of the same name — a frequent source of bugs and an **important viva question**.

## 4.3 Assignment Operator Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Need to assign a value?] --> B{Inside a function?}
    B -->|No| C[Use assignment arrow]
    C --> C1[Variable on left, value on right]
    B -->|Yes| D{Need to update global?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use super-assign arrow]
    D -->|No| F[Use assignment arrow locally]
```

Explanation: This decision-tree helps students choose the correct operator during the KTU lab exam and viva, where the examiner often asks *"When do you use `<<-`?"*.

## 4.4 Data-Type Classification of Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[R Variables] --> A[Atomic Types]
    V --> B[Composite Types]
    A --> A1[Numeric - double]
    A --> A2[Integer - L suffix]
    A --> A3[Character - string]
    A --> A4[Logical - TRUE or FALSE]
    A --> A5[Complex - with i]
    A --> A6[Raw - bytes]
    B --> B1[Vector]
    B --> B2[Matrix]
    B --> B3[List]
    B --> B4[Data Frame]
    B --> B5[Factor]
```

Explanation: A complete taxonomy of the data that can be stored in an R variable. Module 3 builds the foundation; subsequent modules explore each composite type in depth.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the assignment operators `<-` and `=` in R. When is it mandatory to use `=` instead of `<-`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The operator `<-` is the conventional R assignment operator that binds a value to a name in the current environment; it is the recommended style in all R code (1 mark).
- The operator `=` performs the same binding operation in most cases, but it is *also* used to pass arguments to functions, e.g., `mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)` (1 mark).
- It is mandatory to use `=` when supplying named arguments inside a function call, e.g., `read.csv(file = "data.csv", header = TRUE)`; using `<-` there would be a syntax error (1 mark).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any six rules for naming variables in R programming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Variable names are **case-sensitive** (`Age` ≠ `age`).
2. They may contain letters, digits, dot (`.`), and underscore (`_`).
3. They must begin with a letter or a dot (a leading dot must not be followed by a digit).
4. Reserved words such as `TRUE`, `FALSE`, `if`, `else`, `function`, `for`, `while`, `NULL`, `NA`, `Inf`, `NaN` cannot be used.
5. Names should be descriptive and follow snake_case or camelCase for readability.
6. They cannot contain spaces or mathematical operators (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer any **one** full question from the two alternatives below. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the different data types that an R variable can hold. Provide R code snippets demonstrating the creation of a numeric, integer, character, logical, and complex variable, along with the output of `typeof()` for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| S.No | R Code | Value | `typeof()` Output |
|------|--------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | `v1 <- 42.5` | `42.5` | `"double"` |
| 2 | `v2 <- 42L` | `42` | `"integer"` |
| 3 | `v3 <- "KTU"` | `"KTU"` | `"character"` |
| 4 | `v4 <- (5 > 3)` | `TRUE` | `"logical"` |
| 5 | `v5 <- 2 + 3i` | `2+3i` | `"complex"` |

Explanation (1 mark per type):
- **Numeric (double):** Default for any number containing a decimal or written without `L` suffix.
- **Integer:** Created explicitly using the `L` suffix or `as.integer()`; uses 32-bit storage.
- **Character:** Enclosed in double or single quotes; stored as a string of UTF-8 characters.
- **Logical:** Boolean values `TRUE`, `FALSE`, `NA`, or results of comparisons.
- **Complex:** Stores real + imaginary parts; created using the literal `i` suffix.

Verification snippet:
```
typeof(v1) ; typeof(v2) ; typeof(v3) ; typeof(v4) ; typeof(v5)
```

**(b)** Write an R program that creates the vector `c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)`, computes its mean and standard deviation, stores them in named variables, and prints the results. Explain each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```r
# Step 1: Create the numeric vector
data <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)

# Step 2: Compute mean and standard deviation
mean_val <- mean(data)
sd_val   <- sd(data)

# Step 3: Combine into a named vector
summary_stats <- c(mean = mean_val, standard_deviation = sd_val)

# Step 4: Print
print(summary_stats)
```

**Output:**
```
       mean standard_deviation 
         30           15.81139 
```

Explanation (1 mark per conceptual step):
1. `c()` concatenates values into a numeric vector. (1 mark)
2. `mean()` computes arithmetic average; `sd()` computes sample standard deviation with denominator $n - 1$. (2 marks)
3. Named vectors allow retrieval using the syntax `summary_stats["mean"]`. (1 mark)
4. The mean is $\frac{10+20+30+40+50}{5} = 30$ and the standard deviation is $\sqrt{\frac{1000}{4}} = \sqrt{250} \approx 15.81$. (2 marks)
5. The `print()` function displays the result on the console. (1 mark)

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is variable scoping in R? Differentiate between global and local variables with a suitable example program demonstrating the use of `<-` and `<<-`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** *Scoping* refers to the set of rules that determine where a given identifier (variable name) is visible and accessible within a program. R uses **lexical (static) scoping**, meaning a function looks up free variables in the environment where it was *defined*, not where it was *called*.

| Aspect | Global Variable | Local Variable |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Definition Location | Outside any function | Inside a function body |
| Visibility | Accessible everywhere | Accessible only inside the defining function |
| Lifetime | Until the end of the R session | Until the function returns |
| Created Using | `<-` or `=` | `<-` or `=` |
| Modified From Inside Function | Only via `<<-` | Cannot be modified from outside |

**Example Program (5 marks):**

```r
counter <- 0          # Global variable

increment <- function() {
  local_x <- 5        # Local variable — invisible outside
  counter <<- counter + 1   # Super-assignment updates global
  cat("Inside function -> local_x =", local_x, 
      "| counter =", counter, "\n")
}

increment()
increment()
increment()
cat("Outside function -> counter =", counter, "\n")
cat("Trying to access local_x -> ")
print(local_x)         # Error: object 'local_x' not found
```

**Output:**
```
Inside function -> local_x = 5 | counter = 1 
Inside function -> local_x = 5 | counter = 2 
Inside function -> local_x = 5 | counter = 3 
Outside function -> counter = 3 
Error in print(local_x) : object 'local_x' not found
```

Explanation:
- `counter` is global; `<-` would create a *local* `counter` that disappears when the function returns; hence `<<-` is mandatory to mutate the global. (2 marks)
- `local_x` is created *inside* the function; trying to print it in the global scope raises an error. (1 mark)
- The function demonstrates **lexical scoping** — the variable `counter` is resolved by searching outwards through enclosing environments until the binding is found in the global environment. (2 marks)

**(b)** Explain the following built-in R functions used for variable management: `ls()`, `rm()`, `exists()`, `assign()`, `get()`. Provide one illustrative example for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Function | Purpose | Example | Output / Effect |
|----------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| `ls()` | Lists variable names in current environment | `ls()` after creating `x <- 1`, `y <- 2` | `[1] "x" "y"` |
| `rm(x)` | Removes variable `x` from memory | `rm(x); ls()` | `[1] "y"` |
| `exists("x")` | Checks whether `x` is defined | `exists("y")` | `TRUE` |
| `assign("k", 99)` | Programmatic assignment (string name) | `assign("k", 99); k` | `99` |
| `get("k")` | Programmatic retrieval of value | `get("k")` | `99` |

Combined demonstration:
```r
a <- 10 ; b <- 20 ; c <- 30
ls()                        # "a" "b" "c"
exists("b")                 # TRUE
rm("b")
ls()                        # "a" "c"
exists("b")                 # FALSE
assign("new_var", 500)
new_var                     # 500
get("new_var")              # 500
```

**Explanation:**
- `ls()` is used in housekeeping before saving a workspace (1 mark).
- `rm()` frees memory — vital when handling large datasets (1 mark).
- `exists()` is useful inside conditional blocks to avoid re-creating expensive objects (1 mark).
- `assign()` and `get()` allow **metaprogramming** — variable names can be computed at run-time from strings (2 marks).
- This pattern is heavily used in `for`-loops that create many variables programmatically, e.g., `assign(paste0("col_", i), data[, i])` (2 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Using `=` instead of `<-` for general assignment** — KTU examiners treat this as poor coding style and deduct up to **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing `<<-` with `<-` inside functions** — Students often write `counter <- counter + 1`, which silently creates a *local* variable and leaves the global untouched. This loses **2–3 marks** in scoping questions.
> 3. **Forgetting the `L` suffix when demonstrating integers** — Writing `x <- 5` makes `x` a *double*, not an integer. Examiners explicitly check for this. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Not mentioning that `nchar()` returns the number of characters** when asked about string-variable inspection — a frequent viva trap.
> 5. **Writing `var name <- 5`** (with a space) — this is a syntax error; the underscore `_` or camelCase must be used instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] A **variable** in R is a name-value binding created at the moment of assignment; no prior declaration is needed.
- [x] R is **dynamically typed**: a variable may be re-bound to a value of any type at run-time.
- [x] Preferred assignment operator is **`<-`** (left arrow). Use `<<-` only to update a global from inside a function.
- [x] Variable names are **case-sensitive**, must start with a letter or dot (not followed by a digit), and cannot be reserved words.
- [x] Six atomic data types: **numeric (double), integer, character, logical, complex, raw**.
- [x] Inspection toolkit: **`typeof()`, `class()`, `mode()`, `is.*()`, `as.*()`**.
- [x] Workspace management: **`ls()`, `rm()`, `exists()`, `assign()`, `get()`**.
- [x] Scoping rule: R uses **lexical (static) scoping**; local variables shadow globals of the same name.
- [x] **Constants** like `NULL`, `NA`, `NaN`, `Inf`, `TRUE`, `FALSE` are pre-defined immutable bindings.
- [x] Always prefer **snake_case** or **camelCase** for readability in KTU lab records.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **R programming language**, a **Data Type** is a classification that specifies the kind of value a variable can hold and the operations that can be performed on it. R is a **dynamically typed**, functional programming language in which every object carries an internal type tag determining how its bit pattern is interpreted by the interpreter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Accepted):**
> "A data type in R is an attribute of an R-object which describes the type of value stored within it and the operations that can be performed on that value without causing a runtime type error."

R has **six atomic (basic) data types**:

| # | Data Type | Description | Example Literal |
|---|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Character** | String/text data | `"KTU"`, `'R'` |
| 2 | **Numeric (Double)** | Decimal real numbers | `3.14`, `-2.5` |
| 3 | **Integer** | Whole numbers (suffix `L`) | `42L`, `0L` |
| 4 | **Logical** | Boolean TRUE/FALSE | `TRUE`, `FALSE` |
| 5 | **Complex** | Numbers with imaginary part | `3+2i` |
| 6 | **Raw** | Raw bytes | `as.raw(0x1A)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** R's numeric type is *internally stored as a double-precision floating-point number* conforming to the **IEEE 754** standard. This is critical for understanding precision and rounding behaviour in statistical computations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Labelled Storage Boxes"

Imagine you walk into a **warehouse** (your computer's RAM) and you have an unlimited supply of **labelled storage boxes** (R variables). Before storing anything, you must decide:

> *"What *kind* of item is this box going to hold?"*

- 📦 A box labelled `student_name` will hold **paper slips with names** → *Character*
- 📦 A box labelled `temperature_celsius` will hold **decimal measurements** → *Numeric (Double)*
- 📦 A box labelled `num_students` will hold **whole counts** → *Integer*
- 📦 A box labelled `is_passed` will hold **a light switch (ON/OFF)** → *Logical*

The **label** of the box is the *variable name*; the **type of contents** is the *data type*. R, unlike C or Java, does not force you to declare the box type in advance — it **looks at what you put inside** and tags the box automatically. This is the meaning of *dynamic typing*.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** If data were *vehicles on a highway*, then data types would be the **vehicle categories** — bikes, cars, buses, trucks. Each category has its own speed limit, lane rules, and toll structure. Mixing them causes "collisions" (type errors).

---

## 1.3 Why Data Types Matter in R

R is purpose-built for **statistical computing and data analytics**. Choosing the correct data type:

1. **Optimises memory** — an `integer` uses ~4 bytes; a `character` may use many more.
2. **Ensures correctness** — `1/3` in integer arithmetic returns `0` (truncation), in numeric returns `0.3333...`
3. **Enables valid operations** — you cannot multiply a string by an integer in R without coercion.
4. **Affects vectorised operations** — R's strength is vectorised arithmetic, which requires homogeneous typing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** "R has no separate float and double like C." **Wrong** — R's `numeric` *is* the IEEE-754 double. There is no single-precision float type in base R.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Atomic Type Hierarchy in R

R classifies its objects in **two parallel ways**:

1. **Mode** — the *storage type* (what the bits physically are)
2. **Class** — the *behavioural type* (what methods/operations apply to it)

> Example: A `data.frame` has mode `list` but class `data.frame`.

### 2.1.1 Type Inspection Functions (High-Yield)

| Function | Returns | Example | Output |
|----------|---------|---------|--------|
| `typeof(x)` | The internal type (R's "true" type) | `typeof(3.14)` | `"double"` |
| `class(x)` | The class attribute | `class(3.14)` | `"numeric"` |
| `mode(x)` | The storage mode | `mode(3.14)` | `"numeric"` |
| `storage.mode(x)` | Low-level mode (mutable) | `storage.mode(3.14)` | `"double"` |
| `is.numeric(x)` | Logical check | `is.numeric(3L)` | `TRUE` |
| `is.integer(x)` | Logical check | `is.integer(3L)` | `TRUE` |
| `is.character(x)` | Logical check | `is.character("R")` | `TRUE` |
| `is.logical(x)` | Logical check | `is.logical(TRUE)` | `TRUE` |
| `is.complex(x)` | Logical check | `is.complex(2+3i)` | `TRUE` |
| `is.raw(x)` | Logical check | `is.raw(as.raw(7))` | `TRUE` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** `is.numeric()` returns `TRUE` for *both* `integer` and `double`, because both are numbers. To strictly check for an integer, use `is.integer()`.

---

## 2.2 Type Coercion (Implicit & Explicit)

R will **automatically convert** (coerce) data types when:

- A vector is created with mixed types
- An operation requires a common type
- Functions receive arguments of a different type

### 2.2.1 Coercion Hierarchy (Lowest → Highest)

> Logical → Integer → Numeric (Double) → Complex → Character

Anything on the **left** is silently converted to the type on the **right** when mixed.

**Example — mixed vector:**
```r
> c(1, 2.5, TRUE, "hello")
[1] "1"     "2.5"   "TRUE"  "hello"
```
The presence of one *character* forces *all* elements to become characters.

### 2.2.2 Explicit Coercion Functions

| Function | Converts To | Example | Output |
|----------|-------------|---------|--------|
| `as.numeric(x)` | Double | `as.numeric("3.14")` | `3.14` |
| `as.integer(x)` | Integer (truncates decimals) | `as.integer(3.9)` | `3` |
| `as.character(x)` | Character | `as.character(42)` | `"42"` |
| `as.logical(x)` | Logical (`0→FALSE`, others `→TRUE`) | `as.logical(0)` | `FALSE` |
| `as.complex(x)` | Complex | `as.complex(2)` | `2+0i` |
| `as.raw(x)` | Raw byte (0–255) | `as.raw(65)` | `41` (ASCII 'A') |

> [!WARNING]
> **`as.integer()` TRUNCATES, it does NOT round.** `as.integer(3.9)` gives `3`, not `4`. Use `round(3.9)` for proper rounding.

---

## 2.3 Special Values in R

| Constant | Meaning | Type | Example |
|----------|---------|------|---------|
| `NA` | **N**ot **A**vailable (missing value) | Logical (with `NA` flag) | `c(1, NA, 3)` |
| `NaN` | **N**ot **a** **N**umber (indeterminate) | Numeric | `0/0` |
| `Inf` / `-Inf` | Positive/Negative infinity | Numeric | `1/0` |
| `NULL` | Absence of value (empty object) | `NULL` (its own type) | `c()` returns `NULL` |
| `NA_integer_`, `NA_real_`, `NA_character_`, `NA_complex_` | Typed missing values | Match the type | `NA_real_` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Favourite Trap:** `is.na(NaN)` returns **`TRUE`**, but `is.nan(NA)` returns **`FALSE`**. This is a guaranteed exam question.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---------|----------------|-------|
| Type of numeric literal `3.14` | `typeof()` → `"double"` | Default for decimals |
| Type of literal `3L` | `typeof()` → `"integer"` | The `L` suffix is mandatory |
| Boolean to integer | `as.integer(TRUE)` = **1**, `as.integer(FALSE)` = **0** | Used in counting |
| Coercion priority | `logical < integer < double < complex < character` | Lowest to highest |
| `Inf` check | `is.infinite(x)` | Returns `TRUE` for `±Inf` |
| `NaN` check | `is.nan(x)` | Strict subset of `NA` |
| `NA` check | `is.na(x)` | Catches both `NA` and `NaN` |
| `NULL` check | `is.null(x)` | `NULL` has length 0 |
| Length of a scalar | `length(x)` = **1** | Even single values are vectors of length 1 |
| `Inf - Inf` | `NaN` | Undefined operation |
| `0/0` | `NaN` | Indeterminate |
| `1/0` | `Inf` | Division by zero is allowed in R |
| `exp(Inf)` | `Inf` | Mathematical consistency |
| `exp(-Inf)` | `0` | Asymptote |
| `log(0)` | `-Inf` | Mathematical limit |
| `sqrt(-1)` | `NaN` with a warning | R does not auto-promote to complex |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of R Data Types |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Bioinformatics** | Storing DNA sequences as `character` vectors (`"ATGC..."`) |
| **IoT / Sensor Data** | `numeric` for temperatures, `integer` for counts, `logical` for alarm flags |
| **Financial Analytics** | `numeric` for prices, `integer` for transaction IDs, `character` for tickers |
| **Quality Control** | `logical` for pass/fail, `numeric` for measurements, `character` for batch IDs |
| **Statistical Modelling** | `integer` for sample sizes, `complex` for Fourier transforms in signal processing |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In R, **every value is technically a vector** — even a single number `42` is a numeric vector of length 1. This is a *fundamental paradigm* of R and a guaranteed high-weightage point in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Demonstration 1 — Type Identification in R

**Problem:** Identify the data type of the following R values, showing the output of `typeof()` and `class()` for each.

| # | R Value | Expected `typeof()` | Expected `class()` |
|---|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | `42` | `"double"` | `"numeric"` |
| 2 | `42L` | `"integer"` | `"integer"` |
| 3 | `"Hello"` | `"character"` | `"character"` |
| 4 | `TRUE` | `"logical"` | `"logical"` |
| 5 | `3+2i` | `"complex"` | `"complex"` |
| 6 | `as.raw(7)` | `"raw"` | `"raw"` |
| 7 | `NA` | `"logical"` | `"logical"` |
| 8 | `NaN` | `"double"` | `"numeric"` |
| 9 | `NULL` | `"NULL"` | `"NULL"` |
| 10 | `Inf` | `"double"` | `"numeric"` |

### Step-by-Step Walk-through

**Step 1:** For `42`
- By default, any decimal-looking literal in R is stored as an **IEEE 754 double**.
- Therefore `typeof(42)` returns the string `"double"`.
- However, R's `class()` function returns `"numeric"` because R exposes the user-facing name "numeric" to mean "any number type".
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **double**, `class`: **numeric**.

**Step 2:** For `42L`
- The capital `L` suffix explicitly tells R to store the value as a **32-bit signed integer**.
- `typeof(42L)` returns `"integer"`, and because integers are a *subclass* of numeric, `class(42L)` also returns `"integer"`.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **integer**, `class`: **integer**.

**Step 3:** For `"Hello"`
- Quoted values are **character strings** in R (both single and double quotes work).
- `typeof("Hello")` and `class("Hello")` both return `"character"`.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **character**, `class`: **character**.

**Step 4:** For `TRUE`
- Logical values are stored as `TRUE` or `FALSE` (no quotes).
- `typeof(TRUE)` returns `"logical"`, and `class(TRUE)` also returns `"logical"`.
- Internally, R stores `TRUE` as `1` and `FALSE` as `0` for arithmetic purposes.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **logical**, `class`: **logical**.

**Step 5:** For `3+2i`
- A number with an `i` suffix is treated as the **imaginary component** of a complex number.
- The real part is `3`, the imaginary part is `2`, hence the complex number $3 + 2i$.
- `typeof(3+2i)` returns `"complex"`, and `class(3+2i)` returns `"complex"`.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **complex**, `class`: **complex**.

**Step 6:** For `as.raw(7)`
- The `as.raw()` function converts a number into a **single raw byte** (range 0–255).
- `as.raw(7)` produces the byte value `07`, of type `"raw"`.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **raw**, `class`: **raw**.

**Step 7:** For `NA`
- `NA` (Not Available) is the sentinel for **missing values**.
- By default, `NA` is of mode `logical` — it is *untyped* missing data.
- `typeof(NA)` returns `"logical"`, and `class(NA)` returns `"logical"`.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **logical**, `class`: **logical**.

**Step 8:** For `NaN`
- `NaN` (Not a Number) arises from **indeterminate numeric operations** like $0/0$.
- `NaN` is *typed* as a numeric, specifically a double.
- `typeof(NaN)` returns `"double"`, and `class(NaN)` returns `"numeric"`.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **double**, `class`: **numeric**.

**Step 9:** For `NULL`
- `NULL` represents the **absence of any value** — its own unique type.
- It has `length(NULL) == 0` and cannot participate in vector arithmetic.
- `typeof(NULL)` returns `"NULL"`, and `class(NULL)` returns `"NULL"`.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **NULL**, `class`: **NULL**.

**Step 10:** For `Inf`
- `Inf` is **positive infinity**, an IEEE 754 special value, stored as a double.
- `typeof(Inf)` returns `"double"`, and `class(Inf)` returns `"numeric"`.
- Conclusion → `typeof`: **double**, `class`: **numeric**.

---

## 3.2 Demonstration 2 — Coercion Chain Analysis

**Problem:** Given the mixed vector `v = c(2, 3.5, TRUE, "R")`, determine the final type of every element after R's automatic coercion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Initial heterogeneous vector:**
$$
v = c(2,\ 3.5,\ TRUE,\ "R")
$$

**Step 2 — Identify each element's intrinsic type:**

| Position | Element | Intrinsic Type |
|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | `2` | integer-like (numeric) |
| 2 | `3.5` | double (numeric) |
| 3 | `TRUE` | logical |
| 4 | `"R"` | character |

**Step 3 — Apply the coercion hierarchy** $\text{logical} \rightarrow \text{integer} \rightarrow \text{double} \rightarrow \text{complex} \rightarrow \text{character}$

Since one element is **`character`** (the highest in the hierarchy), R coerces **all** elements to character strings.

**Step 4 — Final converted vector:**

$$
v = c(\ "2",\ "3.5",\ "TRUE",\ "R"\ )
$$

**Step 5 — Verification by R-style code:**

```r
v <- c(2, 3.5, TRUE, "R")
typeof(v)        # returns "character"
class(v)         # returns "character"
as.numeric(v)    # would convert back: 2, 3.5, NA, NA  (coercion warning)
```

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** The presence of **one character element** polluted the entire vector to type `character`. This is a **type-homogeneity rule** central to R's vector semantics.

---

## 3.3 Demonstration 3 — Logical-to-Integer Conversion Proof

**Problem:** Prove that in R, `as.integer(TRUE) + as.integer(FALSE) + as.integer(NA)` returns `NA`, and explain why.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Evaluate the left operand:**

`as.integer(TRUE)` → R internally stores `TRUE` as the integer **1**.

**Step 2 — Evaluate the middle operand:**

`as.integer(FALSE)` → R stores `FALSE` as the integer **0**.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the right operand:**

`as.integer(NA)` → R has no integer representation for "missing" — the conversion yields `NA_integer_`.

**Step 4 — Combine the three integers in the sum:**

$$
\text{result} = 1 + 0 + \text{NA}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply R's "missing value propagation" rule:**

In R, any arithmetic operation involving `NA` yields `NA`. This is called **NA propagation**.

$$
1 + 0 + \text{NA} = \text{NA}
$$

**Step 6 — Final R output:**

```r
> as.integer(TRUE) + as.integer(FALSE) + as.integer(NA)
[1] NA
```

**Step 7 — Why this matters:** When working with real datasets (e.g., survey responses with missing data), `NA` values **contaminate** all downstream calculations. To ignore them, use `na.rm = TRUE` in functions like `sum()`, `mean()`, etc.

---

## 3.4 Demonstration 4 — Special Value Edge Cases

**Problem:** Determine the R output (and type) of the following expressions:

| # | Expression | Result | Type |
|---|------------|--------|------|
| 1 | `1/0` | `Inf` | `double` |
| 2 | `0/0` | `NaN` | `double` |
| 3 | `Inf - Inf` | `NaN` | `double` |
| 4 | `Inf + (-Inf)` | `NaN` | `double` |
| 5 | `sqrt(-4)` | `NaN` (with warning) | `double` |
| 6 | `log(0)` | `-Inf` | `double` |
| 7 | `is.na(NaN)` | `TRUE` | `logical` |
| 8 | `is.nan(NA)` | `FALSE` | `logical` |
| 9 | `is.null(NULL)` | `TRUE` | `logical` |
| 10 | `is.finite(Inf)` | `FALSE` | `logical` |

**Derivation Highlights:**

- **Row 1:** $1/0$ is a positive overflow. R does not raise an error — it returns `Inf` per IEEE 754.
- **Row 2:** $0/0$ is *indeterminate* ($0 \times \infty$ ambiguity). Per IEEE 754, the result is `NaN`.
- **Row 3:** $\infty - \infty$ is also indeterminate → `NaN`.
- **Row 5:** $\sqrt{-4} = 2i$ mathematically, but R keeps the working type as `double` and returns `NaN` with a warning `"NaNs produced"`. To get a complex result, explicitly use `sqrt(-4 + 0i)` or `as.complex(-4)`.
- **Row 7 vs 8:** `NaN` is a *sub-class* of `NA` in R's missing-value taxonomy, so `is.na()` catches both, but `is.nan()` only catches `NaN`.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Type-Checking Decision Table

Use this decision matrix to identify any R object's type in an exam:

| Test Condition | Returns TRUE for |
|----------------|------------------|
| `is.numeric(x)` | `integer` and `double` (any number) |
| `is.integer(x)` | only `integer` (no doubles) |
| `is.double(x)` | only `double` (no integers) |
| `is.character(x)` | any string |
| `is.logical(x)` | `TRUE`, `FALSE`, `NA` |
| `is.complex(x)` | numbers with `i` component |
| `is.raw(x)` | raw bytes only |
| `is.null(x)` | the `NULL` constant |
| `is.na(x)` | `NA`, `NaN` |
| `is.nan(x)` | only `NaN` |
| `is.infinite(x)` | `Inf`, `-Inf` |
| `is.finite(x)` | everything except `NA`, `NaN`, `Inf` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 R Data Type Hierarchy (Mermaid Tree)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete R data type taxonomy as covered in Module 3. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping how the six atomic types relate to the special values, type-coercion functions, and inspection functions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    RObject["R Object - Anything assigned a name"]
    Atomic["Atomic Types - Homogeneous single value"]
    NonAtomic["Non-Atomic Types - Composite structures"]
    
    Character["Character - Strings"]
    Numeric["Numeric - Numbers"]
    Logical["Logical - TRUE FALSE"]
    Complex["Complex - a + bi"]
    Raw["Raw - Bytes 0 to 255"]
    
    Double["Double - decimal IEEE 754"]
    Integer["Integer - whole numbers suffix L"]
    
    Special["Special Values"]
    NA["NA - Not Available missing"]
    NaN["NaN - Not a Number indeterminate"]
    Inf["Inf and minus Inf - Infinity"]
    Null["NULL - Empty object"]
    
    Inspect["Type Inspection Functions"]
    Typeof["typeof returns internal type"]
    Class["class returns user class"]
    Mode["mode returns storage mode"]
    IsX["is dot family - logical checks"]
    AsX["as dot family - explicit coercion"]
    
    RObject --> Atomic
    RObject --> NonAtomic
    Atomic --> Character
    Atomic --> Numeric
    Atomic --> Logical
    Atomic --> Complex
    Atomic --> Raw
    Numeric --> Double
    Numeric --> Integer
    Atomic --> Special
    Special --> NA
    Special --> NaN
    Special --> Inf
    Special --> Null
    RObject --> Inspect
    Inspect --> Typeof
    Inspect --> Class
    Inspect --> Mode
    Inspect --> IsX
    Inspect --> AsX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Non-atomic types (`vector`, `list`, `matrix`, `data.frame`, `array`, `factor`) are covered in detail in Module 4 of the syllabus. Module 3 focuses exclusively on the **atomic types** and **special values** shown in the upper branch of the diagram.

---

## 4.2 Coercion Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block shows the **type-coercion direction** in R. An arrow `A --> B` means *"A can be coerced to B"*. Note that coercion is generally *lossy* when going from complex to lower types.

```mermaid
graph LR
    L["Logical"] --> I["Integer"]
    I --> D["Double / Numeric"]
    D --> C["Complex"]
    C --> Ch["Character"]
    
    L -. "as dot logical" .- I
    I -. "as dot integer" .- D
    D -. "as dot numeric" .- C
    C -. "as dot complex" .- Ch
    Ch -. "as dot character" .- Ch
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Coercion Direction is ONE-WAY for safety.** You can convert integer → character, but converting character → integer may fail or produce `NA` for non-numeric strings. R will issue a warning: *"NAs introduced by coercion"*.

---

## 4.3 Type Inspection Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Receive an R object x"] --> B{"is x missing?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["is dot na catches NA and NaN"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"is x numeric-like?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["is dot integer OR is dot double"]
    D -- "No" --> F{"is x text?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["is dot character"]
    F -- "No" --> H{"is x boolean?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["is dot logical"]
    H -- "No" --> J["Use typeof x for definitive type"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** If a question asks *"Write an R expression to test whether a value is an integer"*, **never write `is.numeric()`** — use `is.integer()`. Board examiners specifically test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Rule (2024 Scheme):** Part A carries 3 marks per question, expected answer length is 3–5 sentences, with a single R expression where applicable.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "data type" in the R programming language. List ALL six atomic data types in R and give one example literal for each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **data type** in R is a classification assigned to every R object that determines the kind of value it stores and the set of operations permissible on that value. R is dynamically typed, so the type is inferred at assignment time, not declared.

The **six atomic data types** in R are:

| # | Data Type | Example |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Character | `"Kerala"` |
| 2 | Numeric (double) | `3.1415` |
| 3 | Integer | `2024L` |
| 4 | Logical | `TRUE` |
| 5 | Complex | `2 + 3i` |
| 6 | Raw | `as.raw(65)` |

*(Marks: Definition 1, List of six 1, Examples 1)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the difference between `NA`, `NaN`, and `NULL` in R. Which of the following returns `TRUE`: `is.na(NaN)` or `is.nan(NA)`? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**

- **`NA`** stands for "Not Available" and represents a **missing value** in a dataset. It is a sentinel of mode `logical` by default but can be typed (`NA_integer_`, `NA_real_`, etc.).
- **`NaN`** stands for "Not a Number" and represents an **undefined or indeterminate numeric result** such as $0/0$ or $\infty - \infty$. It is of type `double`.
- **`NULL`** represents the **complete absence of an R object**. It has `length(NULL) = 0` and is its own unique type.

**Evaluation of the two expressions:**

- `is.na(NaN)` returns **`TRUE`** — because in R, `NaN` is a special sub-category of `NA`, so any `NaN` is also considered "not available".
- `is.nan(NA)` returns **`FALSE`** — because `NA` is *not* a number; it is the absence of a value, hence it cannot be "not-a-number".

*(Marks: 3 definitions 1.5 marks, evaluation 0.75 + justification 0.75)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B (internal choice). Total = **14 marks**.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain any **four atomic data types** in R with suitable examples. Write R code to demonstrate the use of `typeof()`, `class()`, and `mode()` on each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What is **type coercion** in R? Explain **implicit and explicit coercion** with an example each. Write R code to convert a character vector `c("10", "20", "30")` to a numeric vector, and a numeric vector `c(1.7, 2.3, 3.9)` to an integer vector, showing the difference in output. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) Solution

**Four atomic data types in R:**

**1. Character Data Type:**
- Stores text strings enclosed in single `''` or double `""` quotes.
- Example: `name <- "Aparna"`

**2. Numeric (Double) Data Type:**
- Stores decimal numbers; internally stored as **IEEE 754 double precision (8 bytes)**.
- Example: `temperature <- 36.6`

**3. Integer Data Type:**
- Stores whole numbers; must be suffixed with capital `L` to distinguish from double.
- Example: `roll_no <- 47L`

**4. Logical Data Type:**
- Stores boolean values `TRUE` or `FALSE` (no quotes). Internally `TRUE = 1` and `FALSE = 0`.
- Example: `is_valid <- TRUE`

**R Demonstration Code:**

```r
# Character
x1 <- "KTU"
cat("x1:", x1, " | typeof:", typeof(x1), " | class:", class(x1), " | mode:", mode(x1), "\n")

# Numeric (Double)
x2 <- 3.14
cat("x2:", x2, " | typeof:", typeof(x2), " | class:", class(x2), " | mode:", mode(x2), "\n")

# Integer
x3 <- 100L
cat("x3:", x3, " | typeof:", typeof(x3), " | class:", class(x3), " | mode:", mode(x3), "\n")

# Logical
x4 <- TRUE
cat("x4:", x4, " | typeof:", typeof(x4), " | class:", class(x4), " | mode:", mode(x4), "\n")
```

**Expected Output:**
```
x1: KTU  | typeof: character | class: character | mode: character 
x2: 3.14 | typeof: double     | class: numeric   | mode: numeric 
x3: 100  | typeof: integer    | class: integer   | mode: numeric 
x4: TRUE | typeof: logical    | class: logical   | mode: logical 
```

*(Marks: 4 types explained 4 × 1 = 4, code demonstration 3)*

---

### Part (b) Solution

**Type Coercion** is the automatic or manual conversion of one data type to another in R.

**Implicit Coercion** happens automatically by the R interpreter when types are mixed in a vector or operation.

**Example of Implicit Coercion:**
```r
> v <- c(2, 3.5, TRUE)
> typeof(v)
[1] "double"
```
The `TRUE` is silently coerced to `1.0` (double) to match the numeric type.

**Explicit Coercion** is performed by the programmer using `as.*` functions.

**Example 1 — Character to Numeric:**
```r
char_vec <- c("10", "20", "30")
num_vec <- as.numeric(char_vec)
print(num_vec)
typeof(num_vec)
```
**Output:**
```
[1] 10 20 30
[1] "double"
```

**Example 2 — Numeric to Integer (Truncation):**
```r
num_vec2 <- c(1.7, 2.3, 3.9)
int_vec <- as.integer(num_vec2)
print(int_vec)
typeof(int_vec)
```
**Output:**
```
[1] 1 2 3
[1] "integer"
```

**Key Observation:** The values `1.7`, `2.3`, and `3.9` are *truncated* (not rounded) to `1`, `2`, `3` respectively. This is because `as.integer()` performs *floor towards zero* truncation, not mathematical rounding. To round, one would use `round(1.7) = 2`.

*(Marks: Definition 2, implicit example 1, explicit example 4)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **special values** `NA`, `NaN`, `Inf`, and `NULL` in R. For each, state its type, meaning, and a scenario that would produce it. Write R code to verify `is.na(NaN)` and `is.nan(NA)`. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the difference between `is.numeric()`, `is.integer()`, and `is.double()` in R. With R code examples, demonstrate why `is.numeric(5L)` returns `TRUE` while `is.integer(5)` returns `FALSE`. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) Solution

**Special Values in R — A Complete Discussion:**

| Special Value | Type | Meaning | How It Is Produced |
|---------------|------|---------|---------------------|
| `NA` | logical (with NA flag) | Not Available — a missing datum | Survey non-response, unrecorded sensor value |
| `NaN` | double | Not a Number — indeterminate result | `0/0`, `Inf - Inf`, `sqrt(-1)` |
| `Inf` | double | Positive infinity | `1/0`, `exp(1000)` (overflow) |
| `-Inf` | double | Negative infinity | `-1/0`, `-exp(1000)` |
| `NULL` | NULL | Complete absence of an R object | `c()` with no arguments |

**R Verification Code:**

```r
# Verify is.na(NaN) returns TRUE
cat("is.na(NaN):", is.na(NaN), "\n")
# Output: TRUE

# Verify is.nan(NA) returns FALSE
cat("is.nan(NA):", is.nan(NA), "\n")
# Output: FALSE

# Demonstration of producing each special value
cat("1/0  =", 1/0,  "\n")          # Inf
cat("0/0  =", 0/0,  "\n")          # NaN
cat("NA   =", NA,   "\n")          # NA
cat("NULL has length:", length(NULL), "\n")  # 0
```

**Why `is.na(NaN)` is TRUE but `is.nan(NA)` is FALSE:**

In R's classification system, `NaN` is a *sub-type* of `NA` (a NaN is missing AND non-numeric). Hence `is.na()` catches both `NA` and `NaN`. However, `NA` is *not* a number at all — it is a sentinel for absence. Hence `is.nan()` excludes it.

*(Marks: 4 values × 1.5 = 6, verification code 1)*

---

### Part (b) Solution

**The `is.*` Family of Type-Check Functions:**

In R, the `is.*` family returns a logical (`TRUE` / `FALSE`) indicating whether a value matches a particular type.

| Function | Returns TRUE for | Returns FALSE for |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| `is.numeric(x)` | both `integer` AND `double` | character, logical, complex, etc. |
| `is.integer(x)` | only values with `typeof(x) == "integer"` (i.e., suffixed with `L`) | any double, character, etc. |
| `is.double(x)` | only values with `typeof(x) == "double"` | any integer, character, etc. |

**Why `is.numeric(5L)` is TRUE:**

The literal `5L` is of type `integer`. R's `is.numeric()` is a *general* check: it asks *"Is this value a number of any kind?"* Since integers ARE numbers, the check passes and returns `TRUE`.

**Why `is.integer(5)` is FALSE:**

The literal `5` (without `L` suffix) is of type `double` by default. R's `is.integer()` is a *strict* check: it asks *"Is the internal type exactly integer?"* Since `5` is a double, the check fails and returns `FALSE`.

**R Demonstration Code:**

```r
# Demonstration 1: integer is numeric, but numeric is not always integer
cat("is.numeric(5L):", is.numeric(5L), "\n")   # TRUE
cat("is.integer(5L):", is.integer(5L), "\n")   # TRUE
cat("is.numeric(5):",  is.numeric(5),  "\n")   # TRUE
cat("is.integer(5):",  is.integer(5),  "\n")   # FALSE
cat("is.double(5):",   is.double(5),   "\n")   # TRUE
cat("is.double(5L):",  is.double(5L),  "\n")   # FALSE
```

**Conceptual Hierarchy:**

$$
\text{Integer} \subset \text{Numeric} \subset \text{Atomic Types}
$$

The integer type is a *subset* of the numeric type. Hence `is.numeric()` is a broader, more inclusive test than `is.integer()`.

*(Marks: Definitions 3, demonstration 4)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (2024 Scheme Examiners' Report):**
> 
> 1. **The `L` Suffix Trap (–1 mark):** Writing `x <- 5` and claiming it is an *integer*. **It is a double.** Always use `5L` for integers.
> 
> 2. **`is.numeric()` vs `is.integer()` Confusion (–1 mark):** Many students interchange these. Remember: `is.numeric()` accepts *both* integer and double; `is.integer()` is *strict*.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting `typeof()` vs `class()` distinction (–1 mark):** `typeof(3.14)` is `"double"`, but `class(3.14)` is `"numeric"`. Writing one for the other loses marks.
> 
> 4. **`as.integer()` Truncation vs Rounding (–2 marks):** Saying `as.integer(3.9)` gives `4`. **It gives `3`**. This is the single most-failed question in R data-type exams.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring `is.na(NaN) = TRUE` (–1 mark):** Saying `is.na()` only catches `NA` and not `NaN` is a factual error.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting that R has no "float" type (–1 mark):** R's `numeric` is double-precision. There is no single-precision float in base R.
> 
> 7. **Mixing `NA` and `NULL` in code (–1 mark):** `NA` is a *value* (a missing one); `NULL` is the *absence* of a value. They are not interchangeable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Data Types**
> 
> 📌 **R is dynamically typed** — type is inferred from the value, not declared.
> 
> 📌 **Six atomic types** in R: `character`, `numeric (double)`, `integer`, `logical`, `complex`, `raw`.
> 
> 📌 **Default numeric type is double**, not integer. Use `L` suffix to force integer.
> 
> 📌 **`typeof()` returns the internal type** (e.g., `"double"`); `class()` returns the user-facing class (e.g., `"numeric"`).
> 
> 📌 **`is.numeric()` = TRUE for both integer AND double.** Use `is.integer()` or `is.double()` for strict checks.
> 
> 📌 **Coercion hierarchy (lowest to highest):** logical → integer → double → complex → character.
> 
> 📌 **`as.integer()` truncates toward zero**, it does not round. `as.integer(3.9) = 3`, not `4`.
> 
> 📌 **`NA` is logical by default**; typed NAs exist: `NA_integer_`, `NA_real_`, `NA_character_`, `NA_complex_`.
> 
> 📌 **`NaN` is a sub-type of `NA`**: `is.na(NaN) = TRUE`, but `is.nan(NA) = FALSE`.
> 
> 📌 **`NULL` is the absence of an R object**, length 0, its own type.
> 
> 📌 **`Inf` and `-Inf` are valid doubles** produced by division by zero or overflow.
> 
> 📌 **`1/0 = Inf`**, **`0/0 = NaN`**, **`Inf - Inf = NaN`**, **`sqrt(-1) = NaN` with a warning**.
> 
> 📌 **R has no single-precision `float`** — `numeric` = IEEE 754 double (8 bytes).
> 
> 📌 **In R, every value is a vector** — even a scalar is a vector of length 1.
> 
> 📌 **Homogeneity rule:** A vector can hold only ONE data type; mixed vectors are auto-coerced to the highest type present.
> 
> 📌 **The `as.*` family** is used for explicit conversion: `as.numeric`, `as.integer`, `as.character`, `as.logical`, `as.complex`, `as.raw`.
> 
> 📌 **The `is.*` family** is used for type testing: `is.numeric`, `is.integer`, `is.double`, `is.character`, `is.logical`, `is.complex`, `is.raw`, `is.null`, `is.na`, `is.nan`, `is.infinite`, `is.finite`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vectors - Vector Operations and Factor Vectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vectors - Vector Operations and Factor Vectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vector in R? (Formal Definition)

In the R programming language, a **vector** is the most fundamental and atomic data structure. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Data Visualization and Programming with R*, a vector is formally defined as:

> **Vector:** A **one-dimensional, homogeneous data structure** that stores a sequence of elements of the **same data type** (numeric, character, logical, integer, or complex) arranged in a linear order. Vectors are the *building blocks* of all other R objects — even a single scalar (e.g., `x <- 5`) is internally treated as a vector of length 1.

R does **not** have a scalar data type. When you write `5`, R treats it as a numeric vector of length 1. This is a unique and important design philosophy of R that sets it apart from languages like C, Java, or Python.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** "Everything in R is an object, and everything in an object is a vector." — This is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU ESE papers.

### Types of Atomic Vectors

| Vector Type | Function Used | Example | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Numeric (Double)** | `c()`, `numeric()` | `c(1.5, 2.8, 3.1)` | Default numeric type in R; stores real numbers |
| **Integer** | `c()`, `integer()`, `L` suffix | `c(1L, 2L, 3L)` | Whole numbers; saves memory |
| **Character** | `c()`, `character()` | `c("a", "b")` | Stores text strings |
| **Logical** | `c()`, `logical()` | `c(TRUE, FALSE)` | Boolean values |
| **Complex** | `c()`, `complex()` | `c(1+2i, 3+4i)` | Stores complex numbers |

## 1.2 What is a Factor Vector?

A **factor** is a special type of vector in R used to represent **categorical data** (data that takes on a limited number of discrete values, such as "Male"/"Female", "Low"/"Medium"/"High"). Internally, factors store the data as **integers** with associated **character labels** (called *levels*).

> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** A factor is an R object built on top of an integer vector with two additional attributes: a `class` attribute set to `"factor"` and a `levels` attribute that defines the set of permissible categories.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯

### Analogy for Vectors: A Train with Identical Wagons 🚂
Imagine a **train** as a vector. Each **wagon** of the train carries exactly the **same type of cargo** — either all coal wagons, all passenger wagons, or all container wagons. You can never mix coal wagons and passenger wagons in the same train.

- **Length of the vector** = number of wagons
- **Type of the vector** = type of cargo (numeric, character, logical)
- **Indexing** = the position number painted on each wagon
- **Vectorization** = a single instruction applied simultaneously to all wagons (e.g., "add 10 kg of coal to *every* wagon")

### Analogy for Factors: A T-Shirt Size Chart 👕
Think of a factor as a **T-shirt inventory** in a clothing store. The store has T-shirts in only 3 sizes: **S**, **M**, **L**. But the actual stock count might be 50 S-shirts, 30 M-shirts, and 20 L-shirts.

- The **data** (50, 30, 20) are stored internally as integers (1, 2, 3)
- The **levels** (S, M, L) are the character labels
- R knows the allowed categories are only S, M, L — even if the warehouse gets a new shipment of XL shirts, you must explicitly add XL to the levels
- Factors are memory-efficient: storing `"Male"` and `"Female"` 1 million times is expensive, but storing `1` and `2` with level labels is cheap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Factors Matter in Data Science:** Statistical models in R (like `lm()` for linear regression) and visualization libraries (like `ggplot2`) treat factors specially. They automatically perform **group-wise analysis** and produce correctly separated plots/legends. Forgetting to convert a character column to a factor is one of the most common causes of incorrect statistical output in R.

## 1.4 Why Vectors are the Heart of R

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept — Vectorization:** R is designed so that most operations work on entire vectors at once **without explicit loops**. This makes R code concise, fast, and mathematically elegant. A single statement like `c(1,2,3) * 2` multiplies every element by 2 in a single operation.

**The Three Pillars of R's Vector Design:**
1. **Homogeneity** — One type per vector (atomic)
2. **Recycling** — Operations between vectors of unequal lengths "recycle" the shorter one
3. **Coercion** — When types are mixed, R silently promotes to the most flexible type (logical → integer → numeric → character)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Vector Operations

### Pillar 1: Vector Creation Functions

R provides multiple ways to create vectors. Each function has specific use-cases that frequently appear in KTU exams.

| Function | Syntax | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `c()` | `c(x, y, ...)` | **Concatenate** elements into a vector | `c(10, 20, 30)` |
| `:` (Colon Operator) | `from:to` | Generate a sequence with step 1 | `1:10` |
| `seq()` | `seq(from, to, by, length.out)` | Generate a custom sequence | `seq(0, 1, by=0.25)` |
| `rep()` | `rep(x, times, each, length.out)` | Replicate elements | `rep(c(1,2), times=3)` |
| `numeric(n)` | `numeric(5)` | Create zero-filled numeric vector of length n | `numeric(5)` → `c(0,0,0,0,0)` |
| `vector()` | `vector(mode, length)` | Generic vector creator | `vector("character", 3)` |

### Pillar 2: Vector Indexing & Subsetting

Indexing is the **gateway to data manipulation** in R. R uses **1-based indexing** (the first element is at position 1, unlike Python/C which use 0-based).

$$v_{indexed} = v[i] \quad \text{where } i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, \text{length}(v)\}$$

**Five Indexing Strategies:**

1. **Positive Integer indexing** — Select elements by position
   ```r
   v <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
   v[c(1, 3)]   # Returns c(10, 30)
   ```

2. **Negative integer indexing** — *Exclude* elements by position
   ```r
   v[-c(1, 3)]  # Returns c(20, 40, 50)
   ```

3. **Logical indexing** — Select where condition is `TRUE`
   ```r
   v[v > 25]    # Returns c(30, 40, 50)
   ```

4. **Name-based indexing** — Use named vector elements
   ```r
   scores <- c(Ram=85, Sham=90, Geeta=95)
   scores["Ram"]   # Returns 85
   ```

5. **Out-of-range / empty indexing**
   ```r
   v[10]        # Returns NA (out of range)
   v[0]         # Returns numeric(0) — empty vector
   ```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Alert:** Negative and logical indexing cannot be mixed in the same operation. `v[c(-1, v>20)]` will throw an error. This is a classic 3-mark trick question.

### Pillar 3: Vectorized Arithmetic Operations

When you perform arithmetic between two vectors (or a vector and a scalar), R applies the operation **element-wise** in parallel — not as matrix multiplication.

$$z_i = x_i \odot y_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where $\odot \in \{+, -, \times, /, \hat{}, \text{mod}\}$

| Operation | R Expression | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Element-wise addition | `x + y` | `c(1,2) + c(3,4)` | `c(4, 6)` |
| Element-wise multiplication | `x * y` | `c(1,2) * c(3,4)` | `c(3, 8)` |
| Scalar broadcasting | `x * k` | `c(1,2,3) * 10` | `c(10, 20, 30)` |
| Power | `x ^ n` | `c(2,3) ^ 2` | `c(4, 9)` |
| Modulo | `x %% m` | `c(7,8) %% 3` | `c(1, 2)` |
| Integer division | `x %/% m` | `c(7,8) %/% 3` | `c(2, 2)` |

### Pillar 4: The Recycling Rule ♻️

When two vectors of **unequal length** are combined, R **recycles** the shorter vector by repeating it from the beginning.

**Mathematical Statement of Recycling Rule:**
$$\text{Let } \vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m) \text{ and } \vec{b} = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n)$$

If $m \neq n$, the operation is valid **only if** the longer length is a **multiple of the shorter length** (or vice versa). R repeats the shorter vector to match.

$$z_i = a_{(i \bmod m)} + b_{(i \bmod n)} \quad \text{(with recycling)}$$

**Example:**
```r
c(1, 2, 3, 4) + c(10, 20)
# Recycled as: c(1,2,3,4) + c(10,20,10,20)
# Result:     c(11, 22, 13, 24)
```

> [!WARNING]
> If the longer length is **not a multiple** of the shorter length, R throws a warning but still performs the operation: *"longer object length is not a multiple of shorter object length"*. This is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

### Pillar 5: Logical Operations on Vectors

Logical operators return a logical vector of the same length as the input.

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `&` | Element-wise AND | `c(T,T,F) & c(T,F,F)` | `c(T, F, F)` |
| `\|` | Element-wise OR | `c(T,F,F) \| c(F,F,T)` | `c(T, F, T)` |
| `!` | Element-wise NOT | `!c(T,F,T)` | `c(F, T, F)` |
| `&&`, `\|\|` | Scalar AND/OR (first element only) | `c(T,F) && c(F,T)` | `F` |
| `any()` | TRUE if any element is TRUE | `any(c(F,F,T))` | `TRUE` |
| `all()` | TRUE if all elements are TRUE | `all(c(T,T,F))` | `FALSE` |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet: Vector Operations Cheat Sheet

| Category | Formula / Syntax | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length | $\text{length}(v) = n$ | Number of elements | `length(c(1,2,3)) = 3` |
| Sum | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ | Sum of all elements | `sum(c(1,2,3)) = 6` |
| Mean | $\bar{v} = \frac{1}{n}\sum v_i$ | Arithmetic mean | `mean(c(2,4,6)) = 4` |
| Variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(v_i - \bar{v})^2$ | Sample variance | `var(c(1,2,3,4,5))` |
| Sorting | $\text{sort}(v)$ | Ascending sort | `sort(c(3,1,2)) = c(1,2,3)` |
| Cumulative Sum | $S_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} v_i$ | Running total | `cumsum(c(1,2,3)) = c(1,3,6)` |
| Element-wise Sum | $z_i = a_i + b_i$ | Vectorized addition | `c(1,2) + c(3,4) = c(4,6)` |
| Dot Product | $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \sum a_i b_i$ | Inner product | `sum(a * b)` |
| Recycling | $\vec{b}_{recycled} = (b_1, \ldots, b_n, b_1, \ldots)$ | Repeat shorter vector | `c(1,2,3) + c(10,20) = c(11,22,13)` |
| Factor Creation | $f = \text{factor}(x)$ | Convert vector to factor | `factor(c("A","B","A"))` |
| Factor Levels | $L = \text{levels}(f)$ | Distinct categories | `levels(f) = c("A","B")` |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility of Vector Operations

1. **Signal Processing (ECE)**: Audio signals are stored as numeric vectors. Operations like `signal * gain` apply amplification to all samples simultaneously.
2. **Sensor Data Analysis (Mechanical/Civil)**: Temperature readings from IoT sensors are time-series vectors. `cumsum()` integrates signals; `diff()` differentiates.
3. **Statistical Modeling (All Branches)**: Linear regression $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ is solved using vectorized matrix operations.
4. **Image Processing**: A grayscale image is a matrix of pixel intensities. Vectors represent rows/columns; vector operations enable filtering, thresholding, and edge detection.
5. **Database Operations**: Loading CSV columns into vectors enables fast filtering, aggregation, and transformation — the foundation of data analytics.
6. **Factor Application in Surveys**: Survey responses (Likert scale: Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree) are factors enabling grouped statistical analysis and ordered visualizations.

## 2.4 Deep Theory: Factor Vectors — Order, Levels, and Labels

A factor has three critical properties:

$$\text{Factor} = (\text{Integer Vector}, \text{Levels Attribute}, \text{Class Attribute})$$

### Types of Factors

| Factor Type | Creation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Factor** | `factor(c("Red","Blue"))` | Unordered categories (colors, names) |
| **Ordered Factor** | `factor(c("Low","High"), ordered=TRUE, levels=c("Low","High"))` | Ordinal data (rankings, education levels) |
| **Numeric Factor** | `factor(c(1,2,1), labels=c("M","F"))` | Categories with numeric input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ordered vs Unordered Factors:** In **ordered factors**, comparisons like `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` are meaningful. R uses the order of the `levels` argument. In **nominal factors**, only equality `==` and inequality `!=` are valid. This distinction is critical for ANOVA and ordinal regression models.

### `tapply()` — The Power Function for Factors

`tapply(X, INDEX, FUN)` applies a function `FUN` to vector `X` grouped by factor `INDEX`.

$$\text{tapply}(X, \text{Factor}, \text{mean}) = \{\bar{x}_1, \bar{x}_2, \ldots, \bar{x}_k\}$$

This is the conceptual equivalent of SQL's `GROUP BY` clause.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Vector Creation, Indexing, and Operations

**Problem:** Given the following R code, predict the output and explain each step.

```r
# Step 1: Create vectors
x <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)
y <- 1:5

# Step 2: Vectorized arithmetic
z <- x + y^2

# Step 3: Logical indexing
filtered <- x[x > 5]

# Step 4: Apply function
total <- sum(z)
mean_val <- mean(filtered)
```

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Vector Creation:**

$$x = (2, 4, 6, 8, 10) \quad \text{length} = 5$$

$$y = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) \quad \text{(using colon operator 1:5)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $y^2$ (vectorized squaring):**

$$y^2 = (1^2, 2^2, 3^2, 4^2, 5^2) = (1, 4, 9, 16, 25)$$

**Step 3 — Element-wise addition $z = x + y^2$:**

$$z_i = x_i + y_i^2$$

$$z = (2+1, \; 4+4, \; 6+9, \; 8+16, \; 10+25)$$

$$z = (3, 8, 15, 24, 35)$$

**Step 4 — Logical indexing `x > 5`:**

$$x > 5 \rightarrow (F, F, T, T, T)$$

**Step 5 — Filter `x[x > 5]`:**

$$\text{filtered} = x[\text{where } x_i > 5] = (6, 8, 10)$$

**Step 6 — Aggregate functions:**

$$\text{total} = \sum z_i = 3 + 8 + 15 + 24 + 35 = 85$$

$$\text{mean\_val} = \frac{1}{3}\sum \text{filtered}_i = \frac{6+8+10}{3} = \frac{24}{3} = 8$$

### Final Output:

```
> z
[1]  3  8 15 24 35
> filtered
[1]  6  8 10
> total
[1] 85
> mean_val
[1] 8
```

## 3.2 Worked Example: Recycling Rule in Action

**Problem:** Compute the result of the following R code and explain the recycling process.

```r
a <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60)
b <- c(1, 2, 3)
result <- a + b
```

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify vector lengths:**

$$\text{length}(a) = 6, \quad \text{length}(b) = 3$$

**Step 2 — Apply recycling (length of `a` is 6, which is 2× the length of `b`):**

The shorter vector `b` is recycled to match the length of `a`:

$$b_{recycled} = (1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3)$$

**Step 3 — Element-wise addition:**

$$r_i = a_i + b_{recycled, i}$$

$$r = (10+1, \; 20+2, \; 30+3, \; 40+1, \; 50+2, \; 60+3)$$

$$r = (11, 22, 33, 41, 52, 63)$$

**Step 4 — Incompatible length case:**

If `a` had length 5 (not a multiple of 3), R would issue a warning:
```r
a <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)  # length 5
a + c(1, 2, 3)              # length 3
# Warning: longer object length is not a multiple of shorter object length
# Result:  (11, 22, 33, 41, 52)  # b recycled once, truncated at length 5
```

## 3.3 Worked Example: Factor Vector — Creation, Levels, and tapply

**Problem:** A survey records the blood groups of 10 patients along with their ages. Perform factor-based grouping analysis.

```r
# Step 1: Create data
age <- c(25, 30, 22, 40, 35, 28, 50, 45, 33, 27)
blood_group <- c("A", "B", "O", "A", "AB", "O", "B", "A", "AB", "O")

# Step 2: Convert to factor
bg_factor <- factor(blood_group)

# Step 3: Inspect factor
print(levels(bg_factor))
print(table(bg_factor))

# Step 4: Group-wise mean age
group_means <- tapply(age, bg_factor, mean)
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Data Setup:**

$$\text{age} = (25, 30, 22, 40, 35, 28, 50, 45, 33, 27)$$

$$\text{blood\_group} = (A, B, O, A, AB, O, B, A, AB, O)$$

**Step 2 — Factor Conversion (Internal Representation):**

R sorts the unique values alphabetically and assigns integer codes:

| Level | Integer Code |
|---|---|
| A | 1 |
| AB | 2 |
| B | 3 |
| O | 4 |

$$\text{bg\_factor}_{internal} = (1, 3, 4, 1, 2, 4, 3, 1, 2, 4)$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Table Computation:**

$$n_A = 3, \quad n_{AB} = 2, \quad n_B = 2, \quad n_O = 3$$

**Step 4 — Group-wise Mean (tapply derivation):**

For group A: ages are 25, 40, 45

$$\bar{x}_A = \frac{25 + 40 + 45}{3} = \frac{110}{3} \approx 36.67$$

For group AB: ages are 35, 33

$$\bar{x}_{AB} = \frac{35 + 33}{2} = \frac{68}{2} = 34$$

For group B: ages are 30, 50

$$\bar{x}_B = \frac{30 + 50}{2} = \frac{80}{2} = 40$$

For group O: ages are 22, 28, 27

$$\bar{x}_O = \frac{22 + 28 + 27}{3} = \frac{77}{3} \approx 25.67$$

### Final Output:

```
> levels(bg_factor)
[1] "A"  "AB" "B"  "O"
> table(bg_factor)
bg_factor
 A  AB   B   O 
 3   2   2   3 
> group_means
       A       AB        B        O 
36.66667 34.00000 40.00000 25.66667 
```

| `sum()` | numeric | $\sum x_i$ | numeric | `c(1,2,3)` | `6` |
| `prod()` | numeric | $\prod x_i$ | numeric | `c(1,2,3)` | `6` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coercion Hierarchy (lowest to highest flexibility):** `logical` → `integer` → `numeric` → `complex` → `character`. R always promotes to the **more flexible** type. Mixing `c(1, "a")` produces `c("1", "a")` (character), not numeric.

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a vector in R. Explain the different types of atomic vectors with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A vector in R is a one-dimensional, homogeneous data structure that stores a sequence of elements of the **same data type**. Even a single value is treated as a vector of length 1. R has five atomic vector types:

1. **Numeric (Double):** Default numeric type, stores real numbers.
   ```r
   x <- c(1.5, 2.8, 3.1)
   ```
2. **Integer:** Whole numbers, indicated with `L` suffix.
   ```r
   y <- c(1L, 2L, 3L)
   ```
3. **Character:** Stores text strings enclosed in quotes.
   ```r
   z <- c("apple", "banana")
   ```
4. **Logical:** Boolean values `TRUE` or `FALSE`.
   ```r
   w <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
   ```
5. **Complex:** Stores complex numbers.
   ```r
   v <- c(1+2i, 3+4i)
   ```

All elements in a vector must be of the same type; otherwise, R performs automatic type coercion to the most flexible type.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
