```r
# 'gre' uniquely matches 'greeting'; 'punc' uniquely matches 'punctuation'
# Output: "Welcome Kiran ."
```

**Example of Failed Partial Match:**

```r
greet("Kiran", g = "Hi")
# Error: 'g' is a prefix of both 'greeting' AND ... wait, in this function, 'g' only matches 'greeting'.
# A genuine failure: greet("Kiran", u = TRUE)  -> 'u' is not a prefix of any parameter -> falls through to positional match, causing 'u' to bind positionally to 'punctuation', which fails because TRUE is not a string.
```

In the **second genuine failure scenario**, R will:
- Attempt exact match for `u` → fail.
- Attempt partial match for `u` → fail (no parameter starts with `u`).
- Fall through to positional match → bind `u = TRUE` to `punctuation` position.
- Result: `paste("Hello", "Kiran", "TRUE", sep = " ")` → `"Hello Kiran TRUE"`.

> [!NOTE]
> This silent fall-through to positional matching is a **classic KTU trap**. Students who rely on partial matching without verifying uniqueness may introduce **silent bugs** that produce wrong outputs rather than errors.

*(Allocation: 2 marks for conditions, 2 marks for successful example, 2 marks for failure example, 1 mark for the silent-fallthrough insight.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Named arguments** use `param = value` syntax; `=` is mandatory inside function calls (never `<-`).
- **Matching order in R:** Exact name → Partial (prefix) name → Positional → Default fallback.
- **Default arguments** make parameters optional and improve API flexibility.
- **`formals(f)`** returns the formal parameter list with defaults — useful for introspection.
- **`missing(x)`** detects whether an argument was *supplied* (distinct from its value).
- **Partial matching** requires *uniqueness* of the prefix; ambiguous prefixes raise an error.
- **Named + Positional mixing** is legal *only* if all positional arguments come *before* named ones.
- **R uses lazy evaluation** — arguments are evaluated only when accessed inside the function body.
- **The `...` (dot-dot-dot)** argument is used to forward variable arguments to inner functions (e.g., `plot(...)`).
- **Common exam pitfall:** Using `<-` instead of `=` in function calls (lose 0.5–1 mark).
- **Common exam pitfall:** Assuming partial matching is always safe — it silently falls through to positional if no prefix matches.
- **Best practice (KTU-favored):** Always use **named arguments** for any function with more than 2 parameters.
- **Performance note:** Named arguments carry *zero runtime overhead* in R — they are resolved at call-construction time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importing Data from Text Files and Other Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Importing Data from Text Files and Other Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Import** in the context of the R programming language is formally defined as the process of reading, parsing, and loading external data — stored in heterogeneous file formats (text, CSV, Excel, SPSS, SAS, Stata, databases, web APIs, JSON, XML) — into the R workspace as an in-memory R object (typically a `data.frame` or `tibble`) suitable for statistical analysis, modelling, and visualization.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this is classified under the **Data Acquisition Phase** of the data analysis pipeline: `Import → Tidy → Transform → Visualize → Model → Communicate` (the Wickham/Grolemund *R for Data Science* paradigm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The function family `read.table()`, `read.csv()`, `read.delim()`, `read.fwf()`, `scan()` is **explicitly named** in the PBCDT604 Module 4 syllabus. Importing from SPSS, SAS, Minitab, Stata, and Excel using the `foreign` and `haven` packages is also a high-yield topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Think of R as a **master chef's kitchen**. Before the chef can cook any dish (perform analysis or visualization), the kitchen must first **receive raw ingredients** from suppliers. These ingredients come in different packaging:
> - **Plastic crates** → CSV files (`.csv`)
> - **Cardboard boxes** → Tab-delimited text files (`.txt`)
> - **Sealed jars** → Excel files (`.xlsx`)
> - **Labelled cans** → SPSS/SAS/Stata files (`.sav`, `.sas7bdat`, `.dta`)
>
> Each package type needs a **specific unwrapping tool** — a different knife, can-opener, or jar-lid remover. In R, these tools are functions like `read.csv()`, `read_excel()`, `read_sav()`, etc. The output is always the same: a clean tray of fresh ingredients (a `data.frame`) ready for the chef (your code) to use.

**Key R Object Created:** `data.frame` — a rectangular, column-oriented data structure where each column is a vector of the same length but potentially different types (numeric, character, factor, Date).

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Working Directory Convention:** R reads files relative to the **working directory**. Always verify with `getwd()` and set with `setwd("path/to/folder")`. Best practice in KTU labs is to use `file.choose()` for interactive selection or to place files in the project root.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `read.table()` Family — Architectural Foundation

The `read.table()` function is the **parent function** from which all other delimited readers (`read.csv`, `read.delim`) are derived. Understanding it is the "Why" behind every other import function.

#### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Steps
1. **Open a file connection** to the specified path or URL.
2. **Read the first line** to determine the number of columns (when `header = TRUE`, the first row becomes column names).
3. **Scan subsequent lines**, splitting each by the `sep` delimiter.
4. **Type-coerce** each column based on its content (`read.table` defaults to `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` in R ≥ 4.0).
5. **Handle `NA` strings** as specified by the `na.strings` argument.
6. **Return** a `data.frame`.

#### 2.1.2 Common Arguments (All Variants Share These)

| Argument | Default | Purpose | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| `file` | — (required) | Path, URL, or connection | `"data/sales.csv"` |
| `header` | `FALSE` (for `read.table`); `TRUE` for `read.csv`/`read.delim` | First row = column names? | `TRUE` |
| `sep` | `""` (whitespace) | Field delimiter | `","`, `"\t"`, `";"` |
| `dec` | `"."` | Decimal point character | `"."` or `","` |
| `quote` | `"\""` | Quoting character | `'\"'` or `'` |
| `stringsAsFactors` | `FALSE` (R 4.0+) | Coerce strings to factors? | `FALSE` |
| `na.strings` | `"NA"` | Characters to interpret as missing | `c("NA","-","?")` |
| `nrows` | `-1` (all) | Max rows to read | `1000` |
| `skip` | `0` | Number of lines to skip | `2` |
| `fill` | `FALSE` | Pad short rows with `NA`? | `TRUE` |
| `comment.char` | `""` | Lines beginning with this are ignored | `"#"` |
| `encoding` | `"unknown"` | File encoding | `"UTF-8"` |

### 2.2 The Specialized Variants

```r
# Generic - whitespace separated, no header by default
read.table("data.txt", header = TRUE)

# Comma-Separated Values (default header = TRUE, sep = ",")
read.csv("data.csv")

# European CSV (semicolon separator, comma decimal)
read.csv2("data.csv")

# Tab-Delimited
read.delim("data.txt")

# European tab-delimited
read.delim2("data.txt")

# Fixed-Width Files
read.fwf("data.txt", widths = c(10, 8, 5, 12))

# Scan - lowest level, returns a vector
scan("data.txt", what = "", sep = "\n")
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| File Format | R Function | Package | Output Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| `.csv` (comma) | `read.csv("f.csv")` | `utils` (base) | `data.frame` |
| `.csv` (semicolon) | `read.csv2("f.csv")` | `utils` (base) | `data.frame` |
| `.txt` (tab) | `read.delim("f.txt")` | `utils` (base) | `data.frame` |
| `.txt` (any delim) | `read.table("f.txt", sep="\t")` | `utils` (base) | `data.frame` |
| `.txt` (fixed-width) | `read.fwf("f.txt", widths=c(...))` | `utils` (base) | `data.frame` |
| `.xlsx`, `.xls` | `read_excel("f.xlsx")` | `readxl` | `tibble` |
| `.sav` (SPSS) | `read_sav("f.sav")` | `haven` | `tibble` |
| `.por` (SPSS portable) | `read_por("f.por")` | `haven` | `tibble` |
| `.sas7bdat` (SAS) | `read_sas("f.sas7bdat")` | `haven` | `tibble` |
| `.dta` (Stata) | `read_dta("f.dta")` | `haven` | `tibble` |
| `.syd`, `.syp` (Systat) | `read.systat("f.syd")` | `foreign` | `data.frame` |
| `.mtp`, `.mtw` (Minitab) | `read.mtp("f.mtp")` | `foreign` | `data.frame` |
| `.dbf` (DBase) | `read.dbf("f.dbf")` | `foreign` | `data.frame` |
| JSON | `fromJSON("f.json")` | `jsonlite` | `list`/`data.frame` |
| XML | `xmlToDataFrame("f.xml")` | `XML` | `data.frame` |
| SQL DB | `dbReadTable(con, "table")` | `DBI`/`RODBC`/`RMySQL` | `data.frame` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production engineering and data analytics pipelines at companies like TCS, Infosys, and Bosch (recruiters active in KTU placements), data ingestion is the **first bottleneck**. Engineers routinely:
- Ingest **IoT sensor CSV logs** for predictive maintenance.
- Pull **Excel MIS reports** from shop-floor supervisors.
- Connect to **SQL Server/PostgreSQL** manufacturing databases.
- Import **SPSS clinical trial datasets** (common in biomedical engineering projects at SCTIMST, Trivandrum).
- Pull **JSON data from REST APIs** (weather data, stock prices for engineering economics projects).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hands-on Walkthrough:** Below is a complete, executable analytical matrix covering the four most-tested import scenarios in the KTU lab exam.

### 3.1 Scenario A — Reading a CSV File

**Step 1: Create the data file** `sales.csv`
```
Month,Sales,Profit
Jan,12000,3000
Feb,15000,4200
Mar,18000,5100
Apr,14000,3800
```

**Step 2: Set working directory and import**
```r
# Verify current working directory
getwd()
#> [1] "C:/Users/Student/Documents"

# Set new working directory (where the CSV resides)
setwd("C:/Users/Student/Documents/R_Lab_Module4")

# Read the file using read.csv()
sales_data <- read.csv("sales.csv")

# Inspect the result
print(sales_data)
print(class(sales_data))
print(str(sales_data))
```

**Step 3: Verify output**
```
  Month Sales Profit
1   Jan 12000   3000
2   Feb 15000   4200
3   Mar 18000   5100
4   Apr 14000   3800

[1] "data.frame"

'data.frame':    4 obs. of  3 variables:
 $ Month : chr  "Jan" "Feb" "Mar" "Apr"
 $ Sales : int  12000 15000 18000 14000
 $ Profit: int  3000 4200 5100 3800
```

**Step 4: Argument-tuning example with NA handling**
```r
# Suppose the file has -99 and ? representing missing values
sales_data2 <- read.csv("sales_dirty.csv",
                        header = TRUE,
                        sep = ",",
                        na.strings = c("NA", "-99", "?", ""),
                        stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

### 3.2 Scenario B — Reading a Tab-Delimited Text File

**Step 1: File `students.txt`**
```
RollNo	Name	Marks	Grade
101	Anand	85	A
102	Beena	92	A
103	Chitra	78	B
```

**Step 2: Import using `read.delim()`**
```r
students <- read.delim("students.txt",
                       header = TRUE,
                       sep = "\t",
                       stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Alternative equivalent using read.table()
students2 <- read.table("students.txt",
                        header = TRUE,
                        sep = "\t",
                        stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

identical(students, students2)
#> [1] TRUE
```

### 3.3 Scenario C — Reading a Fixed-Width File (`.fwf`)

**Step 1: File `addresses.txt`** (each field has fixed character width)
```
Kerala     Trivandrum695001
Karnataka  Bangalore 560001
TamilNadu  Chennai   600001
```

**Step 2: Specify widths and read**
```r
# Field widths: State=10, City=10, PIN=6
addresses <- read.fwf("addresses.txt",
                      widths = c(10, 10, 6),
                      header = FALSE,
                      col.names = c("State", "City", "PIN"))

print(addresses)
```
```
      State      City   PIN
1    Kerala Trivandrum 695001
2 Karnataka  Bangalore 560001
3  TamilNadu    Chennai 600001
```

### 3.4 Scenario D — Importing from SPSS, Excel, and SQL

**Excel (`.xlsx`) using `readxl`:**
```r
# Install once: install.packages("readxl")
library(readxl)

excel_data <- read_excel("sensor_readings.xlsx",
                         sheet = 1,             # Sheet number
                         col_names = TRUE,
                         na = "NA")

# Or specify a range
excel_data2 <- read_excel("sensor_readings.xlsx",
                          range = "A1:D100")
```

**SPSS (`.sav`) using `haven`:**
```r
# Install once: install.packages("haven")
library(haven)

spss_data <- read_sav("clinical_trial.sav")

# SPSS labelled values are preserved as 'haven_labelled' class
# Convert to factors for analysis:
spss_data$gender <- as_factor(spss_data$gender)
```

**SAS using `haven`:**
```r
library(haven)
sas_data <- read_sas("patient_cohort.sas7bdat")
# For .xpt (XPORT) files:
sas_xpt <- read_xpt("transport_file.xpt")
```

**Stata using `haven`:**
```r
library(haven)
stata_data <- read_dta("survey_results.dta")
```

**MySQL Database using `DBI` + `RMySQL`:**
```r
# Install once:
# install.packages(c("DBI", "RMySQL"))

library(DBI)
con <- dbConnect(RMySQL::MySQL(),
                 dbname = "manufacturing_db",
                 host   = "localhost",
                 port   = 3306,
                 user   = "root",
                 password = "your_password")

# Method 1: Run a query and get a data.frame
db_data <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM machine_logs WHERE date >= '2024-01-01'")

# Method 2: Read a whole table
full_table <- dbReadTable(con, "machine_logs")

# Always close the connection
dbDisconnect(con)
```

### 3.5 Connecting to URLs Directly

R can import over the network without downloading:
```r
# Read directly from a URL
url_data <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/datasets/covid-19/master/data/countries-aggregated.csv")

# Compressed files are auto-decompressed
gz_data <- read.csv(gzfile("large_file.csv.gz"))
```

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Error → Cause → Fix

| Common Error | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `cannot open file 'X.csv'` | Wrong working directory | `setwd()` or use full path |
| All columns are `chr` | Decimals use `,` not `.` | Set `dec = ","` |
| Column names become data | File lacks header | Set `header = FALSE` |
| String with commas splits columns | `sep` mismatch or quoting issue | Use `sep = ","` or specify `quote` |
| `embedded nul in string` | Encoding mismatch | Set `encoding = "UTF-8"` or `fileEncoding = "latin1"` |
| `more columns than column names` | Missing `fill = TRUE` | Add `fill = TRUE` |
| Factors created unintentionally | Older R or `stringsAsFactors = TRUE` | Set `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Import Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Has External Data] --> B{File Format?}
    B -->|CSV / TXT| C{Delimiter Type}
    B -->|XLSX / XLS| D[read_excel from readxl]
    B -->|SAV / SAS7BDAT / DTA| E[haven Package Functions]
    B -->|SQL Database| F[DBI plus RMySQL or RODBC]
    B -->|JSON| G[jsonlite Package]
    B -->|XML| H[XML Package]
    B -->|Fixed Width| I[read.fwf]
    B -->|Any Custom Format| J[scan or readLines]

    C -->|Comma| K[read.csv]
    C -->|Semicolon| L[read.csv2]
    C -->|Tab| M[read.delim]
    C -->|Whitespace| N[read.table]

    D --> O[Function reads file into tibble]
    E --> O
    F --> O
    G --> O
    H --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O

    O --> P{Validate Data}
    P -->|Check str head dim| Q[Clean and Type Coerce]
    P -->|Issues Found| R[Adjust arguments and re-read]
    R --> O
    Q --> S[Ready for Analysis and Visualization]
```

### 4.2 R Data Import Architecture (Layered Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Layer1[Layer 1 - Base R utils package]
        A1[read.table]
        A2[read.csv]
        A3[read.delim]
        A4[read.fwf]
        A5[scan]
        A6[readLines]
    end

    subgraph Layer2[Layer 2 - Tidyverse]
        B1[readr read_csv]
        B2[readr read_tsv]
        B3[readr read_delim]
    end

    subgraph Layer3[Layer 3 - Specialized Format Packages]
        C1[readxl - Excel]
        C2[haven - SPSS SAS Stata]
        C3[foreign - Minitab Systat DBF]
        C4[jsonlite - JSON]
        C5[XML - XML]
    end

    subgraph Layer4[Layer 4 - Database Connectors]
        D1[DBI plus RSQLite]
        D2[DBI plus RMySQL]
        D3[DBI plus RPostgreSQL]
        D4[RODBC - SQL Server Oracle]
    end

    Layer1 --> E[In Memory data.frame or tibble]
    Layer2 --> E
    Layer3 --> E
    Layer4 --> E
    E --> F[Ready for dplyr ggplot2 modeling]
```

### 4.3 Process Sequence Diagram for CSV Import

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant RConsole
    participant FileSystem
    participant RMemory

    User->>RConsole: getwd() and setwd(path)
    RConsole->>FileSystem: Verify directory exists
    FileSystem-->>RConsole: Confirmed
    User->>RConsole: read.csv("data.csv", header=TRUE)
    RConsole->>FileSystem: Open file connection
    FileSystem-->>RConsole: Stream raw bytes
    RConsole->>RConsole: Parse header into column names
    RConsole->>RConsole: Split rows by comma delimiter
    RConsole->>RConsole: Type coerce each column
    RConsole->>RConsole: Replace NA strings
    RConsole->>RMemory: Allocate data.frame
    RMemory-->>RConsole: Object handle returned
    RConsole-->>User: data.frame printed or assigned
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q1.** Differentiate between `read.csv()` and `read.csv2()` in R. When would you use each?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> `read.csv()` assumes the default **English/Anglo-American** format with `sep = ","` (comma) and `dec = "."` (period as decimal). `read.csv2()` is its **European** counterpart with `sep = ";"` (semicolon) and `dec = ","` (comma as decimal). Use `read.csv()` for data exported from US/UK Excel installations and `read.csv2()` for data exported from German/French/Indian Excel installations where the OS locale uses commas as decimals.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q2.** Explain the role of the `na.strings` argument in `read.table()`. Provide an example.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The `na.strings` argument is a **character vector** of strings that should be interpreted as missing values (`NA`) when reading the file. By default, only `"NA"` is recognized.
> Example:
> ```r
> data <- read.table("sensor_log.txt", header = TRUE,
+                    na.strings = c("NA", "-99", "?", "missing"))
> ```
> Here, occurrences of `-99`, `?`, or `missing` in the file will be converted to `NA` in the resulting `data.frame`, allowing correct statistical treatment of missing data.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

#### Question A — *Option (a)* and *Option (b)*

**Q.A (a)** [7 Marks]: Explain the various functions available in R for importing data from text files. Compare `read.table()`, `read.csv()`, and `read.delim()` with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The `read.table()` Family Overview:**

The `read.table()` family in R's base `utils` package provides a uniform interface for reading rectangular data from text files. All variants share the same underlying engine but differ in default argument values.

**Step 2 — `read.table()` — The Generic Reader:**

It is the **parent function** with the most general defaults. Defaults: `header = FALSE`, `sep = ""` (any whitespace), `dec = "."`.

```r
# Sample file: data.txt (whitespace-separated, no header)
# 25 Male 78.5
# 30 Female 88.0
# 22 Male 65.2

raw_data <- read.table("data.txt",
                       header = FALSE,
                       col.names = c("Age", "Gender", "Score"))
print(raw_data)
```

**Step 3 — `read.csv()` — Comma-Separated Values:**

Specifically designed for `.csv` files. Defaults: `header = TRUE`, `sep = ","`, `dec = "."`.

```r
# Sample file: marks.csv
# Name,Math,Science,English
# Arun,85,90,78
# Beena,92,88,95

csv_data <- read.csv("marks.csv")
print(csv_data)
```

**Step 4 — `read.delim()` — Tab-Delimited Values:**

Designed for tab-separated files. Defaults: `header = TRUE`, `sep = "\t"`, `dec = "."`.

```r
# Sample file: lab_results.txt (tab-separated)
# Patient_ID  Test_Name  Result_Value
# P001       Hemoglobin  14.2
# P002       Glucose     105.5

delim_data <- read.delim("lab_results.txt")
print(delim_data)
```

**Step 5 — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | `read.table()` | `read.csv()` | `read.delim()` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default `header` | `FALSE` | `TRUE` | `TRUE` |
| Default `sep` | `""` (whitespace) | `","` | `"\t"` |
| Best for | Custom-delimited files | CSV files | TSV files |
| Speed | Slower (auto-detect) | Fast | Fast |

---

**Q.A (b)** [7 Marks]: Describe how to import data from Excel, SPSS, and SAS files into R. Mention the R packages and functions used, and provide syntax examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Excel Files (`.xlsx` / `.xls`):**

The `readxl` package (part of tidyverse) reads Excel files **without requiring Java** or system Excel installation.

```r
# Install once
install.packages("readxl")
library(readxl)

# Read first sheet by default
excel_data <- read_excel("production_log.xlsx")

# Read a specific sheet
quality_data <- read_excel("production_log.xlsx", sheet = "Quality")

# Read a cell range
range_data  <- read_excel("production_log.xlsx", range = "B2:E100")

# Read with explicit NA specification
sensor_data <- read_excel("sensors.xlsx", na = c("NA", "ERR", "-"))
```

**Step 2 — SPSS Files (`.sav`):**

The `haven` package (also part of tidyverse, developed by Hadley Wickham's team) reads SPSS files while preserving variable **labels** and **value labels** as `haven_labelled` class.

```r
install.packages("haven")
library(haven)

# Read SPSS .sav file
spss_data <- read_sav("patient_survey.sav")

# Inspect labelled variables
attr(spss_data$gender, "labels")

# Convert labelled variables to factors
spss_data$gender <- as_factor(spss_data$gender)
spss_data$treatment_group <- as_factor(spss_data$treatment_group)
```

**Step 3 — SAS Files (`.sas7bdat` / `.xpt`):**

```r
library(haven)

# Native SAS data file
sas_data <- read_sas("clinical_trial.sas7bdat")

# SAS XPORT transport file
xpt_data <- read_xpt("vitals.xpt")

# Optionally read only specific columns (efficient for large files)
library(haven)
sas_efficient <- read_sas("big_sas.sas7bdat",
                          col_select = c("patient_id", "age", "outcome"))
```

**Step 4 — Summary Table:**

| Format | Extension | Package | Function | Class Returned |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Excel | `.xlsx`, `.xls` | `readxl` | `read_excel()` | `tibble` |
| SPSS | `.sav` | `haven` | `read_sav()` | `tibble` |
| SAS | `.sas7bdat` | `haven` | `read_sas()` | `tibble` |
| SAS XPORT | `.xpt` | `haven` | `read_xpt()` | `tibble` |
| Stata | `.dta` | `haven` | `read_dta()` | `tibble` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write `read.xlsx()` (the `xlsx` package function) in answers. While technically valid, **always state `readxl::read_excel()` first** as it is the recommended modern choice — it requires **no Java** dependency, unlike `xlsx`/`XLConnect`. Examiners deduct marks for outdated dependencies.

---

#### Question B — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B (a)** [7 Marks]: Explain the function `read.fwf()` with a suitable example. How does it differ from `read.table()`?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**

`read.fwf()` reads **Fixed-Width Format** files where each column occupies a predefined number of characters, **without any delimiter**. This is common in legacy government, banking, and census datasets.

**Step 2 — Syntax:**
```r
read.fwf(file, widths, header = FALSE, sep = "\t",
         col.names, na.strings = "NA", skip = 0)
```

The key argument is `widths` — an integer vector specifying the character width of each field.

**Step 3 — Example:**

Sample file `census.txt`:
```
Kerala     Trivandrum695001India
Karnataka  Bangalore 560001India
TamilNadu  Chennai   600001India
```

```r
census <- read.fwf("census.txt",
                   widths = c(10, 10, 6, 5),
                   header = FALSE,
                   col.names = c("State", "City", "PIN", "Country"))

print(census)
print(str(census))
```

**Step 4 — Difference from `read.table()`:**

| Aspect | `read.table()` | `read.fwf()` |
|---|---|---|
| Delimiter required? | Yes (`sep` argument) | **No** — uses fixed column widths |
| Use case | Delimited files (CSV, TSV) | Fixed-width legacy files |
| Speed | Faster | Slower (column counting) |
| Key argument | `sep` | `widths` |
| Skipping options | `skip`, `nrows` | `skip`, `nrows` |

---

**Q.B (b)** [7 Marks]: Write R code to connect to a MySQL database and import a table named `machine_logs` into a data.frame. Explain the steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Install Required Packages:**
```r
install.packages(c("DBI", "RMySQL"))
```

**Step 2 — Establish Connection:**
```r
library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(RMySQL::MySQL(),
                 dbname = "manufacturing_db",
                 host   = "localhost",
                 port   = 3306,
                 user   = "root",
                 password = "SecurePass123")
```

**Step 3 — List Available Tables (Best Practice):**
```r
dbListTables(con)
#> [1] "machine_logs" "operators" "downtime"
```

**Step 4 — Read Entire Table into a `data.frame`:**
```r
machine_logs_df <- dbReadTable(con, "machine_logs")
print(head(machine_logs_df))
print(dim(machine_logs_df))
```

**Step 5 — Alternative: Run a SQL Query (Preferred for Filtering):**
```r
recent_logs <- dbGetQuery(con,
                          "SELECT machine_id, timestamp, temperature
                           FROM machine_logs
                           WHERE timestamp >= '2024-01-01'
                           ORDER BY timestamp DESC
                           LIMIT 1000")
```

**Step 6 — Always Disconnect:**
```r
dbDisconnect(con)
```

**Step 7 — Key Notes:**
- `DBI` (Database Interface) provides the abstraction layer.
- `RMySQL` is the MySQL-specific driver.
- `dbGetQuery()` returns a `data.frame` directly.
- `dbReadTable()` reads a full table.
- For large tables, use chunked reading with `dbSendQuery()` + `dbFetch()` + `dbClearResult()`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Forgetting `dbDisconnect(con)` → resource leak, **−1 mark**.
> 2. Writing `library(RMySQL)` without `library(DBI)` → code fails because the driver needs the DBI abstraction layer.
> 3. Hard-coding passwords in scripts is a security antipattern; mention this in theory answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Importing Data in R**

- ✅ **Default Working Directory:** Always verify with `getwd()`; use `setwd()` or full paths.
- ✅ **Core Function Family:** `read.table()`, `read.csv()`, `read.csv2()`, `read.delim()`, `read.delim2()`, `read.fwf()`, `scan()`, `readLines()` — all from base R's `utils` package.
- ✅ **`read.csv` defaults:** `header = TRUE`, `sep = ","`, `dec = "."`, `stringsAsFactors = FALSE`.
- ✅ **`read.csv2` defaults:** `header = TRUE`, `sep = ";"`, `dec = ","` — for European locales.
- ✅ **`read.delim` defaults:** `header = TRUE`, `sep = "\t"`.
- ✅ **`na.strings` argument:** Vector of strings to convert to `NA` (e.g., `c("NA","-99","?")`).
- ✅ **`stringsAsFactors = FALSE`:** Critical in R 4.0+; prevents unwanted factor conversion.
- ✅ **Fixed-Width Files:** Use `read.fwf(file, widths = c(10, 8, 6))`.
- ✅ **Excel Files:** Use `readxl::read_excel()` (no Java dependency preferred over `xlsx` package).
- ✅ **SPSS Files:** Use `haven::read_sav()` — preserves labels.
- ✅ **SAS Files:** Use `haven::read_sas()` for `.sas7bdat`, `haven::read_xpt()` for `.xpt`.
- ✅ **Stata Files:** Use `haven::read_dta()`.
- ✅ **Minitab/Systat/DBF:** Use `foreign` package (legacy but still tested).
- ✅ **Databases:** `DBI` + driver (`RMySQL`, `RSQLite`, `RODBC`) → `dbGetQuery()`, `dbReadTable()`.
- ✅ **JSON:** `jsonlite::fromJSON()` → returns list or data.frame.
- ✅ **URL Import:** R reads HTTP/HTTPS URLs directly with `read.csv("https://...")`.
- ✅ **Compressed Files:** Wrap in `gzfile()`, `bzfile()`, `unz()`.
- ✅ **Output Object Class:** All these functions return a `data.frame` (or `tibble` from tidyverse packages).
- ✅ **First Validation Step After Import:** Run `str()`, `head()`, `dim()`, `summary()`, `class()`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exporting Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exporting Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the R programming ecosystem, **Data Export** refers to the systematic process of writing R objects (such as vectors, data frames, matrices, and lists) from the in-memory workspace to persistent external storage media in standardized, human-readable or machine-readable formats. The reverse operation — reading external files into R — is called *Import*. Together, Import and Export constitute the **I/O (Input/Output)** layer of R programming.

The official R Core Team documentation (`R Data Import/Export` manual) treats data export as a critical boundary operation that decouples the analytical environment (the R session) from the data persistence layer (disk, network, or cloud storage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes the *bridge between R memory and external systems*. Exporting is the **output side** of this bridge — without it, all analysis is volatile and lost when the session terminates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **photographer (the R analyst)** working inside a **darkroom (the R session)**. While you are inside the darkroom, you can develop, edit, and manipulate the photo freely. But the moment you walk out, the photo disappears unless you **print it onto a paper and hand it over to the customer** — that final act of printing is **data export**.

In R terms:
- The **darkroom** = the live R workspace (`globalenv()`)
- The **photographic plate** = your `data.frame`, `matrix`, or `vector`
- The **printed paper / digital file** = the `.csv`, `.xlsx`, `.txt`, `.rds`, or `.json` file on disk
- The **customer** = another software (Excel, Python, a database) or another analyst who needs the data

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Exporting is Non-Trivial:** Choosing the *right* export format is similar to choosing the *right courier service* — you must match the medium (postal, express, refrigerated) to the cargo (documents, liquids, fragile items). The same applies to data: tabular numeric data works best as CSV, hierarchical data as JSON, R-native objects as `.rds`, and Excel-collaborative workflows as `.xlsx`.

### 1.3 The R Export Function Taxonomy

R provides a layered set of functions for data export:

| Layer | Function Family | Output Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Base R (always available)** | `write.table()`, `write.csv()`, `writeLines()`, `cat()` | Plain text / delimited |
| **R-native binary** | `save()`, `saveRDS()` | `.RData`, `.rds` |
| **Tidyverse / modern R** | `readr::write_csv()`, `readr::write_rds()` | Tidy, fast, UTF-8 safe |
| **Excel-specific** | `writexl::write_xlsx()`, `openxlsx::write.xlsx()` | `.xlsx` |
| **Statistical software** | `foreign::write.dta()`, `haven::write_sas()`, `haven::write_sav()` | Stata, SAS, SPSS |
| **Web / API formats** | `jsonlite::toJSON()`, `jsonlite::write_json()`, `xml2::write_xml()` | JSON, XML |
| **Database** | `DBI::dbWriteTable()`, `odbc::sqlWriteTable()` | SQL tables |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme questions, you are expected to know the **base R** functions in depth. The `tidyverse` and `haven` packages earn bonus marks for showing breadth of awareness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal Mental Model of R Export

Every R export operation follows a **3-stage pipeline**:

1. **Resolve** — Locate the destination (working directory + relative path → absolute path).
2. **Serialize** — Convert the in-memory R object into a flat, linear stream of bytes (text or binary).
3. **Commit** — Physically flush the bytes to the OS file system via a system call (`write()`).

If any stage fails, the export is non-atomic; R may leave a **partial or corrupted file** on disk.

### 2.2 Deep Dive into Core Base R Export Functions

#### A. `write.table()` — The Mother Function

`write.table()` is the *parent* function from which `write.csv()`, `write.csv2()`, and `write.delim()` are all derived. Understanding it unlocks every other delimited export in R.

**Signature:**
```r
write.table(x, file = "", append = FALSE, quote = TRUE,
            sep = " ", eol = "\n", na = "NA", dec = ".",
            row.names = TRUE, col.names = NA, qmethod = c("escape", "double"),
            fileEncoding = "")
```

**Parameter-by-Parameter Explanation:**

- `x` — Any R object coercible to a `data.frame` (vectors get auto-wrapped).
- `file` — Destination path. Empty string `""` writes to the **console** (used for debugging).
- `append` — `FALSE` (default) overwrites; `TRUE` appends to an existing file.
- `quote` — If `TRUE`, character/string columns are wrapped in `"` quotes.
- `sep` — The field separator. A single space by default; comma for CSV.
- `eol` — End-of-line character (`"\n"` on Unix, `"\r\n"` on Windows).
- `na` — String representation for missing values (`"NA"` by default).
- `dec` — Decimal separator (`.` for English, `,` for European locales).
- `row.names` — Whether to prepend row names as the first column.
- `col.names` — Whether to write column headers; defaults to `!append` (write headers unless appending).
- `qmethod` — How to handle embedded quotes inside strings: `"escape"` (prefix with `\`) or `"double"` (double the quote).
- `fileEncoding` — Character encoding of the output file (e.g., `"UTF-8"`, `"latin1"`).

#### B. `write.csv()` vs `write.csv2()` — The Convenience Twins

- `write.csv()` → `sep = ","`, `dec = "."`, `qmethod = "double"` (Excel/US default)
- `write.csv2()` → `sep = ";"`, `dec = ","`, `qmethod = "double"` (European Excel default)

Both call `write.table()` internally with preset arguments.

#### C. `writeLines()` — Vector to Text File (No Row/Column Structure)

Used for writing a character vector, one element per line. No quoting, no header, no row names. Ideal for logs, lists, and line-oriented output.

```r
writeLines(text, con = stdout(), sep = "\n", useBytes = FALSE)
```

#### D. `cat()` — The Consolemate (File or Stdout)

`cat()` is a low-level writer that converts its arguments to character, concatenates them, and writes them to a connection. It is **not vectorized across rows of a data frame** — use it for narrative output, not tabular export.

```r
cat(... , file = "", sep = " ", fill = FALSE, append = FALSE)
```

#### E. `saveRDS()` / `readRDS()` — Single Object, Compact, Version-Safe

- Writes **exactly one R object** to a file with an associated serialization version header.
- Compact, fast, and preserves R-specific attributes (factor levels, `ts` time-series class, `lm` model structure, etc.).
- Use `compress = "bzip2"` / `"xz"` / `"gzip"` for size reduction.

```r
saveRDS(object, file = "my_data.rds", compress = "auto", version = 2, ascii = FALSE)
```

#### F. `save()` / `load()` — Multi-Object `.RData` Bundles

- Saves **any number of named R objects** into a single binary file (commonly the workspace image `.RData`).
- Loading restores objects under their original names, **without assignment** (a common source of bugs in scripts).
- Often used in `.RData` files that auto-load at session start.

```r
save(..., list = character(), file, envir = parent.frame(),
     compress = "gzip", compression_level, ascii = FALSE, version = 2)
```

### 2.3 Working Directory and Path Resolution

Before exporting, R must know **where on the filesystem to write**. This is governed by the **working directory**.

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `getwd()` | Returns the absolute path of the current working directory |
| `setwd("path")` | Changes the working directory (not recommended in scripts) |
| `here::here()` | Project-root-relative path (recommended in RStudio projects) |
| `file.path("a", "b", "c.csv")` | OS-aware path construction (`"a/b/c.csv"` on Unix, `"a\\b\\c.csv"` on Windows) |
| `normalizePath("data.csv")` | Resolves relative path to absolute path |
| `file.exists("data.csv")` | Boolean check for path validity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Practice:** Never hardcode absolute paths like `"C:\\Users\\John\\..."` in production code. Use `file.path()` and `here::here()` for **cross-platform reproducibility** — a KTU 2024 Scheme best practice explicitly tested in viva voce.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Function Cheat Sheet

| Export Target | Primary Function | Key Argument | Default Value | Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CSV (US/Excel) | `write.csv()` | `sep`, `row.names` | `","`, `TRUE` | Sharing tabular data with Excel users |
| CSV (European) | `write.csv2()` | `sep`, `dec` | `";"`, `","` | German/French Excel locales |
| Tab-delimited | `write.table()` | `sep` | `"\t"` | Bioinformatics (BED, GFF files) |
| Plain text lines | `writeLines()` | `con`, `sep` | `stdout()`, `"\n"` | Logs, reports |
| R binary (1 object) | `saveRDS()` | `compress` | `"auto"` | Caching preprocessed models |
| R binary (many) | `save()` | `compress` | `"gzip"` | Workspace persistence |
| JSON | `jsonlite::toJSON()` | `dataframe`, `pretty` | `"columns"`, `4` | Web APIs, NoSQL storage |
| Excel `.xlsx` | `writexl::write_xlsx()` | `col_names`, `format_headers` | `TRUE`, `TRUE` | Business reporting |
| Stata `.dta` | `haven::write_dta()` | `version` | `14` | Cross-software research workflows |

### 2.5 The Engineering Reality of Each Format

- **CSV/TSV**: Universal, human-readable, but **lossy** for factor levels, dates, and complex R types.
- **`.rds`/`.RData`**: Lossless for R objects, but **incompatible** with Python, Excel, or any non-R tool.
- **`.xlsx`**: Excellent for non-technical stakeholders, but requires Java (via `xlsx` package) or pure-C++ libraries (`writexl`, `openxlsx`).
- **JSON**: Web-standard, hierarchical, supports nested structures natively.
- **Parquet/Feather**: Columnar, fast, ideal for big-data interop (`arrow::write_parquet()`, `feather::write_feather()`).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The "derivations" in this section refer to **exhaustive, line-by-line code walkthroughs** that demonstrate every parameter of every export function — the engineering equivalent of step-by-step algebraic derivation.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Exporting a Data Frame to CSV — The Textbook Case

**Problem:** Given the built-in R data frame `mtcars`, export it to a CSV file named `mtcars_export.csv` in the current working directory, *without* row names and *without* quoting character columns.

**Step-by-Step Code:**

```r
# Step 1: Inspect the object to confirm its class and structure
class(mtcars)        # Expected output: "data.frame"
dim(mtcars)          # Expected output: 32 11  (32 rows, 11 columns)
head(mtcars, 2)      # Quick visual sanity check
```

**Logic:** Before exporting, we verify that `mtcars` is a `data.frame`. Other classes (e.g., `tibble`, `data.table`, `matrix`) require either coercion (`as.data.frame()`) or specialized writer functions.

```r
# Step 2: Resolve the absolute output path to avoid ambiguity
out_path <- file.path(getwd(), "mtcars_export.csv")
cat("Output will be written to:", normalizePath(out_path, mustWork = FALSE), "\n")
```

**Logic:** `file.path()` constructs an OS-correct path (forward slashes on macOS/Linux, backward slashes on Windows). `normalizePath()` resolves any `..` or `.` components. `mustWork = FALSE` allows us to inspect the *intended* path even if the file does not yet exist.

```r
# Step 3: Perform the export using write.csv() with custom arguments
write.csv(
  x         = mtcars,        # The R object to serialize
  file      = out_path,      # The absolute destination path
  row.names = FALSE,         # Suppress the leading row-name column
  quote     = FALSE,         # Disable surrounding double-quotes on strings
  na        = "",            # Write missing values as empty cells (not "NA")
  fileEncoding = "UTF-8"     # Explicit encoding for cross-platform safety
)
```

**Logic Explanation of Each Argument:**
- `row.names = FALSE` → Prevents the row names (e.g., `"Mazda RX4"`, `"Mazda RX4 Wag"`) from appearing as a spurious first column in the CSV.
- `quote = FALSE` → Avoids quoting of column headers and string fields; cleaner output but **risky** if strings contain commas.
- `na = ""` → Empty cells instead of the literal string `"NA"`, which is the convention in many ETL pipelines.
- `fileEncoding = "UTF-8"` → Guarantees that special characters (e.g., `µ`, `é`, `中`) survive the round-trip from R to disk and back.

```r
# Step 4: Verify the export by reading the first few lines back
readLines(out_path, n = 3)
```

**Logic:** Reading back the first 3 lines confirms the file structure. Expected output (truncated):
```
"mpg","cyl","disp","hp","drat","wt","qsec","vs","am","gear","carb"
21,6,160,110,3.9,2.62,16.46,0,1,4,4
21,6,160,110,3.9,2.875,17.02,0,1,4,4
```

### 3.2 Derivation 2: European CSV Variant (`write.csv2()`)

**Problem:** Export the same `mtcars` data frame using European conventions (semicolon separator, comma decimal) — typical of German, French, Spanish, and Italian Excel installations.

```r
euro_path <- file.path(getwd(), "mtcars_europe.csv")

write.csv2(
  x         = mtcars,
  file      = euro_path,
  row.names = FALSE,
  dec       = ","        # Comma as decimal separator (overrides the .csv2 default)
)

# Verification
readLines(euro_path, n = 2)
# Expected: "mpg;cyl;disp;hp;drat;wt;qsec;vs;am;gear;carb"
#           "21;6;160;110;3,9;2,62;16,46;0;1;4;4"
```

**Logic:** Notice how `3.9` becomes `3,9` and the field separator changes from `,` to `;`. This is why `write.csv2()` exists — to bridge R's English-locale defaults with continental European Excel.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Tab-Delimited Export for Bioinformatics

**Problem:** Export a custom data frame to a tab-delimited file (`.tsv`) — a format heavily used in genomics pipelines (e.g., BED, GFF, VCF derivatives).

```r
genes <- data.frame(
  gene_id   = c("BRCA1", "TP53", "EGFR"),
  chrom     = c("chr17", "chr17", "chr7"),
  start     = c(43044295, 7668421, 55019017),
  end       = c(43125483, 7687490, 55211628),
  strand    = c("-", "-", "+"),
  score     = c(960, 832, 712),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

tsv_path <- file.path(getwd(), "annotation.tsv")

write.table(
  x          = genes,
  file       = tsv_path,
  sep        = "\t",          # Tab character as delimiter
  quote      = FALSE,         # Disable quoting for clean tab-delimited output
  row.names  = FALSE,         # No row name column
  col.names  = TRUE,          # Explicitly write header row
  na         = "NULL",        # Custom missing-value marker
  eol        = "\n"           # Unix-style line ending (cross-platform consistency)
)
```

**Logic:** Bioinformatics tools (BEDTools, SAMtools, IGV) typically require **tab-delimited, unquoted, header-row-optional** formats. The `quote = FALSE` + `sep = "\t"` combo is the canonical pattern.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Saving R Objects with `saveRDS()` for Caching

**Problem:** Train a linear model on `mtcars` and cache the fitted object as an `.rds` file to avoid retraining in future sessions.

```r
# Step 1: Fit the model
fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + cyl + hp, data = mtcars)
summary(fit)$r.squared  # 0.83 (sanity check)

# Step 2: Cache the model object
rds_path <- file.path(getwd(), "models", "mpg_model.rds")
dir.create(dirname(rds_path), showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)  # Ensure folder exists

saveRDS(
  object   = fit,
  file     = rds_path,
  compress = "xz",        # Maximum compression (smaller files, slower I/O)
  version  = 2,           # Current serialization version (R 3.5+)
  ascii    = FALSE        # Binary mode (smaller files than ASCII)
)

# Step 3: Verify by loading into a new object name
fit_reloaded <- readRDS(rds_path)
identical(fit, fit_reloaded)  # Should return TRUE
```

**Logic:** `saveRDS()` saves **exactly one object** and does not preserve the original variable name. When loading, you must **assign** the result (`fit_reloaded <-`). This is the key difference from `save()`/`load()`, which restore objects under their original names without assignment — a frequent source of script bugs.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Multi-Object Export with `save()`

**Problem:** Save three related objects (`mtcars`, `fit`, `genes`) into a single `.RData` bundle.

```r
save(mtcars, fit, genes, file = "workspace_bundle.RData")

# Loading restores all three objects under their original names
load("workspace_bundle.RData")
ls()  # Will list: "mtcars" "fit" "genes" (along with anything else in the session)
```

**Logic:** `save()` does **not** return a value or assign; it writes a side-effect-only file. `load()` rehydrates the objects directly into the global environment — caution is required to avoid overwriting existing variables.

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Writing a Log File with `writeLines()`

**Problem:** Append timestamped log messages to a file `session.log`.

```r
log_path <- file.path(getwd(), "session.log")
timestamps <- Sys.time() + seq(0, by = 60, length.out = 5)
messages <- paste0("[", format(timestamps, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"), "] INFO: Processing batch")

writeLines(
  text     = messages,
  con      = log_path,
  sep      = "\n",
  useBytes = FALSE
)
```

**Logic:** Each element of the character vector `messages` becomes one line. `writeLines()` is the canonical tool for non-tabular, line-oriented output.

### 3.7 Derivation 7: Conditional Logic — Choosing the Right Exporter

```r
export_data <- function(data, filename, format = c("csv", "tsv", "xlsx", "rds")) {
  format <- match.arg(format)
  full_path <- file.path(getwd(), filename)

  switch(format,
    "csv"  = write.csv(data, full_path, row.names = FALSE),
    "tsv"  = write.table(data, full_path, sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE),
    "xlsx" = writexl::write_xlsx(data, full_path),
    "rds"  = saveRDS(data, full_path)
  )
  invisible(normalizePath(full_path))
}
```

**Logic:** This abstraction layer hides the function-selection logic from the caller — a hallmark of production-grade R code.

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Exporter | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Sharing with non-R Excel users | `write.csv()` | Universal, lightweight |
| European colleagues using Excel | `write.csv2()` | Locale-correct delimiters |
| Caching large model objects | `saveRDS(x, compress = "xz")` | Lossless + space-efficient |
| Bundling an entire workspace | `save(..., file = "x.RData")` | Atomic multi-object save |
| Bioinformatics pipelines | `write.table(..., sep = "\t", quote = FALSE)` | Tab-delimited convention |
| Web APIs and NoSQL databases | `jsonlite::write_json()` | Hierarchical, JSON-native |
| Business reporting (multi-sheet) | `writexl::write_xlsx()` or `openxlsx::write.xlsx()` | Native Excel with formatting |
| Stata/SAS/SPSS interop | `haven::write_dta()` / `write_sas()` / `write_sav()` | Cross-software compatibility |
| Big-data columnar storage | `arrow::write_parquet()` | High-performance, typed schema |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The R Data Export Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["In-memory R Object"] --> resolveA["Step 1: Path Resolution"]
    resolveA --> checkA{"Path Valid?"}
    checkA -->|No| errA["Error: Cannot Write"]
    checkA -->|Yes| serializeA["Step 2: Serialization"]
    serializeA --> fmtA{"Output Format?"}
    fmtA -->|CSV/TSV| fmtTextA["write.csv / write.table"]
    fmtA -->|R-native| fmtBinA["saveRDS / save"]
    fmtA -->|Excel| fmtXlsxA["writexl / openxlsx"]
    fmtA -->|JSON| fmtJsonA["jsonlite"]
    fmtA -->|Database| fmtDbA["DBI::dbWriteTable"]
    fmtTextA --> commitA["Step 3: File System Commit"]
    fmtBinA --> commitA
    fmtXlsxA --> commitA
    fmtJsonA --> commitA
    fmtDbA --> commitA
    commitA --> verifyA["Step 4: Verification (readLines / file.info)"]
    verifyA --> successA["Success: File Persisted on Disk"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for Selecting an Export Format

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStartA["What is the target consumer?"] --> qExcA{"Excel user?"}
    qExcA -->|Yes| qEurA{"European locale?"}
    qEurA -->|Yes| qEurAnsA["Use write.csv2()"]
    qEurA -->|No| qUsAnsA["Use write.csv()"]
    qExcA -->|No| qRonlyA{"R-only workflow?"}
    qRonlyA -->|Yes| qMultiA{"Multiple objects?"}
    qMultiA -->|Yes| qSaveAnsA["Use save()"]
    qMultiA -->|No| qRdsAnsA["Use saveRDS()"]
    qRonlyA -->|No| qWebA{"Web API / JSON?"}
    qWebA -->|Yes| qJsonAnsA["Use jsonlite::write_json()"]
    qWebA -->|No| qBioA{"Bioinformatics / genomics?"}
    qBioA -->|Yes| qTsvAnsA["Use write.table with sep = tab and quote = FALSE"]
    qBioA -->|No| qDbA{"Database target?"}
    qDbA -->|Yes| qDbAnsA["Use DBI::dbWriteTable()"]
    qDbA -->|No| qFallbackA["Use write.table() as generic fallback"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Layered Architecture of R Export Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserLayerA["User Application Layer"]
        appA["Shiny App / R Markdown / Script"]
    end
    subgraph AbstractionLayerA["Abstraction Layer"]
        ab1A["readr::write_csv"]
        ab2A["writexl::write_xlsx"]
        ab3A["jsonlite::write_json"]
    end
    subgraph BaseLayerA["Base R Layer"]
        base1A["write.table"]
        base2A["write.csv"]
        base3A["writeLines"]
        base4A["save / saveRDS"]
    end
    subgraph SysCallLayerA["OS System Call Layer"]
        sysA["write() syscall to OS"]
        sysB["fopen / fprintf in C"]
    end
    UserLayerA --> AbstractionLayerA
    AbstractionLayerA --> BaseLayerA
    BaseLayerA --> SysCallLayerA
    SysCallLayerA --> diskA[("Persistent Disk Storage")]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each layer calls *downward* into lower-level machinery. The `tidyverse` packages (`readr`, `writexl`) are user-friendly wrappers over `base::write.table()` and underlying C libraries, which in turn invoke OS-level file system calls.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
*Bloom's Level: Remember / Understand*

**Differentiate between `write.csv()` and `write.csv2()` in R. In which scenario would you prefer `write.csv2()`?**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

`write.csv()` and `write.csv2()` are both convenience wrappers around `write.table()` for exporting data frames to disk.

- `write.csv()` uses `sep = ","` and `dec = "."` — the **US/English locale** convention used by Excel in most English-speaking countries.
- `write.csv2()` uses `sep = ";"` and `dec = ","` — the **European locale** convention (German, French, Spanish, Italian Excel).

**Scenario for `write.csv2()`:** When the data will be opened in Excel configured for a European locale (e.g., Germany), where comma is the decimal separator and semicolon is the field separator. Using `write.csv()` in such locales would cause Excel to misinterpret the columns.

```r
write.csv2(mtcars, "cars_eu.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
*Bloom's Level: Understand / Apply*

**Explain the purpose of the `row.names` argument in `write.csv()`. What happens if you set it to `TRUE` versus `FALSE`?**

**Model Answer (≈ 95 words):**

The `row.names` argument controls whether the row identifiers of a data frame are written as the **first column** of the output CSV file.

- If `row.names = TRUE` (the default), the row names appear as an unnamed leading column. For `mtcars`, this means an extra column containing `"Mazda RX4"`, `"Mazda RX4 Wag"`, etc.
- If `row.names = FALSE`, no leading column is written; the file starts directly with the column headers (e.g., `"mpg","cyl",...`).

**Practical recommendation:** Set `row.names = FALSE` when sharing with non-R tools that do not expect the leading row-name column, or when row names are not meaningful (e.g., sequential integers).

```r
write.csv(mtcars, "cars.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain any **five** different functions available in R for exporting data to external files. For each function, mention the output format and provide a small illustrative example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

R provides a rich set of functions for exporting data. Five important ones are:

**1. `write.csv()`** — Exports a data frame to a comma-separated values file.

```r
write.csv(mtcars, file = "mtcars.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```
Output format: `.csv` (comma-separated, Excel-friendly). Internally calls `write.table()` with `sep = ","`, `dec = "."`, `qmethod = "double"`.

**2. `write.table()`** — The generic, parent function for all delimited exports. Allows full control over `sep`, `dec`, `quote`, `na`, etc.

```r
write.table(iris, file = "iris.tsv", sep = "\t", quote = FALSE, row.names = FALSE)
```
Output format: any delimited text (`.tsv`, `.txt`, `.csv`).

**3. `saveRDS()`** — Serializes a **single R object** to a compact binary file with full type fidelity.

```r
fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)
saveRDS(fit, file = "model.rds", compress = "xz")
```
Output format: `.rds` (binary, lossless, R-specific).

**4. `save()`** — Serializes **multiple named objects** to a single binary file.

```r
save(mtcars, fit, file = "workspace.RData")
```
Output format: `.RData` / `.rda` (binary, R-specific, multi-object).

**5. `writeLines()`** — Writes a character vector to a connection, one element per line. Ideal for logs.

```r
writeLines(c("Line 1", "Line 2", "Line 3"), con = "log.txt")
```
Output format: plain `.txt`, one line per vector element.

**Bonus functions** (if time permits, for extra marks):
- `jsonlite::write_json()` → `.json`
- `writexl::write_xlsx()` → `.xlsx`
- `haven::write_dta()` → `.dta` (Stata)

---

**(b)** Describe the parameters `sep`, `quote`, `na`, `dec`, and `fileEncoding` of the `write.table()` function. Write an R script that exports the built-in `airquality` dataset to a file named `air_clean.txt` such that: (i) fields are separated by a tab, (ii) strings are not quoted, (iii) missing values are written as the string `"MISSING"`, (iv) the decimal separator is a period, and (v) the encoding is UTF-8. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Parameter Explanations:**

| Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `sep` | The character string used to separate fields. Common values: `","`, `"\t"`, `";"`. |
| `quote` | A logical — if `TRUE` (default), character and factor columns are surrounded by double quotes. |
| `na` | The string used to represent `NA` (missing) values in the output. Default is `"NA"`. |
| `dec` | The character used for the decimal point. Default is `"."`. Use `","` for European locales. |
| `fileEncoding` | Specifies the character encoding of the output file (e.g., `"UTF-8"`, `"latin1"`). |

**Complete R Script:**

```r
# Step 1: Resolve the output path using file.path (OS-aware)
out_file <- file.path(getwd(), "air_clean.txt")

# Step 2: Verify the dataset before export
head(airquality, 3)
# Confirm: airquality is a data.frame with 153 rows and 6 columns
#          (Ozone, Solar.R, Wind, Temp, Month, Day). It contains NA values.

# Step 3: Export with the specified parameter overrides
write.table(
  x            = airquality,
  file         = out_file,
  sep          = "\t",          # Tab-separated
  quote        = FALSE,         # No quoting
  na           = "MISSING",     # Custom NA marker
  dec          = ".",           # Period decimal
  row.names    = FALSE,         # No row-name column (cleaner for downstream tools)
  fileEncoding = "UTF-8"        # UTF-8 encoding
)

# Step 4: Verify the export
cat("File written to:", normalizePath(out_file), "\n")
readLines(out_file, n = 3)
```

**Expected first 3 lines of `air_clean.txt`:**
```
Ozone	Solar.R	Wind	Temp	Month	Day
41	190	7.4	67	5	1
36	118	8	72	5	2
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students confuse **`sep = " "`** (single space, default) with **`sep = "\t"`** (tab character). Backslash-t (`\t`) is the **escape sequence** for tab.
> - Forgetting `quote = FALSE` while setting `sep = "\t"` leaves quoted strings, which can break tab-delimited parsers in downstream tools.
> - Setting `na = "MISSING"` is a **string literal**, not an R object — write it with quotes inside the function call.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast `saveRDS()` and `save()` for exporting R objects. Which one would you use to cache a trained machine learning model, and why? Provide an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `saveRDS()` | `save()` |
|---|---|---|
| Number of objects | **Exactly one** | **One or more** |
| Object name preserved? | **No** — must assign on load | **Yes** — loaded under original name |
| Output extension | `.rds` | `.RData` / `.rda` |
| Compression support | Yes (`compress = "auto"/"gzip"/"bzip2"/"xz"`) | Yes (`compress = "gzip"/"bzip2"/"xz"`) |
| Typical use case | Caching single objects (models, preprocessed data) | Saving entire workspaces |
| Portability across R versions | Excellent (with `version = 2`) | Good |
| Recommended for production | **Yes** | Less so (overwrite risk) |

**Why `saveRDS()` is preferred for ML model caching:**

1. **Single-object contract** — clean, predictable, refactor-friendly.
2. **No name collision** — when loading, you choose the new variable name, avoiding accidental overwrites.
3. **Lossless type fidelity** — preserves factor levels, model coefficients, attributes.
4. **Cross-script friendly** — multiple scripts can load the same `.rds` into different variable names.

**Example:**

```r
# ---- Training script (run once) ----
library(randomForest)
data(iris)
rf_model <- randomForest(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 500)
saveRDS(rf_model, file = "iris_rf_model.rds", compress = "xz")
cat("Model saved.\n")

# ---- Inference script (run many times) ----
# Load the model into a fresh variable name
my_classifier <- readRDS("iris_rf_model.rds")
new_data <- data.frame(Sepal.Length = 5.1, Sepal.Width = 3.5,
                       Petal.Length = 1.4, Petal.Width = 0.2)
predict(my_classifier, newdata = new_data)
# Expected output: "setosa"
```

---

**(b)** Explain the role of `file.path()`, `getwd()`, and `normalizePath()` in ensuring robust file paths when exporting data. Write an R script that exports the `mtcars` data frame to a subdirectory named `output/`, creating the subdirectory if it does not exist. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Each Function:**

- **`getwd()`** — Returns the **absolute path** of R's current working directory. The base for all relative paths.
- **`file.path(...)`** — Concatenates path components using the **OS-correct separator** (`/` on Unix, `\` on Windows). Cross-platform safe.
- **`normalizePath(path)`** — Resolves any `.` or `..` components and returns the **canonical absolute path**. Useful for debugging and logging.

**Why this matters:** Hardcoding paths like `"C:\\Users\\John\\Desktop\\file.csv"` is fragile. Using `file.path()` + `getwd()` + `dir.create()` makes the script **portable** across machines, operating systems, and RStudio projects.

**Complete R Script:**

```r
# Step 1: Inspect the current working directory
cat("Current working directory:\n")
print(getwd())

# Step 2: Construct the target subdirectory path
subdir <- file.path(getwd(), "output")
cat("Target subdirectory:", normalizePath(subdir, mustWork = FALSE), "\n")

# Step 3: Create the subdirectory if it does not exist
if (!dir.exists(subdir)) {
  dir.create(path = subdir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = TRUE)
  cat("Created subdirectory:", normalizePath(subdir), "\n")
} else {
  cat("Subdirectory already exists.\n")
}

# Step 4: Construct the full output file path
out_csv <- file.path(subdir, "mtcars_export.csv")
out_rds <- file.path(subdir, "mtcars_export.rds")

# Step 5: Export in two formats
write.csv(mtcars, file = out_csv, row.names = FALSE, fileEncoding = "UTF-8")
saveRDS(mtcars, file = out_rds, compress = "xz")

# Step 6: Verify both files exist and report their sizes
cat("\n--- Export Summary ---\n")
for (f in c(out_csv, out_rds)) {
  info <- file.info(f)
  cat(sprintf("File: %s\n  Size: %s bytes\n  Exists: %s\n",
              normalizePath(f), info$size, file.exists(f)))
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Part B (b):**
> 1. **Forgetting `recursive = TRUE`** in `dir.create()` — fails if any parent directory is missing.
> 2. **Using `paste()` instead of `file.path()`** — works on Unix but breaks on Windows due to backslash handling. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests this.
> 3. **Hardcoding the separator** (`"/"` or `"\\"`) inside `paste()` — loses cross-platform behavior.
> 4. **Not verifying with `file.exists()`** — leaves silent failures that the examiner will mark as incomplete scripts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Exporting Data in R**

- **Core principle:** Exporting = writing in-memory R objects to persistent external storage via a 3-stage pipeline (**Resolve → Serialize → Commit**).
- **Function hierarchy:** `write.table()` is the **mother function**; `write.csv()`, `write.csv2()`, and `write.delim()` are convenience wrappers.
- **`write.csv()`** → `sep = ","`, `dec = "."` (US/Excel).
- **`write.csv2()`** → `sep = ";"`, `dec = ","` (European Excel).
- **`row.names = FALSE`** → prevents an unnamed leading column of row identifiers in the CSV.
- **`quote = FALSE`** → suppresses surrounding double-quotes on string columns (clean for TSV/bioinformatics).
- **`na = "MISSING"`** → custom string for `NA` cells (default is `"NA"`).
- **`fileEncoding = "UTF-8"`** → guarantees portability of special characters across systems.
- **`saveRDS(x, file = "...rds")`** → single object, no name preserved on load (must assign). Use for caching models and preprocessed data.
- **`save(..., file = "...RData")`** → multiple objects, names preserved on load. Use for workspace snapshots.
- **`writeLines(text, con = file)`** → one-line-per-element text output, ideal for logs.
- **`file.path()` + `getwd()` + `dir.create(..., recursive = TRUE)`** → cross-platform safe path construction and directory creation.
- **`normalizePath()`** → resolves `.` and `..` components; helpful for logging and debugging.
- **Format selection rule of thumb:**
  - Tabular + Excel-friendly → CSV (`write.csv`)
  - European Excel → `write.csv2()`
  - R-native + lossless → `saveRDS` / `save`
  - Bioinformatics / genomics → tab-delimited `write.table`
  - Web / APIs → `jsonlite::write_json`
  - Business reports → `writexl::write_xlsx`
  - Cross-software → `haven::write_dta` / `write_sas` / `write_sav`
- **Always verify** the export with `readLines()`, `file.exists()`, or `file.info()` — examiners reward the verification step.
- **Never hardcode absolute paths** — use `file.path()` and `here::here()` for reproducibility.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Mile Advice for KTU 2024:** In the exam hall, when asked to export data, *always* state: (1) the **function name**, (2) the **key arguments** you are setting and *why*, (3) a **commented code snippet**, and (4) a **verification step**. This 4-part answer structure consistently scores full marks on KTU Part-B questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importing Data from Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Importing Data from Databases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Importing Data from Databases** in the R ecosystem refers to the systematic process of establishing a **bidirectional communication channel** between an active R session and an external **Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)** through standardized application programming interfaces (APIs), driver libraries, and structured query language (SQL) statements, enabling data analysts to perform statistical computation, visualization, and machine-learning workflows directly on persistent, server-side tabular data without loading the entire dataset into RAM.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (mapped to **Course Outcome CO4**: *Apply data import/export techniques for diverse data sources including files, web, and databases*), this operation is formalized as a three-layer abstraction:

1. **Front-end Layer**: The R console / RStudio IDE where the analyst issues commands.
2. **Interface Layer**: The **DBI (Database Interface)** package, which provides a *consistent, database-agnostic grammar* (functions like `dbConnect()`, `dbGetQuery()`, `dbReadTable()`, `dbDisconnect()`).
3. **Back-end Layer**: The *driver-specific* package (e.g., **RMariaDB**, **RMySQL**, **RSQLite**, **odbc**, **RODBC**, **RPostgres**) that translates DBI calls into the native wire protocol of the target RDBMS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DBI matters**: The DBI package is the *de facto* SQL back-end specification in R. By writing your code against the DBI interface, you insulate your analysis from vendor lock-in — the same R script can theoretically target SQLite, MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Oracle with only the driver swapped.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Universal Translator

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check (Real-World Analogy)**: Imagine you are an Indian diplomat stationed abroad who only speaks **Malayalam**. To talk to a French chef, a Japanese banker, and a Brazilian engineer, you do NOT learn three new languages. Instead, you hire **one universal translator** who knows every language and speaks on your behalf.

- **You** = the R analyst (data scientist working in the comfort of the R language).
- **French chef / Japanese banker / Brazilian engineer** = SQLite, MySQL, PostgreSQL databases.
- **Universal translator** = the **DBI interface** (acts as a universal grammar).
- **Specialized dialect coaches** = the driver packages (`RMariaDB`, `RSQLite`, etc.).

The translator (DBI) takes your Malayalam (R code) and forwards it through the right dialect coach (driver) to the right foreign expert (database). The expert replies back through the same pipeline, and you only ever speak Malayalam.

## 1.3 The Common Workflow at a Glance

| Stage | R Function | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Load Interface | `library(DBI)` | Activate the universal translator |
| 2. Load Driver | `library(RMariaDB)` | Bring the dialect coach |
| 3. Connect | `dbConnect()` | Establish the phone call |
| 4. Inspect | `dbListTables()` | Ask "what data do you have?" |
| 5. Query | `dbGetQuery()` | Ask a question (SQL) |
| 6. Read | `dbReadTable()` | Fetch a whole table |
| 7. Write | `dbWriteTable()` | Push results back |
| 8. Disconnect | `dbDisconnect()` | Hang up politely |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Stack — How Database Connectivity Actually Works

The R-to-database pipeline is a layered architecture where each layer abstracts the layer below it. Understanding this stack is **mandatory** for KTU viva and ESE questions because examiners frequently test "What happens under the hood?".

**Layer 1 — Application Layer (R Session)**

The analyst writes R code. R itself is *not* a database client; it has no native knowledge of MySQL, PostgreSQL, or any RDBMS wire protocol.

**Layer 2 — DBI Specification (Front-end Interface)**

The DBI package defines a *generic, vendor-neutral grammar*. Functions like `dbConnect()`, `dbGetQuery()`, and `dbReadTable()` are defined here as generic S4/S3 methods. They do **not** do the real work — they simply dispatch to the appropriate driver.

**Layer 3 — Driver Implementation (Back-end Bridge)**

Each driver package (e.g., `RMariaDB`) contains *concrete method definitions* for every DBI generic. This is where the actual TCP/IP socket connection is opened, SQL statements are serialized, and result sets are deserialized back into R data frames.

**Layer 4 — Native Client Library (C/C++ Glue)**

Most driver packages link to a compiled native client library (e.g., `libmysqlclient` for MySQL, `libpq` for PostgreSQL, `sqlite3` for SQLite). This is the only layer that *actually* talks to the database server.

**Layer 5 — Database Server**

The RDBMS process receives the SQL, parses it, plans the execution, retrieves rows, and streams them back through the network socket.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this layered design?**
> 1. **Portability** — Swap the driver, keep the script.
> 2. **Security** — Credentials and SSL/TLS negotiation are isolated in one layer.
> 3. **Memory efficiency** — `dbGetQuery()` materializes results lazily; large tables can be streamed using `dbSendQuery()` + `dbFetch()` + `dbClearResult()`.
> 4. **Maintainability** — Bug fixes in the driver do not affect analyst code.

## 2.2 Core Driver Packages — The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Package | Target RDBMS | Protocol | Native Dep | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `RSQLite` | SQLite (embedded, file-based) | In-process, no server | Bundled `sqlite3` C lib | **★★★★★ Very High** |
| `RMariaDB` | MariaDB & MySQL | TCP, native wire | `libmariadb` / `libmysqlclient` | **★★★★★ Very High** |
| `RMySQL` | MySQL (legacy) | TCP, native wire | `libmysqlclient` | **★★★ Moderate** |
| `odbc` | Any ODBC-compliant DB | ODBC | OS-level ODBC manager | **★★★★ High** |
| `RODBC` | ODBC-compliant DB (legacy) | ODBC | OS-level ODBC manager | **★★★ Moderate** |
| `RPostgres` | PostgreSQL | TCP, `libpq` | `libpq` | **★★★ Moderate** |
| `bigrquery` | Google BigQuery | REST API | None (HTTP) | **★ Low** |
| `duckdb` | DuckDB (analytical OLAP) | In-process | Bundled | **★★★ Moderate** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint**: When the question says *"connect R to a database"*, 8 times out of 10 the expected answer involves either **`RSQLite`** (for local, file-based scenarios) or **`RMariaDB`** (for networked MySQL scenarios). Memorize the function call templates for both.

## 2.3 The Canonical Connection String Anatomy

Every `dbConnect()` call ultimately reduces to a *connection string* — a structured piece of metadata that tells the driver **where**, **who**, and **how** to connect. The general template is:

$$
\text{Connection} \;=\; f(\text{Driver},\ \text{Host},\ \text{Port},\ \text{Database},\ \text{User},\ \text{Password},\ \text{Options})
$$

For example, with `RMariaDB`:

```r
con <- dbConnect(
  RMariaDB::MariaDB(),
  host     = "localhost",
  port     = 3306,
  dbname   = "student_db",
  user     = "root",
  password = "secret"
)
```

For `RSQLite` (note: *no server* — just a file path):

```r
con <- dbConnect(
  RSQLite::SQLite(),
  dbname = "/data/kerala_univ.sqlite"
)
```

## 2.4 SQL ↔ R Data-Type Mapping

| SQL Type | R Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `INTEGER`, `INT` | `integer` | 32-bit signed |
| `BIGINT` | `numeric` (double) | May lose precision; use `bit64::integer64` |
| `FLOAT`, `REAL`, `DOUBLE` | `numeric` | Double precision |
| `VARCHAR`, `TEXT`, `CHAR` | `character` | UTF-8 encoded |
| `DATE` | `Date` | Class "Date" in R |
| `DATETIME`, `TIMESTAMP` | `POSIXct` | Class "POSIXct" in R |
| `BOOLEAN`, `TINYINT(1)` | `logical` | TRUE / FALSE |
| `BLOB` | `raw` | Binary large object |
| `NULL` | `NA` | R's missing-value sentinel |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall**: `BIGINT` values larger than $2^{53} \approx 9.007 \times 10^{15}$ lose precision when converted to R double. For monetary IDs or scientific timestamps, use `bit64::integer64` or specify the column type explicitly with `coerce=` or `type=` arguments.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Civil Engineering**: Connect to a structural-monitoring MySQL DB storing strain-gauge readings from bridges and pull last-24-hour rows for time-series plotting in `ggplot2`.
- **Biomedical**: Query a hospital PostgreSQL DB for patient cohorts matching inclusion criteria.
- **IoT / Industry 4.0**: Stream sensor data from a TimeScaleDB / InfluxDB into R for predictive maintenance.
- **Academic Research**: Pull survey responses from a SQLite file shared by collaborators worldwide.
