y' &= y + k_y \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix form:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & k_x & 0 \\ k_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
```

---

### 2.1.2 Type 2 — Coordinate Transformations

These change the **coordinate system** itself, not just the data points. This is a critical KTU distinction.

| R Function | Transformation | Mathematical Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `coord_cartesian()` | Identity (default) | $x' = x$ |
| `coord_flip()` | Swap X and Y axes | $x' = y, \ y' = x$ |
| `coord_polar()` | Cartesian → Polar | $r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}, \ \theta = \arctan(y/x)$ |
| `coord_trans()` | Custom functional | $x' = f(x), \ y' = g(y)$ |
| `coord_map()` | Spherical/Map projection | Mercator, Albers, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Polar Coordinate Conversion Formula**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> r &= \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \\
> \theta &= \arctan\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> Where $r$ is the **radius** and $\theta$ is the **angle** (in radians).

---

### 2.1.3 Type 3 — Statistical Transformations (`stat_*`)

These transform the **raw data** into a **summarized visual representation**.

| `stat_*` | Input Data | Output Visual |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `stat_bin()` | Continuous variable | Histogram bins |
| `stat_count()` | Categorical variable | Bar heights |
| `stat_smooth()` | (x, y) pairs | Fitted regression line |
| `stat_summary()` | Grouped data | Mean, median, CI |
| `stat_identity()` | Pre-computed values | Direct mapping |
| `stat_ecdf()` | Numeric vector | Empirical CDF curve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** `stat_identity()` is the **default** in many geoms because it means "use the data as-is, no transformation."

---

### 2.1.4 Type 4 — Data Reshaping Transformations (Tidy Data)

This is the **tidyr** family of transformations, essential for preparing data before visualization.

| Function | Operation | Shape Change |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `pivot_longer()` | Wide → Long | More rows, fewer columns |
| `pivot_wider()` | Long → Wide | More columns, fewer rows |
| `separate()` | Split one column → many | Increases column count |
| `unite()` | Combine many columns → one | Decreases column count |
| `nest()` | Group into list-columns | Hierarchical structure |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Transformation | Mathematical Form | Matrix (3×3) | R Implementation | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Translation | $x' = x + t_x, \ y' = y + t_y$ | $T$ | `scale_x_continuous(limits=...)` | Axis shift |
| 2 | Uniform Scaling | $x' = sx, \ y' = sy$ | $S$ | `scale_x_log10()` | Compress range |
| 3 | Rotation | $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$ | $R$ | `coord_polar()` | Polar plots |
| 4 | Reflection (Y-axis) | $x' = -x, \ y' = y$ | $M_y$ | `scale_x_reverse()` | Mirror axis |
| 5 | Shear | $x' = x + k_y \cdot y$ | $H$ | Custom `coord_trans()` | Italic effect |
| 6 | Polar | $r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}, \ \theta = \arctan(y/x)$ | N/A | `coord_polar()` | Pie/radar |
| 7 | Log Scale | $x' = \log_b(x)$ | N/A | `scale_x_log10()` | Skewed data |
| 8 | Sqrt Scale | $x' = \sqrt{x}$ | N/A | `scale_x_sqrt()` | Moderate skew |
| 9 | Composit (T·R·S) | $M = T \cdot R \cdot S$ | Combined | Layered in ggplot | Pipeline |

---

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Transformation

- **Why Scale?** Compresses data spanning multiple orders of magnitude (e.g., population from thousands to billions).
- **Why Rotate?** Polar transformations make cyclical data (wind direction, months of year) intuitively visible.
- **Why Reflect?** Comparing two distributions side-by-side in opposing directions.
- **Why Shear?** Used in 3D projection simulation on a 2D plane.
- **Why Reshape?** Tidy data (one observation per row) is the prerequisite for `ggplot2`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In **CAD software** (used by Civil and Mechanical engineers), transformations enable zooming, panning, and rotating engineering drawings. In **GIS systems**, coordinate transformations convert between UTM, Mercator, and lat/long systems — the same mathematical foundations as `coord_map()` in R.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Composite Transformation Matrix (KTU Favourite)

When multiple transformations are chained (e.g., Scale → Rotate → Translate), the resulting matrix is the **product** of individual matrices, applied **right-to-left** to a point.

### Goal
Derive the composite matrix $M = T \cdot R \cdot S$ that first scales, then rotates, then translates a point.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the individual matrices.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $S$ scales first, $R$ rotates next, $T$ translates last.

**Step 2:** Compute $R \cdot S$ (intermediate matrix).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R \cdot S &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x\cos\theta & -s_y\sin\theta & 0 \\ s_x\sin\theta & s_y\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each row of $R$ is multiplied element-wise with the columns of $S$.

**Step 3:** Compute the final composite $M = T \cdot (R \cdot S)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= T \cdot (R \cdot S) \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} s_x\cos\theta & -s_y\sin\theta & 0 \\ s_x\sin\theta & s_y\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x\cos\theta & -s_y\sin\theta & t_x \\ s_x\sin\theta & s_y\cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Multiplication of the translation matrix adds $t_x$ to row 1 and $t_y$ to row 2 of the result, while leaving the rotation-scaling part intact.

**Step 4:** Apply $M$ to a point $P = (x, y, 1)^T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P' &= M \cdot P \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x\cos\theta & -s_y\sin\theta & t_x \\ s_x\sin\theta & s_y\cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} s_x x\cos\theta - s_y y\sin\theta + t_x \\ s_x x\sin\theta + s_y y\cos\theta + t_y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The transformed point $P'$ is computed as a linear combination of $x$, $y$, and the translation offsets, weighted by the scale and rotation factors.

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
x' &= s_x x \cos\theta - s_y y \sin\theta + t_x \\
y' &= s_x x \sin\theta + s_y y \cos\theta + t_y
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This single equation represents the **complete composite transformation** that scales by $(s_x, s_y)$, rotates by $\theta$, and translates by $(t_x, t_y)$ simultaneously.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Logarithmic Scale Transformation

### Goal
Show how `scale_x_log10()` in R mathematically transforms a data axis.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the original variable $x$ and the transformed variable $x'$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' = \log_{10}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* A log base-10 transformation compresses multiplicative ranges into additive ranges.

**Step 2:** Compute the Jacobian (rate of change) — important for density transformations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dx'}{dx} &= \frac{1}{x \ln(10)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* As $x$ increases, the rate of change of $x'$ decreases — this is the **compression effect**.

**Step 3:** Apply the density correction (for histograms on log scale).

If $f(x)$ is a probability density on the original scale, the transformed density $g(x')$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(x') = f(x) \cdot \left\vert \frac{dx}{dx'} \right\vert = f(x) \cdot x \ln(10)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This ensures that **area under the curve is preserved** (a fundamental probability axiom). Without this correction, log-scale histograms would be visually misleading.

**Step 4:** Example — Data point $x = 1000$ becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' = \log_{10}(1000) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

A data point $x = 1{,}000{,}000$ becomes $x' = 6$. Both are now on a comparable linear scale, which is the **core purpose** of log transformation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Polar Coordinate Transformation (Used in `coord_polar()`)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with Cartesian coordinates $(x, y)$.

**Step 2:** Compute radius $r$ (distance from origin).

$$
\begin{aligned}
r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Derived from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the right triangle formed by $(x, y)$ and the origin.

**Step 3:** Compute the angle $\theta$ (counter-clockwise from positive X-axis).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The tangent of the angle equals the ratio of opposite ($y$) to adjacent ($x$) in the right triangle.

**Step 4:** Reverse transformation (polar → Cartesian, used in plotting).

$$
\begin{aligned}
x = r \cos\theta \\
y = r \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* These are the parametric equations of a circle — every point at distance $r$ from the origin traces a circle as $\theta$ varies from $0$ to $2\pi$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: "Which Transformation Should I Use?"

This is a high-yield KTU question pattern. Given a scenario, identify the correct transformation.

| Problem Symptom | Diagnosis | Recommended Transformation | R Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data spans 6+ orders of magnitude | Exponential range | Log scale | `scale_x_log10()` |
| Highly right-skewed distribution | Long tail of outliers | Sqrt or log | `scale_x_sqrt()` |
| Cyclical data (time of day, months) | Angular/periodic nature | Polar coordinates | `coord_polar()` |
| X and Y are symmetric, want to swap | Axis interchange | Coordinate flip | `coord_flip()` |
| Comparing a value to its negative | Mirror comparison | Reflection | `scale_x_reverse()` |
| Categorical summary needed | Raw values too noisy | Statistical summary | `stat_summary(fun = "mean")` |
| Wide-format data won't facet | Tidy principle violated | Reshape | `pivot_longer()` |
| Overplotting of points | Too many overlapping markers | Position jitter | `geom_jitter()` |
| Bars and lines on same plot | Dual Y-axis needed | Twin scale | `scale_y_continuous(sec.axis = ...)` |
| 3D data on 2D plot | Projection needed | Shear + Scale | `coord_trans()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse `scale_x_log10()` (transforms the **axis scale**) with `coord_trans(x = "log10")` (transforms the **coordinate system**). Both produce similar visuals, but the underlying math differs — `coord_trans` applies the transform **after** computing statistics, while `scale_x_log10` applies it **before**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Transformation Pipeline in ggplot2

This diagram shows the order of operations when `ggplot2` renders a plot, highlighting where each transformation type intervenes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Dataframe\n(rows and columns)"] --> B["Data Reshaping\npivot_longer, pivot_wider"]
    B --> C["Statistical Transformation\nstat_bin, stat_smooth"]
    C --> D["Scale Transformation\nscale_x_log10, scale_y_sqrt"]
    D --> E["Geometric Layer\ngeom_point, geom_bar"]
    E --> F["Coordinate System\ncoord_polar, coord_flip"]
    F --> G["Final Rendered Plot\non screen or file"]
    
    H["User Input"] --> A
    I["Aesthetic Mappings\nx, y, color, size"] --> C
    J["Axis Limits and Breaks"] --> D
    K["Theme and Labels"] --> G
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1f5
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f5e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style G fill:#e1e1ff
```

*Logic:* Data flows top-to-bottom through **six transformation stages**. The **reshaping** (tidyr) happens first, followed by **statistical** (stat_*), then **scale** (scale_*), then **geometric** (geom_*), and finally **coordinate** (coord_*) transformations. This order is **enforced by the Grammar of Graphics** specification.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: 2D Coordinate Transformation Mapping

This diagram illustrates how a single point $(x, y)$ is mapped to $(x', y')$ through a rotation transformation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Original["Original Cartesian Plane"]
        P1["Point P = (x, y)"]
        AX1["X-axis"]
        AY1["Y-axis"]
        O1["Origin (0, 0)"]
    end
    
    subgraph Transform["Rotation by angle theta"]
        R1["Rotation Matrix R"]
        T1["theta radians"]
    end
    
    subgraph Result["Transformed Plane"]
        P2["Point P prime = (x prime, y prime)"]
        AX2["X prime axis"]
        AY2["Y prime axis"]
        O2["Origin (0, 0)"]
    end
    
    P1 --> R1
    R1 --> P2
    T1 --> R1
    
    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P2 fill:#ffe1e1
    style R1 fill:#fff4e1
    style T1 fill:#e1ffe1
```

*Logic:* The original point $P$ enters the rotation matrix $R$ along with the rotation angle $\theta$, and the matrix multiplication produces the transformed point $P'$ in the new coordinate frame.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Decision Tree for Choosing a Transformation

This is a flowchart a KTU student can use during exams to select the correct transformation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["What is the data issue?"]) --> Q1{"Is the data\nspanning large ranges?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Log["Use log transformation\nscale_x_log10()"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Is the data\ncyclical or angular?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Polar["Use polar coordinates\ncoord_polar()"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Are X and Y\ninterchangeable?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Flip["Use coordinate flip\ncoord_flip()"]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{"Is there\noverplotting?"}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Jitter["Use position jitter\ngeom_jitter() or geom_count()"]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{"Is the data\nin wide format?"}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Pivot["Reshape with tidyr\npivot_longer()"]
    Q5 -->|No| Q6{"Are summary\nstatistics needed?"}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Stat["Use stat transformation\nstat_summary()"]
    Q6 -->|No| Done(["Use default\nno transformation needed"])
    
    style Start fill:#e1f5ff
    style Log fill:#ffe1e1
    style Polar fill:#e1ffe1
    style Flip fill:#f5e1ff
    style Jitter fill:#fff4e1
    style Pivot fill:#ffe1f5
    style Stat fill:#e1ffe1
    style Done fill:#e1e1ff
```

*Logic:* This is a **binary decision tree** for visualization diagnostics. Students should walk through each question to arrive at the correct transformation choice — a common KTU problem-solving approach.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Composite Transformation Order (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        IP["Input Point\n(x, y, 1)"]
    end
    
    subgraph SBlock["Scale Block S"]
        SF["Multiply by sx and sy"]
    end
    
    subgraph RBlock["Rotation Block R"]
        RF["Apply cos and sin of theta"]
    end
    
    subgraph TBlock["Translation Block T"]
        TF["Add tx and ty"]
    end
    
    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"]
        OP["Output Point\n(x prime, y prime, 1)"]
    end
    
    IP --> SF
    SF --> RF
    RF --> TF
    TF --> OP
    
    style IP fill:#e1f5ff
    style SF fill:#ffe1e1
    style RF fill:#fff4e1
    style TF fill:#e1ffe1
    style OP fill:#f5e1ff
```

*Logic:* The **composite transformation pipeline** processes input points sequentially: scale first, then rotate, then translate. The final point is the result of all three operations applied in this fixed order (right-to-left matrix multiplication in linear algebra terms).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define the term "transformation" in the context of data visualization with R. List any FOUR types of transformations supported by ggplot2.**

**Model Answer:**

A **transformation** in data visualization is a mathematical operation that alters the position, scale, orientation, or statistical representation of data points or graphical elements to enhance the interpretability of a visual output. In R's `ggplot2`, transformations are implemented through layered functions that follow the Grammar of Graphics philosophy.

The **four major types** of transformations in `ggplot2` are:

1. **Scale Transformations** — e.g., `scale_x_log10()`, `scale_y_sqrt()` — modify axis scales non-linearly.
2. **Coordinate Transformations** — e.g., `coord_flip()`, `coord_polar()` — change the coordinate system.
3. **Statistical Transformations** — e.g., `stat_bin()`, `stat_smooth()` — summarize raw data into visual summaries.
4. **Position Transformations** — e.g., `position_jitter()`, `position_dodge()` — adjust element placement to reduce overplotting.

> *(Word count: ~85 words. Marks: Definition = 1, List with examples = 2)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between `scale_x_log10()` and `coord_trans(x = "log10")` in R. Why does this distinction matter for data visualization?**

**Model Answer:**

Both functions apply a logarithmic transformation, but they operate at **different stages** of the ggplot2 rendering pipeline.

| Aspect | `scale_x_log10()` | `coord_trans(x = "log10")` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stage of application** | Before statistical computation | After statistical computation |
| **Effect on statistics** | Stats computed in log space | Stats computed in original space, then transformed |
| **Effect on geom layers** | All downstream layers use log scale | Layers receive original-scale data |
| **Use case** | When log space analysis is desired | When preserving original linear relationships is needed |

**Why it matters:** When using `stat_smooth()` (linear regression line) with `coord_trans`, the line is fitted in original space and then visually compressed. With `scale_x_log10`, the fit is performed in log space, producing a different curve. Choosing the wrong one leads to **misleading trend lines** — a critical error in data analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 4 Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **coordinate transformations** in data visualization. With suitable R code examples, describe how `coord_flip()` and `coord_polar()` differ in their mathematical and visual effects. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A dataset contains house prices ranging from ₹50,000 to ₹50,000,000 (six orders of magnitude). Describe and justify the most appropriate scale transformation to visualize this data, including the relevant R code. Show the mathematical reasoning behind your choice. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of Coordinate Transformation (1 mark):**
A **coordinate transformation** changes the **reference frame** in which data points are plotted, without altering the underlying data values. In `ggplot2`, this is implemented through the `coord_*()` family of functions.

**Step 2 — `coord_flip()` Explanation (2 marks):**

```r
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, y = hwy)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  coord_flip()
```

**Mathematical form:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= y \\
y' &= x
\end{aligned}
$$

This swaps the X and Y axes, which is useful when category labels on the X-axis are long and would overlap in horizontal orientation. The **mathematical identity** is preserved (no scaling, no rotation), only the **display orientation** changes.

**Step 3 — `coord_polar()` Explanation (3 marks):**

```r
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, y = hwy, fill = class)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_polar()
```

**Mathematical form (Cartesian → Polar):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \\
\theta &= \arctan\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The data point's **X-coordinate becomes the angle** $\theta$ and the **Y-coordinate becomes the radius** $r$. This produces circular bar charts (rose plots) that are ideal for **cyclical data** like months of the year, wind direction, or clock times. The visual effect is that bars radiate from a central point.

**Step 4 — Comparison (1 mark):**
`coord_flip()` is a **discrete axis swap** (linear → linear), while `coord_polar()` is a **non-linear topological mapping** (linear → circular). They serve fundamentally different visualization purposes.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Problem Diagnosis (1 mark):**
The data range spans **₹50,000 to ₹50,000,000**, which is a **ratio of 1:1000** (three orders of magnitude). On a linear scale, values below ₹5,000,000 would appear as nearly invisible bars near zero.

**Step 2 — Justification of Log Transformation (2 marks):**
A **logarithmic scale transformation** is appropriate because:
- It **compresses** multiplicative ranges into additive ranges.
- It **equalizes** visual weight across orders of magnitude.
- The logarithm is the **inverse of the exponential**, and since house prices tend to grow exponentially, log scale linearizes the relationship.

**Step 3 — Mathematical Justification (2 marks):**

The transformation function is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' = \log_{10}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying this to the extremes:
- Lower bound: $\log_{10}(50{,}000) = 4.699$
- Upper bound: $\log_{10}(50{,}000{,}000) = 7.699$

The transformed range is $[4.699, 7.699]$, a span of only **3 units** — a perfectly linear, visualizable range.

**Step 4 — R Code Implementation (2 marks):**

```r
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(houses, aes(x = price)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_comma()) +
  labs(title = "House Price Distribution (Log Scale)",
       x = "Price (log10 scale, in INR)",
       y = "Count")
```

The function `scale_x_log10()` applies the log transformation to the X-axis, and `label_comma()` formats the tick labels with thousand separators for readability.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 4 Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** With neat diagrams and mathematical formulations, explain the following geometric transformations: **(i) Translation, (ii) Rotation, (iii) Scaling**. Show the 3×3 homogeneous coordinate matrices for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the role of **statistical transformations** in ggplot2. Write R code that demonstrates the use of `stat_smooth()` to overlay a linear regression line on a scatter plot, and explain the mathematical method used to fit the line. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of Geometric Transformations (1 mark):**
Geometric transformations are **matrix-based operations** that map points from one location to another in a 2D plane. They are represented using **3×3 homogeneous matrices** to enable translation, which is not possible with a 2×2 matrix.

**Step 2 — Translation (2 marks):**
Translation shifts every point by a constant vector $(t_x, t_y)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x + t_x \\
y' &= y + t_y
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**In R:** `theme(plot.margin = margin(t = 10, r = 10, b = 10, l = 10))` shifts the entire plot region.

**Step 3 — Rotation (2 marks):**
Rotation by angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise about the origin:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x \cos\theta - y \sin\theta \\
y' &= x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**In R:** `theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))` rotates X-axis labels by 45°.

**Step 4 — Scaling (2 marks):**
Scaling by factors $(s_x, s_y)$ about the origin:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= s_x \cdot x \\
y' &= s_y \cdot y
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

When $s_x = s_y = 2$, the figure doubles in size. When $s_x \neq s_y$, the figure is **stretched** anisotropically. In R, `scale_x_log10()` is a **non-linear conceptual scaling** that compresses the axis exponentially.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of Statistical Transformations (1 mark):**
A **statistical transformation** (`stat_*()`) takes raw input data and applies a mathematical function to compute a **summary representation** suitable for plotting. In `ggplot2`, every geom has a default `stat` and vice versa.

**Step 2 — Role in ggplot2 (2 marks):**
- Converts raw observations into visual primitives (bins, counts, fitted curves).
- Decouples **data representation** from **geometric representation** — the Grammar of Graphics principle.
- Enables reproducibility: the same data can be visualized with different stats to reveal different patterns.

**Step 3 — R Code for `stat_smooth()` (2 marks):**

```r
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  stat_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Engine Displacement vs. Highway MPG",
       x = "Displacement (L)",
       y = "Highway MPG")
```

**Step 4 — Mathematical Method (2 marks):**
`stat_smooth(method = "lm")` fits a **linear regression model** using **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**. The model is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the coefficients are computed by minimizing the **sum of squared residuals**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\beta_0, \beta_1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The solution is obtained via the **normal equations**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_1 &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2} \\
\beta_0 &= \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

The shaded band (`se = TRUE`) represents the **95% confidence interval** of the regression line, computed from the standard error of the residuals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing `scale_*` with `coord_*` (–2 marks typical):** Many students interchange these. **Scale transformations** modify the axis data values; **coordinate transformations** modify the visual reference frame.
> 2. **Forgetting the Jacobian correction in log-scale histograms (–2 marks):** When asked about density preservation, students often forget the $x \ln(10)$ correction factor.
> 3. **Matrix multiplication order (–1 to –3 marks):** Composite transformations follow a **strict right-to-left order** (last listed operation applied first to the point). Reversing this order gives a different result.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions of linear regression (–1 mark):** When explaining `stat_smooth(method = "lm")`, students must mention that OLS assumes **linearity**, **independence**, **homoscedasticity**, and **normality of residuals**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- [ ] A **transformation** maps data from one representation to another; it is the cornerstone of the Grammar of Graphics in R.
- [ ] **Four types**: Geometric (matrix-based), Coordinate (system-based), Statistical (summary-based), and Data Reshaping (tidy-based).
- [ ] **Translation** uses a 3×3 matrix because the operation $(x + t_x)$ cannot be represented by a 2×2 linear map.
- [ ] **Rotation matrix** for counter-clockwise rotation by $\theta$: 
  $$
  R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
  $$
- [ ] **Log scale** is the go-to transformation for data spanning **multiple orders of magnitude** (e.g., prices, populations).
- [ ] `scale_x_log10()` transforms data **before** stat computation; `coord_trans(x = "log10")` transforms **after**.
- [ ] `coord_polar()` uses the conversion $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \ \theta = \arctan(y/x)$ to produce circular plots.
- [ ] `stat_smooth(method = "lm")` fits a line via **Ordinary Least Squares** — minimize $\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$.
- [ ] The **composite transformation** $M = T \cdot R \cdot S$ is applied to a point as $P' = M \cdot P$ — **right-to-left** in linear algebra convention.
- [ ] **`pivot_longer()`** converts wide to long format — the first step in tidying data for ggplot2.
- [ ] **Reflection** across Y-axis: $x' = -x$; in R this is `scale_x_reverse()`.
- [ ] For **density-corrected log histograms**, multiply density by $x \ln(10)$ to preserve area.
- [ ] **Shear transformation** $H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & k_x \\ k_y & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is used to simulate **italic** or **3D-projection** effects.
- [ ] When asked "which transformation", always start with the **diagnostic question**: *Is the issue scale, shape, orientation, or summary?*
- [ ] **Homogeneous coordinates** add a third row/column of $(0, 0, 1)$ to make translation expressible as matrix multiplication.

---

*End of Module 4 — Transformations Notes*
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — PBCDT604*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binning Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Binning Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Binning Data** (also known as **Data Binning** or **Discretization**) is a data preprocessing technique in which continuous numerical variables are transformed into a finite set of discrete categorical intervals, known as *bins* or *buckets*. Each original data point is then mapped to one of these bins based on a defined boundary condition.

In the R programming environment (as per the KTU PBCDT604 syllabus), binning is principally executed using the **`cut()`** function, which categorizes a numeric vector into a factor of specified levels (bins) by slicing the continuous range into discrete intervals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Binning falls under *Data Transformation and Preprocessing* in R. Students are expected to know how to convert continuous variables into categorical factors using `cut()`, `ifelse()`, and the `Hmisc::cut2()` functions, and to compute frequency tables from the resulting binned factor.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **postmaster** at a large post office receiving thousands of letters a day, each stamped with a different weight — some 12 grams, some 47 grams, some 350 grams. If you tried to calculate postage by treating each weight as unique, you would have to maintain millions of rules.

Instead, postal services **group** letters into weight "slots":
- 0–20 g
- 21–50 g
- 51–100 g
- 101–250 g
- 251–500 g

Every letter is then dropped into the slot it belongs to. You have *binned* a continuous variable (weight in grams) into a discrete categorical variable (postal slab). This is exactly what binning does to numbers in a dataset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bin at all?**
> 1. To reduce the effect of minor observation errors (smoothing).
> 2. To convert continuous predictors into categorical predictors for **classification algorithms** (Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes, Decision Trees).
> 3. To enable **frequency distribution tables and histograms** (which require bins).
> 4. To make data **easier to visualize and interpret**.

---

## 1.3 Types of Binning (Classification by Strategy)

| # | Binning Strategy | Description | Common Use |
|---|------------------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | **Equal-Width Binning** | Range is divided into *k* intervals of identical width | Histograms with uniform width |
| 2 | **Equal-Frequency Binning** | Each bin contains (approximately) the same number of observations | Quantile-based discretization |
| 3 | **Custom / Manual Binning** | User defines explicit cut points based on domain knowledge | Age groups, income brackets, grade boundaries |
| 4 | **k-Means Binning** | 1-D clustering (k-means on a single feature) to find natural breaks | Unsupervised discretization |

> [!NOTE]
> In R's base package, **`cut()`** performs **equal-width** and **custom** binning. For **equal-frequency** binning, R users typically use **`Hmisc::cut2()`** or compute quantile-based breaks manually.

---

## 1.4 Formal Mathematical Setup

Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n\}$ be a one-dimensional continuous numeric vector. A **binning function** $\beta$ maps $X$ into a finite ordered set of $k$ bins:

$$\beta : X \rightarrow B = \{B_1, B_2, B_3, \ldots, B_k\}$$

such that every element $x_i$ belongs to **exactly one** bin:

$$\forall\, x_i \in X,\; \exists\, j \in \{1,\ldots,k\} \text{ such that } x_i \in B_j$$

and the bins form a **partition** of the data range (with the caveat that `NA` values may fall outside any bin):

$$\bigcup_{j=1}^{k} B_j \subseteq X, \qquad B_j \cap B_m = \emptyset \text{ for } j \neq m$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the `cut()` Function

The signature in base R is:

```r
cut(x, breaks, labels = NULL, include.lowest = FALSE,
    right = TRUE, dig.lab = 3, ordered_result = FALSE,
    ...)
```

### Argument-by-Argument Logic

- **`x`** → A numeric vector to be binned (the continuous variable).
- **`breaks`** → Either:
  - A **numeric vector** of 2 or more unique cut points (length must equal `number of bins + 1`), **or**
  - A **single integer** specifying the desired number of bins. R will then compute "pretty" cut points automatically using `pretty()`.
- **`labels`** → A character vector of length equal to the number of bins. If `NULL`, R generates interval labels such as `(10,20]`, `(20,30]`. Set `labels = FALSE` to return integer codes (1, 2, 3, ...) instead.
- **`include.lowest`** → A logical value. If `TRUE`, the smallest value of `x` is forced into the lowest bin (useful when the lowest value equals a break point).
- **`right`** → Logical. If `TRUE` (default), intervals are *right-open / left-closed*, e.g., `(10, 20]`. If `FALSE`, intervals are *left-open / right-closed*, e.g., `[10, 20)`.
- **`ordered_result`** → If `TRUE`, the returned factor is an *ordered* factor, useful for ordinal categorical variables (Low < Medium < High).
- **`dig.lab`** → Integer controlling how many digits are used in auto-generated labels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Right-closed vs Left-closed — The Most Common Exam Trap:**
> With `right = TRUE`, a value exactly equal to a break point (say, 20) falls into the bin to the **right** of that break (the higher bin). Students frequently misassign boundary values.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Bin Width (Equal-Width)** | $w = \dfrac{\max(X) - \min(X)}{k}$ | Range divided by $k$ bins; dimensionless when $X$ is unitless |
| **Bin Edges (Equal-Width)** | $e_j = \min(X) + (j-1) \cdot w$, for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, k+1$ | $e_1 = \min(X)$, $e_{k+1} = \max(X)$ |
| **Bin Size (Equal-Frequency)** | Each bin $\approx \dfrac{n}{k}$ observations | $n$ = sample size, $k$ = number of bins |
| **Quantile Breaks** | $e_j = Q\!\left(\dfrac{j-1}{k}\right)$ for $j = 1, \ldots, k+1$ | $Q$ = quantile function; $e_1 = Q(0) = \min(X)$ |
| **Bin Frequency** | $f_j = \vert B_j \vert$ | Count of observations in bin $j$ |
| **Relative Frequency** | $p_j = \dfrac{f_j}{n}$ | Sums to $\vert 1 \vert$ over all bins |
| **Cumulative Frequency** | $F_j = \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i$ | Non-decreasing |
| **Right-closed Interval** | $(a_j, a_{j+1}]$ | Default in `cut()` |
| **Left-closed Interval** | $[a_j, a_{j+1})$ | Use `right = FALSE` in `cut()` |
| **Number of Bins (Sturges' Rule)** | $k = \lceil \log_2(n) + 1 \rceil$ | Histogram reference rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the question states "the interval $(a, b]$", the value $b$ is included and the value $a$ is excluded. This is the **default** in R's `cut()`. Forgetting this costs students 2 marks routinely.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Binning

1. **Telecommunications (KTU ECE relevance):** Binning signal-to-noise ratios into "Excellent / Good / Fair / Poor" bins for consumer dashboards.
2. **Civil Engineering:** Converting continuous concrete compressive strength (MPa) into IS-code grade categories (M20, M25, M30…).
3. **Machine Learning Pipelines:** Logistic regression on continuous variables often performs better after equal-frequency binning (a coarse form of *smoothing*).
4. **Quality Control (Six Sigma):** Process measurements grouped into control zones.
5. **Finance:** Credit scores compressed into rating buckets (AAA, AA, A, BBB, ...).

---

## 2.4 Comparison of Binning Strategies — When to Use What

| Strategy | Strength | Weakness | Best Use Case |
|----------|----------|----------|---------------|
| **Equal-Width** | Simple, intuitive, visually uniform | Sensitive to outliers (one bin becomes empty) | Uniformly distributed data |
| **Equal-Frequency** | Robust to outliers; each bin informative | Bin widths vary; may not align with natural thresholds | Skewed data, ML preprocessing |
| **Custom** | Respects domain knowledge | Subjective; requires expertise | Demographic / grade data |
| **k-Means** | Adapts to data shape | Random initialization; need to choose $k$ | Mixed-distribution data |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Equal-Width Bin Edges

**Problem Setup:** Given a numeric vector $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ and a desired number of bins $k$, derive the $k+1$ bin edges.

**Step 1 — Compute the observed range.**

$$R = \max(X) - \min(X)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bin width.**

$$w = \frac{R}{k} = \frac{\max(X) - \min(X)}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Define the edges using a closed-form expression.**

For each bin index $j \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, k+1\}$, the $j$-th edge is:

$$e_j = \min(X) + (j - 1) \cdot w$$

**Step 4 — Verification for endpoints.**

$$e_1 = \min(X) + (1-1)\cdot w = \min(X)$$
$$e_{k+1} = \min(X) + k \cdot w = \min(X) + \frac{\max(X) - \min(X)}{k}\cdot k = \max(X)$$

**Step 5 — Each observation is then assigned by:**

$$\beta(x_i) = B_j \quad \text{iff} \quad e_j < x_i \leq e_{j+1} \text{ (default right = TRUE)}$$

The "Why": Equal-width binning preserves visual symmetry in histograms. The "How": the linear spacing $(j-1)\cdot w$ ensures uniform coverage of the data range.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Equal-Frequency (Quantile) Bin Edges

**Step 1 — Decide target frequency.**

Each bin should hold approximately $\dfrac{n}{k}$ observations.

**Step 2 — Compute empirical quantiles.**

$$e_j = Q\!\left(\frac{j-1}{k}\right), \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, k+1$$

where $Q(p)$ denotes the empirical $p$-th quantile of $X$.

**Step 3 — In R, this is achieved via:**

```r
breaks_vec <- quantile(x, probs = seq(0, 1, length.out = k + 1))
binned     <- cut(x, breaks = breaks_vec, include.lowest = TRUE)
```

**Step 4 — Verify frequency count.**

```r
table(binned)
```

must produce counts of approximately $n/k$ in every bin.

The "Why": equal-frequency binning is *information-preserving* per bin — no bin is sparse. The "How": quantile sampling guarantees equal count regardless of skew.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Equal-Width)

Let $X = \{2, 5, 7, 10, 14, 18, 22, 25, 30\}$ with $n = 9$, and let $k = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute the range.**

$$\max(X) = 30, \quad \min(X) = 2, \quad R = 30 - 2 = 28$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bin width.**

$$w = \frac{28}{3} \approx 9.333\ldots$$

**Step 3 — Compute the four edges.**

$$e_1 = 2 + 0\cdot\frac{28}{3} = 2.000$$
$$e_2 = 2 + 1\cdot\frac{28}{3} \approx 11.333$$
$$e_3 = 2 + 2\cdot\frac{28}{3} \approx 20.667$$
$$e_4 = 2 + 3\cdot\frac{28}{3} = 30.000$$

**Step 4 — Assign each observation (right-closed: `(a, b]`).**

$$B_1 : (2.00,\; 11.33] \;\Rightarrow\; \{5, 7, 10\}$$
$$B_2 : (11.33,\; 20.67] \;\Rightarrow\; \{14, 18\}$$
$$B_3 : (20.67,\; 30.00] \;\Rightarrow\; \{22, 25, 30\}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of partition property.**

$$|B_1| + |B_2| + |B_3| = 3 + 2 + 3 = 8$$

(Note: the value $2$ itself is not assigned because $B_1$ is *right-open*. This is where `include.lowest = TRUE` would rescue it.)

---

## 3.4 R Implementation Walkthrough

```r
# --- Setup ---------------------------------------------------------------
x <- c(2, 5, 7, 10, 14, 18, 22, 25, 30)

# --- (A) Equal-Width Binning using cut() with an integer break count -----
bins_eqw <- cut(x, breaks = 3, labels = c("Low", "Mid", "High"))
print(bins_eqw)

# --- (B) Equal-Width Binning with explicit numeric breaks ----------------
edges <- c(2, 11.333, 20.667, 30)
bins_explicit <- cut(x,
                     breaks = edges,
                     labels = c("Low", "Mid", "High"),
                     include.lowest = TRUE)
print(bins_explicit)
print(table(bins_explicit))         # frequency table

# --- (C) Equal-Frequency Binning using quantile() ------------------------
probs  <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 4)   # 4 edges -> 3 bins
q_breaks <- quantile(x, probs = probs)
bins_eqf <- cut(x,
                breaks = q_breaks,
                labels = c("Q1", "Q2", "Q3"),
                include.lowest = TRUE)
print(bins_eqf)
print(table(bins_eqf))

# --- (D) Custom (Manual) Binning ----------------------------------------
bins_custom <- cut(x,
                   breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 30),
                   labels = c("0-10", "11-20", "21-30"),
                   right = TRUE)
print(bins_custom)
print(table(bins_custom))

# --- (E) Ordered Factor Result -------------------------------------------
bins_ord <- cut(x, breaks = 3, labels = c("Low", "Mid", "High"),
                ordered_result = TRUE)
class(bins_ord)                      # "ordered" "factor"
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **(D)**, the manual breaks `c(0, 10, 20, 30)` produce cleaner, human-readable labels than the floating-point edges from **(A)**. This is why domain-driven custom binning is preferred in real engineering reports.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Arguments to Outputs

| Argument | Effect on Output Factor | Typical Mistake by Students |
|----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| `breaks = 3` | Three bins, auto edges via `pretty()` | Forgetting that this calls `pretty()`, not pure equal-width |
| `breaks = c(a,b,c)` | Two bins: `(a, b]` and `(b, c]` | Writing 3 bins; in fact `length(breaks)` bins = `n` means `n-1` bins |
| `labels = FALSE` | Returns integer codes (1, 2, …) | Expecting it to suppress labels but still produce a factor |
| `include.lowest = TRUE` | Smallest $x$ forced into first bin | Omitting this when `min(x)` equals an edge |
| `right = FALSE` | Intervals become `[a, b)` | Misreading the boundary convention in exam answers |
| `ordered_result = TRUE` | Returns an `ordered` factor | Treating it as the default; by default it is *not* ordered |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — How `cut()` Transforms a Numeric Vector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Numeric Vector X] --> B{Choose Binning Strategy}
    B --> C1[Strategy 1 Equal Width breaks integer]
    B --> C2[Strategy 2 Custom Breaks numeric vector]
    B --> C3[Strategy 3 Quantile Equal Frequency using quantile]
    C1 --> D1[R calls pretty to auto compute edges]
    C2 --> D2[Edges supplied directly by user]
    C3 --> D3[Edges derived from empirical quantiles]
    D1 --> E[Apply cut function with chosen options]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{Set include lowest and right flag}
    F --> G[Map each x_i to interval B_j]
    G --> H[Return factor or ordered factor]
    H --> I[Optional table for frequency]
    I --> J[Optional hist or barplot for visualization]
    J --> K[END Categorical Bins Ready for Analysis]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Binning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Raw Numeric Vector X of size n] --> PRE[Preprocessing Step]
    PRE -->|handle NA| CLEAN[Cleaned Vector X dash]
    CLEAN --> PARAM[Parameter Selection Module]
    PARAM -->|breaks| EDGE[Edge Computation Module]
    PARAM -->|labels| LBL[Label Generation Module]
    PARAM -->|right and include lowest| FLAG[Boundary Logic Module]
    EDGE --> CORE[cut Function Core]
    LBL --> CORE
    FLAG --> CORE
    CORE --> OUT[Factor Output with k Bins]
    OUT --> FREQ[Frequency Table Function]
    OUT --> VIZ[Visualization Histogram or Barplot]
    OUT --> ML[Downstream ML Classifier]
    FREQ --> REPORT[Engineering Report]
    VIZ --> REPORT
    ML --> REPORT
```

---

## 4.3 Mapping of Edge Cases in `cut()`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Observation xi arrives] --> Q{Does xi fall inside any bin interval}
    Q -->|Yes and right is TRUE| R1[Assigned to right closed bin a j, a j plus 1]
    Q -->|Yes and right is FALSE| R2[Assigned to left closed bin a j, a j plus 1]
    Q -->|No and equals smallest edge| S1{Is include lowest TRUE}
    Q -->|No and above max edge| S2[Marked as NA]
    S1 -->|Yes| R1
    S1 -->|No| S3[Marked as NA]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading note for the diagram:** All node labels use plain uppercase alphanumeric text (e.g., `R1`, `S2`) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards. No markdown formatting is embedded inside the Mermaid labels.

---

## 4.4 Visualizing the Three Binning Strategies on the Same Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Strategy_Comparison
    A1[Data X 2,5,7,10,14,18,22,25,30]
    A1 --> B1[Equal Width k equals 3 w approx 9.33]
    A1 --> B2[Equal Frequency k equals 3 each bin has 3 obs]
    A1 --> B3[Custom 0 to 10, 11 to 20, 21 to 30]
    B1 --> C1[Bins Low 3 obs Mid 2 obs High 4 obs]
    B2 --> C2[Bins Q1 3 obs Q2 3 obs Q3 3 obs]
    B3 --> C3[Bins 0 to 10 has 4 obs 11 to 20 has 2 obs 21 to 30 has 3 obs]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PBCDT604, Module 4]**

**Q: Define data binning. List any two functions in R used for binning a continuous variable.**

**Model Answer:**

*Data binning* (or *discretization*) is the process of converting a continuous numeric variable into a discrete categorical variable by grouping values into intervals called **bins**. Each original observation is mapped into exactly one bin based on a set of cut points.

Two R functions commonly used for binning are:

1. **`cut(x, breaks, labels)`** — base R function that segments a numeric vector `x` into intervals defined by `breaks`, returning a factor.
2. **`Hmisc::cut2(x, g = k)`** — function from the `Hmisc` package that performs **equal-frequency** binning (each of the `g` groups contains approximately the same number of observations).

(Other acceptable answers: `ifelse()` with nested conditions, `findInterval()`, `discretize()` from the `arules` package.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBCDT604, Module 4]**

**Q: Differentiate between equal-width binning and equal-frequency binning.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Equal-Width Binning | Equal-Frequency Binning |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Definition** | Range is divided into $k$ intervals of identical width $w = (\max - \min)/k$. | Each bin contains (approximately) the same number of observations ($n/k$). |
| **Implementation in R** | `cut(x, breaks = k)` | `Hmisc::cut2(x, g = k)` or `cut(x, breaks = quantile(x, probs = seq(0,1,1/k)))` |
| **Strength** | Simple, intuitive, visually uniform | Robust to outliers; every bin informative |
| **Weakness** | Sensitive to outliers (may produce empty bins) | Bin widths vary; may not align with domain meaning |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PBCDT604, Module 4]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the syntax and arguments of the `cut()` function in R with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the vector `x <- c(5, 12, 18, 25, 33, 41, 47, 55, 62, 70)`, perform **equal-width binning** into 4 bins using `cut()`. Write the R code and display the resulting factor along with the frequency table. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Syntax:**

```r
cut(x, breaks, labels = NULL,
    include.lowest = FALSE,
    right = TRUE,
    dig.lab = 3,
    ordered_result = FALSE, ...)
```

**Argument-by-argument explanation (each ~1 mark):**

1. **`x`** — A numeric vector to be discretized.
2. **`breaks`** — Either a numeric vector of cut points OR a single integer specifying the desired number of bins. Length of vector = number of bins + 1.
3. **`labels`** — Character vector of bin names. If `NULL`, R auto-generates labels like `(a, b]`. Use `labels = FALSE` to get integer codes.
4. **`include.lowest`** — Logical; if `TRUE`, the lowest value is always included in the first bin.
5. **`right`** — Logical; if `TRUE` (default), intervals are right-closed `(a, b]`; if `FALSE`, intervals are left-closed `[a, b)`.
6. **`dig.lab`** — Number of digits used in auto-generated labels (default 3).
7. **`ordered_result`** — If `TRUE`, returns an *ordered* factor.

**Illustrative example:**

```r
ages <- c(12, 19, 25, 38, 47, 53, 61, 70)
age_bins <- cut(ages,
                breaks = c(0, 18, 35, 55, 75),
                labels = c("Child", "Young Adult", "Adult", "Senior"),
                include.lowest = TRUE,
                right = TRUE)
print(age_bins)
table(age_bins)
```

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the data and parameters.**

Given `x <- c(5, 12, 18, 25, 33, 41, 47, 55, 62, 70)`, with $n = 10$ observations and $k = 4$ bins.

**Step 2 — Compute the range and bin width analytically (for full marks, show this).**

$$\min(x) = 5,\quad \max(x) = 70,\quad R = 65$$
$$w = \frac{R}{k} = \frac{65}{4} = 16.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute edges.**

$$e_1 = 5.00,\quad e_2 = 21.25,\quad e_3 = 37.50,\quad e_4 = 53.75,\quad e_5 = 70.00$$

**Step 4 — R code (relying on `cut()` to compute edges automatically):**

```r
x <- c(5, 12, 18, 25, 33, 41, 47, 55, 62, 70)
bins <- cut(x,
            breaks = 4,
            labels = c("Bin1", "Bin2", "Bin3", "Bin4"),
            include.lowest = TRUE)
print(bins)
table(bins)
```

**Step 5 — Expected output.**

```
 [1] Bin1 Bin1 Bin1 Bin2 Bin2 Bin3 Bin3 Bin4 Bin4 Bin4
Levels: Bin1 < Bin2 < Bin3 < Bin4

bins
Bin1 Bin2 Bin3 Bin4
   3    2    2    3
```

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** Three observations fall in `Bin1` (5, 12, 18), two in `Bin2` (25, 33), two in `Bin3` (41, 47), and three in `Bin4` (55, 62, 70). The frequency distribution is approximately uniform because the data is well-spread.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PBCDT604, Module 4]**

**Q: (a)** What is equal-frequency binning? Write an R program to perform equal-frequency binning into 3 bins on the vector `marks <- c(34, 56, 78, 89, 92, 45, 67, 73, 81, 95)`. Display the frequency table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the parameters `right` and `include.lowest` in `cut()` with a small example. What happens if a value exactly equals a bin edge in each case? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Conceptual explanation (2 marks):**
Equal-frequency (quantile) binning divides the data such that each bin contains approximately $n/k$ observations. It is implemented in R by computing quantile-based break points via `quantile()` and passing them to `cut()`.

**Computation (2 marks):**

$n = 10$, $k = 3$, target frequency $\approx 10/3 \approx 3.33$ per bin.

$$\text{Break probs} = \left(0,\; \tfrac{1}{3},\; \tfrac{2}{3},\; 1\right)$$

$$\text{Breaks} = Q(0),\; Q(1/3),\; Q(2/3),\; Q(1)$$

**R Program (3 marks):**

```r
marks <- c(34, 56, 78, 89, 92, 45, 67, 73, 81, 95)

# Step 1: compute quantile-based break points
probs    <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 4)         # 0, 1/3, 2/3, 1
q_breaks <- quantile(marks, probs = probs)
print(q_breaks)
#  0%  33.33%  66.67%   100%
#   34      62      80    95

# Step 2: apply cut() with these breaks
marks_binned <- cut(marks,
                    breaks = q_breaks,
                    labels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"),
                    include.lowest = TRUE)
print(marks_binned)
table(marks_binned)
```

**Expected Output:**

```
 [1] Low Low High High High Low Medium Medium High High
Levels: Low < Medium < High

marks_binned
   Low Medium   High
     3       2      5
```

(Note: exact frequencies depend on R's default quantile algorithm — type 7. The point distribution is approximately equal.)

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explanation of `right` (3 marks):**

- `right = TRUE` (default): Intervals are of the form `(a, b]`. The right edge is **included** in the bin; the left edge is **excluded**.
- `right = FALSE`: Intervals are of the form `[a, b)`. The left edge is **included**; the right edge is **excluded**.

**Explanation of `include.lowest` (2 marks):**

- By default, `cut()` excludes the lowest edge from any bin, which can result in `NA` for observations that exactly equal the smallest break point.
- Setting `include.lowest = TRUE` forces the minimum value of `x` to be included in the first bin.

**Example demonstrating edge-case behavior (2 marks):**

```r
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40)

# Case 1: right = TRUE, include.lowest = FALSE
cut(x, breaks = c(10, 20, 30, 40), right = TRUE)
# Returns: NA (10,20] (20,30] (30,40]
#  -> 10 becomes NA

# Case 2: right = TRUE, include.lowest = TRUE
cut(x, breaks = c(10, 20, 30, 40), right = TRUE, include.lowest = TRUE)
# Returns: [10,20] (20,30] (30,40]
#  -> 10 included in first bin

# Case 3: right = FALSE, include.lowest = FALSE
cut(x, breaks = c(10, 20, 30, 40), right = FALSE)
# Returns: [10,20) [20,30) [30,40)
#  -> 40 becomes NA
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Boundary confusion:** Students frequently write `(a, b)` instead of `(a, b]` in viva/papers, costing 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting `include.lowest = TRUE`:** When `min(x)` matches a break, it silently turns into `NA`. Always check after binning with `sum(is.na(binned))`.
> 3. **Misreading `labels = FALSE`:** It does **not** mean "no labels"; it returns **integer codes** as a factor.
> 4. **Confusion between `breaks = k` (integer) vs `breaks = c(...)` (vector):** The first calls `pretty()` internally; the second uses supplied edges literally.
> 5. **Forgetting to install/load `Hmisc`** when recommending `cut2()` in the answer — examiners will deduct 1 mark for unrealizable code.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Binning = conversion of a continuous variable into discrete categorical bins.
- **Core R function:** `cut(x, breaks, labels, include.lowest, right, ordered_result)`.
- **Default interval convention:** Right-closed `(a, b]` with `right = TRUE`.
- **Equal-width bin width:** $w = (\max(X) - \min(X))/k$.
- **Equal-frequency target count:** $\approx n/k$ per bin, computed via `quantile()`.
- **Custom binning:** Use explicit numeric vector of break points.
- **`include.lowest = TRUE`** rescues `min(x)` from becoming `NA`.
- **`labels = FALSE`** returns integer codes (1, 2, 3, ...).
- **`ordered_result = TRUE`** returns an *ordered* factor — important for ordinal data (Low < Medium < High).
- **Sturges' rule** for histogram bin count: $k = \lceil \log_2(n) + 1 \rceil$.
- **Equal-frequency function outside base R:** `Hmisc::cut2(x, g = k)`.
- **Frequency table after binning:** Always use `table(binned_factor)`.
- **Visualization:** `hist()` uses binning internally; `plot(binned_factor)` shows a bar chart.
- **Engineering applications:** Signal quality grading, compressive strength categories, credit scoring, age groups.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Mixing up `(a, b]` vs `[a, b)` conventions in written answers.
- **Final sanity check after `cut()`:** Always inspect `sum(is.na(binned))` and `table(binned)` before proceeding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subsets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Subsets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Subsetting** in R is the formal, index-based operation of extracting one or more elements from a data structure (atomic vector, list, matrix, data frame, or tibble) by applying the bracket operator along with either a positive integer, negative integer, logical vector, or character name. According to the KTU 2024 scheme (Hadley Wickham's *Advanced R* canonical framework), subsetting is the act of *indexing* an object with `[`, *extracting* with `[[`, or *simplifying* with `$`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBCDT604 – Module 4):**
> Subsetting is the foundational operation that drives data wrangling in R. Mastery of the **three operators** — `[`, `[[`, `$` — and the **six data types** that can be passed inside them is the **single highest-weightage concept** for University ESE questions on data manipulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **library bookshelf with 5 numbered slots** (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) — each holding a different book.

- **Positive integers `[c(1, 4)]`** → "Give me the books in **slots 1 and 4**." (You keep the *order* of the slots you asked for, and duplicates are allowed.)
- **Negative integers `[-c(2, 5)]`** → "Give me the books in **all slots EXCEPT 2 and 5**." (You cannot mix positive and negative.)
- **Logical vector `[c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)]`** → "Hand me a book from a slot **only if the mask says TRUE**." (The mask must be the same length as the shelf, or a multiple of it.)
- **Character names `[c("math", "physics")]`** → "Hand me the book whose **label** is *math* or *physics*." (Only works for *named* objects.)
- **Empty `[]`** → "Give me **everything**." (Useful with assignment to replace contents.)
- **Zero `[0]`** → "Give me **nothing**." (Surprisingly useful for *zero-length assignment / structure reshaping*.)

This is the **mental model** you must internalize before any KTU board question.

## 1.3 The Three Subset Operators — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — The Subsetting Operator Trinity:**
> - `[` → **Subsetting** (always returns an object of the **same class** as the input).
> - `[[` → **Extraction** (returns a **single element**, dropping one level of the list/data-frame hierarchy).
> - `$` → **Extraction by name** (syntactic sugar for `[[` on lists and data frames).

| Operator | Name in R-lingo | Returns |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| `x[i]`   | Subset | Same class as `x` |
| `x[[i]]` | Extract | Single element |
| `x$i`    | Extract (name) | Single element |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Types of Subsetting — Structured Logic

R supports **six distinct ways** to pass the index argument. Each has a precise, deterministic behaviour mandated by the language specification.

### 2.1.1 Positive Integer Subsetting

**Logic:** "Return the elements at these positions, in this exact order."

- Behaviour: Duplicates are *preserved*; out-of-range indices return `NA`.
- Why: The most common form — corresponds to direct positional addressing.

### 2.1.2 Negative Integer Subsetting

**Logic:** "Return every element EXCEPT these positions."

- Behaviour: Cannot be mixed with positive integers; `0` is silently ignored.
- Why: Useful for *removing* columns/rows from a data frame.

### 2.1.3 Logical Subsetting

**Logic:** "Keep only those elements where the mask is `TRUE`."

- Behaviour: Mask is **recycled** if shorter than `x`; `NA` in mask → `NA` in output.
- Why: Enables **filtering** (e.g., `df[df$age >= 18, ]`).

### 2.1.4 Character (Named) Subsetting

**Logic:** "Return the element whose name matches the provided string."

- Behaviour: Works only on **named** objects; missing names return `NA`.
- Why: Used heavily in **list-of-lists** and **JSON-like** R structures.

### 2.1.5 Empty Subsetting `[]`

**Logic:** "Return the whole object."

- Behaviour: Preserves class; used in assignment to *replace all elements* (`x[] <- 0`).

### 2.1.6 Zero Subsetting `[0]`

**Logic:** "Return a zero-length object of the same class."

- Behaviour: Preserves class; used to *test* or *initialize* empty typed structures.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** The behaviour of the bracket operator is fully determined by the **type of vector** placed inside it. Memorize the table below — it is the single most-tested concept in this module.

| Index Type Passed to `[` | Behaviour on Atomic Vector | Behaviour on List | Behaviour on Data Frame |
|---|---|---|---|
| Positive Integer `c(2,4)` | Returns positions 2 & 4 (in that order) | Returns a **list** with those elements | Returns a **data frame** with those columns |
| Negative Integer `c(-1)` | Drops position 1 | Drops that list element | Drops that column |
| Logical `c(T,F,T)` | Returns elements where TRUE | Returns a sub-list | Returns a sub-data-frame (after row-filter) |
| Character (names) | Returns named elements | Returns sub-list | Returns those columns |
| Empty `[]` | Returns the entire vector | Returns the entire list | Returns the entire data frame |
| Zero `[0]` | Returns numeric(0) | Returns list() | Returns data.frame() |

## 2.3 Operator Selection Cheat Sheet

| If you need to... | Use |
|---|---|
| Select multiple elements, preserve class | `x[c(...)]` |
| Drill into a single element of a list | `x[[i]]` |
| Pull a column by name (data frame / list) | `x$name` or `x[["name"]]` |
| Apply a partial-match on a list name | `x$na` (matches `x$name` if unique) |
| Remove columns/rows by name | `x[-c(...)]` |
| Filter rows by condition | `df[df$col > val, ]` |
| Replace values in-place preserving shape | `x[i] <- newval` |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **bioinformatics pipelines**, subsetting is used to extract gene-expression rows for a specific tissue (`counts[counts$tissue == "Liver", ]`). In **IoT analytics** (a common KTU project domain), you subset a `data.frame` of sensor readings to retain only rows where `temperature > threshold`. In **financial modelling**, you extract a column by name (`df$PnL`) and pipe it into a vectorised function. Every downstream operation in the **tidyverse** (`dplyr::filter`, `dplyr::select`) is, internally, a wrapper around `[`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every example below is fully worked out. You should be able to reproduce the output **without running R** once you finish reading this section.

## 3.1 Subsetting Atomic Vectors

### Example 3.1.1 — Positive Integer Subsetting

```r
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)

x[c(1, 3)]
# Step 1: R sees a positive integer vector c(1, 3) of length 2.
# Step 2: It looks up positions 1 and 3 of x → values 10 and 30.
# Step 3: It returns a numeric vector of length 2.
# Output: [1] 10 30

x[c(1, 1, 4)]
# Duplicate indexing → value 10 is returned twice.
# Output: [1] 10 10 40

x[c(1, 99)]
# Position 99 does not exist → R returns NA.
# Output: [1] 10 NA
```

### Example 3.1.2 — Negative Integer Subsetting

```r
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)

x[-c(2, 4)]
# Step 1: R sees negative integers.
# Step 2: It drops positions 2 (value 20) and 4 (value 40).
# Output: [1] 10 30 50

x[-c(1, 1)]
# Step 1: R sees c(-1, -1).
# Step 2: It tries to drop position 1 twice → ERROR: "can't include negative
#         and positive indices" and "duplicate negatives are not allowed".
# Output: Error in x[-c(1, 1)] : only 0's may be mixed with negative subscripts
```

### Example 3.1.3 — Logical Subsetting

```r
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)

x[c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)]
# Step 1: Mask length = 5 = length(x). Aligned element-wise.
# Step 2: Keep positions where mask is TRUE: 1, 3, 5.
# Output: [1] 10 30 50

x[x > 25]
# Step 1: The expression x > 25 is vectorised.
# Step 2: Evaluates to: c(FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE).
# Step 3: Subsetting picks positions 3, 4, 5.
# Output: [1] 30 40 50

x[c(TRUE, FALSE)]   # Recycling rule
# Step 1: Mask length = 2; length(x) = 5.
# Step 2: R recycles mask: c(T, F, T, F, T).
# Step 3: Picks positions 1, 3, 5.
# Output: [1] 10 30 50
```

### Example 3.1.4 — Character (Named) Subsetting

```r
scores <- c(alice = 88, bob = 72, clara = 95, david = 60)

scores[c("alice", "clara")]
# Step 1: R looks up the names "alice" and "clara".
# Step 2: Returns the corresponding values 88 and 95.
# Output:  alice clara
#            88    95
```

## 3.2 Subsetting Matrices

A matrix is a 2D atomic vector with a `dim` attribute. Subsetting uses a comma-separated pair: `mat[row_index, col_index]`.

```r
m <- matrix(1:9, nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
#        [,1] [,2] [,3]
# [1,]     1    2    3
# [2,]     4    5    6
# [3,]     7    8    9

m[1, ]            # First row, all columns      → c(1, 2, 3)
m[, 2]            # All rows, second column     → c(2, 5, 8)
m[2, 3]           # Single element at (2,3)     → 6
m[c(1, 3), c(2, 3)]   # Sub-matrix
#        [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    2    3
# [2,]    8    9
m[-1, ]           # Drop first row
#        [,1] [,2] [,3]
# [1,]    4    5    6
# [2,]    7    8    9
m[ , "col2"]      # If dimnames set, supports character subsetting
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** Notice that `m[1, ]` returns a **numeric vector**, not a 1×3 matrix. To *preserve* the matrix class, use `m[1, , drop = FALSE]`. This is a **favourite KTU examiner trap**.

## 3.3 Subsetting Lists — The `[` vs `[[` vs `$` Distinction

This is the **most heavily tested** concept in the entire module.

```r
l <- list(a = 10, b = "hello", c = c(1, 2, 3))

# ---- [ operator (sub-setting) ----
l[1]
# Step 1: R returns a *list* of length 1, containing element 'a'.
# $a
# [1] 10
class(l[1])     # "list"

l[c("a", "c")]
# $a
# [1] 10
# $c
# [1] 1 2 3
class(l[c("a","c")])   # "list"

# ---- [[ operator (ex-tract) ----
l[[1]]
# Step 1: R drills *one level deeper* and returns the raw value 10.
# [1] 10
class(l[[1]])     # "numeric"

l[["a"]]          # 10
l[[c(1, 2)]]      # First element (which is a list), then 2nd element of that list.

# ---- $ operator (syntactic sugar) ----
l$a               # Equivalent to l[["a"]]  → 10
l$b               # → "hello"
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall (3-mark favourite):**
> - `l[1]` returns a **list** containing element 1.
> - `l[[1]]` returns the **value** of element 1 directly.
> - `l$a` is equivalent to `l[["a"]]` and **does partial matching** (e.g., `l$a` matches `l$abc` if `a` is unambiguous).

## 3.4 Subsetting Data Frames

A data frame is a **list of equal-length vectors** with class `data.frame`. Hence it inherits BOTH list-subsetting (`df$col`) AND matrix-subsetting (`df[row, col]`).

```r
df <- data.frame(
  name  = c("A", "B", "C", "D"),
  age   = c(20, 25, 30, 35),
  score = c(88, 72, 95, 60)
)

# 1. Column extraction (list-style)
df$name              # c("A", "B", "C", "D")
df[["age"]]          # c(20, 25, 30, 35)
df[c("name", "score")]   # Returns a 4×2 data frame

# 2. Row + Column extraction (matrix-style)
df[1, ]              # First row as a 1×3 data frame
df[ , "age"]         # Just the age column as a vector
df[1:2, c("name", "score")]

# 3. Logical row filtering (the bread-and-butter operation)
df[df$age > 22, ]
# Step 1: df$age > 22 evaluates to c(F, T, T, T).
# Step 2: Used as row index → keeps rows 2, 3, 4.
#    name age score
# 2    B  25    72
# 3    C  30    95
# 4    D  35    60

# 4. Combined column + row
df[df$score > 80, c("name", "score")]
```

## 3.5 Handling `NA` in Subsetting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `NA` in an index position is **propagated** through `[` and **errors** through `[[`. Use `which()` to convert logical `NA`s into concrete integer positions.

```r
x <- c(1, NA, 3, NA, 5)
x[x > 1]              # c(NA, 3, NA, 5)  ← NAs retained
x[which(x > 1)]       # c(3, 5)          ← NAs dropped
```

## 3.6 The `subset()` Function — A Readable Wrapper

`subset()` is a convenience function (NOT a replacement for `[`) that evaluates its argument in a non-standard way, allowing you to drop quotes and brackets on column names.

```r
subset(df, age > 22, select = c(name, score))
# Equivalent (almost) to:
# df[df$age > 22, c("name", "score")]

# Multiple conditions
subset(df, age > 22 & score > 70, select = name:score)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Use inside functions with caution:** `subset()` performs *non-standard evaluation*. In production code / programming assignments, prefer the explicit `df[df$age > 22, ]` form, which is **predictable** and **vectorised**.

## 3.7 Assignment — Subsetting as an L-value

Subsetting is not just for reading — it is also for **writing back** into specific positions.

```r
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
x[c(2, 4)] <- c(99, 88)
# x is now: 10 99 30 88 50

df <- data.frame(a = 1:3, b = 4:6)
df[df$a > 1, "b"] <- 0
#    a b
# 1  1 4
# 2  2 0
# 3  3 0
```

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| If your data structure is... | And you want... | Use... | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atomic vector | Multiple elements by position | `[c(...)]` | `x[c(1,3)]` |
| Atomic vector | All except some | `[-c(...)]` | `x[-2]` |
| Atomic vector | Filtered subset | `[logical]` | `x[x > 5]` |
| List | Sub-list (preserve class) | `[c(...)]` | `l[c("a","c")]` |
| List | Single element value | `[[i]]` or `$` | `l[["a"]]` |
| Data frame | One column (vector) | `$` or `[[ ]]` | `df$age` |
| Data frame | Multiple columns (data frame) | `[c(...)]` | `df[c("a","b")]` |
| Data frame | Filtered rows | `[row_logical, ]` | `df[df$age>18, ]` |
| Data frame | Specific cell | `[i, j]` | `df[2, 3]` |
| Matrix | Row / column / cell | `[i, j]` | `m[1, ]` |
| Any | Nothing (zero-length) | `[0]` | `x[0]` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — The Subsetting Operator Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: You have an R object x"] --> B{"What do you want?"}
    B -->|Multiple elements| C{"Same class as x?"}
    C -->|Yes| D["Use x[c i]"
    ]
    C -->|No, single value| E["Use x[[i]]"]

    B -->|By name| F{"Data frame / list?"}
    F -->|Yes, simple name| G["Use x$name"]
    F -->|Yes, computed name| H["Use x[['name']]"]
    F -->|Atomic vector with names| I["Use x['name']"]

    B -->|Drop elements| J["Use x[-c i]"]
    B -->|Filter by condition| K["Use x[logical mask]"]
    B -->|Everything| L["Use x"]
    B -->|Nothing| M["Use x[0]"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — The Three Operators and Their Effect on List Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["list\nA: 10\nB: 20\nC: 30"] -->|x%5B1%5D| L1["list\nA: 10\n*class: list*"]
    L -->|x%5B%5B1%5D%5D| V1["10\n*class: numeric*"]
    L -->|x%24A| V2["10\n*partial match allowed*"]

    style L1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style V1 fill:#90EE90
    style V2 fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Orange box** = `[` preserves the list class (you get a smaller list).
> - **Green box** = `[[` strips one level of the hierarchy (you get the value).
> - **Blue box** = `$` behaves like `[[` but accepts a *bare name* and supports partial matching.

## 4.3 Mermaid — Data Frame Subsetting Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DF["data.frame\n4 rows x 3 cols"] --> P1["df$age\n*Single column as vector*"]
    DF --> P2["df%5B%5B'age'%5D%5D\n*Single column as vector*"]
    DF --> P3["df%5Bc name score %5D%5D\n*Multiple columns as data.frame*"]
    DF --> P4["df%5B1:2, %5D\n*Rows 1 and 2 as data.frame*"]
    DF --> P5["df%5Bdf$age > 22, %5D\n*Filtered rows as data.frame*"]
    DF --> P6["df%5B1, 2%5D\n*Single cell*"]
    DF --> P7["df%5B%5D\n*Entire data.frame unchanged*"]

    style P1 fill:#FFDAB9
    style P2 fill:#FFDAB9
    style P3 fill:#B0E0E6
    style P4 fill:#B0E0E6
    style P5 fill:#B0E0E6
    style P6 fill:#98FB98
    style P7 fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Lifecycle of a Subsetting Expression

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Code
    participant R as R Interpreter
    participant X as Data Object
    participant O as Output

    U->>R: x[c 1 3 ]
    R->>X: Inspect class of x
    R->>R: Validate index type
    R->>X: Extract positions 1 and 3
    X->>R: Return values
    R->>R: Coerce to class of x
    R->>O: Print / assign result
    O-->>U: Display / store
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4, Q1]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between `[` and `[[` operators in R with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `[` (subset) | `[[` (extract) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of levels removed | Zero | One |
| Returned class | **Same** as input | **Element's** class |
| Works on atomic vector? | Yes | Yes (returns scalar) |
| Most common use | Filtering rows / multiple cols | Drilling into a list element |

```r
l <- list(a = 1:3, b = "hi")
l[1]    # Returns list(a = 1:3)        — class "list"
l[[1]]  # Returns c(1, 2, 3)           — class "integer"
```

**(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark table, 1 mark example.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4, Q2]**
> **Q2. Explain any three types of subsetting in R with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Positive integer** — `x[c(1, 3)]` returns the 1st and 3rd elements in that order; duplicates are allowed.
2. **Negative integer** — `x[-2]` returns all elements except the 2nd; cannot be mixed with positive indices.
3. **Logical** — `x[x > 5]` returns elements for which the boolean mask is TRUE; the mask is recycled to the length of `x`.

**(Mark split: 1 mark per type with example.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4, Q3A]**
> **Q3 (a).** Explain the six different ways of subsetting an atomic vector in R with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b).** Consider the following list:
> `emp <- list(name = c("Anu", "Rahul"), salary = c(50000, 60000), dept = "IT")`
> Write R code to (i) extract the salary vector, (ii) extract the second element of `name`, (iii) create a sub-list containing only `name` and `dept`, (iv) demonstrate the difference between `emp[1]` and `emp[[1]]`. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Q3 (a) [7 marks]

**Six ways of subsetting an atomic vector:**

1. **Positive integers** — select by position. Order is preserved, duplicates allowed.
   ```r
   x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40)
   x[c(2, 4)]      # 20 40
   x[c(2, 2, 2)]   # 20 20 20  (duplication)
   ```
2. **Negative integers** — drop by position.
   ```r
   x[-c(1, 3)]     # 20 40
   ```
3. **Logical vectors** — keep where mask is TRUE; mask is recycled to length of `x`.
   ```r
   x[x > 15]       # 20 30 40
   x[c(TRUE, FALSE)]   # 10 30  (recycling: T F T F)
   ```
4. **Character (named)** — select by element name.
   ```r
   y <- c(math = 90, phy = 85, chem = 78)
   y[c("math", "chem")]   # 90 78
   ```
5. **Empty `[]`** — select everything (preserves class).
   ```r
   x[]              # 10 20 30 40
   x[] <- 0         # Replace ALL elements with 0
   ```
6. **Zero `[0]`** — zero-length object of the same class.
   ```r
   x[0]             # numeric(0)
   class(x[0])      # "numeric"
   ```

**(Mark split: 1 mark per type × 6 = 6 marks + 1 mark for syntax/usage note.)**

### Model Solution — Q3 (b) [7 marks]

```r
emp <- list(name = c("Anu", "Rahul"),
            salary = c(50000, 60000),
            dept = "IT")

# (i) Extract the salary vector
emp$salary              # c(50000, 60000)
# OR
emp[["salary"]]         # c(50000, 60000)

# (ii) Extract the second element of 'name'
emp$name[2]             # "Rahul"
# OR
emp[["name"]][2]        # "Rahul"

# (iii) Sub-list containing only 'name' and 'dept'
emp[c("name", "dept")]
# $name
# [1] "Anu"  "Rahul"
# $dept
# [1] "IT"
class(emp[c("name", "dept")])   # "list"  ← Still a list!

# (iv) emp[1] vs emp[[1]]
emp[1]
# $name
# [1] "Anu" "Rahul"
class(emp[1])           # "list"  — returns a sub-list

emp[[1]]
# [1] "Anu" "Rahul"
class(emp[[1]])         # "character" — returns the raw vector
```

**Key contrast:** `emp[1]` returns a *list of length 1* (preserves class). `emp[[1]]` *drills one level deeper* and returns the actual character vector. **(7 marks: i = 1, ii = 2, iii = 2, iv = 2)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4, Q3B — Internal Choice]**
> **Q3 (a) Alternative.** With reference to a data frame, explain the different subsetting operations possible on a data frame. Provide R code snippets for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b) Alternative.** What is the `subset()` function in R? Compare it with explicit `[` indexing. Demonstrate its use to filter rows of a data frame `marks` (with columns `name`, `sub1`, `sub2`, `sub3`) where `sub1 > 50` and select only `name` and `sub2` columns. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Q3B (a) [7 marks]

A data frame is a **list of equal-length vectors** with class `data.frame`. It therefore supports **BOTH list-style and matrix-style** subsetting.

```r
df <- data.frame(name = c("A", "B", "C"),
                 age  = c(20, 25, 30),
                 gpa  = c(8.5, 7.2, 9.1))
```

**1. List-style column extraction (returns a vector):**
```r
df$age                  # c(20, 25, 30)
df[["gpa"]]             # c(8.5, 7.2, 9.1)
```

**2. List-style multi-column (returns a data frame):**
```r
df[c("name", "gpa")]    # 3 × 2 data frame
```

**3. Matrix-style single row (returns a data frame, NOT a vector):**
```r
df[1, ]                 # 1 × 3 data frame
```

**4. Matrix-style single column (returns a vector — class drops):**
```r
df[ , "age"]            # c(20, 25, 30)
```

**5. Logical row filtering (the most important operation):**
```r
df[df$age > 22, ]       # Returns rows where age > 22
```

**6. Combined row + column subsetting:**
```r
df[df$gpa > 8, c("name", "gpa")]
```

**7. The `drop = FALSE` safety switch:**
```r
df[ , "age", drop = FALSE]    # Preserves data frame class
```

**(Mark split: 0.5 mark per operation + 3.5 marks for correct R code demonstration.)**

### Model Solution — Q3B (b) [7 marks]

**The `subset()` function** is a wrapper around `[` that uses *non-standard evaluation*, allowing you to write column names **without quotes** and reference them like variables.

**Comparison with explicit `[` indexing:**

| Aspect | `subset()` | `df[condition, ]` |
|---|---|---|
| Quote column names? | **No** | **Yes** (`df$"col"`) |
| Lazy evaluation? | Yes | No |
| Safe inside functions? | **Caution** (NSE) | **Yes** (predictable) |
| Speed for large data? | Slower | Faster |
| Recommended in production? | Interactive only | Preferred |

**Demonstration:**
```r
marks <- data.frame(
  name = c("Anu", "Balu", "Chitra", "Diya"),
  sub1 = c(60, 45, 78, 33),
  sub2 = c(70, 55, 82, 49),
  sub3 = c(80, 60, 90, 50)
)

# Filter rows where sub1 > 50 and select only 'name' and 'sub2'
