  2. Rank employees within each department by Salary using `ave()`.
  3. Handle `NA` salaries by placing them at the end. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

```r
# (a) sort() vs rev() and their combination
x <- c(5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3)

# Standard ascending sort
sort(x)         # 1 2 3 5 8 9

# Standard descending sort (preferred)
sort(x, decreasing = TRUE)   # 9 8 5 3 2 1

# rev() reverses whatever order it is given
rev(x)          # 3 9 1 8 2 5   (just flips positions)

# Composition: rev(sort(x))
rev(sort(x))    # 9 8 5 3 2 1   (== sort(x, decreasing=TRUE) here)
```

**Discussion — Is `rev(sort(x))` always equivalent to `sort(x, decreasing = TRUE)`?**

**Yes, for atomic vectors**, `rev(sort(x))` and `sort(x, decreasing = TRUE)` produce **identical results** in the default `na.last = NA` case because both:
1. First compute the ascending sorted order.
2. Then reverse it.

**However, they differ when `na.last` is specified:**

```r
y <- c(5, NA, 2, NA, 8)

# Case 1: explicit descending
sort(y, decreasing = TRUE, na.last = TRUE)
# NA NA 8 5 2  (NAs at end, then descending values)

# Case 2: rev(sort(asc, na.last = TRUE))
rev(sort(y, na.last = TRUE))
# NA 2 5 8 NA  (last NA flips to front — WRONG!)

# Correct approach to get NAs at the end with descending
rev(sort(y, na.last = FALSE))
# NA 8 5 2 NA  (sort puts NAs first, rev flips to end ✓)
```

**Conclusion:** `rev(sort(x))` is **only** equivalent to `sort(x, decreasing = TRUE)` when **NAs are absent** or set to be removed (`na.last = NA`). With explicit NA placement, the order of operations matters.

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Code demonstration of both: 2 marks
- Discussion of equivalence: 2 marks
- NA handling edge case: 2 marks
- Final conclusion: 1 mark

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

```r
# (b) Employee data manipulation
employees <- data.frame(
  Name        = c("Anu", "Balu", "Chitra", "Diya", "Esha", "Farhan", NA),
  Department  = c("CSE", "ECE", "CSE", "ECE", "CSE", "ECE", "CSE"),
  Salary      = c(70000, NA, 60000, 65000, 80000, 55000, 72000),
  JoiningYear = c(2018, 2019, 2020, 2017, 2016, 2021, 2019)
)

# 1. Multi-key sort: Department asc, Salary desc, NAs at end
emp_sorted <- employees[order(employees$Department, 
                              -employees$Salary, 
                              na.last = TRUE), ]
print(emp_sorted)

# 2. Rank within each department by Salary
employees$DeptRank <- ave(-employees$Salary, 
                          employees$Department, 
                          FUN = function(x) rank(x, na.last = "keep"))
print(employees)

# 3. NA salaries placed at the end
# Handled via na.last = TRUE in order()
```

**Output (Conceptual):**

```
     Name Department Salary JoiningYear
5    Esha        CSE  80000        2016
1     Anu        CSE  70000        2018
7    <NA>        CSE  72000        2019
3  Chitra        CSE  60000        2020
4    Diya        ECE  65000        2017
2    Balu        ECE     NA        2019
6  Farhan        ECE  55000        2021
```

**Mark Allocation (7 marks):**
- Multi-key sort with `order()`: 3 marks
- `ave()` for grouped ranking: 2 marks
- NA handling demonstration: 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `sort()` with `order()`** — most frequent error. Remember: `sort` returns *values*, `order` returns *indices*.
> 2. **Forgetting the unary minus** (`-df$Salary`) when descending-sorting inside `order()`. Without it, you get ascending order.
> 3. **Sorting factors by label** instead of by `levels()`. Always inspect `levels(f)` before sorting factors.
> 4. **Assuming sort stability** — R's default sort is **unstable** for equal keys. If stability matters, use `dplyr::arrange()` or implement a tie-breaker key.
> 5. **NA propagation** — forgetting `na.last = TRUE` results in NAs being silently dropped, which can corrupt downstream analysis.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`sort(x)`** → returns sorted **values**; use for single vectors.
- **`order(x)`** → returns sorted **indices**; use for data frames and multi-key sorting.
- **`rank(x)`** → returns **positional rank** of each element; use for statistical analysis.
- **`rev(x)`** → reverses element order; use for display or final NA flip.
- **Descending sort in `order()`** → use the unary minus trick: `order(-df$Salary)`.
- **Multi-key sort** → `order(df$key1, -df$key2)` (primary, then secondary tie-breaker).
- **NA handling** → `na.last = TRUE/FALSE/NA` controls NA placement in `sort()`/`order()`.
- **Tie-breaking in `rank()`** → 6 methods: `average`, `min`, `max`, `first`, `last`, `random`.
- **R's default sort is unstable** — equal keys may appear in any order.
- **Algorithm selection** is automatic: integer → radix; double/character → Shell.
- **`partial = k`** in `sort()` enables top-k selection in $O(n)$ time.
- **Factors sort by level order**, not alphabetical label order — verify `levels()` first.
- **`dplyr::arrange()`** is the stable, tidyverse alternative to `order()` for data frames.
- **Complex sorting** (e.g., sort by `nchar(x)`) requires `order(nchar(x))` — sort the *key*, not the data.
- **For grouped ranking**, use `ave(-x, group, FUN = rank)` — this is a **highly-tested KTU pattern**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analyzing Data: Summary Statistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analyzing Data: Summary Statistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📊 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary Statistics** are a collection of quantitative measures that distill a large dataset into a small set of numerical descriptors, capturing the **central tendency**, **dispersion (spread)**, **shape**, and **positional placement** of observations. In the context of **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**, they form the first analytical layer before visualization or inferential modeling.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for *PBCDT604*, summary statistics are the numerical backbone of any descriptive analytics pipeline in **R**, providing single-value descriptors that summarize a vector, data frame column, or distribution.

### The Four Pillars of Summary Statistics

| # | Pillar | What It Tells Us | Example Metric |
|---|--------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Measures of Central Tendency** | Where is the "center" of the data? | Mean, Median, Mode |
| 2 | **Measures of Dispersion** | How spread out is the data? | Variance, SD, IQR, Range |
| 3 | **Measures of Shape** | Is it skewed? Is it peaked? | Skewness, Kurtosis |
| 4 | **Measures of Position** | Where does a value rank? | Quartiles, Percentiles, Z-score |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis: Module 4 of *PBCDT604* expects students to **compute**, **interpret**, and **code** these statistics in **R**, not just memorize definitions. R's built-in functions (`mean()`, `sd()`, `summary()`, `fivenum()`) are board-relevant.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Class Photograph" Intuition

Imagine your engineering class of **60 students** appearing for a group photograph. The photographer asks: *"Where should I focus the camera?"*

- **Mean** → The point where the camera lens perfectly balances the entire group's *average* height. 📏
- **Median** → The student standing exactly in the *middle* when everyone lines up by height. 🚶
- **Mode** → The most common shoe size in the class. 👟
- **Standard Deviation** → How *tall* the spread is — whether students are all 5'6"–5'10" (low SD) or range from 5'2" to 6'4" (high SD). 📐
- **Skewness** → Whether the crowd leans left or right. If most are 5'6" but a few are 6'5", the distribution **right-skews**. ↗️
- **Kurtosis** → Whether the crowd clusters tightly at one height (peaked) or spreads evenly (flat). 🏔️

> [!TIP]
> **Single-line intuition:** Summary statistics are the *one-paragraph biography* of a dataset — short enough to read in a second, yet rich enough to describe its character.

---

## 1.3 Why Summary Statistics Matter in R / Data Science

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before plotting a single histogram, every data scientist computes summary statistics. They are the **first sanity check** — revealing outliers, missing values encoded as `NA`, and fundamental distributional properties that guide every downstream decision (scaling, transformation, model choice).

In **R**, the `summary()` function returns the **five-number summary** (Min, Q1, Median, Q3, Max) plus the arithmetic mean in one call — a hallmark KTU-priority function.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Measures of Central Tendency

### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Mean

The arithmetic mean is the sum of all observations divided by the count. For a sample of size **n**:

$$\bar{x} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

- **Why it works:** It is the *unique* point that minimizes the sum of squared deviations — making it the *least-squares center*.
- **Sensitivity:** Highly sensitive to **outliers** (one extreme value pulls the mean).
- **Use case:** Symmetric, outlier-free data (e.g., heights of homogeneous group).

### 2.1.2 Median

The median is the **middle value** when observations are sorted. For *n* observations:

- If *n* is odd → median is the $\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)$-th value.
- If *n* is even → median is the average of the $\frac{n}{2}$-th and $\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)$-th values.

- **Robustness:** Insensitive to outliers. KTU often tests this property.
- **Use case:** Skewed data, income data, housing prices.

### 2.1.3 Mode

The most frequently occurring value. For **categorical** data, mode is often the *only* valid central measure. In **continuous unimodal** distributions, mode lies at the peak of the PDF.

> [!NOTE]
> R does not have a built-in `mode()` function for numeric data (it returns storage type). To compute mode, students must write a custom function — a **frequent KTU practical question**.

---

## 2.2 Measures of Dispersion (Spread)

### 2.2.1 Range

$$R \;=\; x_{\max} \;-\; x_{\min}$$

Simplest but weakest measure — depends on only two values.

### 2.2.2 Variance

**Population variance:**

$$\sigma^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(x_{i}-\mu\right)^{2}$$

**Sample variance (Bessel's correction — denominator n−1):**

$$s^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i}-\bar{x}\right)^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why n − 1 and not n?** Because using $n$ underestimates the true population variance. Dividing by $n-1$ gives an **unbiased estimator**. This is a **classic KTU 2-mark conceptual question**.

### 2.2.3 Standard Deviation

$$\sigma \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma^{2}} \qquad s \;=\; \sqrt{s^{2}}$$

SD is in the **same units as the data**, unlike variance (which is in squared units). KTU prefers SD in interpretation answers.

### 2.2.4 Interquartile Range (IQR)

$$\mathrm{IQR} \;=\; Q_{3} \;-\; Q_{1}$$

Where $Q_1$ = 25th percentile, $Q_3$ = 75th percentile. The IQR contains the **middle 50%** of data and is the foundation of the **Tukey boxplot** outlier rule.

> Outliers: any point $< Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}$ or $> Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}$.

---

## 2.3 Measures of Shape

### 2.3.1 Skewness

Pearson's **second** skewness coefficient (moment-based, used in R):

$$\mathrm{Skew} \;=\; \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i}-\bar{x}\right)^{3}}{s^{3}}$$

- Skew = 0 → symmetric
- Skew > 0 → **right-skewed** (tail on right)
- Skew < 0 → **left-skewed** (tail on left)

### 2.3.2 Kurtosis (Excess)

$$\mathrm{Kurt} \;=\; \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i}-\bar{x}\right)^{4}}{s^{4}} \;-\; 3$$

- Kurt = 0 → mesokurtic (normal-like)
- Kurt > 0 → **leptokurtic** (heavy tails, sharp peak)
- Kurt < 0 → **platykurtic** (light tails, flat)

---

## 2.4 Measures of Position

### 2.4.1 Quantiles / Percentiles

The $p$-th percentile $Q_p$ is the value below which $p$% of observations lie.

$$Q_{p} \;=\; \text{value at position } \frac{p}{100}\,(n+1)$$

### 2.4.2 Z-Score (Standard Score)

$$Z_{i} \;=\; \frac{x_{i} \;-\; \bar{x}}{s}$$

Indicates how many SDs an observation lies from the mean. $\vert Z \vert > 3$ is a typical outlier flag.

---

## 2.5 🎯 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula KTU 2024 may test** for Module 4 Summary Statistics. Memorize these — they appear in Part A (3-markers) and Part B (14-markers).

| # | Statistic | Formula | Units | R Function | Sensitivity to Outliers |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | Same as data | `mean(x)` | High |
| 2 | Median | Middle sorted value | Same as data | `median(x)` | Low (robust) |
| 3 | Mode | Most frequent value | Same as data | Custom (no built-in) | Medium |
| 4 | Range | $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Same as data | `range(x)` | Very High |
| 5 | Population Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \mu)^{2}$ | Squared units | `var(x) * (n-1)/n` | High |
| 6 | Sample Variance | $s^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ | Squared units | `var(x)` | High |
| 7 | Sample SD | $s = \sqrt{s^{2}}$ | Same as data | `sd(x)` | High |
| 8 | IQR | $\mathrm{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ | Same as data | `IQR(x)` | Low |
| 9 | Skewness | $\mathrm{Skew} = \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{3}}{s^{3}}$ | Dimensionless | `moments::skewness(x)` or `e1071::skewness(x)` | Medium |
| 10 | Kurtosis (excess) | $\mathrm{Kurt} = \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{4}}{s^{4}} - 3$ | Dimensionless | `moments::kurtosis(x)` | Medium |
| 11 | Z-score | $Z_i = (x_i - \bar{x}) / s$ | Dimensionless | `scale(x)` | High |
| 12 | Five-number summary | Min, Q1, Median, Q3, Max | Same as data | `summary(x)` or `fivenum(x)` | Mixed |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Summary Statistics |
|--------|-----------------------------------|
| **Civil Engineering** | Mean compressive strength of concrete cubes; SD as quality-control indicator |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Mean & variance of signal noise (SNR analysis) |
| **Data Science / ML** | Feature scaling (mean-centering), outlier detection via IQR/Z-score, normality assumption checks |
| **Quality Control (SPC)** | $\pm 3\sigma$ control charts (Shewhart) — kurtosis identifies non-normal process shifts |
| **Economics / Finance** | Median income (robust), skewness of returns (Black-Scholes risk modeling) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Hand-Calculation Mastery)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently gives a small dataset in Part B and asks students to compute **Mean, Variance, SD, Skewness, and interpret**. We now exhaustively solve one such problem.

### Given Dataset

$$x \;=\; \{4,\; 8,\; 6,\; 5,\; 3,\; 8,\; 9,\; 2\}$$

Here, $n = 8$.

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean $\bar{x}$

$$\bar{x} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \;=\; \frac{4+8+6+5+3+8+9+2}{8} \;=\; \frac{45}{8} \;=\; 5.625$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute Each Deviation $(x_i - \bar{x})$

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^{3}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^{4}$ |
|-----|-------|-----------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 4 | $-1.625$ | $2.6406$ | $-4.2910$ | $6.9729$ |
| 2 | 8 | $+2.375$ | $5.6406$ | $+13.3965$ | $+31.8167$ |
| 3 | 6 | $+0.375$ | $0.1406$ | $+0.0527$ | $+0.0198$ |
| 4 | 5 | $-0.625$ | $0.3906$ | $-0.2441$ | $+0.1526$ |
| 5 | 3 | $-2.625$ | $6.8906$ | $-18.0879$ | $+47.4807$ |
| 6 | 8 | $+2.375$ | $5.6406$ | $+13.3965$ | $+31.8167$ |
| 7 | 9 | $+3.375$ | $11.3906$ | $+38.4434$ | $+129.7463$ |
| 8 | 2 | $-3.625$ | $13.1406$ | $-47.6348$ | $+172.6762$ |
| **Σ** | **45** | **0.000** | **45.8750** | **$-4.9688$** | **$420.6819$** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** The column $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})$ **must** equal 0. Any non-zero value indicates a calculation error — examiners award marks for this verification step.

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Sample Variance $s^{2}$

Using Bessel's correction ($n - 1 = 7$):

$$s^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{i}-\bar{x}\right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{45.8750}{7} \;\approx\; 6.5536$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Standard Deviation $s$

$$s \;=\; \sqrt{s^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{6.5536} \;\approx\; 2.5600$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Skewness

First, compute the cube of $s$:

$$s^{3} \;=\; (2.5600)^{3} \;\approx\; 16.7772$$

Then:

$$\mathrm{Skew} \;=\; \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^{3}}{s^{3}} \;=\; \frac{-4.9688 / 8}{16.7772} \;=\; \frac{-0.6211}{16.7772} \;\approx\; -0.0370$$

**Interpretation:** Skew $\approx -0.037 \approx 0$ → the distribution is **approximately symmetric**, but very slightly **left-skewed**.

---

### Step 6 — Compute the Excess Kurtosis

First, compute $s^{4}$:

$$s^{4} \;=\; (2.5600)^{4} \;\approx\; 42.9497$$

Then:

$$\mathrm{Kurt} \;=\; \frac{\frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^{4}}{s^{4}} - 3 \;=\; \frac{420.6819 / 8}{42.9497} - 3 \;=\; \frac{52.5852}{42.9497} - 3$$

$$\mathrm{Kurt} \;\approx\; 1.2244 \;-\; 3 \;=\; -1.7756$$

**Interpretation:** Kurtosis $= -1.78 < 0$ → distribution is **platykurtic** (flatter than normal, lighter tails).

---

### Step 7 — Compute the Five-Number Summary & IQR

Sort: $x = \{2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 8, 9\}$

- **Min** $= 2$
- **Max** $= 9$
- **Median** $= \frac{5+6}{2} = 5.5$
- **Q1** (median of lower half $\{2,3,4,5\}$) $= \frac{3+4}{2} = 3.5$
- **Q3** (median of upper half $\{6,8,8,9\}$) $= \frac{8+8}{2} = 8.0$
- **IQR** $= 8.0 - 3.5 = 4.5$

> [!TIP]
> Note that **mean (5.625) ≠ median (5.5)** — a 0.125 gap, confirming near-symmetry. Always report **both** in KTU answers.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation Decision Table

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **examiner's secret weapon** — the table below lets you instantly interpret any combination of skewness and kurtosis KTU may give.

| Skewness | Kurtosis | Distribution Type | Real-World Example |
|----------|----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | Normal (symmetric, mesokurtic) | Heights of adult males |
| $> 0$ | $\approx 0$ | Right-skewed, normal-tailed | Wealth distribution |
| $> 0$ | $> 0$ | Right-skewed, heavy-tailed (leptokurtic) | Stock market crash returns |
| $< 0$ | $< 0$ | Left-skewed, light-tailed | Easy exam scores |
| $\approx 0$ | $> 0$ | Symmetric, heavy-tailed | Measurement error in physics |
| $> 0$ | $< 0$ | Right-skewed, flat-tailed | Insurance claim sizes |

---

## 3.3 R Implementation — The Code You Must Know

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 4 practical exams (lab) and viva heavily test R code. The block below is the **canonical R script** for computing every summary statistic in one pass.

```r
# ============================================================
# KTU PBCDT604 — Module 4: Summary Statistics in R
# ============================================================

# Sample dataset
x <- c(4, 8, 6, 5, 3, 8, 9, 2)

# 1. Central tendency
mean_x    <- mean(x)        # Arithmetic mean
median_x  <- median(x)      # Median
mode_x    <- as.numeric(names(which.max(table(x))))  # Custom mode

# 2. Dispersion
range_x   <- range(x)                       # Min, Max
var_x     <- var(x)                         # Sample variance (n-1)
sd_x      <- sd(x)                          # Sample SD
iqr_x     <- IQR(x)                         # Interquartile range

# 3. Shape
# Install if needed: install.packages(c("moments","e1071"))
library(moments)
skew_x    <- skewness(x)    # Skewness
kurt_x    <- kurtosis(x)    # Excess kurtosis (pearson = FALSE gives excess)

# 4. Position
z_scores  <- scale(x)       # Z-score standardization
quantiles <- quantile(x, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75))  # Q1, Q2, Q3

# 5. Five-number summary + mean
summary_x <- summary(x)     # KTU all-time favourite function

# 6. Outlier detection via 1.5*IQR rule (Tukey's fences)
lower_fence <- quantile(x, 0.25) - 1.5 * IQR(x)
upper_fence <- quantile(x, 0.75) + 1.5 * IQR(x)
outliers    <- x[x < lower_fence | x > upper_fence]

# Print everything
print(summary_x)
cat("Variance:", var_x, "\nSD:", sd_x, "\nIQR:", iqr_x, "\n")
cat("Skewness:", skew_x, "\nKurtosis:", kurt_x, "\n")
cat("Outliers:", outliers, "\n")
```

### Expected Console Output

```
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.
   2.00    3.75    5.50    5.625    8.00    9.00

Variance: 6.5536
SD: 2.56
IQR: 4.5
Skewness: -0.037
Kurtosis: -1.776
Outliers: (none)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Tip:** If asked *"Why does `summary()` return Mean but `fivenum()` does not?"* — answer: `fivenum()` returns Tukey's original five-number summary (no mean), while `summary()` is a generic method that adds the mean for numeric vectors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics


## 4.2 Workflow Pipeline — From Raw Data to Statistical Insight

```mermaid

    R[Raw Dataset x] --> S1[Step 1: Validate and Clean NA]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Compute Central Tendency]

```

## 4.3 Outlier Detection Decision Logic

```mermaid

    START[For each observation x_i] --> Q{Is x_i less than Q1 minus 1.5 times IQR?}
    Q -- Yes --> OUT[Flag as Outlier - Lower]
    Q -- No --> Q2{Is x_i greater than Q3 plus 1.5 times IQR?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> OUT2[Flag as Outlier - Upper]
    Q2 -- No --> NORM[Normal Observation]

    OUT2 --> ACT
    NORM --> KEEP[Keep in Dataset]
```

```mermaid

    A2[Skatosis greater than 0, Kurtosis equals 0] --> N2[Right Skewed Mesokurtic - Example Wealth]

    A4[Skatosis greater than 0, Kurtosis greater than 0] --> N4[Right Skewed Leptokurtic - Example Financial Crashes]
    A5[Skatosis less than 0, Kurtosis greater than 0] --> N5[Left Skewed Leptokurtic - Rare Extreme]
    A6[Skatosis approximately 0, Kurtosis greater than 0] --> N6[Symmetric Heavy Tails - Example Measurement Noise]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below mirror the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Marks are tagged per KTU regulation. Models answers are **examiner-grade** — concise, formula-anchored, and terminology-correct.

---

## 5.1 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

> Differentiate between **population variance** and **sample variance**. Why is Bessel's correction (n−1) used in the sample variance formula?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Population Variance $\sigma^{2}$ | Sample Variance $s^{2}$ |
|--------|--------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Denominator | $N$ (population size) | $n - 1$ (sample size − 1) |
| Used when | Entire population is known | Inferential statistics on a sample |
| Bias | Unbiased (by definition) | Unbiased *only* because of $n-1$ |

**Why $n - 1$ (Bessel's correction):** When we use the sample mean $\bar{x}$ instead of the true population mean $\mu$, the deviations $(x_i - \bar{x})$ are systematically smaller than $(x_i - \mu)$. Dividing by $n$ underestimates the true variance. Using $n - 1$ inflates the result, producing an **unbiased estimator** $E[s^{2}] = \sigma^{2}$. This loss of one **degree of freedom** corresponds to the one parameter ($\mu$) already estimated from the sample.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**

> Explain the difference between **skewness** and **kurtosis**. How would you interpret a dataset with skewness = 1.2 and kurtosis = 4.5?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Skewness** measures the **asymmetry** of the distribution. It is the standardized third central moment.
- **Kurtosis** measures the **tailedness/peakedness** of the distribution. It is the standardized fourth central moment (excess form subtracts 3).

**Interpretation of the given values:**

| Metric | Value | Reading |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Skewness | $+1.2$ | **Moderately right-skewed** — tail extends to the right; mean > median |
| Excess Kurtosis | $+4.5$ | **Strongly leptokurtic** — very heavy tails and a sharp peak (highly outlier-prone) |

**Engineering interpretation:** Such a dataset resembles **financial crash returns** or **insurance claim amounts** — most observations cluster near a low mean, but extreme outliers occur frequently. Robust statistics (median, IQR) should be preferred over mean/SD.

---

## 5.2 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU ESE rules, Part B offers an **internal choice**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each is split into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### 🔷 **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Hand Computation & Interpretation*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

For the following dataset of monthly sales (in ₹ thousands) of a small business:

$$S \;=\; \{12,\; 15,\; 14,\; 16,\; 13,\; 50,\; 15,\; 14,\; 16,\; 13\}$$

#### **(a) Compute the arithmetic mean, median, sample variance, and standard deviation. Comment on the relationship between mean and median.** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$\bar{S} \;=\; \frac{12+15+14+16+13+50+15+14+16+13}{10} \;=\; \frac{178}{10} \;=\; 17.8$$

**Step 2 — Median:** Sort the data: $\{12, 13, 13, 14, 14, 15, 15, 16, 16, 50\}$. With $n = 10$, median is average of 5th and 6th values:

$$\text{Median} \;=\; \frac{14 + 15}{2} \;=\; 14.5$$

**Step 3 — Deviations and squared deviations:**

| $S_i$ | $S_i - \bar{S}$ | $(S_i - \bar{S})^{2}$ |
|-------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 12 | $-5.8$ | $33.64$ |
| 15 | $-2.8$ | $7.84$ |
| 14 | $-3.8$ | $14.44$ |
| 16 | $-1.8$ | $3.24$ |
| 13 | $-4.8$ | $23.04$ |
| 50 | $+32.2$ | $1036.84$ |
| 15 | $-2.8$ | $7.84$ |
| 14 | $-3.8$ | $14.44$ |
| 16 | $-1.8$ | $3.24$ |
| 13 | $-4.8$ | $23.04$ |
| **Σ** | **0.0** | **$1167.60$** |

> ✅ Verification: $\sum(S_i - \bar{S}) = 0$ confirms correct mean.

**Step 4 — Sample variance** (denominator $n-1 = 9$):

$$s^{2} \;=\; \frac{1167.60}{9} \;\approx\; 129.733$$

**Step 5 — Standard deviation:**

$$s \;=\; \sqrt{129.733} \;\approx\; 11.389$$

**Comment on Mean vs Median:**

$$\bar{S} = 17.8 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Median} = 14.5$$

Since **Mean > Median by 3.3**, the distribution is **right-skewed (positively skewed)** — caused by the outlier $S = 50$. The single large observation has pulled the mean upward, while the median remains robust. This is a textbook illustration of the **lack of robustness of the arithmetic mean** in the presence of outliers.

---

#### **(b) Compute the five-number summary, IQR, and identify any outliers using Tukey's 1.5×IQR rule.** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Sorted data:** $\{12, 13, 13, 14, 14, 15, 15, 16, 16, 50\}$

**Step 2 — Five-number summary:**

| Statistic | Position | Value |
|-----------|----------|-------|
| Min | 1st | $12$ |
| Q1 | Median of lower half $\{12,13,13,14,14\}$ | $13$ |
| Median (Q2) | Avg of 5th & 6th | $14.5$ |
| Q3 | Median of upper half $\{15,15,16,16,50\}$ | $16$ |
| Max | 10th | $50$ |

**Step 3 — IQR:**

$$\mathrm{IQR} \;=\; Q_3 - Q_1 \;=\; 16 - 13 \;=\; 3$$

**Step 4 — Tukey's fences:**

$$\text{Lower fence} \;=\; Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR} \;=\; 13 - 1.5(3) \;=\; 8.5$$

$$\text{Upper fence} \;=\; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR} \;=\; 16 + 1.5(3) \;=\; 20.5$$

**Step 5 — Outlier identification:**

- Lower fence check: $12 \geq 8.5$ → no lower outliers.
- Upper fence check: $50 > 20.5$ → **50 is an outlier** ✅.

**Conclusion:** The single observation **50** is an outlier by Tukey's rule. It inflates both the mean and SD. The IQR-based robust summary (median = 14.5, IQR = 3) is a more faithful description of the central business performance.

---

### 🔷 **Question B (14 Marks)** — *R Programming & Interpretation*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### **(a) Write an R program to compute the mean, median, variance, and standard deviation of the vector `marks = c(45, 67, 89, 34, 56, 78, 90, 12, 65, 78)`. Display all results using `cat()`.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model R Code:**

```r
# KTU PBCDT604 — Q.B(a) Model Solution
marks <- c(45, 67, 89, 34, 56, 78, 90, 12, 65, 78)

# Central tendency
m_mean   <- mean(marks)
m_median <- median(marks)

# Dispersion
m_var    <- var(marks)        # Sample variance
m_sd     <- sd(marks)         # Sample SD

# Display
cat("Arithmetic Mean      :", m_mean,   "\n")
cat("Median               :", m_median, "\n")
cat("Sample Variance      :", m_var,    "\n")
cat("Standard Deviation   :", m_sd,     "\n")

# Bonus: Five-number summary using fivenum()
cat("Five-number summary  :", fivenum(marks), "\n")
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Arithmetic Mean      : 61.4
Median               : 66
Sample Variance      : 631.1556
Standard Deviation   : 25.12301
Five-number summary  : 12  39.5  66  78  90
```

**Explanation (for full 7 marks):**

- `mean(marks)` — sums and divides by $n = 10$, giving $\bar{x} = 61.4$.
- `median(marks)` — sorts and picks the average of the 5th and 6th values, giving $66$.
- `var(marks)` — uses $n - 1 = 9$ in the denominator (Bessel-corrected).
- `sd(marks)` — square root of `var(marks)`.
- `fivenum(marks)` — Tukey's five-number summary (Min, Q1, Median, Q3, Max).

---

#### **(b) Using the same vector `marks`, write R code to:** *(7 Marks)*
1. **Detect outliers using the IQR method.**
2. **Compute skewness and kurtosis using the `moments` package.**
3. **Interpret whether the distribution is symmetric and the type of kurtosis.**

**Model R Code:**

```r
# KTU PBCDT604 — Q.B(b) Model Solution
library(moments)

marks <- c(45, 67, 89, 34, 56, 78, 90, 12, 65, 78)

# (i) Outlier detection via IQR rule
Q1   <- quantile(marks, 0.25)
Q3   <- quantile(marks, 0.75)
iqr  <- IQR(marks)
lower_fence <- Q1 - 1.5 * iqr
upper_fence <- Q3 + 1.5 * iqr
outliers    <- marks[marks < lower_fence | marks > upper_fence]

cat("Q1         :", Q1, "\n")
cat("Q3         :", Q3, "\n")
cat("IQR        :", iqr, "\n")
cat("Lower Fence:", lower_fence, "\n")
cat("Upper Fence:", upper_fence, "\n")
cat("Outliers   :", outliers, "\n")

# (ii) Skewness and Kurtosis
cat("Skewness   :", skewness(marks), "\n")
cat("Kurtosis   :", kurtosis(marks), "\n")

# (iii) Z-score standardization
z <- scale(marks)
print(z)
```

**Expected Output (Approximate):**

```
Q1         : 39.5
Q3         : 78
IQR        : 38.5
Lower Fence: -18.25
Upper Fence: 135.75
Outliers   : (none)
Skewness   : -0.5162
Kurtosis   : -0.9568
```

**Interpretation:**

1. **Outliers:** Lower fence = $-18.25$, upper fence = $135.75$. Since all marks lie within $[12, 90]$, **no outliers** are detected — even though 12 looks low, it is not statistically extreme by the IQR rule.

2. **Skewness = $-0.52$:** Distribution is **moderately left-skewed** — the tail extends toward lower values. Confirmed by **mean (61.4) < median (66)**.

3. **Kurtosis = $-0.96$:** Excess kurtosis is **negative** → distribution is **platykurtic** — flatter than a normal distribution with lighter tails. The data spread is relatively even without sharp peaks.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Summary Statistics questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Bessel's correction** — using $n$ instead of $n-1$ for sample variance. *(−2 marks guaranteed)*
> 2. **Not verifying $\sum(x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$** — examiners award a **free 1 mark** for this verification step.
> 3. **Confusing `mode()` in R** — `mode()` returns the *storage type* (numeric, character), NOT the statistical mode. Students must write a custom function using `table()`.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation** — KTU deducts up to **2 marks** if you only compute but do not interpret (e.g., saying "skewness is positive" without saying "right-skewed, mean > median").
> 5. **Wrong fence formula** — Tukey fences use $1.5 \times \mathrm{IQR}$, **not** $2 \times \mathrm{IQR}$ or $\pm \sigma$.
> 6. **Forgetting to install `moments`** — R does not have a built-in `skewness()` function. Code that calls it without `library(moments)` will throw an error and lose full marks.

---

## 5.4 📚 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — print this before every KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Mean** uses *all* data → sensitive to outliers.
- ✅ **Median** uses only the middle → robust to outliers.
- ✅ **Mode** → most frequent; for continuous data, often multimodal.
- ✅ **Sample variance uses $n-1$** (Bessel's correction); population variance uses $N$.
- ✅ **SD is the square root of variance**; it is in the *same unit* as data.
- ✅ **IQR = $Q_3 - Q_1$**; contains the middle 50% of data.
- ✅ **Tukey outlier rule:** $x_i < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}$ **or** $x_i > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}$.
- ✅ **Skewness = 0** → symmetric; **>0** → right; **<0** → left.
- ✅ **Kurtosis excess = 0** → normal; **>0** → leptokurtic (heavy tails); **<0** → platykurtic (flat).
- ✅ **Z-score** $= (x - \bar{x}) / s$; flag if $\vert Z \vert > 3$.
- ✅ **R essentials:** `mean()`, `median()`, `var()`, `sd()`, `range()`, `IQR()`, `quantile()`, `summary()`, `scale()`, `fivenum()`.
- ✅ **Custom mode in R:** `as.numeric(names(which.max(table(x))))`.
- ✅ **`skewness()` and `kurtosis()`** require `library(moments)` (or `e1071`).
- ✅ **Five-number summary** = Min, Q1, Median (Q2), Q3, Max.
- ✅ **Always interpret** skewness/kurtosis results — never leave numbers uncontextualized.
- ✅ **Verification step:** $\sum(x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$ — earns a free mark.
- ✅ **Population vs Sample matters** — KTU will test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Tests: Continuous Data, Discrete Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Statistical Tests: Continuous Data, Discrete Data ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Statistical Test? A **statistical hypothesis test** is a formal, mathematically rigorous procedure used to determine whether there is enough statistical evidence in a sample of data to infer that a particular condition holds true for the entire population. In the KTU 2024 R programming framework, this translates directly to using R's built-in functions (like `t.test()`, `chisq.test()`, `aov()`) to validate assumptions and draw data-driven conclusions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A statistical test is a decision-making tool that uses sample data to evaluate two competing hypotheses — the **Null Hypothesis (H₀)** and the **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁)** — by calculating a test statistic and comparing it against a critical value or **p-value**. ### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Data Types **Discrete Data (Categorical/Count Data):** - Values that can only take specific, countable values (often integers). - Examples: Number of defective items in a batch, gender (Male/Female), blood group (A/B/AB/O), number of customer complaints per day. - In R: represented as `factor()`, `integer`, or `logical` vectors. **Continuous Data (Numerical/Measurement Data):** - Values that can take any value within a given range, including fractions and decimals. - Examples: Height, weight, temperature, time, voltage, blood pressure. - In R: represented as `numeric` (double) vectors. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Courtroom Trial > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Courtroom:** > Imagine a courtroom trial. The **Null Hypothesis (H₀)** is the defendant's innocence — the default assumption that nothing unusual is happening. The **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁)** is the prosecution's claim — that something has changed or there is a real effect. A statistical test is like the judge weighing the **evidence** (test statistic) against a strict standard of proof (**significance level α**). If the evidence is strong enough (p-value < α), we "reject innocence" and conclude the effect is real. ### 1.4 Why Distinguish Continuous vs Discrete Data? The choice of statistical test depends **entirely** on: 1. **Type of data** (continuous or discrete/categorical) 2. **Number of groups** being compared (1, 2, or k≥3) 3. **Independence** of samples (paired or unpaired) 4. **Assumptions** met (normality, equal variance, expected frequencies ≥ 5) | Data Type | Common Tests | R Function | |-----------|--------------|------------| | Continuous (1 group) | One-sample t-test | `t.test()` | | Continuous (2 groups) | Two-sample t-test, Wilcoxon rank-sum | `t.test()`, `wilcox.test()` | | Continuous (paired) | Paired t-test, Wilcoxon signed-rank | `t.test(paired=TRUE)`, `wilcox.test(paired=TRUE)` | | Continuous (3+ groups) | One-way ANOVA, Kruskal-Wallis | `aov()`, `kruskal.test()` | | Discrete/Categorical | Chi-square test of independence, Fisher's exact | `chisq.test()`, `fisher.test()` | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Hypothesis Testing Workflow (5 Logical Steps) 1. **State the Hypotheses:** Formulate H₀ (no effect/difference) and H₁ (real effect/difference exists). This is always done *before* looking at the data. 2. **Choose the Test Statistic:** Select based on data type, sample size, and assumptions (normality, variance homogeneity). 3. **Determine the Sampling Distribution:** Identify the reference distribution under H₀ (t-distribution, F-distribution, χ²-distribution). 4. **Compute the Test Statistic & p-value:** Calculate from the sample data. 5. **Make a Decision:** If **p-value < α (typically 0.05)**, reject H₀. Otherwise, fail to reject H₀. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pearl:** Always state the decision in terms of "**rejecting**" or "**failing to reject**" H₀ — *never* say "accept H₀". This is a common board-level deduction trigger. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Test | Test Statistic Formula | Degrees of Freedom | R Syntax | |------|------------------------|-------------------|----------| | One-sample t | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}$ | $df = n - 1$ | `t.test(x, mu=μ₀)` | | Two-sample t (Welch) | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}$ | Welch-Satterthwaite | `t.test(x~g)` | | Paired t | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d}}{s_d/\sqrt{n}}$ | $df = n - 1$ | `t.test(x,y,paired=TRUE)` | | One-way ANOVA | $F = \dfrac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}} = \dfrac{SS_B / (k-1)}{SS_W / (N-k)}$ | $df_1 = k-1$, $df_2 = N-k$ | `aov(y~group, data=df)` | | Chi-square | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $df = (r-1)(c-1)$ | `chisq.test(table)` | | Chi-square goodness-of-fit | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $df = k - 1$ | `chisq.test(x, p=p_expected)` | | Wilcoxon rank-sum (Mann-Whitney U) | $U = n_1 n_2 + \dfrac{n_1(n_1+1)}{2} - R_1$ | $df = n_1+n_2-2$ | `wilcox.test(x~g)` | | Kruskal-Wallis | $H = \dfrac{12}{N(N+1)} \sum \dfrac{R_j^2}{n_j} - 3(N+1)$ | $df = k - 1$ | `kruskal.test(y~group)` | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** When writing absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or determinants $\vert A \vert$ in your exam paper, **always use \vert x\vert or \mid x\mid in LaTeX**, never a single vertical bar `|x|` — KTU's digital valuation systems frequently mis-render bare pipes inside math. ### 2.3 Parametric vs Non-Parametric Tests **Parametric Tests** (assume data is normally distributed): - Used for continuous data - More **statistical power** when assumptions hold - Examples: t-tests, ANOVA, Pearson correlation **Non-Parametric Tests** (distribution-free, based on ranks): - Used when normality assumption is violated or for ordinal data - Examples: Wilcoxon signed-rank, Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis, Spearman correlation > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In manufacturing quality control, t-tests verify whether a machine's average output meets the target specification. In clinical trials, ANOVA compares the effectiveness of 3+ drug formulations. In survey analysis, Chi-square tests determine if customer preference is independent of demographic category. ### 2.4 Key Assumptions Checklist | Test | Normality | Equal Variance | Independence | Sample Size | |------|-----------|----------------|--------------|-------------| | One-sample t | Required | N/A | Required | n ≥ 30 preferred | | Two-sample t | Required | Required (Welch relaxes this) | Required | n₁, n₂ ≥ 20 | | One-way ANOVA | Required | Required (Levene's test) | Required | n ≥ 5 per cell | | Chi-square | N/A | N/A | Required | All Eᵢ ≥ 5 | | Fisher's exact | N/A | N/A | Required | For small samples (Eᵢ < 5) | **Shapiro-Wilk Test** (normality check): `shapiro.test(x)` — H₀: data is normally distributed **Levene's Test** (equal variance check): Use `car::leveneTest(y~group)` — H₀: variances are equal ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Two-Sample t-Test (Welch's) **Problem:** A factory has two machines producing steel rods. Machine A produces rods with mean diameter **15.02 mm** (n=10, s=0.08). Machine B produces rods with mean diameter **14.95 mm** (n=12, s=0.10). Is there a significant difference at α = 0.05? **Step 1 — State hypotheses:** $$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu_A \neq \mu_B$$ **Step 2 — Compute the test statistic** (Welch's t for unequal variances): $$ \begin{aligned} t &= \frac{\bar{x}_A - \bar{x}_B}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_A^2}{n_A} + \dfrac{s_B^2}{n_B}}} \\[8pt] &= \frac{15.02 - 14.95}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.08^2}{10} + \dfrac{0.10^2}{12}}} \\[8pt] &= \frac{0.07}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.0064}{10} + \dfrac{0.0100}{12}}} \\[8pt] &= \frac{0.07}{\sqrt{0.00064 + 0.000833}} \\[8pt] &= \frac{0.07}{\sqrt{0.001473}} \\[8pt] &= \frac{0.07}{0.03838} \\[8pt] &\approx 1.824 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute degrees of freedom** (Welch-Satterthwaite): $$ \begin{aligned} df &= \frac{\left(\dfrac{s_A^2}{n_A} + \dfrac{s_B^2}{n_B}\right)^2}{\dfrac{(s_A^2/n_A)^2}{n_A - 1} + \dfrac{(s_B^2/n_B)^2}{n_B - 1}} \\[8pt] &= \frac{(0.00064 + 0.000833)^2}{\dfrac{0.00064^2}{9} + \dfrac{0.000833^2}{11}} \\[8pt] &\approx \frac{(0.001473)^2}{4.55 \times 10^{-8} + 6.31 \times 10^{-8}} \\[8pt] &\approx \frac{2.17 \times 10^{-6}}{1.09 \times 10^{-7}} \\[8pt] &\approx 19.9 \approx 19 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Find the critical value** at α = 0.05 (two-tailed) with df ≈ 19: $$t_{critical} = \pm 2.093$$ **Step 5 — Decision:** Since $\vert t_{calc} \vert = 1.824 < 2.093$, we **fail to reject H₀**. There is insufficient evidence at the 5% level to claim the machines produce different mean diameters. **R Implementation:** ```r machine_A <- c(15.02, 15.05, 14.98, 15.01, 15.03, 14.99, 15.00, 15.04, 15.02, 15.01) machine_B <- c(14.95, 14.97, 14.93, 14.96, 14.94, 14.98, 14.92, 14.95, 14.96, 14.94, 14.93, 14.95) t.test(machine_A, machine_B) # Welch's t-test by default ``` ### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Chi-Square Test of Independence **Problem:** A marketing survey classifies 300 customers by gender (M/F) and product preference (X/Y). The contingency table is: | | Prefers X | Prefers Y | Row Total | |--|-----------|-----------|-----------| | Male | 80 | 70 | 150 | | Female | 60 | 90 | 150 | | **Col Total** | **140** | **160** | **300** | **Step 1 — Hypotheses:** $$H_0: \text{Gender and preference are independent}$$ $$H_1: \text{Gender and preference are dependent (associated)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute expected frequencies** $E_{ij} = \frac{(\text{Row}_i)(\text{Col}_j)}{N}$: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{11} &= \frac{150 \times 140}{300} = 70.0, \quad E_{12} = \frac{150 \times 160}{300} = 80.0 \\ E_{21} &= \frac{150 \times 140}{300} = 70.0, \quad E_{22} = \frac{150 \times 160}{300} = 80.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the Chi-square statistic:** $$ \begin{aligned} \chi^2 &= \sum_{i,j} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(80-70)^2}{70} + \frac{(70-80)^2}{80} + \frac{(60-70)^2}{70} + \frac{(90-80)^2}{80} \\[6pt] &= \frac{100}{70} + \frac{100}{80} + \frac{100}{70} + \frac{100}{80} \\[6pt] &= 1.4286 + 1.2500 + 1.4286 + 1.2500 \\[6pt] &= 5.3571 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Degrees of freedom:** $df = (r-1)(c-1) = (2-1)(2-1) = 1$ **Step 5 — Critical value at α = 0.05, df = 1:** $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$ **Step 6 — Decision:** Since $\chi^2_{calc} = 5.357 > 3.841$, **reject H₀**. Gender and product preference are significantly associated. **R Implementation:** ```r table_data <- matrix(c(80, 70, 60, 90), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE) colnames(table_data) <- c("Prefers_X", "Prefers_Y") rownames(table_data) <- c("Male", "Female") chisq.test(table_data, correct = FALSE) ``` ### 3.3 Worked Example 3: One-Way ANOVA (Conceptual Numerical) **Problem:** Three fertilizers (A, B, C) are tested on plant growth. Each applied to 5 plants. Group means: $\bar{x}_A = 22$, $\bar{x}_B = 28$, $\bar{x}_C = 25$, Grand mean = 25. $SS_B = 180$, $SS_W = 240$, k=3, N=15. **Step 1 — Hypotheses:** $$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{At least one mean differs}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Mean Squares:** $$ \begin{aligned} MS_B &= \frac{SS_B}{k-1} = \frac{180}{3-1} = 90 \\ MS_W &= \frac{SS_W}{N-k} = \frac{240}{15-3} = 20 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — F-statistic:** $$ F = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W} = \frac{90}{20} = 4.50 $$ **Step 4 — Critical value:** $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$ **Step 5 — Decision:** Since $F_{calc} = 4.50 > 3.89$, **reject H₀**. At least one fertilizer produces a significantly different mean growth. **R Implementation:** ```r growth <- c(20,22,21,24,23, 27,28,29,28,28, 25,24,26,25,25) fertilizer <- factor(rep(c("A","B","C"), each=5)) df <- data.frame(growth, fertilizer) summary(aov(growth ~ fertilizer, data = df)) # Post-hoc: TukeyHSD(aov(growth ~ fertilizer, data = df)) ``` ### 3.4 Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Test ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Q1: Is the response variable continuous? │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ YES NO (Categorical) │ │ Q2: How many groups? Q3: Are expected ├── 1 group frequencies all ≥ 5? │ └─► One-sample t-test ├── YES → Chi-square test ├── 2 groups (independent) └── NO → Fisher's exact test │ ├─► Paired? ──YES──► Paired t-test │ └─► Unpaired ─► Two-sample t-test (Welch) ├── 2 groups (dependent) │ └─► Paired t-test / Wilcoxon signed-rank └── 3+ groups ├─► Normal data ──► One-way ANOVA (+ Tukey HSD) └─► Non-normal ──► Kruskal-Wallis ``` ### 3.5 Non-Parametric Test: Wilcoxon Rank-Sum (Mann-Whitney U) **When to use:** Two independent groups, continuous/ordinal data, normality assumption violated. **Workflow:** 1. Pool all observations, rank them from smallest to largest (ties get average rank). 2. Sum the ranks for each group: $R_1$ and $R_2$. 3. Compute $U_i = n_1 n_2 + \frac{n_i(n_i+1)}{2} - R_i$. 4. Use the smaller $U$ and compare to critical table value, or compute p-value from normal approximation. **R Implementation:** ```r wilcox.test(group1, group2, exact = FALSE) # for large samples ``` ### 3.6 KTU Analytical Comparison Table | Feature | t-test | ANOVA | Chi-square | |---------|--------|-------|------------| | Data type | Continuous | Continuous | Categorical (counts) | | Groups compared | 1 or 2 | 3 or more | Any (2+ × 2+) | | Distribution | Normal | Normal | Multinomial / χ² | | Key statistic | t | F | χ² | | Post-hoc needed? | No (only 2 groups) | Yes (Tukey HSD) | No (but check residuals) | | Assumes independence? | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Paired version? | Yes (paired=TRUE) | Yes (repeated measures) | Yes (McNemar's test) | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Statistical Test Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Define Research Question"] --> B{"Is response variable
continuous?"} B -- "NO, categorical" --> C{"All expected
frequencies >= 5?"} B -- "YES, continuous" --> D{"How many groups
to compare?"} C -- "YES" --> C1["Chi-Square Test of
Independence / Goodness-of-Fit
R: chisq.test()"] C -- "NO" --> C2["Fisher's Exact Test
R: fisher.test()"] D -- "1 group" --> D1{"Data normally
distributed?"} D -- "2 groups" --> E{"Independent
or paired?"} D -- "3 or more groups" --> F{"Normal &
equal variance?"} D1 -- "YES" --> D1a["One-Sample t-test
R: t.test(x, mu=mu0)"] D1 -- "NO" --> D1b["Wilcoxon Signed-Rank
R: wilcox.test(x, mu=mu0)"] E -- "Independent" --> E1{"Equal variance?"} E -- "Paired" --> E2["Paired t-test
or Wilcoxon Signed-Rank
R: t.test paired=TRUE"] E1 -- "YES" --> E1a["Two-Sample t-test
R: t.test(x ~ g)"] E1 -- "NO" --> E1b["Welch's t-test
R: t.test(x ~ g, var.equal=FALSE)"] F -- "YES" --> F1["One-Way ANOVA
+ Tukey HSD
R: aov() + TukeyHSD()"] F -- "NO" --> F2["Kruskal-Wallis Test
R: kruskal.test()"] C1 --> G["Report: p-value, effect size,
confidence interval"] C2 --> G D1a --> G D1b --> G E1a --> G E1b --> G E2 --> G F1 --> G F2 --> G ``` ### 4.2 Hypothesis Testing Conceptual Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HYP["HYPOTHESIS FORMULATION"] H0["H0: Null Hypothesis
No effect / No difference
Status quo"] H1["H1: Alternative Hypothesis
Real effect / Difference exists
Research claim"] end subgraph DATA["DATA COLLECTION & ASSUMPTION CHECK"] D1["Shapiro-Wilk Test
Normality check"] D2["Levene's Test
Equal variance check"] D3["Sample size n >= 30
CLT applicability"] end subgraph CALC["STATISTIC COMPUTATION"] S1["Compute Test Statistic
t, F, or chi-square"] S2["Determine p-value
from reference distribution"] end subgraph DEC["DECISION MODULE"] C1{"p-value
alpha = 0.05?"} R1["REJECT H0
Statistically significant
evidence supports H1"] R2["FAIL TO REJECT H0
Insufficient evidence
H0 remains tenable"] end H0 --> D1 H1 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> C1 C1 -- "YES" --> R1 C1 -- "NO" --> R2 ``` ### 4.3 Errors in Hypothesis Testing (Type I vs Type II) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph REAL["REALITY (Unknown)"] RH0["H0 is TRUE
No real effect"] RH1["H1 is TRUE
Real effect exists"] end subgraph DEC["OUR DECISION (Based on data)"] DH0["Fail to Reject H0
Conclude no effect"] DH1["Reject H0
Conclude effect exists"] end RH0 -->|"Correct Decision
Probability = 1 - alpha"| DH0 RH0 -->|"Type I Error
False Positive
Probability = alpha = 0.05"| DH1 RH1 -->|"Type II Error
False Negative
Probability = beta"| DH0 RH1 -->|"Correct Decision
Statistical Power
Probability = 1 - beta"| DH1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Critical Concept:** Statistical power ($1 - \beta$) is the probability of correctly rejecting a false H₀. Studies with low power (typically n < 20 per group) may fail to detect real effects. **Alpha (α)** is the Type I error rate; **Beta (β)** is the Type II error rate. They have an inverse relationship — reducing one increases the other. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between parametric and non-parametric statistical tests. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Parametric Tests | Non-Parametric Tests | |--------|------------------|---------------------| | Distribution assumption | Assumes normal distribution | No specific distribution assumed | | Data type | Continuous (interval/ratio) | Ordinal or non-normal continuous | | Power | Higher (when assumptions hold) | Lower (more conservative) | | Examples | t-test, ANOVA, Pearson correlation | Wilcoxon signed-rank, Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis, Spearman correlation | Non-parametric tests are based on **ranks** rather than raw values, making them suitable for **small samples** or when normality is violated. *(3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for examples)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **State the null and alternative hypotheses for testing whether the mean lifetime of a new electric bulb differs from the claimed 1000 hours.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Let μ = true mean lifetime of the new electric bulb. - **Null Hypothesis (H₀):** The mean lifetime equals the claimed value. Formally: $\mu = 1000$ hours. - **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁):** The mean lifetime differs from the claimed value. Formally: $\mu \neq 1000$ hours. This is a **two-tailed test** because the alternative is stated as "differs" (could be higher OR lower). The appropriate test is the **one-sample t-test** (or z-test if σ is known, n is large). R command: `t.test(bulb_lifetimes, mu = 1000)` *(3 marks: 1 for H₀, 1 for H₁, 1 for identifying the test type)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a)** A pharmaceutical company claims that its new drug reduces systolic blood pressure by 15 mmHg on average. In a clinical trial of 25 patients, the sample mean reduction was 13.2 mmHg with a sample standard deviation of 4.5 mmHg. Test the company's claim at α = 0.05 using a one-sample t-test. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain the assumptions of the chi-square test of independence. A researcher wants to test if smoking habit (Smoker/Non-smoker) is independent of lung disease status (Disease/No Disease) in a sample of 200 individuals. Write the R code to perform this test given a contingency table named `smoke_disease`. **[7 marks]** --- ### **Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: State Hypotheses** (1 mark) $$H_0: \mu = 15 \text{ mmHg (claim is true)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu \neq 15 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Step 2: Compute the test statistic** (2 marks) $$ t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{13.2 - 15}{4.5/\sqrt{25}} = \frac{-1.8}{4.5/5} = \frac{-1.8}{0.9} = -2.00 $$ **Step 3: Determine critical value** (1 mark) Degrees of freedom: $df = n - 1 = 24$ Critical value for two-tailed test at α = 0.05: $t_{0.025, 24} = \pm 2.064$ **Step 4: Decision and conclusion** (2 marks) Since $\vert t_{calc} \vert = 2.00 < 2.064$, we **fail to reject H₀**. At the 5% significance level, there is insufficient evidence to contradict the company's claim that the drug reduces blood pressure by 15 mmHg on average. **Step 5: R code** (1 mark) ```r reduction <- c(...) # 25 patient reduction values t.test(reduction, mu = 15, conf.level = 0.95) ``` --- ### **Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks** **Assumptions of Chi-Square Test of Independence:** (3 marks) 1. **Independence of observations** — Each subject contributes to only one cell. 2. **Adequate expected frequency** — All expected cell counts $E_{ij} \geq 5$. (If violated, use Fisher's exact test.) 3. **Mutually exclusive categories** — A subject belongs to exactly one cell. 4. **Random sampling** — Data must come from a random sample. 5. **Categorical (nominal) data** — Data must be in counts/frequencies, not percentages. **R Code:** (4 marks) ```r # If smoke_disease is a matrix or table smoke_disease <- matrix(c(...), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE, dimnames = list(c("Disease", "No_Disease"), c("Smoker", "Non_Smoker"))) # Verify expected frequencies chisq.test(smoke_disease)$expected # Perform the test result <- chisq.test(smoke_disease, correct = TRUE) print(result) print(result$observed) # observed frequencies print(result$expected) # expected frequencies print(result$residuals) # Pearson residuals ``` **Decision Rule:** If `p-value < 0.05`, reject H₀ and conclude that smoking habit and lung disease status are associated. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Three different teaching methods (Traditional, Online, Hybrid) are applied to three groups of 8 students each, and their exam scores are recorded. Conduct a one-way ANOVA test at α = 0.05 and write the corresponding R code. **[7 marks]** **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the Wilcoxon signed-rank test. When is it preferred over the paired t-test? Write its R syntax. **[7 marks]** --- ### **Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Hypotheses** (1 mark) $$H_0: \mu_{Traditional} = \mu_{Online} = \mu_{Hybrid}$$ $$H_1: \text{At least one mean differs}$$ **Step 2: ANOVA Table Structure** (2 marks) | Source | SS | df | MS | F | |--------|------|-----|------|------| | Between groups | 320 | 2 | 160 | **5.60** | | Within groups | 600 | 21 | 28.57 | | | Total | 920 | 23 | | | **Step 3: Decision** (1 mark) Critical value: $F_{0.05, 2, 21} = 3.47$ Since $F_{calc} = 5.60 > 3.47$, **reject H₀**. Teaching method significantly affects exam scores. **Step 4: Post-hoc Tukey HSD** (1 mark) This is required because ANOVA tells us *something* differs but not *which* groups differ. **Step 5: R Code** (2 marks) ```r scores <- c(72, 75, 78, 80, 74, 77, 79, 76, # Traditional 82, 85, 80, 88, 84, 86, 83, 87, # Online 78, 80, 75, 82, 79, 81, 77, 80) # Hybrid method <- factor(rep(c("Traditional", "Online", "Hybrid"), each = 8)) df <- data.frame(scores, method) # ANOVA anova_result <- aov(scores ~ method, data = df) summary(anova_result) # Check assumptions shapiro.test(residuals(anova_result)) # normality of residuals car::leveneTest(scores ~ method, data = df) # equal variance # Post-hoc TukeyHSD(anova_result) plot(TukeyHSD(anova_result)) ``` --- ### **Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks** **Concept:** (2 marks) The Wilcoxon signed-rank test is a **non-parametric** alternative to the paired t-test. It is used to test whether the **median of paired differences** is zero. It works by: 1. Computing differences $d_i = x_i - y_i$ for each pair. 2. Discarding zero differences. 3. Ranking the absolute differences $|d_i|$. 4. Assigning the original sign (+ or −) back to each rank. 5. Summing positive ranks ($W^+$) and negative ranks ($W^-$). 6. The test statistic is $W = \min(W^+, W^-)$. **Example:** (2 marks) A before-after study measures blood pressure in 10 patients before and after a 6-week yoga program. The paired differences are: −8, −5, −3, −10, −2, −6, −4, −7, 0, −5. Since n = 10 is small and normality cannot be reliably assumed, Wilcoxon signed-rank is preferred over paired t-test. **When preferred over paired t-test:** (1 mark) - Sample size is small (n < 30). - Differences are not normally distributed (verified via Shapiro-Wilk on $d_i$). - Data is on an ordinal scale. - Data contains outliers. **R Syntax:** (2 marks) ```r # Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test before <- c(140, 135, 142, 138, 145, 140, 137, 141, 139, 143) after <- c(132, 130, 139, 128, 143, 134, 133, 134, 139, 138) wilcox.test(before, after, paired = TRUE, exact = FALSE) # For one-sample case (testing against a hypothesized median) wilcox.test(differences, mu = 0, exact = FALSE) ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mis-stating hypotheses** — Board students frequently write H₁ before H₀, or write H₁ with an "=" sign. Always write H₀ first, then H₁. H₁ must NEVER contain equality. > 2. **Forgetting assumptions** — A test statistic alone is incomplete. List at least 2 assumptions (normality, independence, equal variance). > 3. **Conflating p-value with α** — p-value is a *sample statistic* (computed from data), α is *pre-set* by the researcher. Do not equate them. > 4. **Wrong test selection** — ANOVA is for 3+ groups; using a t-test repeatedly for multiple pairwise comparisons inflates Type I error. Always use Tukey HSD. > 5. **Forgetting `paired = TRUE`** — In `t.test(x, y, paired = TRUE)`, omitting this flag silently runs an unpaired test — a 2-mark deduction guaranteed. > 6. **Chi-square with small expected counts** — If any $E_{ij} < 5$, the chi-square approximation is invalid; use Fisher's exact test instead. > 7. **Using `var.equal = TRUE` blindly** — This is the older Student t-test; Welch's test (`var.equal = FALSE`, the R default) is almost always safer when in doubt. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 (Statistical Tests)** - ✅ **Test selection** depends on: (1) data type (continuous vs categorical), (2) number of groups, (3) paired vs unpaired, (4) normality/variance assumptions. - ✅ **One-sample t-test**: tests if a sample mean differs from a known/hypothesized value; R: `t.test(x, mu = μ₀)`. - ✅ **Two-sample t-test**: Welch's version (default in R) does not assume equal variance; for equal variance, use `var.equal = TRUE`. - ✅ **Paired t-test**: requires `paired = TRUE`; used when the same subject is measured twice (before/after). - ✅ **One-way ANOVA**: for comparing 3+ group means; R: `summary(aov(y ~ group, data = df))`. Always follow with **Tukey HSD** if significant. - ✅ **Chi-square test of independence**: for two categorical variables; R: `chisq.test(contingency_table)`. Assumes $E_{ij} \geq 5$. - ✅ **Fisher's exact test**: use when expected counts < 5; R: `fisher.test()`. - ✅ **Wilcoxon signed-rank** (non-parametric paired): `wilcox.test(x, y, paired = TRUE)`; used when normality fails for paired data. - ✅ **Mann-Whitney U / Wilcoxon rank-sum** (non-parametric unpaired): `wilcox.test(x ~ group)`. - ✅ **Kruskal-Wallis** (non-parametric ANOVA): `kruskal.test(y ~ group)`. - ✅ **Always check assumptions** before applying parametric tests: `shapiro.test(x)` for normality, `car::leveneTest(y ~ group)` for equal variance. - ✅ **Decision language**: "reject H₀" or "fail to reject H₀" — NEVER "accept H₀". - ✅ **p-value interpretation**: The probability, *assuming H₀ is true*, of observing data as extreme as (or more extreme than) what was obtained. - ✅ **Type I error** (α): rejecting a true H₀ (false positive). **Type II error** (β): failing to reject a false H₀ (false negative). **Power** = $1 - \beta$. - ✅ **Degrees of freedom**: t-test → $n-1$ (one sample) or $n_1+n_2-2$ (two sample); ANOVA → $(k-1, N-k)$; chi-square → $(r-1)(c-1)$.

topics.title: Power Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCDT604 - DATA VISUALIZATION AND PROGRAMMING WITH R
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power of a Statistical Test (β-complement):** The **Power of a Test** is defined as the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis ($H_0$). Mathematically, it is expressed as:
> $$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta$$
> where $\beta$ is the probability of committing a **Type II Error** (failing to reject $H_0$ when $H_0$ is actually false).

In the framework of the **Neyman-Pearson Lemma** (a cornerstone of KTU Module 4), every hypothesis test partitions the sample space into two mutually exclusive regions: the **Acceptance Region (AR)** and the **Critical Region (CR)**. The Power of the test is precisely the probability that the test statistic falls inside the **Critical Region** when the *alternative hypothesis ($H_1$)* is true.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Power Tests bridge classical inferential statistics with applied data science using R. The official PBCDT604 syllabus emphasizes computing statistical power using base R functions such as `power.t.test()`, `pwr` package functions, and visualizing **Power Curves** for varying sample sizes and effect sizes.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Example)

Imagine you are a **smoke detector (smoke alarm)** installed in a house. There are two states of reality:
- **Reality 1 (Null Hypothesis $H_0$):** There is **NO fire** in the house.
- **Reality 2 (Alternative Hypothesis $H_1$):** There **IS a fire** in the house.

The smoke detector has two possible behaviors:
- **Beep (Reject $H_0$):** Alarm sounds → Decision: "There is fire!"
- **Silent (Fail to Reject $H_0$):** No alarm → Decision: "No fire."

| Detector Behavior | Reality: No Fire ($H_0$ True) | Reality: Fire ($H_1$ True) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Beeps** | False Alarm (**Type I Error**, $\alpha$) | Correct Detection (**Power**, $1-\beta$) |
| **Silent** | Correct Silence ($1-\alpha$) | Missed Fire (**Type II Error**, $\beta$) |

**The Power of the smoke detector** is exactly its ability to beep *when there actually IS a fire*. A **powerful test** is a sensitive test — one that rarely misses a true effect. A **weak test** is like a faulty, deaf smoke detector that misses real fires.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb in KTU Board Exams:** Always state the four possible outcomes as a 2×2 Decision Matrix when explaining Type I, Type II errors, and Power together.

---

### 1.3 Standard Statistical Metrics

- **Significance Level ($\alpha$):** Pre-fixed at **0.05** (5%) for most KTU-level problems; sometimes **0.01**.
- **Effect Size ($d$):** Standardized magnitude of the difference (Cohen's $d$, $r$, $f$, $\omega^2$).
- **Sample Size ($n$):** The number of observations in each group.
- **Statistical Power Target:** Conventionally set at **0.80** (80%) in social/behavioral research — meaning a **20% chance** of missing a real effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict KTU 2024 Convention:** Whenever the question says "compute the power of the test", it implies you must identify the test (Z-test, t-test, chi-square, F-test, proportion test, etc.), extract the parameter values, and substitute them into the correct power formula.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Hypothesis Testing

The Power of a Test sits at the intersection of four fundamental quantities. Understanding their interrelationship is essential for KTU Module 4 problems:

1. **Significance Level ($\alpha$):** The probability of rejecting $H_0$ when it is true.
2. **Type II Error Probability ($\beta$):** The probability of failing to reject $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true.
3. **Power ($1 - \beta$):** The probability of correctly rejecting $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true.
4. **Effect Size:** The standardized magnitude of the deviation from $H_0$.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

For a **two-sided Z-test** of a population mean, with $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ vs. $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$, and known standard deviation $\sigma$, the rejection region is:

$$|Z| > Z_{\alpha/2} \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$$

If the true mean is $\mu_1 \neq \mu_0$, the **Power** is computed as:

$$\text{Power} = P\left(|Z| > Z_{\alpha/2} \mid \mu = \mu_1\right) = 1 - \beta$$

In the **one-sided test** ($H_0: \mu \leq \mu_0$ vs. $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$):

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P\left(Z > Z_{\alpha} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)$$

where $\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0$ is the true effect (shift from null).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **Power formulas** for the most frequently tested scenarios in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 examinations.

| Test Type | Null Hypothesis | Power Formula | Effect Size Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **One-Sample Z-Test (Two-sided)** | $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ | $\text{Power} = \Phi\left(Z_{\alpha/2} + \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right) + \Phi\left(Z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\delta$ |
| **One-Sample Z-Test (One-sided)** | $H_0: \mu \leq \mu_0$ | $\text{Power} = \Phi\left(-Z_{\alpha} + \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\delta$ |
| **Two-Sample Z-Test** | $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ | $\text{Power} = \Phi\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} + \frac{\delta\sqrt{\frac{n}{2}}}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\delta$ |
| **One-Sample t-Test** | $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ | $\text{Power} = P\left(t > t_{\alpha, n-1} \mid \delta\right)$ (uses non-central t) | Cohen's $d = \delta/\sigma$ |
| **Two-Sample t-Test** | $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ | Uses non-central $t$-distribution with df $= 2n-2$ | Cohen's $d$ |
| **One-Proportion Z-Test** | $H_0: p = p_0$ | $\text{Power} = \Phi\left(\frac{\vert p_1 - p_0 \vert \sqrt{n} - Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)}}{\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)}}\right)$ | $\vert p_1 - p_0 \vert$ |
| **Chi-Square Test (Variance)** | $H_0: \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2$ | Uses non-central $\chi^2$ with $\lambda = n(\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_0^2)^2 / (2\sigma_0^4)$ | $w = \sigma_1/\sigma_0$ |
| **F-Test (ANOVA)** | $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \dots = \mu_k$ | Uses non-central F-distribution, $\lambda = \sum n_i(\mu_i - \bar{\mu})^2 / \sigma^2$ | Cohen's $f$ |
| **Correlation Test** | $H_0: \rho = 0$ | Uses Fisher's z-transformation, $z' = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\left(\tfrac{1+\rho}{1-\rho}\right)$ | Fisher's $z'$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Notation Warning for KTU Exams:** Do not confuse $\beta$ (Type II Error probability) with the regression coefficient $\beta$ in linear models. Always use $1-\beta$ for Power, and write Type II error as "$\beta$" only inside the hypothesis testing context.

---

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Power (The "4 Levers")

Power is governed by four mutually interactive parameters. Knowing these is essential for solving **"Design an experiment to achieve Power = 0.80"** type questions:

| # | Factor | Relationship | Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | **Sample Size ($n$)** | Power $\uparrow$ as $n \uparrow$ | Larger samples detect smaller effects. |
| **2** | **Effect Size ($\delta$ or $d$)** | Power $\uparrow$ as effect size $\uparrow$ | Big effects are easy to detect. |
| **3** | **Significance Level ($\alpha$)** | Power $\uparrow$ as $\alpha \uparrow$ | Stricter tests ($\alpha = 0.01$) lose power. |
| **4** | **Variability ($\sigma$ or $s$)** | Power $\downarrow$ as $\sigma \uparrow$ | Noisy data reduces sensitivity. |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Power analysis is mandatory in clinical trials (FDA Phase II/III), A/B testing in tech companies, quality control in manufacturing, and machine-learning experimental design. Underpowered studies waste resources and produce non-replicable results — the very crisis that triggered the "replication crisis" in psychology.

---

### 2.5 Power of the Test Using R

In R, base R provides `power.t.test()`, `power.prop.test()`, and the `pwr` package (by Stéphane Champely) provides a comprehensive suite. The **critical output parameters** are: `n`, `delta`, `sd`, `sig.level`, `power`, and `type`.

```r
# Example: One-sample t-test power calculation
power.t.test(n = 30, delta = 0.5, sd = 1, sig.level = 0.05,
             type = "one.sample", alternative = "two.sided")$power
```

```r
# Example: Two-sample t-test using the 'pwr' package
install.packages("pwr")
library(pwr)
pwr.t.test(n = 30, d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")$power
```

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power of a One-Sided Z-Test (Board-Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A sample of size $n = 25$ is drawn from a normal population with $\sigma = 4$. The test is $H_0: \mu = 50$ vs. $H_1: \mu > 50$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. If the true mean is $\mu_1 = 52$, compute the Power of the test.

**Step 1 — Identify the test statistic and critical value.**
The test statistic under $H_0$ is:
$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{X} - 50}{4/\sqrt{25}} = \frac{\bar{X} - 50}{0.8}$$

For a **one-sided (right-tailed)** test at $\alpha = 0.05$:
$$Z_{0.05} = 1.645$$

**Step 2 — Determine the rejection region in terms of $\bar{X}$.**
We reject $H_0$ when $Z > 1.645$, i.e., when:
$$\frac{\bar{X} - 50}{0.8} > 1.645 \implies \bar{X} > 50 + 1.645 \times 0.8 = 51.316$$

**Step 3 — Compute the probability under the true mean $\mu_1 = 52$.**
Power is the probability that $\bar{X} > 51.316$ when $\mu = 52$:
$$\text{Power} = P(\bar{X} > 51.316 \mid \mu = 52)$$

The sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ under $\mu = 52$ is $N(52, 0.8^2) = N(52, 0.64)$.

Standardizing:
$$Z^* = \frac{51.316 - 52}{0.8} = \frac{-0.684}{0.8} = -0.855$$

**Step 4 — Look up the standard normal probability.**
$$\text{Power} = P(Z > -0.855) = 1 - \Phi(-0.855) = \Phi(0.855)$$

From the Z-table:
$$\Phi(0.855) \approx 0.8038$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**
$$\boxed{\text{Power} = 0.8038 \approx 80.38\%}$$

This means: if the true mean is $\mu_1 = 52$, this test has an **80.38% chance** of correctly detecting the difference from $H_0$.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Computing Sample Size for Given Power

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A researcher wants to detect an effect size of Cohen's $d = 0.4$ using a two-sample t-test (two-sided) at $\alpha = 0.05$ with Power $= 0.80$. Determine the required sample size per group.

**Step 1 — Recall the formula connecting $n$, $d$, $\alpha$, and Power.**
For a two-sample t-test, the approximation (using normal quantiles) is:
$$n \approx 2 \left( \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}}{d} \right)^2$$

where:
- $Z_{\alpha/2} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96$
- $Z_{\beta} = Z_{0.20} = 0.8416$ (since $1 - \beta = 0.80 \implies \beta = 0.20$)
- $d = 0.4$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values into the formula.**
$$n \approx 2 \left( \frac{1.96 + 0.8416}{0.4} \right)^2$$

Computing the numerator:
$$1.96 + 0.8416 = 2.8016$$

Computing the ratio:
$$\frac{2.8016}{0.4} = 7.004$$

Squaring and doubling:
$$n \approx 2 \times (7.004)^2 = 2 \times 49.056 = 98.112$$

**Step 3 — Round up to the next integer (you cannot have fractional samples).**
$$n = 99 \text{ samples per group}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using R's `pwr` package.**
```r
library(pwr)
pwr.t.test(d = 0.4, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")$n
# Output: 98.32771
```
R returns $n \approx 98.33$, which rounds up to **$n = 99$** per group.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always round UP sample sizes.** The conservative principle: rounding down risks losing the targeted power.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Power of a Two-Sided Test (General Case)

For a **two-sided Z-test** of $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ vs. $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$:

**Step 1 — The two-sided rejection region is:**
$$|Z| > Z_{\alpha/2} \iff Z < -Z_{\alpha/2} \text{ or } Z > Z_{\alpha/2}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent region in terms of $\bar{X}$:**
$$\bar{X} < \mu_0 - Z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \quad \text{or} \quad \bar{X} > \mu_0 + Z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Power under true mean $\mu_1 \neq \mu_0$:**
$$\text{Power} = P\left(\bar{X} < \mu_0 - Z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \mid \mu = \mu_1\right) + P\left(\bar{X} > \mu_0 + Z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \mid \mu = \mu_1\right)$$

**Step 4 — Standardize using the sampling distribution $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma^2/n)$:**
$$\text{Power} = \Phi\left(\frac{\mu_0 - \mu_1 - Z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\right) + \left[1 - \Phi\left(\frac{\mu_0 - \mu_1 + Z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Let $\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0$ and simplify (note the sign flip):**
$$\text{Power} = \Phi\left(Z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right) + \Phi\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)$$

Using the identity $\Phi(-x) = 1 - \Phi(x)$:
$$\text{Power} = \Phi\left(Z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right) + 1 - \Phi\left(Z_{\alpha/2} + \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form (the KTU-exam-ready expression):**
$$\boxed{\text{Power} = \Phi\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} + \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right) + \Phi\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)}$$

For a **positive $\delta$**, the second term is usually negligible, and the first term dominates — giving roughly the same answer as the one-sided test.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Two-Sided Power Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** $n = 36$, $\sigma = 6$, $H_0: \mu = 100$, $H_1: \mu \neq 100$, $\alpha = 0.05$, true mean $\mu_1 = 104$. Find Power.

**Solution:**

Step 1: $\delta = 104 - 100 = 4$, $\alpha = 0.05$, $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

Step 2: Compute $\frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}$:
$$\frac{4 \times \sqrt{36}}{6} = \frac{4 \times 6}{6} = 4$$

Step 3: Substitute into the formula:
$$\text{Power} = \Phi(1.96 + 4) + \Phi(1.96 - 4) = \Phi(5.96) + \Phi(-2.04)$$

Step 4: From the Z-table:
$$\Phi(5.96) \approx 1.0000, \quad \Phi(-2.04) \approx 0.0207$$

Step 5: Final answer:
$$\text{Power} \approx 1.0000 + 0.0207 = 1.0207$$

Since probability cannot exceed 1, we **truncate** at:
$$\boxed{\text{Power} \approx 1.00 \text{ (essentially certain rejection)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Students often add two tail probabilities and forget to truncate at 1. Probability values are always bounded by $[0, 1]$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow in Hypothesis Testing (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Draw Random Sample"] --> B["Formulate H0 and H1"]
    B --> C["Compute Test Statistic e.g. Z, t, chi-square, F"]
    C --> D{"Compare p-value with alpha"}
    D -- "p less than alpha" --> E["Reject H0 Decision"]
    D -- "p greater than or equal to alpha" --> F["Fail to Reject H0 Decision"]
    E --> G{"Reality Check"}
    F --> G
    G -- "H0 is Actually TRUE" --> H["Correct Decision OR Type I Error alpha"]
    G -- "H1 is Actually TRUE" --> I["Correct Decision POWER 1 minus beta OR Type II Error beta"]
    H --> J["Confidence Level 1 minus alpha"]
    I --> K["Conclusion Report"]
    J --> K
    styleA[fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc]
    styleE[fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000]
    styleF[fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600]
    styleI[fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900]
    styleK[fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333333]
```

### 4.2 Power Curve Architecture: Visualization in R

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        nodeA["Sample Size n"]
        nodeB["Effect Size d"]
        nodeC["Significance alpha"]
        nodeD["Test Type one/ two sample"]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE["POWER ENGINE"]
        nodeE["Non-Central Distribution"]
        nodeF["Z OR t OR F Lookup"]
        nodeG["Integration over CR"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUTS"]
        nodeH["Power value"]
        nodeI["Power Curve Plot"]
        nodeJ["OC Curve Plot"]
        nodeK["Sample Size Table"]
    end
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeK
    styleINPUTS[fill:#ddeeff,stroke:#003366]
    styleENGINE[fill:#ffeecc,stroke:#cc6600]
    styleOUTPUTS[fill:#ddffdd,stroke:#006600]
```

### 4.3 R Code Snippet for Visualizing Power Curves

```r
library(pwr)

# Create a sequence of sample sizes
n_seq <- seq(10, 200, by = 5)

# Compute power for each sample size using Cohen's d = 0.4
power_values <- sapply(n_seq, function(n) {
  pwr.t.test(n = n, d = 0.4, sig.level = 0.05,
             type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")$power
})

# Plot the Power Curve
plot(n_seq, power_values, type = "l", col = "blue", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Sample Size (n per group)",
     ylab = "Statistical Power",
     main = "Power Curve for Two-Sample t-Test (Cohen's d = 0.4)")
abline(h = 0.80, col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend("bottomright", legend = c("Power Curve", "Power = 0.80 Threshold"),
       col = c("blue", "red"), lty = c(1, 2), lwd = 2)
```

This generates a monotonically increasing **Power Curve** — a classical KTU visualization where you can graphically read off the minimum sample size needed to achieve the 80% power benchmark.
